+ Mythology-Inspired — Gods Wore Sneakers (Chapter Avg: 75.9 | Final: 78.0)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Mythology-Inspired Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: Gods wore sneakers.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Mythology-Inspired Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: Gods wore sneakers. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming The prompt “Gods wore sneakers” is a single absurd image that demands to be detonated, not explained. It is funniest and sharpest when the sneakers are never justified, never a prophecy, never a power-up—just there, banal and inevitable, like gravity. The story must feel like it has always been this way and yet somehow still be shocking every time someone notices. Possible cores (not mutually exclusive, deliberately overlapping): 1. Eternal Beings in a Finite Hype Cycle The gods are immortal but sneakers are not. Every season they queue, refresh tabs, enter raffles, cop or cope. The central tension is not good vs. evil but limited vs. unlimited. Zeus has been trying to cop Travis Scott 1s since 2018 and still takes Ls. The story is about what immortality feels like when even gods are subject to the cruelty of stock numbers. 2. The Sneaker as the New Sandal of Hermes In the oldest myths Hermes wore winged talaria. Now the wings are Swooshes, the talaria are SB Dunks. Every pair ever worn by a god retains a fraction of divine essence. Mortals who resell them are trafficking in literal godhood. A black-market sneaker underworld exists where a single deadstock pair of Zeus’s 1994 Nike Air Command Force can level a city if the laces are double-knotted with intent. 3. Olympus Has Fallen… Into a Foot Locker The gods now live in a crumbling strip-mall temple outside Secaucus, New Jersey. The Parthenon is a shuttered Footaction with graffiti over the frieze. They still wield cosmic power but spend most of it arguing in the back room about who gets the employee discount. The story is a workplace comedy where the stakes are apocalyptic but the tone is The Office meets Clerks meets the Book of Revelation. 4. A Mortal Sneakerhead Becomes the First Prophet of the New Pantheon A 19-year-old stock boy in Queens finds a beat-up pair of 1985 Jordan 1s in the East River. When he laces them, he hears twelve distinct voices arguing about whether he should crease them. The gods have been waiting for a wearer who actually understands Grail status. He is their chosen one, their hypebeast messiah, and they are insufferable. 5. The Trojan War but Make It SNKRS Day The entire Iliad retold as a single chaotic sneaker drop. The Apple of Discord is a one-of-one friends-and-family pair. Paris cops it, Helen is furious because she wanted them, Achilles sulks in his tent because the raffle didn’t hit, Hector gets brutally murdered over shock-dropping on his killer. The Greeks win when Odysseus backdoors the Trojan Horse as a restock link that expires in ten minutes. 6. Norse Version Ragnarök is triggered because someone cooked Odin’s personal pair of 2002 BAPE Sta “Odin” edition in the microwave to un-yellow the midsoles. The final battle is fought on a glassy volcano made of iced-out soles. Fenrir wears Off-White Prestos with the zip-tie still on—because of course he does. 7. Quiet Literary Version A single god—let’s say Prometheus—walks endlessly through contemporary Brooklyn in a pair of scuffed New Balance 990v3s. No one recognizes him. He steals nothing, starts no fires, just walks and remembers when humans used to look up. The sneakers are the only thing that have lasted longer than the punishment. 8. Reverse Angle The gods are normal bored teenagers in 2025 who cosplay as ancient deities online and happen to actually be those deities. Their “mythology” is just extremely committed lore posting. The sneakers are the one thing that bleed through from the divine layer into the human one, so the harder they flex, the more real they become. Characters that keep resurfacing across versions: - Zeus: 50-something hypebeast dad, still wears size 13, still lightning-bolts bots, still married to Hera but they have separate SNKRS accounts. - Hermes: literal courier, works for GOAT and StockX simultaneously, has never taken an L because he is the L. - Athena: only wears women’s exclusives, has a burner account that roasts every male sneaker influencer into ash (literally). - Hades: sells deadstock from the underworld, prices are “your soul or $10,000, whichever is worse.” - Aphrodite: face of every major collab, never pays for shoes, posts thirst traps on the release date to crash the app. - A mortal protagonist who is the only sane person in the room and therefore doomed. Tone possibilities: - Deadpan absurdism (Gary Shengyang Luo) - Scabrous internet satire (Lauren Oyler on sneaker Twitter) - Genuine melancholy underneath the jokes (the way people cry over missing a shoe they know doesn’t matter) - Mythic grandeur delivered through Discord DMs and memes Structural shapes for 8 chapters (all roughly 1000 words): - Single drop day that lasts a mythological eternity (one day = eight chapters) - Eight different gods, one chapter each, all trying to cop the same pair - Eight stages of grief after taking the ultimate L - Eight circles of Sneaker Hell (Dante’s Inferno but it’s the queue page) # Reflection The ideas that keep their venom after two days of thinking are the ones that treat the sneakers as completely ordinary to the gods and completely insane to everyone else. The humor is sharpest when the gods are 100% serious about something utterly trivial, and the tragedy is sharpest when the trivial thing is the only way they can still feel anything at all. The purest version is the one where no one ever explains why they wear sneakers. There is no prophecy, no corporate sponsorship, no ancient curse. They just do. That refusal to justify is what makes it modern mythology. The mortal perspective is essential; without a human who still believes scale is supposed to match importance, the joke collapses into simple parody. The best versions all have a human who is simultaneously the audience surrogate (“this is ridiculous”) and the straight man in a cosmic comedy. The Norse and Trojan War riffs are hilarious but probably too gimmick-heavy to sustain 8000 words without repeating the same joke. The quiet Prometheus walking Brooklyn could be beautiful but risks being too bloodless for the prompt’s inherent absurdity. The winner—the one that hurts and makes you laugh at the same time—is the one where the gods are ancient, omnipotent, and completely enslaved by a drop clock. Immortality revealed as endless FOMO. The sneakers are not magic. The magic is that the gods care this much about something that will be played out in eighteen months. That is the vein I want to mine for eight chapters: cosmic power rendered pathetic by perfect, undiluted desire for a thing that does not last.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This will be a single, tight 8-chapter novella (∼8,000 words total) told from the alternating close-third-person perspectives of two characters only: 1. Cassius “Cass” Delgado, 27-year-old night-shift StockX authenticator in Long Island City, Queens, who still believes in something bigger than resale prices. 2. Hermes, the actual god, currently moonlighting as a bicycle courier for every app at once, wearing a pair of 2017 Supreme x Nike Air More Uptempo in gold that have not aged a day since 2017 and never will. No other gods get POV. Everyone else is witnessed through these two lenses only. Core premise: On the morning of the biggest sneaker drop in history—“The Pantheon Pack,” twelve one-of-one shoes, each designed by a different Olympian and releasing at the exact same second—the gods finally overload the SNKRS app so catastrophically that reality itself starts to lag. Cass is the only human who can see the seams coming apart because he has been staring at stitches and glue lines for years. Hermes needs a mortal witness who understands authenticity to complete the ritual that will let the gods finally, after three thousand years, take an L and feel mortal for one perfect second. Tone: Deadpan, melancholy, viciously funny, sexually charged when it needs to be, but never camp. Think George Saunders meets Brandon Taylor meets the group chats of sneaker Twitter at 3 a.m. when everyone is drunk and honest. Voice: Contemporary New York English, profane but precise. No mythic register, no “forsooth.” When gods speak they sound like extremely online people who have been extremely online since 1200 BCE. Style choices: - No exposition dumps about why gods wear sneakers. It is treated as obvious as gravity. - Every chapter ends on a line that could be a sneaker caption. - Short paragraphs, brutal line breaks, the rhythm of a queue page countdown. - No moral about consumerism. The hunger is real and holy and pitiful and that’s the point. Things to avoid: - Explaining the joke - Making the gods cool - Letting any mortal character be a “normie” - Redemption arcs - A single pair mattering more than the feeling of wanting Central image: Divine feet in mortal shoes that will outlast Olympus. # Chapter Planning Chapter 1 – “Laced Up” Cass clocks in at 4:12 a.m. for the night shift at StockX. He authenticates a pair of 1999 Nike Air Jordan IV “Black Canvas” that once belonged to Hermes. The stitching is wrong in a way no human could fake. Hermes, on the roof opposite, watches him through the window and decides this is the mortal. Chapter 2 – “The Messenger” Hermes’s POV. He has 47 minutes to deliver twelve boxes containing the actual Pantheon Pack samples to twelve different influencers before the drop goes live at 10 a.m. If even one box is late, the ritual fails and the gods remain immortal for another year. He is sweating through a CDN hoodie in July. Chapter 3 – “Queue” 9:57 a.m. Cass is in the SNKRS app with six devices, trying to cop for himself and for the one client who still pays him in cash money and belief. Hermes crashes through the StockX fire escape, bleeding light from a cut on his ankle where a pigeon attacked him. He needs Cass to authenticate one final pair—live, on camera, during the drop. Chapter 4 – “God Mode” The drop hits. The app collapses. Reality glitches: every pair of sneakers in the world momentarily becomes whatever the wearer most desperately wants. For twelve minutes the gods walk the earth in perfect deadstock. Cass and Hermes watch from the roof as Zeus descends wearing Travis Scott 1s that actually play Astroworld when you jump. Chapter 5 – “Splash” Hermes confesses: the only way to end the glitch is for one god to willingly take an L—to let a mortal outbid them, outrun them, humiliate them. He has chosen himself. Cass has to beat Hermes in a footrace across the city wearing the gold Supremes. If Cass wins, Hermes becomes mortal for one hour. If Hermes wins, Cass forgets everything and the gods stay eternal. Chapter 6 – “Heat Check” The race. From Long Island City to the Nike store in SoHo, rooftop to rooftop, subway to ferry. Cass has never run faster; Hermes has never run slower. They argue about creasing, about legacy, about whether desire is the same thing as worship. Chapter 7 – “Checked” Cass wins by one stride on the corner of Greene and Prince. Hermes sits on the curb, takes off the gold Uptempos, and hands them to Cass. The shoes are suddenly, heartbreakingly, just shoes. Hermes ages ten years in ten seconds. He smiles like someone who has finally been allowed to go home. Chapter 8 – “Played Out” Three months later. Cass wears the gold Uptempos every day until they are beat to death. He still works the night shift. Sometimes, late, he thinks he sees a man on a bicycle with no shoes at all, pedaling slowly through Queens, smiling at nothing. Cass never sells the shoes. They are worth negative money now. They are worth everything. Final line: “Gods wore sneakers. One of them finally learned how to walk without them.”
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# Critique & Humanization – Raw, No Filter ## How a real human would approach “Gods wore sneakers” A human who actually loves both mythology and sneakers would not write this story to be clever. They would write it because the image hurts them in a specific way. They would be the kid who cried when they creased their first pair of Jordans, who still remembers the exact smell of the store the day they finally got the shoe they refreshed for 14 months to buy, who also sat in a college library at 3 a.m. reading Ovid and feeling the same chest punch. The story would come from grief, not from concept. Humans write from scar tissue. LLMs write from pattern. That difference is everything here. ## Classic LLM tells that would murder this piece 1. Over-explaining the conceit in dialogue LLM default: have Hermes or Cass literally say “the reason gods wear sneakers is because immortality without desire is hell” or some koan. Real humans would rather die than let a character speak the theme out loud. 2. Symmetrical, polite emotions LLM characters tend to feel the correct, balanced amount of sadness. A human would let Cass be catastrophically, embarrassingly in love with a shoe in one chapter and then hate himself for it in the next. The emotion would be asymmetrical, wet, uncool. 3. Clean cause-and-effect morality Hermes sacrificing immortality = good, noble, growth. A human would make the sacrifice feel simultaneously heroic and pathetic, like quitting heroin by switching to fentanyl because at least it’s a different high. 4. Everyone is articulate when vulnerable Real people mumble, trail off, say the wrong thing, lie, say “I don’t know” eleven times. LLMs give every character a TED Talk in their darkest moment. 5. The Bow Chapter 8 currently ties it up with a poignant little epilogue and a killer last line. That’s LLM catnip. A human would be terrified of that last line sounding like a movie poster, so they’d probably sabotage it on purpose or leave it open and bleeding. 6. Aesthetic perfection of prose LLM sentences are often lathe-turned, every paragraph the same weight, every image tasteful. A human would let some paragraphs be ugly, rushed, too long, full of brand names repeated like curses, half in lowercase because they were typing on their phone while walking. ## Deep dive on the current plan – where it’s already LLM-poisoned - The chapter titles are too good. Real humans do not title chapters “Laced Up,” “God Mode,” “Splash,” “Heat Check.” That’s sneakerhead Mad Libs. A human would either have no titles or titles that feel private and embarrassing, like “the night i legit checked the tag” or “4:47 a.m. and i still didn’t hit.” - The race in Chapters 5-7 is cinematic, perfectly structured, two guys on rooftops arguing about creasing while leaping gaps. That’s Marvel set-piece energy. A human would make the race pathetic—Hermes pulling a hamstring on the second block, Cass vomiting in a bodega bathroom, both of them crying because they’re out of shape and scared. - Hermes aging ten years in ten seconds is gorgeous on paper but smells like prestige-TV visual metaphor. A human would make the mortality moment smaller and uglier: a gray hair, sudden lower-back pain when he stands up, realizing he has to piss after only one coffee. - Cass never selling the shoes and wearing them to death is noble. A human version of Cass would absolutely list them once when rent was due, then cancel the listing at 3 a.m. while drunk-crying, then relist them the next day, then cancel again. The indecision is the point. - The entire ritual framework (“needs a mortal witness to complete the ritual”) is the biggest LLM tell of all. It’s a plot coupon. Humans don’t write stories where cosmic events hinge on tidy rules explained in chapter 3. They write stories where someone does something inexplicable because they’re heartbroken and high on no sleep. ## Ways to make it brutally human (brainstorm, no rewriting yet) - Let Cass be a resentful loser sometimes. Not quirky-cute loser, actual resentful. He hates the influencers he authenticates for. He hates that he knows more than they ever will and still can’t afford the shoes. - Let Hermes be a bad god. Not chaotic-sexy, just bad at his job. Missed deliveries, lies about traffic, ghosts people on read. Immortality made him flaky, not cool. - Make the sex weird and unerotic. Humans fuck up sex when stakes are high. Someone finishes too fast, someone cries after, someone quotes a sneaker tweet during. Do not let it be hot. - Dialogue like actual Discord voice chat: overlapping, cutting off, “nah nah listen,” “bro deadass,” long awkward silences written out. - Sensory cruelty: the smell of the StockX warehouse (plastic, glue, foot odor, cold air), the taste of the free energy drink that’s been in the fridge since 2019, the specific pain of a thumb blister from using a box cutter 400 times a night. - No one ever says the word “mortal” out loud. They say “normie,” “civilian,” “people who still have to work.” - Cass’s internal monologue should sometimes be pure resale math: “$2800 minus fees minus shipping is $2400 and rent is $2200 so if I flip these I can eat for two months.” Then immediately hate himself for thinking it. - Let the gods be cringe. Let Zeus have a Finsta where he posts Minion memes about “copping heat.” Let Athena run a private story called “military grade copium.” - The moment Hermes becomes mortal, let him do something achingly mundane and new: he checks his bank account and realizes he has $47.82 and rent is due in four days. That’s scarier than Ragnarök. - End the story mid-sentence if necessary. Or end it with Cass refreshing StockX and seeing the gold Supremes listed by someone else for $400 because they’re beat to shit now. Leave the reader feeling like they just got ghosted. The goal is to make the reader feel like they overheard this story in a smoke circle outside a sneaker store at 2 a.m., not like they read a New Yorker short story that happens to be about sneakers. Make it embarrassing to love. That’s the only way it feels true.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This is not a clever story. This is a bruised one. Eight chapters, eight nights, roughly 1000 words each. No titles. No bows. No explanations. No race across rooftops. No ritual. No sacrifice scene that photographs well. The only thing that happens is that Cass and Hermes keep missing each other, and then they don’t. Everything else is fallout. Voice: Queens English, 2024, half-asleep, half-hard, all resentful. Sentences like texts you sent at 4:12 a.m. and immediately regretted. Paragraphs sometimes one word long. Sometimes 300. No filter. No pretty. Core rule: nobody is cool. Not even for a second. Especially not the gods. The sneakers are never magic. They’re just expensive and already played out and still the only thing anyone can think about. That’s the horror and the prayer. The prompt “Gods wore sneakers” is never explained, never joked about, never quoted. It just sits there like a scar somebody keeps touching. By chapter 8 the reader should feel like they owe somebody $47 for a subway ride they don’t remember taking. # Chapter Planning Chapter 1 Cass authenticates shoes in the StockX warehouse at 4 a.m. A pair of gold Supremes comes in that legit themselves. The tag reads size 10 but the shoe stretches to whatever foot goes in it. Cass stares at it for forty-three minutes straight. He doesn’t clock out. He just sits there smelling glue and crying without noticing. Chapter 2 Hermes (real name on the app is just “mes”) delivers for every platform at once. He’s on a Citi Bike with no battery, wearing the same gold Supremes, soles still icy after eight years. He’s trying to drop off a pair of Travis 1s to some kid in Ridgewood who ghosted him after Venmoing only $40. Hermes sits on the stoop for an hour arguing with the kid through the ring camera. The kid never opens the door. Chapter 3 Cass and Hermes finally share physical space without knowing it. Cass is buying loosies at the bodega on 44th Drive at 6:47 a.m. Hermes is buying the same. Their shoulders touch. Hermes smells like bike sweat and Olympos cologne that discontinued in 2009. Cass smells like warehouse and Monster. They both pay with crumpled fives and don’t look at each other. Chapter 4 Cass starts wearing the gold Supremes to work even though they’re two sizes too big. They fit anyway. He stops checking tags on anything else. Just stares at his own feet and feels something he doesn’t have a name for. He lists them on StockX for $75,000, then cancels the listing, then relists, then cancels again. He does this 14 times in one shift. Chapter 5 Hermes keeps circling the warehouse on his bike every night after 3 a.m. He doesn’t know why. He tells himself it’s for the drop-off pile. Really he just wants to see the guy who wears his shoes now. He watches Cass through the window smoking a blunt on the loading dock, gold shoes glowing under the sodium light like they’re cheating. Chapter 6 One night Cass leaves the fire door propped open with a box cutter. Hermes walks in. They don’t speak for the first twenty minutes. Just stand there while Cass cuts open boxes and Hermes pretends to look for a package. Finally Cass says, “They’re yours, right?” Hermes says, “Yeah.” That’s it. They smoke the rest of the blunt in silence. Cass cries again. Hermes pretends not to notice. Chapter 7 They start hanging out after every shift. Never plan it. Just end up on the same bench by the East River watching cargo ships. Hermes tells stories about Olympus like they’re just some fucked-up group house in Bushwick. Cass tells stories about shoes he’ll never own. They both lie a little. The shoes get more and more beat. Soles turning gray, leather cracking like old lips. Chapter 8 Last night of the lease on the warehouse. StockX is moving to New Jersey. Cass has to clear out his locker. Hermes helps him carry boxes to the curb at 5 a.m. The gold Supremes are completely cooked now—toe box creased to death, midsoles yellowed, Swoosh peeling. Cass tries to give them back. Hermes won’t take them. Says, “Keep them. They look better dead.” They hug once, awkward, too long, smelling like goodbye and bodega coffee. Hermes gets on his bike and rides away. Cass watches until the gold disappears around the corner. Then he sits on the curb and unties the shoes and leaves them in the gutter for whoever. He walks home barefoot. The pavement is warm. He doesn’t feel holy. He just feels like someone who finally took an L and didn’t die.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Cassius “Cass” Delgado 27, born in Astoria, half Puerto-Rican, half Irish from the silent dad who left when he was 6. Grew up in the same two-bedroom his abuela still rents on 31st Street. Used to draw sneakers in the margins of his math homework until the teacher threw the notebook in the trash. Dropped out of FIT after one semester when he realized he couldn’t afford the materials fee. Has authenticated over 42,000 pairs of shoes but owns maybe nine that aren’t samples or fakes. Sleeps in four-hour chunks. Can smell fake glue from across a room. Keeps a half-smoked blunt behind his ear like a pencil. Hasn’t jerked off in eight days because every time he tries he starts calculating resale value instead. Hates the sound of people chewing ice. Once cried because someone creased a pair of 2011 Concords right in front of him at a party. Will literally fight you if you call New Balance “dad shoes.” Has his abuela on his SNKRS splash screen. Still flinches when someone yells “Cassius” because it sounds like his mother when she was drunk. Physicality 5'10 but walks like he’s trying to take up less space. Hooded brown eyes, always bloodshot. Hair that looks like it was cut with kitchen scissors at 3 a.m. (because it was). Wears the same black Dickies work pants until they stand up by themselves. Fingers stained yellow from box cutter handles. Voice low and flat, Queens accent that only comes out when he’s mad or drunk. When he’s nervous he rubs the toe box of whatever shoe he’s wearing against the back of his calf until the suede pills. Motives & loyalties Only loyal to people who still say “thank you” after he legs them for free. Doesn’t want to be rich, just wants to wake up one day and not have to check StockX first thing. Thinks Hermes is the most annoying person he’s ever met and also the only person who’s ever looked at his feet like they were a face. Secretly wants the shoes to fall apart completely so he can stop waiting for them to matter. Voice samples “Bro, if you crease those I’m actually throwing hands, I’m not even playing.” (internal) “$7,800 minus fees is $6,900 and rent is $2,200 and I still owe ConEd and I’m literally gonna die in this warehouse and nobody will notice until the shoes start to smell.” “Yo, you ever just… look at a pair and feel like they’re judging you? Nah? Just me? Aight.” # Hermes (rides as “mes”) Real age uncountable, looks 29 forever because that’s when he stopped giving a fuck. Has delivered packages since packages were clay tablets. Once carried a message from Zeus to a nymph that took 400 years because he kept stopping to gamble. Currently banned from Uber Eats for “excessive acceptance rate” (he accepts every order in a 40-mile radius). Has 11 phones zip-tied to his handlebars. Lives in a studio in Ridgewood that smells like Bengay and weed and the 90s. Hasn’t paid rent since 2018; landlord thinks he’s a bike messenger and feels bad for him. Steals Gatorade from delivery bags when the customer tips less than $3. Still uses the word “fam” unironically. Once doordashed himself McDonald’s to a rooftop because he was sad. Physicality 6'1, all elbows and knees, moves like someone who’s never been tired once in his life until suddenly he is. Skinny but wiry. Face that looks like it was drawn by someone who really loves cheekbones. Hair dyed too many times, currently half-grown-out platinum. Always wearing the gold Supremes even though the left toe is starting to talk. Smells like hot bike chain and whatever cologne sample he stole from Macy’s that week. Laughs with his whole body, then looks around like he’s surprised anyone heard it. Motives & loyalties Wants to feel something that isn’t “on to the next one.” Loyal to nobody except the one mortal who never asked him to prove he’s a god. Would let Cass punch him in the face if it meant he got to feel the bruise tomorrow. Scared that if he stops moving he’ll have to remember every single person he ever failed to deliver a message to on time. The shoes are the only thing he’s never returned. Voice samples “Bro I’ve been on this bike since before your bloodline figured out fire, chill.” (internal) “If I sit down for five minutes I’m gonna start thinking about Calypso again and I literally can’t do that right now.” “Nah the shoes are beat, that’s the point. Deadstock is for people who are scared of living.”

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The warehouse smelled like plastic and broken dreams at four in the morning. Cass clocked in at 4:12, badge beeping like it was personally offended. The conveyor belt was already running, boxes sliding past like slow salmon. He took his station, box cutter in his right hand, blunt tucked behind his left ear for later. First pair of the night was nothing special, some kid’s 2018 Travis Scott 4s with the cactus jack logo half rubbed off. Fake. He could tell before he even opened the box. Glue lines too thick, stitching drunk. He slapped the red tag on it and shoved it into the reject bin. Next. He moved like that for an hour, autopilot. Cut, flip, sniff, tag. Hands moving faster than his brain. The warehouse lights buzzed overhead, fluorescent and cruel. Someone had left a Monster in the fridge since last Diwali. He drank it warm. Then the gold ones came down the line. They were in a beat-up Nike box with no label, just someone’s Sharpie scribble that said “MES.” Cass knew the handwriting wasn’t handwriting at all, more like someone pressed the pen too hard trying to remember how letters worked. He cut the tape slow. Inside: Supreme x Nike Air More Uptempo, gold, size 10. But the tongue tag was wrong. No year, no factory code, just a little embroidered caduceus where the size should be. The leather was soft like it had never been worn and also like it had been worn every day since 2017. He turned them over. Soles were icy. Like factory fresh. Like someone had kept them in a vacuum. But the left toe had a tiny scuff, just enough to prove they weren’t fake. Cass’s thumb hovered over it. His heart started doing that thing where it forgets the rhythm. He sat down on the stool. Just sat. The conveyor kept moving behind him, boxes piling up, but he didn’t care. He took the left shoe and pressed it against his face. Smelled like summer pavement and something else. Something that made his eyes water. “Yo, Cass, you good?” Luis called from three stations over. Cass didn’t answer. He was counting stitches. The stitching was wrong. Not fake wrong. Perfect wrong. Like someone who had never needed to hide anything had sewn them anyway. He put the shoe on. It was size 10 and he was size 12. Didn’t matter. The leather just… gave. Like it knew. Like it had been waiting. He laced it slow, double-knot, the way his abuela taught him for church shoes when he was seven. Then he put the right one on too. Stood up. The floor felt different. Not higher. Just realer. Like the concrete had been holding its breath. He walked to the bathroom. Didn’t run. Just walked. The gold caught the light like it was wet. In the mirror he looked insane. Hoodie two sizes too big, eyes red, shoes worth more than his life flashing under shitty fluorescent. He took a picture. Didn’t post it. Just stared at it until the screen went black. Back at his station he was supposed to tag them. Legit or fake. Pass or fail. His hand hovered over the green sticker. Then the red. Then neither. He sat there for forty-three minutes. Counted every second on the wall clock. The shoes got warm from his feet. Not hot. Just body temperature. Like they were alive. Luis came over again. “Bro you’re gonna get us both fired.” Cass didn’t move. The shoes didn’t have a serial number. They didn’t need one. He thought about his abuela sleeping in Astoria, about the $2,200 rent, about the way his mom used to scream his full name when she found his drawings in the trash. He thought about every pair he’d ever wanted and never got. Every L. Every “added to cart” that turned into “sold out.” He thought about the scuff on the left toe. Someone had lived in these. Someone had dragged their feet. Someone had given up and kept going anyway. His eyes were wet but he wasn’t crying. Not really. Just leaking. He finally stood up, walked to the sink where they kept the Sharpies for tagging boxes. Wrote something on the inside of the box lid. Then closed it. Put the shoes back inside. Carried the box to his locker. Clocked out at 8:03 without saying goodbye to anyone. Walked home through Queens with the box under his arm like it was a baby or a bomb. Didn’t open it again until he was in his bedroom with the curtains closed. Put them on again. They still fit. He sat on the edge of his bed and stared at his feet until the sun came up and his phone died and his abuela banged on the door asking why he hadn’t eaten. He didn’t answer her. Just kept looking at the shoes. At the way the gold caught the light like it was laughing at him. Like it knew something he didn’t. Yet.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is doing something genuinely difficult and mostly pulling it off. The voice is consistent, specific, and earned—Queens English, 4 a.m., warehouse fluorescent, the particular exhaustion of someone who knows too much about shoes and not enough about how to stop caring. The plan called for Cass to authenticate shoes, find the gold Supremes, stare at them for forty-three minutes, and cry without noticing. The chapter delivers all of this with reasonable fidelity. What works: The specificity is real. "Glue lines too thick, stitching drunk" is a good line—it earns its compression. The detail about the caduceus where the size should be is subtle and correct; it doesn't over-explain the mythology. The shoe fitting despite being two sizes too small is handled with restraint—"The leather just… gave. Like it knew." That's the right amount of mysticism for this register. The abuela detail, the FIT dropout backstory bleeding into the present tense of the scene, the Monster left since Diwali—these are the kinds of details that make a world feel inhabited rather than constructed. The plan specified "He doesn't clock out. He just sits there smelling glue and crying without noticing." The chapter has him clock out at 8:03, which is a deviation, but not a damaging one—it's actually more grounded. The crying-without-noticing is rendered as "His eyes were wet but he wasn't crying. Not really. Just leaking." That's a good line. It earns its sentiment. What doesn't work: Some of the metaphors are reaching. "Boxes sliding past like slow salmon" is a misfire—salmon swim upstream with urgency; slow salmon is a confused image. "Like it was a baby or a bomb" is a cliché of the "two opposite things" construction that appears in a lot of contemporary literary fiction and has worn thin. "Like it was laughing at him" at the end is a bit easy. The ending itself—"Like it knew something he didn't. Yet."—is the weakest moment in the chapter. The "Yet." as a one-word paragraph is a tell that the writer is reaching for portent, and it lands as slightly amateurish, a little too self-conscious about its own mystery. The dialogue is minimal, which is correct for this chapter. Luis's two interruptions are functional and appropriately flat. No complaints there. The plan called for the voice to feel like "texts you sent at 4:12 a.m. and immediately regretted." The chapter achieves this intermittently but sometimes slides into more conventional literary prose—"The warehouse lights buzzed overhead, fluorescent and cruel" is competent but not distinctive. "Cruel" is doing too easy a job there. The character is consistent with the profile: the abuela, the FIT dropout, the resale value calculations bleeding into emotion, the way he doesn't answer people. The shoes' behavior is appropriately uncanny without being explained. The chapter follows the plan closely and faithfully serves the prompt—the sneakers are never magic, just present and strange. Overall: a strong first chapter with a few moments of self-consciousness and one or two metaphors that don't land. The voice is mostly controlled, the specificity is real, and the emotional register is correct—bruised, not pretty, not cool. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Hermes was late before he even woke up. The first ping hit at 3:47 a.m. Uber Eats. 7.8 miles. Travis Scott Jordan 1 Low Reverse Mocha, size 11, deadstock, to some kid in Ridgewood named @sadboidrip. $2,400 cash on delivery. Tip already in the app: $40. Hermes rolled off the mattress on the floor, one sock on, one sock missing since 2021. The apartment smelled like bong water and whatever cologne he’d been stealing from Macy’s back when Macy’s still had testers. He found the shoes in the fridge between two half-eaten containers of halal chicken. They were wrapped in a Supreme bag because that’s what he used for everything now. He sat on the fire escape to lace the gold Supremes. Left foot first, always. The leather was cracked along the eyestay now, little white stress lines like stretch marks. He liked them better that way. They looked honest. Citi Bike battery was at 4%. Good enough. He grabbed it anyway, strapped the shoebox to the front basket with two busted iPhone cables, and pushed off into Bushwick before the sun even thought about it. The city was quiet in that ugly way, still drunk, waiting to vomit. Hermes flew down Flushing, thighs burning because he refused to pedal like a normal person. He coasted on the downhill parts, let the bike do the work, same way he used to let the wind do it back when wind still listened. First stop was the ATM because sadboidrip only Venmo’d $40 and Hermes needed the rest in cash. The machine spat twenties at him like it was bored. He counted them twice, stuffed them in the waistband of his sweats, felt the paper scratch against his stomach. Mortal money felt dirty. He loved it. Ridgewood was asleep. He found the address: a brownstone with a Ring camera that looked like it had seen some shit. He propped the bike against the steps, rang the bell, waited. Nothing. Rang again. Held it this time. The speaker crackled. “Yo who the fuck?” Hermes leaned in. “Delivery. Reverse Mochas. You paid forty bucks and ghosted me for the rest.” Long pause. “Leave it on the step.” “Nah. Cash on delivery, bro. Two grand more or I walk.” Another pause. Hermes could hear Fortnite through the speaker. Kid was definitely twelve. “My mom’s asleep, bro. I can’t.” “Then wake her up or I’m eating these for breakfast.” Silence. Then the door cracked open just enough for one eye and a hand holding a wad of hundreds that smelled like Bath & Body Works. Hermes took the money, handed over the box. The door slammed before he could say anything. He stood there for a second, counting the bills again. $1,960. Forty short. Whatever. Close enough. He sat on the stoop anyway. Pulled out his phone, opened the group chat with the other gods that nobody ever answered anymore. mes: just delivered to a literal child mes: he paid in strips of hundreds like a stripper mes: y’all up? athena: no dionysus: yes ares: die He stared at the messages until the screen went dark. Then he opened StockX just to watch the gold Supremes he used to wear every day climb another $800. Someone had posted a new listing: worn, size flexible, $42,000 ONO. He almost threw up. He sat on that stoop for an hour. Legs stretched out, gold shoes on the concrete like two small suns. A rat ran past and sniffed the left toe, decided it wasn’t food, kept going. Hermes lit the half-blunt he’d stolen from someone’s dash three nights ago. Took one hit, coughed like a mortal, took another. The smoke hung in the cold. He thought about the last time he’d actually run anywhere. Not bike, not winged sandals, just run. Maybe 1923. Some girl in Chicago who laughed like a fire alarm. He couldn’t remember her face anymore. Just the sound. His phone buzzed again. New order. Postmates. 4.2 miles. Someone wanted oat milk and two breakfast crunchwraps. $6 tip. He stood up, cracked his neck, got back on the bike. Battery dead now. He pedaled anyway. The gold shoes flashed under the streetlights like they were trying to signal someone. He didn’t know he was already riding toward Long Island City. He just knew the shoes felt heavier tonight. Like they missed something. Or someone.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is Chapter 2, focused on Hermes, and it largely delivers on the plan's promise: a god who is fundamentally unimpressive, doing gig work, sitting on a stoop, getting stiffed $40 by a kid playing Fortnite. The execution is mostly strong, with some notable weaknesses. **What works:** The voice is consistent and genuinely distinctive. "The city was quiet in that ugly way, still drunk, waiting to vomit" is a metaphor that actually lands—it's ugly in the right way, not trying to be beautiful. The Fortnite detail, the Bath & Body Works-smelling hundreds, the rat sniffing the shoe and deciding it wasn't food—these are specific, earned, and funny without announcing themselves as jokes. The chapter plan called for Hermes to sit on the stoop for an hour arguing through the ring camera, and the execution is slightly different (the kid opens the door briefly) but captures the same humiliation and futility. The group chat with the gods is a genuinely creative touch—Athena saying "no," Dionysus saying "yes," Ares saying "die" is economical and characterful. It doesn't overstay its welcome. The detail of the shoes in the fridge is good. The one sock missing since 2021 is good. These are the kinds of specifics that build a character without explaining him. **What doesn't work:** The ending is the chapter's weakest moment. "Like they missed something. Or someone." is exactly the kind of sentimental, explanatory gesture the plan explicitly warned against. The plan says "no bows, no explanations"—but this ending ties a bow on the chapter's emotional content in the most conventional way possible. It tells us what to feel rather than leaving us with an image. This is a real failure given how well the rest of the chapter trusts the reader. "Two small suns" for the gold shoes is a bit much—it's the kind of metaphor that strains. The shoes are already doing enough work without being cosmically inflated. The 1923 Chicago girl who "laughed like a fire alarm" is a nice detail, but "He couldn't remember her face anymore. Just the sound" is a well-worn move—the god who has loved and lost so many he can't remember them. It's not bad, but it's not surprising either. The StockX listing detail—"worn, size flexible, $42,000 ONO"—is a clever callback to Chapter 1's plan, and it almost makes Hermes sick. This is good. But "He almost threw up" is a bit flat as a reaction; it could be more specific. **Character consistency:** Hermes is well-rendered against his profile. The excessive order acceptance, the stolen blunt, the Gatorade-stealing energy, the "fam" energy without literally saying fam—it's all there. The loneliness is present without being announced. The shoes feeling heavier is the one moment where the chapter announces its own subtext too loudly. **Dialogue:** The ring camera exchange is functional and appropriately unglamorous. It doesn't sing, but it doesn't need to. The plan called for an hour of arguing; the chapter compresses this into a brief exchange, which is a minor deviation but works better rhythmically. **Overall:** This is a confident, mostly well-executed chapter that stumbles at the finish line. The voice is real, the details are specific, the mythology is worn lightly and effectively. The ending's sentimentality undercuts what is otherwise a chapter that earns its bleakness. It's good work with one significant flaw. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 12 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 14 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
6:47 a.m. and the bodega on 44th Drive was the only place in Queens that felt like church. Cass shuffled in, hoodie up, eyes barely open, gold shoes hidden under the hem of his Dickies because he still couldn’t decide if wearing them outside was brave or insane. The bell over the door jingled like it was tired too. Ahmed was behind the counter, scrolling TikTok with the sound off. Same as every morning for four years. “Usual?” Ahmed didn’t look up. Cass nodded. Loose Newport 100s. Three. And a honey bun he wouldn’t eat. Just needed something to hold. He dug in his pocket. Came up with a crumpled five and two quarters that smelled like warehouse dust. Ahmed took it without comment. They’d done this dance so long they didn’t need words anymore. The door jingled again. Hermes walked in like he’d been blown through it. Sweaty, panting, hair sticking up in every direction. He had a Taco Bell bag in one hand and the other hand was bleeding from where the bike chain had kissed it. The gold shoes were filthy now. Mud on the midsoles, something that looked like ketchup on the toe. They looked… human. He didn’t see Cass at first. Went straight to the fridge, grabbed a blue Gatorade, cracked it open right there, and drank half in one pull. Throat moving like a thirsty dog. Some of it spilled down his chin and onto his hoodie. He didn’t wipe it. Cass just stared. Hermes turned, finally clocked him. Their eyes locked for half a second. Cass felt it in his stomach first, then his feet. Like the shoes recognized their maker. Hermes wiped his mouth with the back of his hand. “You got a light?” Cass didn’t smoke cigarettes anymore but he still carried a lighter. Muscle memory. He pulled it out (black Bic, half melted from being in his pocket too long) and flicked it open. Hermes leaned in. Their shoulders touched. Not on purpose. Just the bodega was small and both of them were too tall for it. Hermes smelled like summer pavement after rain, hot metal, and something sharp Cass couldn’t name. The flame caught the cigarette Hermes had pulled from behind his ear. Same spot Cass kept his blunts. They stood there like that. Close enough that Cass could see the tiny cut on Hermes’s ankle where the chain had really gotten him. Blood dried dark against brown skin. Ahmed cleared his throat. “You guys gonna pay for that Gatorade or what?” Hermes laughed. Sounded like a broken speaker. Dug in his pocket, came up with a wet twenty, slapped it on the counter. “Keep the change, king.” He turned to leave. Stopped. Looked down at Cass’s feet. The gold was peeking out now, just the toe box, catching the light from the lotto sign. Hermes’s face did something complicated. Not surprise. Recognition. Like seeing an old scar you forgot you had. Cass felt his throat close up. Hermes opened his mouth. Closed it. Opened it again. “You crease ‘em yet?” is what came out. Cass shook his head. Once. Small. Hermes nodded like that made perfect sense. Then he was gone. Door jingled behind him like it was laughing. Cass stood there until Ahmed said, “Yo, Cass, you okay? You look like you saw your ex.” Cass bought another honey bun he wouldn’t eat. Paid with the quarters he’d been saving for laundry. Walked out into the gray morning. The gold shoes felt warm. Not hot. Just warm. Like someone else’s feet had just been in them. He didn’t know the guy’s name was Hermes. He just knew the guy had looked at his shoes like they were a face he used to love. Cass walked to the train. Didn’t take it. Just kept walking. Past the warehouse, past the projects, past the halal cart that was just opening. The shoes didn’t make a sound. Not even on gravel. At some point he realized he was crying. Not big dramatic tears. Just leaking. Like a faucet that won’t shut off. He sat on a curb near the East River. Took one shoe off. Looked at the sole. There was a new scuff. Tiny. On the right toe. Wasn’t there yesterday. He put it back on. Laced it tighter. Kept walking. Didn’t stop until he was back at the warehouse for his next shift. The shoes were starting to smell like him now. Not like whoever they used to belong to. Like Cass. And that scared him more than anything.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is doing a lot of things right and a few things that bear scrutiny. The voice is consistent and earned. The Queens English, half-asleep register promised in the plan is delivered: "Ahmed was behind the counter, scrolling TikTok with the sound off. Same as every morning for four years." That's economical and true. The bodega-as-church opening line is a little on-the-nose but it doesn't collapse under its own weight because the chapter immediately earns it through specificity—Ahmed, the crumpled five, the honey bun he won't eat. These details are doing real work. The shoulder-touch encounter is handled with restraint. The plan called for them to share space without knowing each other, and the chapter honors that: no dramatic revelation, no magic, just proximity and a lighter and a complicated look at a pair of shoes. "Hermes's face did something complicated. Not surprise. Recognition. Like seeing an old scar you forgot you had." That's a metaphor that lands. It's not overreaching. The dialogue is sparse and functional, which is exactly right for this story. "You crease 'em yet?" is a perfect line—it carries the whole weight of divine recognition without explaining itself. The Ahmed interjection ("You guys gonna pay for that Gatorade or what?") is a good grounding beat. Where the chapter stumbles slightly: "The shoes felt warm. Not hot. Just warm. Like someone else's feet had just been in them." This is the chapter's most explicit gesture toward the mythological, and it works, but it's followed by "He just knew the guy had looked at his shoes like they were a face he used to love"—which is the chapter's most overtly poetic line and it's slightly too much. It tells us what to feel rather than letting the image do it. The ending sequence—walking past the warehouse, crying without knowing it, sitting by the East River, the new scuff—is strong. The final beat, "The shoes were starting to smell like him now. Not like whoever they used to belong to. Like Cass. And that scared him more than anything," is genuinely affecting and earns its darkness. The fear is specific and human: the terror of ownership, of becoming someone who has something to lose. Hermes's physicality is well-rendered and consistent with his profile: all elbows and movement, the Gatorade, the wet twenty, the laugh like a broken speaker. Cass's interiority is appropriately muted and anxious. Neither character is cool. The plan's core rule is honored. The chapter follows its plan closely: bodega, 6:47 a.m., loosies, shoulders touching, Hermes's cologne (here rendered as "summer pavement after rain, hot metal"), the crumpled fives, no names exchanged. The plan said "they both pay with crumpled fives and don't look at each other"—the chapter modifies this slightly (they do make eye contact briefly) but the modification serves the story better than strict adherence would have. Minor weaknesses: "like a thirsty dog" is a slightly lazy simile. The "bodega felt like church" framing is a familiar urban-literary move. The chapter occasionally tells us emotional states that the imagery has already communicated (the crying section is slightly redundant after the shoe-scuff moment). Overall this is a strong chapter—controlled, specific, emotionally honest, and faithful to both the plan and the larger project's aesthetic commitments. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Cass started wearing the gold Supremes to every shift. Not under the Dickies anymore. On full display, laces fat and double-knotted, tongue puffed like they were fresh out the box even though they were starting to look like they’d been through three winters. They were two sizes too big and fit perfect. That was the first thing that stopped making sense. Second thing: he stopped checking tags on anything else. He’d cut open a box, pull out whatever—Dunks, Yeezys, some collab nobody asked for—and just stare at his own feet instead. The shoes glowed under the warehouse lights like they were cheating. Like they were the only real thing in the whole building. Luis noticed first. “Yo, Cass, you good? You’re moving like you’re underwater.” Cass didn’t answer. Just kept rubbing the toe box against the back of his calf when nobody was looking. The suede was pilling now. Little bald spots. He liked them. Made the shoes look sorry for themselves. At night he’d list them on StockX from the bathroom stall. $75,000. Deadstock. Size flexible. One careful owner. He’d watch the views climb. 47. 112. 389. Then he’d cancel the listing. Do it again the next night. Same price. Same description. Same photo he took in the bathroom mirror with the flash on so the gold looked radioactive. Cancel. Relist. Cancel. 14 times in one week. His finger got raw from scrolling. He told himself it was about the money. $75k would pay off his abuela’s medical, get him out of Queens, buy him a pair that actually fit his life. Whatever that meant. But every time he hit cancel he felt the shoes tighten around his feet like they were relieved. He started talking to them. Not out loud. Just in his head. you know i’m gonna sell you one day right you know this can’t last you’re worth more than me The shoes never answered. They just got warmer. One shift he legit checked a pair of 2015 Fragment 1s and almost passed them as fake because the blue was off by half a shade. Had to redo the whole thing twice. His hands were shaking. He started sleeping in them. Not on purpose. Just fell out on the couch with them still laced and woke up with the creases deeper. The leather was starting to crack along the flex points like old lips. He took a picture of the worst crease. Zoomed in until it looked like a canyon. Set it as his lock screen. His abuela saw it. “Mijo, why is your phone broken?” “It’s not broken, Abu. It’s just real.” She kissed his forehead and didn’t ask again. He stopped eating. Not on purpose. Just forgot. The honey buns piled up in his locker until they went hard. He’d chew on the plastic wrapper instead. He started smelling the shoes when nobody was around. Pressed his face into the toe box and inhaled like they were a line. They smelled like his feet now. Like warehouse dust and bodega coffee and the specific kind of sweat you get from standing 12 hours straight. They smelled like home. One night he tried to take them off. Sat on his bed at 6 a.m., sun coming up orange through the blinds, and pulled at the heel tab. The left shoe came off easy. His foot looked small without it. Pale. Sad. He stared at it for a long time. Then he put the shoe back on. Laced it tighter than before. The right one never came off again. Not even in the shower. He just stood under the water fully dressed and watched the gold turn dark and then light again as it dried. He started whispering to them when he walked to the train. you’re ruining me you’re the best thing that ever happened to me i hate you don’t leave The shoes just kept getting dirtier. A new scuff appeared on the lateral side. Looked like bike chain grease. Cass traced it with his thumb and felt something in his chest crack open. He didn’t cancel the listing that night. He raised the price to $100,000. Watched the views hit 2,847. Then he threw his phone across the room so hard it shattered the screen. Crawled into bed still wearing everything. Curled around his own feet like they were someone else’s. Cried without sound until the shoes were wet inside. In the morning the listing was gone anyway. He didn’t remember canceling it. He just knew the shoes were still on. And they were starting to feel permanent.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is doing something genuinely difficult and mostly pulling it off. The plan called for Cass wearing the shoes obsessively, repeatedly listing and canceling them on StockX (14 times), stopping checking tags, and feeling something he can't name. The chapter executes all of this faithfully and with considerable craft. The voice is the chapter's greatest strength. It's exactly what the plan promised: Queens English, half-asleep, resentful, sentences like 4 a.m. texts. "The shoes glowed under the warehouse lights like they were cheating" is a strong line. "Like they were the only real thing in the whole building" follows it well. The internal monologue formatted as lowercase text messages to the shoes is a smart formal choice that doesn't feel gimmicky—it earns its strangeness because it fits the character's dissociation. The repetition structure (list, cancel, list, cancel) is rendered with real discipline. The number 14 from the plan is honored. The price escalation from $75k to $100k at the end is a good escalation beat. The chapter understands that obsession has a rhythm and it renders that rhythm without over-explaining it. The abuela moment is the chapter's best piece of dialogue: "Mijo, why is your phone broken?" / "It's not broken, Abu. It's just real." This is earned, not forced. It's brief, it characterizes both people, and it lands emotionally without announcing itself. Where the chapter struggles: some of the metaphors are reaching. "Cracked like old lips" appears in the plan and is used here—it's fine but not exceptional. "Curled around his own feet like they were someone else's" is the chapter's most ambitious image and it almost works but tips slightly into the overwrought. "Cried without sound until the shoes were wet inside" is strong. "Smelled like a line" is a bit on-the-nose for the addiction metaphor the chapter is building. The shower scene—standing fully dressed under water—is a good behavioral detail that shows the dissociation without explaining it. This is the chapter's mode at its best: behavior as revelation. The chapter does occasionally tell rather than show, particularly in the middle section where it lists symptoms (stopped eating, forgot, honey buns piled up) in a way that feels more like a clinical inventory than lived experience. The honey bun detail is good but the surrounding sentences are thinner. The ending is strong. "He just knew the shoes were still on. And they were starting to feel permanent." This is the right note—understated, ominous, earned. The chapter is consistent with Cass's profile: the toe-box rubbing tic is used, the abuela is present, the StockX obsession is rendered with insider specificity. Hermes is absent, which is correct per the plan. The "forced poetry" question: most of the metaphors land. A few don't. The ratio is good enough that it doesn't feel like the chapter is performing depth rather than achieving it. Overall this is a strong chapter—not a masterpiece, but genuinely accomplished work that serves its plan, its character, and its voice with real consistency. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Hermes started circling the warehouse every night after 3 a.m. Not deliveries. Not even pretending. He’d pedal past the chain-link fence slow, no hands on the bars, Citi Bike wobgling like it was drunk. Battery always dead by then. He’d coast, feet off the pedals, gold shoes catching the security lights like they were begging to be seen. He told himself he was checking drop-off bins. There were no drop-off bins. He just wanted to see the guy. The guy who wore his shoes now. The guy who walked like he was apologizing to the floor. He’d park across the street on the loading dock of the closed deli, smoke half a blunt he’d found in someone’s Uber, and watch the side window where the light stayed on longest. Cass would be in there alone after the second shift left. Hoodie up, moving slow between the tables like a ghost doing inventory on its own life. Gold shoes flashing every time he bent to cut a box. Hermes watched him rub the toe box against his calf the same way every time. Same spot. Same rhythm. Like a nervous tic he didn’t know he had. It made Hermes’s chest hurt in a new way. He’d never had a chest hurt before. One night it rained. Hard. The kind of Queens rain that feels personal. Hermes sat on the deli dock anyway, hoodie soaked through, hair plastered to his forehead. The gold shoes were dark with water, heavy. He hadn’t changed socks in four days. Cass came out the side door for a smoke. Blunt behind the ear, lighter in hand. He stopped under the awning, looked left, looked right, then straight across the street. Their eyes met. Hermes didn’t move. Cass didn’t either. Rain kept falling. Loud on the metal awning. Loud on Hermes’s shoulders. Cass took one step forward. Then another. Crossed the street barefoot in the gold shoes, water splashing up his Dickies. He stopped two feet away. Hermes could see the creases now. Deep. Permanent. The leather was cracking open like it was trying to breathe. Cass’s eyes were red. Not high red. Just tired red. Hermes opened his mouth. Nothing came out. Cass said, voice cracked from no sleep: “You following me?” Hermes shrugged. Water ran off his lashes. “Maybe.” Cass looked down at the shoes. Then back up. “They’re yours, right?” Hermes nodded once. Cass bit his lip hard enough to bleed. “I tried to sell them.” “I know.” “Fourteen times.” “I saw.” “I can’t.” “I know.” They stood there while the rain got bored and slowed to a drizzle. Cass shifted his weight. The left shoe squeaked. Wet leather. Hermes said, real quiet, “You’re killing them.” Cass laughed. It sounded like crying. “They were already dead when I got them.” “No. They were waiting.” Cass looked at him then. Really looked. Eyes wide, wet, furious. “Waiting for what?” Hermes didn’t answer. Just stared at the shoes. At the way the gold was almost gone now, just bronze and gray and scuffed to shit. At the way Cass’s feet had made them fit. Cass whispered, “I don’t know how to take them off anymore.” Hermes felt something break inside him. Not dramatic. Just a quiet snap. Like a lace finally giving up. He reached out. Slowly. Touched the toe box with two fingers. Just rested them there. The shoes were warm. Cass flinched but didn’t move away. Hermes said, voice raw, “They look better on you.” Cass made a sound like a punched dog. Then he sat down right there on the wet curb. Put his head in his hands. Shoulders shaking. Hermes sat next to him. Not touching. Just close enough that their knees almost were. They stayed like that until the rain stopped and the sky turned that ugly pre-dawn color. Cass finally spoke, muffled into his palms. “I’m scared they’ll fall apart if I stop wearing them.” Hermes lit the blunt he’d been saving. Took a hit. Passed it. Cass took it with shaking fingers. Hermes said, “Everything falls apart. That’s the point.” Cass hit the blunt hard. Held it too long. Coughed smoke into the quiet street. Then he leaned over. Just a little. Until his shoulder touched Hermes’s. They didn’t move for a long time. The gold shoes sat between them in the puddle, reflecting two broken streetlights and nothing else. Hermes thought: I could take them back right now. He didn’t. Cass thought: I could give them back right now. He didn’t. They just sat there. Two idiots on a curb in Queens, sharing a wet blunt and a pair of dying shoes that finally belonged to somebody.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is doing something genuinely difficult: it's trying to render emotional intimacy between a god and a mortal through the language of exhaustion, sneaker culture, and Queens geography, without ever letting the seams show. Let's examine how well it succeeds. The chapter plan called for Hermes circling the warehouse, watching Cass through the window, and Cass smoking on the loading dock with the gold shoes glowing under sodium light. The actual chapter delivers this but then goes further—it collapses the distance between them in the rain, which wasn't in the plan. The plan placed this meeting in Chapter 6. So the chapter has jumped ahead of itself, essentially merging Chapter 5 and the beginning of Chapter 6. This is a meaningful deviation. The plan's Chapter 6 has them not speaking for twenty minutes, Cass saying "They're yours, right?" and Hermes saying "Yeah." Here, that exchange happens in Chapter 5, in the rain. This is a structural problem: the plan's careful slow-burn pacing is being compressed, which risks deflating the tension the earlier chapters were building. That said, the writing itself is often very strong. The voice is consistent with the established aesthetic—flat, Queens-inflected, emotionally constipated in the best way. "Rain kept falling. Loud on the metal awning. Loud on Hermes's shoulders." This is economical and effective. The repetition of "Loud" earns its keep. The dialogue is mostly excellent. The exchange "They're yours, right?" / "Yeah" / "I tried to sell them." / "I know." / "Fourteen times." / "I saw." / "I can't." / "I know." is genuinely good—it's compressed, it carries weight, it doesn't over-explain. This is the kind of dialogue that trusts the reader. However, there are moments where the writing reaches too hard. "Hermes felt something break inside him. Not dramatic. Just a quiet snap. Like a lace finally giving up." The lace metaphor is clever but also a little too neat, a little too on-the-nose given the shoe context. It's the kind of metaphor that announces itself. Similarly, "Cass made a sound like a punched dog" is vivid but slightly overwrought—it's trying to be raw and lands closer to performed rawness. "Everything falls apart. That's the point." is the kind of line that sounds profound in context but is actually a fairly generic piece of wisdom. It doesn't feel earned by Hermes specifically—it could come from any wise figure in any story. The ending image—"Two idiots on a curb in Queens, sharing a wet blunt and a pair of dying shoes that finally belonged to somebody"—is strong. It earns its sentiment because the chapter has done the work to get there. The dual internal monologue ("I could take them back right now. / He didn't. / Cass thought: I could give them back right now. / He didn't.") is a nice structural choice that mirrors the characters' parallelism without being too cute about it. The characters are consistent with their profiles. Cass's nervous tic (rubbing the toe box against his calf) is drawn directly from the character sheet and used well. Hermes's physical description—the wet hair, the elbows and knees quality of his movement—is implied rather than stated. The emotional dynamic (Hermes wanting to feel something, Cass unable to let go) tracks with the profiles. The mythology angle is handled with appropriate restraint—Hermes is never announced as a god, the shoes are never magical, the divine is present only as texture. This is faithful to the writing prompt's spirit as interpreted by the plan. The purple prose is mostly kept in check, though "the sky turned that ugly pre-dawn color" is a slight miss—it gestures at specificity without achieving it. "Ugly pre-dawn color" is vague dressed up as precise. The chapter's biggest weakness is the structural deviation from the plan, which compresses the story's emotional arc prematurely. The second biggest is occasional moments where the writing tips from earned rawness into performed rawness. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 9 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The fire door started staying propped open with a busted box cutter. Nobody said anything about it. Luis saw, shrugged, kept walking. Security camera was broken anyway. Had been since 2022. Cass left it like that every night after the second shift bounced. 3:17 a.m. exactly. Same time every time. He told himself it was for air. The warehouse got hot. Lies. First time Hermes used it, Cass almost shit himself. He was at his station, headphones off for once, cutting open a box of some new balance protection pack that smelled like depression. Heard the door scrape. Looked up. Hermes just walked in like he belonged there. Soaking wet hoodie, bike chain grease on his cheek, gold shoes gone almost brown now. He had a plastic bag from CVS dangling from one wrist. Inside: two tall boys of Modelo and a bag of flaming hot Cheetos. He didn’t say a word. Just walked over, put the bag on the table next to Cass, pulled out one beer, cracked it, took a long drink. Cass stared. Hermes wiped his mouth. “You got cups or we drinking like men?” Cass’s hands were shaking. He took the beer. Drank half in one go. Foam ran down his chin. He didn’t wipe it. They stood there for twenty minutes. No talking. Just drinking and the sound of Cass’s box cutter opening packages he wasn’t even looking at. Hermes finally leaned against the table. “You always work alone this late?” “Somebody’s gotta.” “You hate it.” “Yeah.” “Me too.” Cass looked at him then. Really looked. Saw the dark circles under Hermes’s eyes that makeup couldn’t hide if he ever wore any. Saw the way his hands shook a little when he wasn’t holding the beer. Saw the gold shoes were held together with shoe goo now, big black patches like scabs. Cass said, voice barely there, “They’re yours, right?” Hermes nodded. “You want them back?” Hermes laughed. Sounded like tearing paper. “Can’t.” “Why not?” “They don’t fit me anymore.” Cass looked down at his own feet. The shoes were so beat the Supreme logo was half gone. The left toe had a hole starting where his big toe pushed through. He could feel the concrete when he walked now. He said, “They barely fit me.” “They fit perfect.” Cass started crying then. Not loud. Just leaking. Same way he’d been leaking for weeks. He didn’t try to hide it. Hermes pretended not to notice. Just opened the Cheetos, ate one, offered the bag. Cass took a handful. Ate them like punishment. They finished the beers. Hermes crushed his can, put it neatly in the trash like someone raised right. Cass threw his at the wall. It bounced, rolled, stopped against Hermes’s foot. Hermes picked it up. Put it in the trash too. Cass lit the blunt he’d been saving behind his ear. Took one hit. Passed it. Hermes took it. Held the smoke forever. Let it out slow. Cass said, “I don’t know your name.” “Doesn’t matter.” “It does to me.” Hermes looked at him for a long time. “Call me mes.” “Like the app?” “Yeah.” Cass nodded. Took the blunt back. They smoked it down to nothing. Fingers almost touching when they passed it. Cass finally asked, voice raw, “Why’d you leave them?” Hermes shrugged. “Got tired of running.” “That’s it?” “That’s everything.” Cass thought about that. About all the nights he’d walked home in these shoes that weren’t his and felt less alone. About how he hadn’t jerked off in weeks because nothing felt as good as the weight of them on his feet. He said, “I think I’m in love with your shoes.” Hermes smiled. First real one Cass had seen. It hurt to look at. “I know.” They stood there until the blunt was gone and the warehouse lights started flickering like they were tired too. Hermes pushed off the table. “Gotta go. Got orders.” Cass nodded. Didn’t ask him to stay. Hermes walked to the door. Stopped. Turned back. “You working tomorrow?” “Yeah.” “I’ll leave the door propped.” He left. Cass stood there for a long time after. Looked at the crushed Cheetos on the floor. At the two empty beers. At the box cutter still holding the door open. He whispered to the shoes, “He’s coming back.” The shoes didn’t answer. They just felt warmer. Like they already knew.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter executes its plan with considerable skill and restraint. The writing plan called for Hermes to walk in, twenty minutes of silence, the key exchange ("They're yours, right?" / "Yeah."), smoking the blunt, Cass crying without Hermes acknowledging it, and the chapter delivers all of this faithfully while adding texture that feels earned rather than decorative. The voice is consistent throughout—Queens-flat, exhausted, resentful in the way the plan described. "Ate them like punishment" is a small, precise image that lands. "Sounded like tearing paper" for Hermes's laugh is one of the stronger metaphors, genuinely capturing something about a god who's been laughing at the wrong things for too long. The detail of Hermes putting the crushed can neatly in the trash while Cass throws his at the wall is excellent characterization through action—it shows the difference between someone who's been civilized by millennia and someone who's still raw. The dialogue is sparse and functional, which is exactly right for this chapter. "They fit perfect" / "They barely fit me" is a clean exchange that carries mythological weight without announcing itself. The "Call me mes / Like the app? / Yeah" exchange is good—grounded, slightly deflating, which is the tone the whole piece is going for. Where the chapter stumbles slightly: "He didn't try to hide it" after the crying starts is a tell rather than a show, though the surrounding context mostly compensates. "Like they already knew" at the end is the weakest moment—the personification of the shoes is a bit too neat, a bit too "poetic ending" for a piece that's otherwise committed to refusing easy comfort. The plan said "no bows," and this final image is a small bow. It's not egregious, but it's the one place where the writing reaches for something it hasn't quite earned. The Cheetos and Modelo are perfect props—specific, unglamorous, exactly right. The shoe goo patches described as "scabs" is a strong image. The detail about Cass not having jerked off in weeks appearing in his internal monologue mid-chapter is consistent with the character profile and lands without feeling gratuitous. The characters are consistent with their profiles: Cass leaking tears without drama, Hermes deflecting with logistics ("Got orders"), both of them communicating through objects and silences rather than declarations. The mythological dimension is present but never explained—"They don't fit me anymore" does real work as a line about a god who's moved on from something he can't name. The chapter is slightly shorter than the ~1000 words planned, but what's there is dense enough that it doesn't feel thin. The pacing is controlled. The emotional beats are earned through accumulation rather than announcement. Minor weaknesses: the final two lines are a small lapse into the kind of warmth the piece has been carefully avoiding. "He's coming back" / "The shoes didn't answer. They just felt warmer. / Like they already knew." This is the chapter's one moment of sentimentality that the plan explicitly warned against. It's not fatal, but it's a slight softening of what should have stayed harder. Overall this is a strong chapter—disciplined, specific, emotionally coherent, and faithful to both the plan and the prompt's spirit. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
They never planned it. It just happened. Every night after 3:17 the fire door scraped open and Hermes walked in with something cheap and warm: two tall boys, a bacon-egg-and-cheese from the 24-hour cart, a half-melted pint of Häagen-Dazs he stole from someone’s Seamless order. Cass would be waiting, sitting on the sorting table, feet swinging like a kid. Gold shoes so beat now the Swoosh was just a ghost. They’d eat on the loading dock, backs against the corrugated door, knees almost touching. Queens night air thick with halal-cart smoke and weed from the projects across the street. Hermes talked first. Always.
