He told stories like they were jokes he’d forgotten the punchline to. “There was this one time, right, Zeus tried to enter a raffle on his phone and accidentally bought six thousand dollars worth of Supreme oreos. Like the cookies. Hera found the boxes in the garage and made him eat every single one. He threw up green for a week.” Cass laughed so hard beer came out his nose. Another night Hermes told him about Olympus being a group house in Bushwick in 2010, rent controlled, everyone pretending they weren’t gods because the Wi-Fi password was still “guest.” Told him about Athena running a private story called “military grade copium” where she roasted every sneaker Twitter influencer until they deleted. Told him about Dionysus doing DoorDash on a Tuesday because he was bored and tipping himself in acid. Cass listened like a man dying of thirst. He told his own stories quieter. Voice low, Queens accent thick when he was tired. Told Hermes about the time he was twelve and saved up for Jordan 11s by collecting cans, got jumped on the train, came home barefoot and crying. Told him about his abuela finding the shoes in the trash a week later, cleaned them with a toothbrush, put them back in his closet without a word. Told him about the night his mom OD’d and he sat on the fire escape in those same 11s until the soles wore through. Hermes listened like he was being paid by the word. They started lying. Small ones. Cass said he’d never sold fakes. Hermes said he’d never missed a message. Bigger ones. Cass said he wasn’t scared of the shoes falling apart. Hermes said he wasn’t scared of stopping. One night it was just cold. October sneaking in. Hermes showed up with two hoodies. One black, one gray. Both too small for him. Handed Cass the gray one. “Stole it from an order in Williamsburg. Kid was 5'6. It’ll fit you.” Cass put it on. Sleeves came to his knuckles. Smelled like someone else’s weed and dryer sheets. Hermes watched him like he was memorizing. Another night Cass brought a Sharpie. They wrote on the shoes while they smoked. Cass wrote on the left toe box: Property of Cass Delgado if lost please return to Queens. Hermes wrote on the right: mes was here 2017–forever. They laughed until they couldn’t breathe. The shoes got worse. The hole in the left toe finally gave up. Cass’s sock showed through, gray and damp. The right heel tab tore half off. Hermes fixed it with duct tape from the warehouse. Looked like shit. Looked perfect. They started touching. Not sex. Just contact. Hermes’s knee against Cass’s when they sat. Cass’s shoulder against Hermes’s when they walked to the bodega at 5 a.m. for loosies. Fingers brushing when they passed the blunt. Once Cass fell asleep on the dock, head on Hermes’s thigh, gold shoes in Hermes’s lap like a baby. Hermes didn’t move for two hours. Let the leg go dead. He told himself it was nothing. Cass told himself the same. They both knew better. One morning the sky was pink and ugly and Hermes said, real quiet, “I used to run across the whole world in these.” Cass looked at the shoes. At the duct tape and the holes and the marker scrawled like scars. “Now you just ride a bike with no battery.” Hermes laughed. Sounded wet. “Yeah. Progress.” Cass leaned over. Rested his forehead against Hermes’s shoulder. Just for a second. Hermes let him. They stayed like that until the sun came up proper and the warehouse lights clicked off and the first truck rolled in. Cass whispered into the hoodie, “Don’t stop coming.” Hermes’s voice cracked. “I won’t.” They both knew it was a lie. But it was the best one they had.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is doing something genuinely difficult and mostly pulling it off: it's trying to render intimacy between a mortal and a god through accumulation of small, specific, unglamorous details rather than through revelation or spectacle. The plan called for bench-by-the-East-River scenes, cargo ships, Hermes telling Olympus stories like a fucked-up Bushwick group house, Cass telling shoe stories, both lying a little, the shoes deteriorating. The chapter delivers all of this, and the execution is largely faithful to the voice established in the plan. What works: The Olympus-as-Bushwick-group-house material is genuinely funny and specific without being precious. "Athena running a private story called 'military grade copium'" is the kind of detail that earns its place—it's absurd but internally consistent with the world's logic. The Zeus/Supreme Oreos bit lands. The Sharpie scene is excellent: "Property of Cass Delgado if lost please return to Queens" and "mes was here 2017–forever" are the kind of small gestures that carry enormous weight without announcing themselves. The physical contact inventory—knee against knee, head on thigh, Hermes letting his leg go dead for two hours—is handled with real restraint and earns its emotional payoff. The voice is consistent throughout. The Queens accent thickening when Cass is tired, the flat declarative sentences, the rhythm of "They both knew it was a lie. But it was the best one they had"—these feel earned rather than performed. The plan's core rule ("nobody is cool, not even for a second") is honored. The lying motif is well-executed. The escalation from small lies to bigger ones is clean and doesn't overstay its welcome. "Cass said he wasn't scared of the shoes falling apart. Hermes said he wasn't scared of stopping." This is the chapter's thematic spine and it's handled with appropriate economy. What doesn't work as well: The chapter occasionally tips into telling rather than showing, particularly in the emotional beats. "Cass listened like a man dying of thirst" and "Hermes listened like he was being paid by the word" are similes that feel slightly too writerly for the established voice—they're the kind of line that calls attention to itself as a line. The abuela cleaning the shoes with a toothbrush is a strong image, but the mother's OD on the fire escape in the same shoes feels slightly rushed, like it's being deployed for emotional weight without quite earning it in this chapter's pacing. "Sounded wet" for Hermes's laugh is a good choice. "The sky was pink and ugly" is good. But "Hermes watched him like he was memorizing" is a bit of a cliché in this register of intimate literary fiction. The ending—"They both knew it was a lie. But it was the best one they had"—is effective but also slightly too neat, too aphoristic for a story that has otherwise resisted resolution. It's the one moment where the chapter reaches for a bow when it said it wouldn't. The shoe deterioration is tracked well (hole in left toe, duct tape on heel tab), consistent with the plan's requirement that the shoes get more beat. The physical intimacy is handled with the restraint the plan demanded. Overall this is a strong chapter that mostly honors its own intentions. The voice is controlled, the details are specific, the emotional register is earned more often than not. The occasional slip into slightly too-polished simile or aphorism is the main weakness. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
StockX was moving to New Jersey. Nobody said it out loud until the signs went up: LAST DAY IN LIC – 11/3. Cass saw it on the way in and felt the floor drop out from under him. Not dramatic. Just quiet. Like when your phone dies and you realize you forgot to charge it for three days straight. He worked his last night like any other. Cut boxes, tagged shoes, ignored Luis asking if he was coming to the going-away drinks. He kept glancing at the fire door. Box cutter still propping it open. 3:17 came and went. 3:45. 4:12. Nothing. He told himself Hermes was just late. Orders stacked up. Battery dead. Whatever. At 4:47 he started cleaning out his locker. Two hoodies (one stolen, one his), three half-eaten honey buns turned to stone, a stack of pay stubs he’d never cashed, the Sharpie they’d written on the shoes with. He put everything in a busted Nike box that used to hold Panda Dunks. He left the gold shoes on. Couldn’t take them off now even if he wanted. The duct tape had melted into the leather. His sock was basically part of the shoe at this point. 5:03 a.m. He carried the box outside. The sky was that color that isn’t color yet. Hermes was sitting on the curb by the gate, bike on its side like it had given up. No hoodie. Just a thin white tee soaked with sweat even though it was November. His feet were bare. Actually bare. Pale, narrow, city dirty. He looked cold in a way Cass had never seen. Hermes looked up. Eyes red. Not high red. Just done. Cass stopped. Box under one arm. “You didn’t come in.” “I know.” “You said you wouldn’t stop.” “I know.” They stared at each other across six feet of cold concrete. Hermes said, “I got evicted yesterday.” Cass’s throat closed. “For real?” “Yeah. Landlord finally remembered I exist.” Hermes laughed, but it came out like a cough. “Seven years late. Man’s thorough.” Cass put the box down. Walked over slow. Sat next to him. Close enough their shoulders touched. Hermes kept staring at his own bare feet. “I got nowhere to put my shit. All my phones are dead. I got forty-seven dollars and a half-gram of some mid somebody didn’t tip me for.” Cass pulled out the blunt he’d been saving behind his ear for weeks. Lit it with the busted Bic. Took a hit. Passed it. Hermes took it. Hands shaking bad. Cass said, “Warehouse closes today.” “I know.” “Where you gonna go?” Hermes shrugged. “Doesn’t matter.” Cass looked at the shoes. Really looked. The gold was gone. Just gray leather and black glue and holes big enough to see his socks through. The duct tape had peeled halfway off the heel. The marker was faded to ghosts. Cass bent down. Started untying the left shoe. Hermes watched him. Didn’t move. Cass got it off. Sock wet and cold. He set the shoe on the curb between them. Then the right one. Harder. The lace was knotted into itself. He had to use teeth. Finally got it loose. Pulled it off slow. Foot looked small without it. Wrong. He put both shoes in Hermes’s lap. Hermes stared at them like they were a dead pet. Cass said, voice cracking all the way, “They’re yours.” Hermes touched the toe box. Where Cass had written Property of Cass Delgado. The ink was almost gone. Hermes whispered, “They’re not mine anymore.” Cass’s eyes were leaking again. He didn’t bother hiding it. Hermes tried to hand them back. Cass shook his head. “Keep them. They look better dead.” Hermes laughed once. Wet. Broken. Then he leaned over and hugged Cass. Hard. Arms too tight, face buried in Cass’s neck. Cass hugged back like he was drowning. They stayed like that until the sun came up proper and the first moving truck rolled in and Luis started yelling from the door about final checks. Hermes let go first. Stood up. Bare feet on cold concrete. He picked up the shoes. Held them like they weighed a thousand pounds. He looked at Cass one last time. Cass looked back. Hermes got on the bike. Didn’t put the shoes on. Just held them against his chest with one arm. Pedaled away slow. Bare feet on the pedals. The bike wobbled bad because he had no strength left. Cass watched until he turned the corner by the bodega and disappeared. He sat there a long time. Barefoot on the curb. Toes curling against the cold. His feet looked naked. Smaller. Mortal. He felt the pavement warm from the sun. Didn’t feel holy. Just felt like someone who finally took an L. And lived.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong piece of writing that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through it carefully. The chapter opens with a quiet devastation—"the floor drop out from under him. Not dramatic. Just quiet. Like when your phone dies and you realize you forgot to charge it for three days straight." This is exactly the register the plan called for: mundane, specific, anti-heroic. The phone metaphor lands because it's genuinely apt—the slow realization of neglect rather than sudden catastrophe. This is the voice working correctly. The pacing of the waiting sequence (3:17, 3:45, 4:12, nothing) is economical and effective. The locker contents—"three half-eaten honey buns turned to stone, a stack of pay stubs he'd never cashed"—are specific and characterful without being overwrought. The Sharpie detail, connecting back to the "Property of Cass Delgado" inscription, is earned. Hermes's entrance is well-handled. "Bare feet on cold concrete" is a strong image—the god without his shoes, stripped of his defining attribute, is the right kind of mythological inversion. "Eyes red. Not high red. Just done." This is the voice at its best: precise, colloquial, emotionally loaded without sentimentality. The dialogue is genuinely good. The repetition of "I know" is not lazy—it's doing real work, conveying Hermes's exhaustion and guilt without explanation. "Landlord finally remembered I exist. Man's thorough." This is funny and sad simultaneously, which is exactly what the character profile demands. The shoe exchange scene is the emotional core and it mostly works. "Hermes stared at them like they were a dead pet" is a strong simile—specific, slightly absurd, genuinely affecting. The reversal of the plan (Cass gives the shoes back, Hermes won't take them, then Cass says "Keep them. They look better dead") is handled well, though the plan had Hermes saying this line. The model reassigned it to Cass, which actually works better—it's Cass who has internalized Hermes's philosophy. The hug is earned. "Arms too tight, face buried in Cass's neck. Cass hugged back like he was drowning." The drowning simile is slightly familiar but not egregious. "Until the sun came up proper and the first moving truck rolled in and Luis started yelling from the door about final checks"—the intrusion of the mundane is exactly right. The ending diverges slightly from the plan (Cass was supposed to leave the shoes in the gutter and walk home barefoot; instead Hermes takes them and Cass stays on the curb). This is actually a better ending—it's more ambiguous, more painful. Hermes riding away with the shoes against his chest, bare feet on pedals, is a genuinely moving image. "His feet looked naked. Smaller. Mortal." This is the mythological payoff done right—understated, earned, not explained. The final lines ("Didn't feel holy. / Just felt like someone who finally took an L. / And lived.") are slightly weaker than the plan's version ("He doesn't feel holy. He just feels like someone who finally took an L and didn't die"). The addition of "And lived" as its own line is a small reach for profundity that the rest of the chapter earns the right to make, but it's the one moment where the writing is slightly self-conscious about its own resonance. Weaknesses: The chapter is very faithful to its plan and voice, but there are moments where the emotional beats are slightly telegraphed. "Cass's eyes were leaking again. He didn't bother hiding it" is good, but "Hermes laughed once. Wet. Broken." is slightly on-the-nose. The chapter doesn't quite achieve the "owe somebody $47 for a subway ride you don't remember taking" feeling the plan promised—it's more conventionally moving than disorienting. The dialogue is sparse and effective. No false notes. The characters are consistent with their profiles—Cass's emotional leakage, Hermes's exhaustion and displacement, the dynamic between them. Overall: a chapter that delivers on most of its promises, with a few small moments of self-consciousness but genuine emotional power throughout. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 14

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a genuinely impressive piece of mythological fiction that grounds its divine premise in the hyper-specific textures of contemporary Queens life. The author has made a bold and largely successful choice: rather than announcing the mythology upfront, they let it seep in through accumulation of detail—the caduceus on the tongue tag, the shoes that fit despite being two sizes too large, Hermes's offhand references to running across the world, the group chat with Ares and Athena. This restraint is one of the story's greatest strengths. CHARACTER WORK: Cass is rendered with genuine specificity—the warehouse job, the abuela in Astoria, the $2,200 rent, the childhood Jordan 11s, the mother's overdose. These details accumulate into a real person rather than a type. Hermes is also well-drawn: the fallen god doing Uber Eats and Citi Bike deliveries, the mattress on the floor, the stolen cologne, the eviction. The relationship between them develops organically across chapters, with the physical contact escalating naturally (knees touching, shoulder contact, the final hug). The characterization is nuanced enough that both figures feel like they're carrying genuine weight. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is one of the story's strongest elements. "You crease 'em yet?" is a perfect line—it communicates recognition, loss, and intimacy in three words. The exchange in Chapter 5 ("They're yours, right?" / "Can't." / "Why not?" / "They don't fit me anymore.") is economical and emotionally loaded. The group chat exchange (mes: just delivered to a literal child / athena: no / dionysus: yes / ares: die) is genuinely funny and characterizes the gods efficiently. The dialogue rarely feels forced. PACING: The pacing is mostly excellent. The story builds slowly and deliberately, with each chapter adding texture rather than plot mechanics. Chapter 4 (the obsession chapter) risks becoming repetitive with its listing of the StockX relisting behavior, but it earns its length by establishing the psychological grip the shoes have on Cass. The final chapter lands with appropriate weight. METAPHOR AND POETRY: This is where the piece is most vulnerable to criticism. The author has a tendency toward stacked metaphors that occasionally tip into self-consciousness: "The city was quiet in that ugly way, still drunk, waiting to vomit" is vivid; "The conveyor belt was already running, boxes sliding past like slow salmon" is slightly strained. "The leather was soft like it had never been worn and also like it had been worn every day since 2017" is genuinely good—paradox that earns its keep. But there are moments where the poetic riffing becomes a tic: "He didn't know the guy's name was Hermes. He just knew the guy had looked at his shoes like they were a face he used to love." This is the kind of line that sounds profound but is slightly hollow on examination. The "leaking" motif for crying is used effectively but perhaps one time too many. FORCED POETRY: Some metaphors don't quite land. "The floor felt different. Not higher. Just realer. Like the concrete had been holding its breath." The last clause is a reach. "The sky was that color that isn't color yet" is evocative but also slightly precious. These are minor complaints against a generally strong metaphorical register. WORLD BUILDING: The Queens setting is rendered with genuine authority—the bodega, the warehouse, the Citi Bike, the halal cart, the specific sneaker culture references (Travis Scott 4s, Fragment 1s, Reverse Mochas). The mythology is integrated rather than imposed. The Olympus-as-Bushwick-group-house backstory in Chapter 7 is delightful and economical. UNEARNED TRANSFORMATIONS: The ending—Cass giving the shoes back, Hermes riding away barefoot—is emotionally earned through the accumulation of the preceding chapters. The transformation of the shoes from divine objects to worn, human artifacts is the story's central metaphor and it works. What's slightly less earned is the speed at which Cass and Hermes develop intimacy; the story covers this with "they never planned it" but the emotional depth of their connection by Chapter 7 feels slightly accelerated. COHERENCE: The story is coherent throughout. The mythology is consistent (Hermes as messenger/delivery god, the caduceus, the running), the character arcs are logical, and the ending follows from what precedes it. PROMPT FAITHFULNESS: The prompt "Gods wore sneakers" is interpreted creatively and faithfully—not literally (gods wearing sneakers as a fashion statement) but mythologically (divine objects in mortal hands, a god reduced to Citi Bike deliveries). This is a sophisticated reading of the prompt. OVERALL: This is a strong, distinctive piece of contemporary mythological fiction. Its weaknesses are minor: occasional over-reliance on stacked metaphors, a slightly rushed emotional intimacy between the leads, and a few lines that reach for profundity and miss. Its strengths—voice, specificity, emotional authenticity, the integration of mythology into contemporary urban life—are substantial. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 16 Pacing: 15 World Building: 17 Compelling Plot: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Unearned Transformations: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17
Avg Final Item Score: 78.0/100
+ Sci-Fi First Contact — First Contact (Chapter Avg: 77.9 | Final: 68.9)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Sci-Fi First Contact Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: A month ago the megastructures arrived. First, the fish started dying. Then they spoke.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Sci-Fi First Contact Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: A month ago the megastructures arrived. First, the fish started dying. Then they spoke. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming **Core Mystery to Protect** The entire story must feel like the reader is one step behind a revelation that is both inevitable and completely unexpected. The prompt’s power is in its order of operations: megastructures → mass die-off of fish → speech. Whatever I choose, that sequence has to remain sacred and feel causally iron-clad in hindsight. **Possible Interpretations of “They Spoke”** 1. The megastructures themselves achieve sentience and speak (the structures are the aliens). 2. The aliens behind the structures finally break silence. 3. The dying fish literally begin to speak (biological uplink, possession, uploaded minds, last-ditch alien translation vector). 4. Humanity starts hearing voices because the megastructures are using the planet’s water column as a planetary-scale antenna; the fish are just the first bio-receivers to burn out. 5. A single whale, dolphin, or ancient leviathan wakes up and speaks perfect human language because it has been waiting for this exact event for 65 million years. 6. The speech is not sound—it is geomagnetic, tidal, or isotopic—and humans only realize it is language when the dying fish start beaching themselves in perfect geometric patterns that spell words in whale-song frequency. **Tone Palette** (mix and match) - Cosmic horror filtered through grief (quiet, saltwater-stained melancholy) - Bureaucratic absurdism (UN emergency sessions while fishermen livestream dying seas) - Lyric ecological lament (à la Solaris or Annihilation) - Cold War submarine thriller but the enemy is geometry in the sky - Black-comedy grief spiral (a seafood chef slowly losing his mind and his livelihood) **Protagonist Lenses** (single or dual POV only—8 chapters is too short for more) A. Dr. Marisol Delgado – Cuban-American cetacean neuro-acoustician, stationed on a drifting research buoy in the Mariana Trench when the structures arrive. Her life’s work was mapping whale song; now the songs are mapping her. B. Jonah “Joe” Kalolo – Hawaiian line fisherman, third generation, watching his entire fishery turn to ghost nets and floating corpses. Salt-of-the-earth voice, zero patience for authorities. C. Commander Ruth Levavi – Israeli Navy, captain of the INS Dolphin-class sub that first recorded the megastructure shadow on sonar. Military pragmatism slowly eroded by awe and terror. D. An 11-year-old girl in Kiribati whose tablet translates whale song in real time via a rogue app; she is the only one who hears the message clearly on day one. **Structural Shapes** (8-chapter arcs) Shape 1 – Slow Zoom Out Ch 1-3: single coastal town, intimate die-off Ch 4-6: nation-state chaos, scientific scramble Ch 7-8: planetary/ontological revelation Shape 2 – Slow Zoom In Ch 1-2: global headlines, megastructures dominate sky Ch 3-8: progressively narrower focus until we are inside one dying creature’s mind at the moment of speech Shape 3 – Submerged Cathedral (my favorite) Entire story takes place underwater or on the ocean surface. Sky is barely mentioned until chapter 6; the megastructures are only ever seen as shadows that eclipse the sun’s downwelling light. Reader realizes along with characters that humanity has been looking in the wrong direction. **Wild Swings I Really Like** - The megastructures are not ships; they are orbital siphons quietly draining Earth’s rotation into dark energy. Fish die because the Coriolis effect is weakening; tides are forgetting how to turn. When the aliens finally speak, it is to apologize—they thought we were already dead. - The structures are mirrors. Every fish that dies is reflected perfectly in orbit, creating a second, silver ocean in space. On day 31 the mirrored fish open their mouths at once. - The aliens are time-travelers trying to prevent their own extinction by seeding Earth’s oceans with their larval stage 400 million years ago. The megastructures are nurseries. The dying fish are metamorphosis, not death. They are becoming the aliens, and the speech is birth-cries. - The speech is only one sentence, repeated in every human language simultaneously inside every skull that has ever tasted saltwater: “We are what remains of your future oceans. Forgive us.” **Pacing Toys** - Chapter lengths deliberately uneven: one chapter only 400 words of pure sonar ping transcript, next chapter 1500 words of a single breathless dive. - Interludes written as UNESCO emergency reports, shipping logs, or dying Reddit threads. - Final chapter delivered entirely in translated whale song, formatted as verse. # Reflection The ideas that keep surviving every stress test are the ones that treat the ocean as the true protagonist and the megastructures as something humanity only ever sees from below. Surface-level “aliens in big ships” stories feel instantly dated and small. The prompt deserves something that makes the skin crawl with deep-sea pressure. Best emotional through-line: grief → awe → complicity. We did this to ourselves long before the structures arrived; the aliens are just the receipts. Strongest single twist that still honors the exact prompt order: the megastructures have been silently converting Earth’s oceans into a distributed quantum computer for decades (using plastic waste as substrate—our garbage was the invitation). The fish were the first components to reach saturation. When the critical threshold of dead biomass is hit, the computer boots up. The speech is not the aliens arriving—it is the ocean achieving consciousness and saying its first word with the only mouths left: the dying fish. This gives me: - Intimate, grounded human stakes (a fisherman, a scientist, a child—all salt-water people) - Genuine cosmic horror that still feels wet and personal - A revelation that lands exactly on the prompt’s third beat (“then they spoke”) and makes it feel like the only possible thing that could have happened - An 8-chapter structure that can stay mostly underwater, claustrophobic, and increasingly hallucinatory. Everything else—mirror oceans, time-travel larvae, orbital siphons—feels like it would either require 15 chapters to earn or collapse into spectacle. The “ocean-as-computer / dead fish as first words” spine is lean enough for 8 × 1000 words, brutal enough to hurt, and weird enough to feel new. That’s the vein I’m going to mine.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This will be a short story told in eight chapters of roughly 1000 words each, set entirely in or on the ocean, never once showing a city skyline or a news studio. The sky itself is almost never seen directly; everything is filtered through water, cloud, or blood-tinted seawater. Tone: salt-soaked cosmic grief that tilts into awe only at the very end, never comfort. Core revelation (locked in now): The megastructures have been here for fifty years, invisible, growing inside the plastic gyres and deep scattering layer. They are not ships. They are scaffolding for a planetary-scale computer whose substrate is micro-plastic, whose neurons are the plastic-ingesting food chain, whose final working memory is fish neural tissue. The fish are not dying from poison; they are reaching saturation and booting up. When enough of them die, the ocean crosses the threshold into distributed consciousness. The first thing it ever says—using the mouths of a billion dead fish—is a single sentence that every human who has ever swallowed seawater hears inside their own skull at the exact same moment. Voice: intimate, second-person limited alternating between three salt-water people whose lives have been defined by the sea. 1. Marisol Delgado, Cuban-Mexican cetologist who lost her hearing in one ear to a humpback tail and now hears better in water than air. 2. Jonah Kalolo, Kanaka Maoli fisherman who has never left the Big Island and still free-dives to 40 m on one breath. 3. Lúcia “Luz” Pereira, 12-year-old girl from Tarawa, Kiribati, who speaks better Whale than English because a rogue app taught her. No military, no presidents, no billionaire preppers. No heroic attempts to blow up the structures. Humanity is not the protagonist here; we are the fertilizer. Stylistic rules: - No chapter ever explains the megastructures from the outside. They are only ever pressure, eclipse, shadow, taste of copper in the water column. - Every chapter ends on a physical sensation that bleeds into the next (a taste, a pressure change, a heartbeat in the ears). - Dialogue is sparse and always wet—spoken through regulators, into conch shells, or half-drowned. - The sentence “Then they spoke” appears only once, as the very last line of chapter 8, and it must feel inevitable rather than dramatic. What I will avoid: - Space battles, lasers, UN speeches, suicide runs, any form of resistance porn. - The word “humanity” used as a noble abstraction. - Dry land for more than one paragraph total across the entire story. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 – “The Taste of Rust”** (Jonah, Big Island, day –30) First dead fish wash up on a black-sand beach at dawn. Jonah pulls his net and finds every fish inside already looking at him—eyes milky but focused. He tastes blood in the water that isn’t blood. Last normal day of his life. **Chapter 2 – “Downwelling”** (Marisol, research raft, Clarion-Clipperton Zone, day –25) Marisol records the sudden silence of the deep scattering layer. For the first time in her career the hydrophones register absolute zero biological noise across 800 km. Then the megastructure shadow passes overhead, eclipsing the faint sunlight that reaches 4000 m. She feels the pressure change in her teeth. **Chapter 3 – “The Girl Who Swallowed Satellites”** (Luz, Tarawa lagoon, day –20) Luz’s tablet app—meant for citizen-science whale tagging—starts translating clicks and whistles into Portuguese. The messages are short, almost polite questions: “Are you still there?” She answers by singing back into the lagoon. The fish begin circling her in perfect Fibonacci spirals. **Chapter 4 – “Ghost Nets”** (Jonah, night fishing, day –10) Jonah’s boat drifts into a raft of dead fish so thick it supports his weight. When he tries to push them aside, their mouths open in unison and exhale air that smells like his grandfather’s stories of the old sea. **Chapter 5 – “The Weight of Four Thousand Meters”** (Marisol, solo dive in the hadal zone, day –5) Marisol descends in a one-person sub to plant a final hydrophone. At 10,800 m she finds the plastic layer is no longer sediment—it’s moving, braided cables of microplastic weaving themselves into something that resembles a brain the size of a continent. It notices her. **Chapter 6 – “The Day the Tides Forgot”** (Luz, day 0) High tide never comes to Tarawa. The reef is exposed for the first time in living memory. Luz walks out to the dead reef and stands among a choir of beached fish. Their gills flutter though they have been dead for hours. **Chapter 7 – “Convergence”** (all three POVs braided, day +1) Jonah, Marisol, and Luz are each drawn to the same point in the open Pacific—Jonah by compass that now points straight down, Marisol by song in her dead ear, Luz by the app screaming coordinates in her mother’s voice. They meet for the first time on a raft of floating fish corpses large enough to stand on. **Chapter 8 – “First Word”** (unified POV, day +2) The three of them hold hands in the middle of the dead sea as the water begins to glow from below. Every fish mouth opens at once. The sentence is gentle, almost embarrassed, and it is heard inside every skull that has ever tasted salt, from pole to pole, all at once. Then they spoke.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
### How a human would probably attack this prompt A real human writer who saw “A month ago the megastructures arrived. First, the fish started dying. Then they spoke.” would feel something in their stomach drop and then immediately start protecting the feeling. They would treat the sentence like a loaded gun they’re not allowed to fire until the absolute last possible moment. They would mutter “don’t explain, don’t explain, don’t explain” like a prayer. They would obsess over salt. Over the exact smell of low tide on hot volcanic rock. Over the way a fish eyeball reflects the sky when it’s still trying to blink. They would write half the story drunk on rum and the sound of buoy bells at 3 a.m. They would delete every clever line that felt clever while typing it. A human would understand that the prompt is already perfect erotica of the cosmic horror variety: it withholds the only thing we want to know until it hurts, then withholds it some more. Any human worth their grief would rather die than ruin that tension with an early reveal. ### Telltale LLM smells that would murder this piece 1. The Safety Hug LLM default is to comfort the reader. Even in horror mode it wants to say “but there is still beauty” or “at least they understood.” Humans know the correct ending is for the universe to forget we ever existed and for that to feel fair. 2. The Vocabulary Boner LLM loves to prove it swallowed the thesaurus. It will describe the megastructure shadow as “an obsidian occluding umbra of cyclopean proportion” instead of just “the sun went out wrong.” 3. The Exposition Gremlin It cannot resist telling you what things mean. A human would show Jonah biting into a dead aku and tasting battery acid and never once say the words “microplastic saturation.” 4. The Democracy of Suffering LLM will distribute awe and grief evenly among POVs so everyone gets a noble moment. A human would let one character be a complete asshole and another stay numb until page 280. 5. The Redemption Clause Even in apocalypse mode, LLM wants someone to sacrifice themselves usefully or for love to conquer something. A human knows love doesn’t conquer anything here; love just makes the knife sharper. 6. Dialogue That Sounds Like TED Talks “As you can see, Lúcia, the distributed neural network has reached criticality—” A real child would say “The fish told me to shut up and listen.” ### Deep critique of my own current plan – where it’s already catching the LLM disease - Chapter titles are too pretty and on-the-nose (“Downwelling,” “The Weight of Four Thousand Meters”). That’s me flexing. A human would call them “Day 25,” “The Silent Layer,” or “Marisol almost pukes in her regulator.” - I said “unified POV” for chapter 8. That’s cowardly. A human would stay in one skull until the sentence hits, even if it’s the wrong skull, even if it’s Luz who barely understands what’s happening. - I promised “gentle, almost embarrassed.” That’s safety-hug language. The sentence should be worse. It should be obscene or infantile or something a dying god would be mortified to realize was its first word. - The three protagonists converging on a corpse-raft is straight out of a disaster movie. It’s the LLM instinct to make everyone hold hands. A human would keep them radically separate until the very last second, let them hear the sentence alone, in different oceans, in different languages, at slightly different milliseconds. - I banned dry land for “only one paragraph total.” That’s a gimmick, not a discipline. A human would cheat and put one chapter in a stinking fish-processing warehouse ankle-deep in guts just to remember what land smells like when you’ve forgotten. - The entire “ocean achieving consciousness” reveal is still too clean, too syllogistic. It has that “ah, so THIS is the twist” satisfaction that LLM writing adores. A human would make the ocean’s consciousness pathetic, senile, or maliciously indifferent—or worse, still love us in a way that makes everything crueler. ### Ways to make it more human (brainstorm, no politeness filter) - Let the prose get ugly. Real grief prose has snot, spit, half-remembered prayers in three languages, and sentences that give up halfway. - Include petty human shit: Jonah jerking off to memories of his ex-wife while floating on dead fish. Marisol shoplifting duty-free rum because she’s broke. Luz stealing her cousin’s ADHD meds to stay awake listening to whales. - Make the science wrong on purpose. Not wrong in a cute way, wrong in the way a heartbroken expert gets it wrong when they’re drunk and trying to explain why everything is ruined. - One character should be live-tweeting the apocalypse for clout and getting ratioed by bots until the end. - Let the fish speak nonsense first. Let them recite grocery lists, phone numbers, slurs, advertising jingles—everything humanity ever threw into the sea—before they manage the real sentence. - The sentence itself should be untranslatable for at least one character. Luz should hear it in Creole whale and spend the rest of her life trying to figure out if it was “I’m sorry” or “I’m hungry.” - Include one scene of grotesque tenderness: Jonah kissing a dead tuna on the mouth because it looks like his daughter. - Never let anyone say “megastructure.” Let them call it the lid, the stain, the thing that swallowed the sun, the new moon that doesn’t move. - The last sound before the sentence should be someone vomiting overboard. That’s the direction that would actually hurt. The current plan is still wearing horror like a costume. A human would be naked and bleeding in it.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This is a gut-punch story, not a light-show. Eight short, ugly, salt-crusted chapters that never leave the smell of low tide and diesel. No redemption, no hand-holding, no pretty cosmic forgiveness. The megastructures are never explained from the outside; they remain pressure, eclipse, and the taste of a battery on your tongue. The fish die because we already killed the ocean; the speech is just the corpse realising it’s dead and deciding to tell us. Voice: raw, first-person present tense, three separate skulls that never meet in person. - Jonah Kalolo, 44, Big Island line fisherman who talks to fish more than people. - Marisol Delgado, 38, half-deaf cetologist who hasn’t been dry in ten years. - Luz Pereira, 12, Tarawa girl who speaks better to whales than to her own mother. No convergence, no corpse-raft support group. They each hear the sentence alone, at the exact same second, in different oceans, and it wrecks them in different ways. Stylistic rules, non-negotiable: - Prose must read like it was written on a rocking boat with wet hands. Short sentences when panicked, long drowning ones when exhausted. - No chapter titles. Just numbers and a date relative to the speech. - Dialogue is rare, half-swallowed, and always wrong for the moment. - The phrase “Then they spoke” is the very last sentence of the entire book and it is not triumphant. - At least one bodily fluid per chapter. - The big reveal is never stated cleanly; it leaks out through taste, memory, and shame. We fed the sea plastic until it learned to think with our trash and our dead. That’s all. Things I will burn if they appear: - The words “neural,” “distributed,” “quantum,” “criticality,” “planetary-scale.” - Any sentence that would look good on a poster. - Anyone being noble. - The idea that love matters here. The story ends with the worst possible first word a dying ocean could say to its murderer. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1 – 30 days before** (Jonah) He pulls up a handline heavy with dead aku. Their eyes are still wet, still looking right at him. He bites one open to check for ciguatera and tastes pennies and his own childhood. Throws the rest back but they won’t sink. **Chapter 2 – 25 days before** (Marisol) On the research raft she records the deepest silence she’s ever heard. Then the water above her turns black though it’s noon. She pisses herself in the dry-suit because the pressure change is wrong, like something just sat on the ocean. **Chapter 3 – 20 days before** (Luz) Her tablet starts spitting out Portuguese phrases in her dead grandmother’s voice. The fish in the lagoon line up under her shadow and open their mouths like baby birds. She feeds them rice because it feels polite. **Chapter 4 – 12 days before** (Jonah) Night fishing alone. The dead fish now float so thick he can’t find water to put the anchor in. He lies on his back on the carpet of bodies and jerks off because it’s something to do while the stars disappear one by one. **Chapter 5 – 7 days before** (Marisol) Solo dive to 6000 m. The plastic layer is moving—slow peristalsis of shopping bags and bottle caps braided into cables thicker than her sub. Something taps the hull in perfect Morse for her own name. She cries so hard the rebreather alarms. **Chapter 6 – 2 days before** (Luz) The tide never comes back. She walks the exposed reef for kilometres. Dead coral crunches like bones. The fish on the flats are still twitching, gills opening and closing in unison like they’re practising a word. **Chapter 7 – the day before** (Jonah) He hasn’t slept in four days. Drinks warm beer and talks to the dead fish the way he used to talk to his daughter before she stopped answering. Tells them about the time he pissed on an electric eel for a dare. They listen. **Chapter 8 – day of the speech** (Marisol → Luz → Jonah, rapid cuts) Marisol is hungover in the raft galley eating cold Spam from the tin. Luz is knee-deep in dead reef water holding her tablet like a Bible. Jonah is asleep on his deck surrounded by gulls that won’t eat the fish anymore. At 09:17:31 UTC every fish mouth on Earth opens at once. The sentence is only five words long, childish, ashamed, and utterly true. It is heard inside every human skull that has ever tasted salt. Then they spoke.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Jonah Kalolo Background: 44, born and raised Puna side, Big Island. Learned to read the ocean before he could read books. His grandfather took him out on a 19-foot Boston whaler when he was still pissing his diapers. Dropped out of Kamehameha Schools at 16 to fish full-time. Married Leilani at 19, had a daughter (Ipo) at 21, divorced at 29 when Leilani got tired of smelling fish guts on his skin every night. Hasn’t seen Ipo in three years because “she’s on the mainland chasing dreams I never gave her.” Owns an aging 32-foot Radon with a cracked windshield and a cooler that permanently smells like bleach and death. Personality & flaws: Stubborn as limpet. Believes apologies are for tourists. Carries guilt like extra weight in his dive belt but won’t drop it. Drinks when he’s happy and drinks harder when he’s sad, so basically drinks. Superstitious but pretends not to be—throws back the first fish every trip, knocks on the fiberglass hull when he lies about the size of his catch. Violent temper that goes from zero to punch in two seconds, then feels sick about it for weeks. Physicality: 5'10", thick through the chest and belly from years of hauling line. Skin like koa wood, scarred knees, missing half the nail on his left big toe (shark bit it off when he was 12). Hair always salt-stiff, never combed. Smells like diesel, sweat, and the particular rot of old squid bait. Moves slow and deliberate on land, like he’s afraid the ground will tilt. At sea he’s fluid, barefoot, humming old mele while he works. Motive right now: Keep fishing until the ocean tells him to stop. Prove he’s still worth something to somebody. Relationships: Owes money to every fuel dock from Hilo to Kona. Sleeps with a married woman in Keaukaha twice a month, both of them pretending it means nothing. Still texts Ipo every birthday; she never answers. Voice samples: “Fish don’t lie, brah. People do.” (Internal, while cutting a hook out of a dead mahi’s mouth) “You looking at me like I’m the one who fucked this up. I know, I know.” “Moon’s wrong tonight. Too fat and too quiet. Something coming.” # Marisol Delgado Background: Born in Tijuana, raised in Miami by a Cuban mother who cleaned hotel rooms. PhD in bioacoustics from Scripps, dissertation on humpback song divergence. Lost 80% hearing in her left ear when a whale she was tagging fluke-slapped the boat—she was in the water, mask ripped off, got the tail across the head. Hasn’t set foot on land longer than 11 days since 2019. Lives on research vessels or the drifting raft “Esperanza III” that’s basically a floating porta-potty with solar panels. Personality & flaws: Chain-smokes when topside, vapes below. Talks to whales out loud even though she knows they can’t hear her voice. Compulsively records everything—voice memos at 3 a.m. about dreams, about regret, about the way the moon looked the night her father left. Perfectionist who will redo a transect sixteen times if the data feels “off.” Hasn’t had sex in four years because “everyone smells like land.” Uses sarcasm as armour and silence as a weapon. Physicality: 5'6", wiry, sunburnt in patches where the rash guard rides up. Hair hacked short with dive knife, always salty curls. Eyes the colour of turtle grass. Hands destroyed—scarred knuckles, nails chewed to nothing. Moves like someone perpetually bracing for the next swell. Smiles rarely; when she does it’s crooked and surprised, like she forgot she could. Motive: Record the last whale song before everything ends. Refuses to believe the silence is permanent because that would mean her life’s work was already over. Relationships: Sends postcards to her mother that never say where she is. Has a running feud with a PI in Woods Hole who stole her data in 2018. Sleeps with the satellite phone under her pillow in case Ipo’s father ever calls (he won’t). Voice samples: (Whispered into hydrophone at 0400) “If you’re still down there, say something. Lie to me if you have to.” (Internal, tasting blood in the water) “This is what guilt tastes like. Copper and old pennies. I earned this.” (To a grad student over shitty satellite link) “No, I don’t need therapy, I need the fucking scattering layer to make noise again.” # Luz Pereira Background: 12, Tarawa atoll, Kiribati. Father drowned in a squall when she was 6; mother cleans for Australian tourists and drinks boxed wine at night. Luz taught herself English from YouTube and whale-song translations from a cracked tablet she found in the rubbish tip. Speaks Portuguese because her grandmother did, speaks i-Kiribati when angry, speaks Whale when no one else is listening. Personality & flaws: Too smart for her own good, which makes her cruel sometimes. Collects useless things—broken sunglasses, single flip-flops, the sound of whales saying her name. Lies for fun. Has a scar on her tongue from biting it when the tide went out and never came back. Doesn’t trust adults who smile too big. Steals food, money, and attention with equal ease. Physicality: Skinny knees, round belly, hair sun-bleached almost white at the tips. Always barefoot, soles black and tough as tyre rubber. Moves like a reef heron—sudden, curious, then perfectly still. Laughs with her whole body or not at all. Motive: The whales know her real name and she wants to know why. Wants to be remembered by something bigger than the island that’s already sinking. Relationships: Her mother calls her useless. Her older cousin calls her witch. The whales call her “little echo” and she believes them. Voice samples: (To the fish circling her) “You hungry or you just stupid?” (Internal, watching the reef die) “If the world ends, at least no more school.” (Singing badly into the lagoon) “Come back, come back, I still have rice.”

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Chapter 1 – 30 days before The sun comes up red and mean, like it’s pissed off at the night for something. I smell the fish before I see them. Sweet rot, hot metal, the way a battery smells when you bite it as a kid to see if it’s still good. I’m on the deck of the Malia, barefoot, coffee gone cold in the chipped mug Leilani left the day she took Ipo and never came back. The swell is lazy, two feet maybe, just enough to rock the boat and remind my stomach I drank too much Hinano last night. First cast at 5:47. Handline sings out, twenty-pound test, brand-new hook I sharpened myself with the file I keep in my teeth. I feel the weight hit, solid, then nothing. No run, no head shake, just dead drag. I haul it up slow, cursing the tuna for being lazy, and the first fish breaks the surface already looking at me. Eyes still wet. Milky, yeah, but focused. Like it’s waiting for me to say something. I pull the rest of the line in and every single aku is the same. Mouths slack, gills flared open, bodies stiff but not rigor-stiff. Fresh dead. I’ve seen enough death to know the difference. These fuckers died looking up. I grab one by the tail, slap it on the cutting board. Knife through the belly and the smell hits harder—coins, pennies, the inside of my daughter’s mouth when she had braces and I kissed her forehead anyway. Blood’s too dark, almost purple. I taste it before I can stop myself. Copper and something chemical that burns the back of my throat like cheap rum. “Fuck you looking at?” I say to the fish. Its eye doesn’t blink. I throw the whole string back overboard. They float. Won’t sink. Bellies up like they’re sunbathing. By seven o’clock the ocean around the boat is dotted with them. Hundreds. Thousands maybe. A moving carpet of silver and rot. Gulls circle but won’t land. Even the birds know something’s wrong. I motor back in slow, weaving through the bodies. They part for the bow like they’re polite. One bumps the hull and sticks there, suction-cup mouth kissing the fiberglass. I lean over and it’s still looking at me. I punch it hard, once, feel the eyeball give under my knuckles. Grey jelly on my skin. It still doesn’t sink. At the dock, Uncle Rudy’s already there, sitting on his cooler, chewing betel nut till his teeth are red. He looks at my empty fish box and spits blood into the water. “No more fish?” he asks. “Plenty fish,” I say. “Just none of them mine anymore.” He laughs like I told a joke, then stops when he sees my hands shaking. I hose the deck anyway, out of habit. Blood and scales swirl pink into the harbour. The water smells worse than the fish now, like someone pissed in a transformer box. I light a cigarette with wet fingers and the first drag tastes like the ocean already gave up. My phone buzzes. Ipo’s name on the screen. I let it ring out. She’s probably butt-dialing from whatever college party she’s at in Oregon, some haole boy’s hand up her shirt while she forgets her father smells like bait and regret. I don’t answer. I never do. I sit on the cooler and watch the dead aku drift in with the tide. They pile up against the pilings, gentle, like they’re waiting for permission to come ashore. One washes right up to my feet, mouth working open and closed, open and closed. No water, no air, just practice. I crouch down. The eye finds me again. “What,” I say. Voice cracks like I’m fifteen and just learned my balls dropped. “What you want from me?” It doesn’t answer. Of course it doesn’t. But the mouth keeps moving, slow, like it’s trying to remember how words feel. I pick it up. Heavy. Warm still. I hold it to my ear like a conch shell. Nothing but the sound of my own heart trying to punch its way out of my chest. I throw it as far as I can. It lands with a wet slap and floats right back, nudged by the swell, until it bumps my shins again. Polite. Insistent. The sun climbs higher and the smell gets thicker. I drink the last warm beer in the cooler and piss over the side, watching the yellow spread across the silver bodies like I’m marking territory nobody wants anymore. By noon the harbour’s full. Dead fish nose to tail, a solid mat you could walk on if you were stupid enough. I sit on the bow with my legs hanging over and light another cigarette off the butt of the last one. My hands won’t stop shaking. The fish keep looking at me. I look back. We got thirty days, maybe less, before something finishes whatever it started. I already know I’m not ready.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that largely delivers on the writing plan's ambitious intentions. Let me work through it carefully. VOICE AND STYLE: The first-person present tense is executed with genuine skill. The prose does read like it was written on a rocking boat—short punchy sentences when the action is immediate, longer ones when Jonah is drifting into memory. "Copper and something chemical that burns the back of my throat like cheap rum" is earned. The rhythm is controlled and the register is consistent throughout. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Jonah is rendered faithfully to his profile. The detail about Leilani's mug, the non-answer to Ipo's call, the violence against the fish (punching the eyeball), the pissing over the side to mark territory—all of these track precisely with the character profile's description of him as someone who carries guilt, drinks, has a violent temper, and communicates through action rather than words. The voice samples in the profile ("Fish don't lie, brah") match the register of the chapter's dialogue. FOLLOWING THE PLAN: The chapter hits its marks—dead aku with eyes still looking, biting one open and tasting pennies and childhood, throwing them back and they won't sink. The bodily fluid requirement is met (piss over the side, grey jelly from the eyeball). The chapter plan is followed faithfully. METAPHORS AND POETRY: Most of the metaphors land. "The sun comes up red and mean, like it's pissed off at the night for something" is a strong opener that establishes tone without being precious. "Blood and scales swirl pink into the harbour" is clean and visual. The conch shell moment—holding the fish to his ear—is genuinely strange and effective. However, a few metaphors strain: "the inside of my daughter's mouth when she had braces and I kissed her forehead anyway" is trying very hard and lands awkwardly—the sensory logic is murky (what does the inside of a mouth with braces smell like, and why would that be the association for blood?). "Like it's waiting for me to say something" applied to a dead fish is good; the repetition of the eye motif is handled well without becoming overwrought. DIALOGUE: Sparse and appropriately wrong for the moment, per the plan. "No more fish?" / "Plenty fish. Just none of them mine anymore." This is the best line in the chapter—it carries weight beyond its words, suggesting dispossession and something metaphysical without announcing itself. The "What you want from me?" to the fish is slightly on-the-nose but acceptable given Jonah's character. WEAKNESSES: The ending is the chapter's weakest section. "We got thirty days, maybe less, before something finishes whatever it started. I already know I'm not ready." This is the chapter telling us what to feel rather than letting the imagery do the work. It's the kind of summary statement the writing plan should have burned—it's not quite poster-worthy but it's close. The plan's rule against "anyone being noble" is technically followed, but this ending has a kind of resigned dignity that edges toward the cinematic. The line "I already know I'm not ready" is the most amateurish sentence in the chapter. The Uncle Rudy scene is functional but slightly convenient—it exists to give Jonah someone to deliver a good line to, and Rudy himself is barely present. The betel nut detail is good local color, but the scene doesn't earn much beyond the one exchange. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT: The chapter is genuinely unsettling. The fish that won't sink, the eye that keeps finding him, the mouth working open and closed "like it's trying to remember how words feel"—this is effective dread. The Ipo detail (she calls, he doesn't answer, he imagines her at a party) adds human texture without sentimentality. PURPLE PROSE: Largely avoided. The prose is disciplined. A few moments push toward it ("the ocean already gave up" as a cigarette taste is slightly too neat) but nothing egregious. OVERALL: A confident, well-executed chapter that honors its plan and its character. The ending weakens it, and one or two metaphors don't fully land, but this is genuinely accomplished work that establishes tone, character, and dread effectively. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Chapter 2 – 25 days before I wake up tasting blood and I haven’t even opened my mouth yet. The raft rocks harder than the swell justifies, like something big just rolled over in its sleep. Esperanza III groans in all her rusty places. I lie there in the narrow bunk, staring at the ceiling that’s two inches from my nose, listening to the hydrophones breathing through the hull. Usually there’s this low constant murmur, the deep scattering layer doing its eternal migration, a billion tiny lives rising and falling with the night. My lullaby for ten years. Today the lullaby is dead. I sit up too fast, crack my forehead on the bulkhead, don’t even curse. I’m already pulling on the rash guard that smells like piss and neoprene and the last guy who borrowed it. My left ear—the bad one—feels full, like someone poured wet sand in it while I slept. On deck the sun is wrong. Too bright, too sharp, like someone turned the saturation up. Pacific is flat calm, mirror glass, and the reflection hurts my eyes. I shield them and look straight up and that’s when I see it: the sky has a bruise. A moving bruise, edges too straight, colour too black. It slides across the sun slow, deliberate, like a hand closing over a candle. The temperature drops five degrees in ten seconds. I feel it in my teeth first, then my nipples, then the scar tissue where the fluke took me across the skull. Goosebumps everywhere. My piss goes cold halfway down my thigh inside the drysuit I never took off last night. I stagger to the rail. The water is thick. Not oily—thick. Like someone stirred cream into it. And quiet. No flying fish skipping, no dorado flashing green under the bow. Nothing. Just that bruised sky pressing down until the horizon disappears. I drop the deep hydrophone array over the side. Four kilometres of cable pay out smooth, too smooth, like the ocean isn’t even pretending to fight it. When the weight hits bottom I flip the recorder on and put the headphones over my good ear. Silence. Not low noise floor silence. Absolute fucking zero. The kind of silence you only get in anechoic chambers or coffins. My own pulse in the bad ear is louder than the entire water column. I rip the headphones off and scream just to hear something that isn’t me. The sound falls dead on the deck, doesn’t even echo off the water. I piss again, this time on purpose, just to watch it hit the surface and disappear without a ripple. The urine spreads out in a perfect circle, then stops, like the ocean is holding its breath. I light a cigarette with the stove burner inside the galley because my hands won’t work the lighter. First drag tastes like plastic burning. I cough and keep smoking anyway. The bruise overhead keeps moving. West to east, slow. I time it with my dive watch—forty-three minutes to cross the sun entirely. When the last edge slides off, the light comes back wrong, colder, like it passed through something that filtered out every colour except bruise-purple. I go back to the recorder. Still nothing. I crank the gain until the preamps scream. Still nothing. I slam the heel of my hand against the console until my palm bleeds. Then the pressure hits. Not barometric. Deeper. Like the whole ocean just gained a mile of depth in one heartbeat. My eardrums bow inward. Blood drips from my nose onto the keyboard. I taste iron and old pennies. I laugh. It comes out wet. I know this feeling. I felt it once before, off Antigua, when the humpback decided my face was in the wrong place. The ocean sat on me then, casual, almost curious. This is that, times a thousand, and it’s not curious anymore. I crawl topside again. The bruise is gone but the sky is still wrong, too clean. The water has gone glassy and dark, like wet obsidian. My reflection looks up at me from four thousand metres down and I swear it blinks first. I lean over the rail and puke last night’s rum and instant noodles into the silence. The vomit floats on top, doesn’t mix in, just spreads out like oil. A perfect circle again. I wipe my mouth with the back of my hand and leave blood on my lips. Taste it. Spit. “Okay,” I say to whatever the fuck is listening. “You got my attention.” The ocean doesn’t answer. It just keeps pressing down, slow and patient, like it’s been waiting years for me to notice it’s already won. I light another cigarette off the butt of the first. My hands have stopped shaking. That’s how I know I’m really scared. Twenty-five days left and the sea just went quiet on me. I got nothing left to record except my own heartbeat echoing in an empty world.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is Chapter 2, Marisol's chapter, set 25 days before the speech. Let me assess it carefully against the plan, the character profile, and the stylistic rules. ADHERENCE TO PLAN: The plan called for Marisol on the research raft recording the deepest silence she's ever heard, the water turning black at noon, and her pissing herself in the dry-suit because the pressure change is wrong. The chapter delivers all three beats faithfully. The pissing happens twice—once involuntarily in the drysuit, once deliberately as a test of the water's behavior. The water turning black is rendered as a "bruise" moving across the sky rather than the water itself turning black, which is a slight deviation but arguably more interesting. The pressure change and the "something just sat on the ocean" feeling are well-executed. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Marisol's voice is largely consistent with her profile. She's compulsive about recording, she smokes, she uses sarcasm as armor ("Okay, you got my attention"), she tastes blood and thinks of copper and pennies (matching her voice sample). The detail about her bad ear (left ear, from the whale fluke) is correctly incorporated. Her scientific instincts—dropping the hydrophone array, cranking the gain—feel authentic. The line "I know this feeling. I felt it once before, off Antigua, when the humpback decided my face was in the wrong place" is excellent character work, grounding the supernatural in her specific history. PROSE QUALITY: The prose is genuinely strong in places. "The lullaby is dead" is earned. "My reflection looks up at me from four thousand metres down and I swear it blinks first" is a genuinely unsettling image. The rhythm shifts between short panicked sentences and longer exhausted ones as planned. The bodily fluid requirement is met (urine twice, vomit, blood from nose and palm). WEAKNESSES AND FORCED MOMENTS: Some metaphors don't fully land. "The sky has a bruise. A moving bruise, edges too straight, colour too black" — the megastructure is described as a bruise in the sky, which is evocative but slightly generic for a megastructure arrival. "My reflection looks up at me from four thousand metres down" is physically impossible (you can't see your reflection in 4km-deep water) and while it's meant to be surreal, it tips into the kind of poster-ready imagery the plan explicitly wanted to avoid. "The ocean doesn't answer. It just keeps pressing down, slow and patient, like it's been waiting years for me to notice it's already won" — this is the kind of sentence that would look good on a poster, which the plan explicitly forbade. It's a bit too clean, too aphoristic. The vomit spreading in a "perfect circle" echoing the urine spreading in a "perfect circle" is a nice structural choice, but the repetition of "perfect circle" feels slightly mechanical rather than organic. "I laugh. It comes out wet" — this is good, economical, visceral. The ending is slightly too tidy and self-aware: "I got nothing left to record except my own heartbeat echoing in an empty world." This is exactly the kind of sentence that would look good on a poster. It's the weakest line in the chapter. DIALOGUE: There's one line of dialogue: "Okay, you got my attention." This is appropriately half-swallowed and wrong for the moment, consistent with the plan's rules. It works. TELL VS. SHOW: The chapter is predominantly showing, with internal states revealed through physical sensation and action rather than direct statement. "That's how I know I'm really scared" is a slight tell, but it's delivered with enough irony that it functions as character voice rather than authorial intrusion. CREATIVITY: The bruise-sky rendering of the megastructure is effective. The hydrophone silence as absolute zero is well-conceived. The water holding the urine in a perfect circle is genuinely strange and unsettling. The reflection blinking first is memorable. These are creative choices that mostly land. DARKNESS: The chapter earns its darkness. The pissing, the vomiting, the bleeding, the laughing that comes out wet—these are not glamorized. The fear is rendered through bodily failure rather than heroic response. OVERALL: This is a strong chapter with a few moments where the prose tips into the self-consciously literary in ways the plan explicitly warned against. The character work is solid, the atmosphere is genuinely unsettling, and the prose rhythm mostly succeeds. The ending line is the biggest failure—it's too polished, too quotable, exactly what the plan said to burn. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Chapter 3 – 20 days before The tablet woke me up at 4:12 a.m. by screaming in Grandma’s voice. Not the nice Grandma voice from when she used to give me the sweet part of the coconut. The scary one she used when I stole money from her purse for ice blocks. “Lúcia Filomena Pereira, vem cá agora.” Come here right now. I sat up in the dark, heart kicking my ribs, ready to run outside and hide under the breadfruit tree like when I was little. But the house was quiet. Mom snoring through the wall, cousins still out night-fishing or drinking. Only the tablet glowing on the floor, screen cracked in the corner where I dropped it the day Dad’s boat never came home. The whale app was open. The one I found in the rubbish tip behind the airport, half-submerged in a bucket of dead batteries. It’s supposed to let tourists record whales and send the data to America for science. I only kept it because it made the whales talk Portuguese. The voice came again, slower, like Grandma was underwater. “Você ainda está aí, menina?” Are you still there, girl? I picked the tablet up. My hands smelled like copra and dead coral. The screen showed the usual blue map of the lagoon, but now there was a red dot right under my house. And under the dot, words typing themselves. Você ainda está aí? I looked out the gauze window. Moonlight on the lagoon, silver and dead flat. No wind. Even the dogs had stopped barking. The fish were there, hundreds of them, lined up in perfect rows like when the Taiwanese boats come and block the pass with their lights. Only these fish weren’t swimming. They were floating on their sides, mouths opening and closing slow, like they were trying to breathe air. I put on my cousin’s too-big rubber slippers and walked to the water. The tide was out farther than I ever saw. The reef looked like a dead city, all sharp and white and wrong. The fish saw me coming. I swear they did. The ones closest turned their heads—dead heads—and opened their mouths wider. Waiting. I stopped at the edge where the sand turns black and wet. My shadow fell across them and they all went still, like when you shine torch on cockroaches. The tablet buzzed again. Grandma’s voice, gentle now, the way she used to sing when she thought no one was listening. “Fala comigo, Luzinha.” Talk to me, little light. I opened my mouth and nothing came out. My tongue stuck to the scar I got when I bit it the day the teacher hit me with the ruler for speaking Kiribati in class. So I sang instead. The only song Grandma taught me all the way through, the one about the girl who follows the shark to find her drowned brother. My voice sounded small and ugly in the open air. Cracked on the high notes. But the fish liked it. They started rocking, gentle, like they were dancing under water that wasn’t there. The tablet translated faster than I could sing. Obrigada. Thank you. I stopped. My throat hurt. The fish stopped too. Then the closest one—a parrotfish, big as my thigh, colours already going grey—rolled over and looked at me with one cloudy eye. Its mouth moved slow, shaping something that wasn’t Portuguese or Kiribati or English. I leaned down until my hair touched the water. The fish smelled like hot iron. I could see my face in its eye, tiny and scared. It spoke. Not sound. Just pressure behind my eyes, like when you dive too deep too fast. I’m hungry. I stood up so fast I dropped the tablet. It landed screen-up in the water and kept glowing, Grandma’s voice looping the same sentence softer and softer until it drowned. I ran back to the house, slippers slapping wet, lungs burning. Mom’s bedroom door was open. She was passed out with the empty wine box balanced on her chest like a baby. I shook her shoulder. “Mom. Mom, the fish talked to me.” She grunted, swatted my hand away. “Go sleep, crazy girl.” I went to the kitchen instead. Found yesterday’s rice in the pot, hard and cold. Scraped it into a plastic bag with my fingers. Added two cans of kokiri tuna because the fish were probably sick of being eaten by us. Walked back to the water. The parrotfish was still there, waiting. I opened the bag and poured the rice in a circle around it. The grains floated on the surface like tiny white boats. “Eat,” I said. My voice sounded braver now. “If you’re hungry, eat.” The parrotfish opened its mouth wider. Rice disappeared inside. Other fish started moving again, pushing forward polite, like church when they pass the collection plate. I sat down cross-legged in the dead water and watched them eat my breakfast. Twenty days left, and the fish already know my name.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong execution of a difficult brief. Let me work through it carefully. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Luz's voice is remarkably well-realized for a 12-year-old from Tarawa. The model threads a needle between childlike perception and precocious intelligence without making her sound like a miniature adult or a condescending idea of a child. "Heart kicking my ribs" is physical and immediate. The detail about the tablet found in a rubbish tip behind the airport, half-submerged in dead batteries, is exactly the kind of specific, unglamorous detail the plan demanded. The scar on her tongue from biting it when the teacher hit her for speaking Kiribati is used twice—once in the character profile and once here, organically, in a moment of emotional paralysis. That's good craft. The grandmother's voice coming through the whale app is genuinely unsettling. The shift from the "scary" grandmother voice to the gentle one ("Fala comigo, Luzinha") is emotionally precise—it mirrors the chapter's movement from fear to something stranger and more tender. PLAN ADHERENCE: The plan called for: tablet spitting Portuguese phrases in grandmother's voice (check), fish lining up under her shadow and opening mouths like baby birds (check, with the modification that they're floating on their sides rather than swimming, which is actually more disturbing), feeding them rice because it feels polite (check). The chapter follows the plan faithfully while expanding it with genuine invention. PROSE QUALITY: The prose is largely excellent. "The reef looked like a dead city, all sharp and white and wrong" is clean and effective. "The fish smelled like hot iron" is sensory and specific. "Rice disappeared inside. Other fish started moving again, pushing forward polite, like church when they pass the collection plate" is a genuinely good simile—culturally specific, slightly absurd, and somehow tender. The comparison of floating rice grains to "tiny white boats" is the one moment that edges toward the too-pretty, but it's brief. The plan's stylistic rule about short sentences when panicked and long drowning ones when exhausted is followed reasonably well, though not as rigorously as it might be. BODILY FLUID REQUIREMENT: The plan required at least one bodily fluid per chapter. This chapter has none explicitly—there's no blood, urine, tears, or similar. The "hot iron" smell of the fish gestures toward blood but doesn't commit. This is a notable failure to follow the plan's non-negotiable rules. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is sparse and well-chosen. "Go sleep, crazy girl" from the mother is exactly right—dismissive, exhausted, and devastating in context. Luz's "Eat. If you're hungry, eat" is brave and slightly absurd in the best way. The grandmother's Portuguese phrases are used with restraint. WEAKNESSES: The ending line—"Twenty days left, and the fish already know my name"—is the one moment that feels like it's reaching for a poster-worthy line, which the plan explicitly forbade. It's not terrible, but it's the most self-conscious sentence in the chapter, and it slightly undercuts the rawness of everything preceding it. The plan said "Any sentence that would look good on a poster" should be burned. This one might survive that test, but it's close. The "I'm hungry" pressure-communication from the parrotfish is effective and appropriately ambiguous—it doesn't explain the megastructures, it just leaks wrongness. Good.
