The chapter is coherent, internally consistent, and advances the larger story's themes (the ocean speaking through the dead, guilt, the intimacy of being known by something dying) without over-explaining. CREATIVITY: The whale app translating fish speech into Portuguese grandmother-voice is a genuinely strange and original conceit. The fish eating rice "polite, like church" is unexpected. The chapter earns its darkness without melodrama. OVERALL: A very strong chapter with one notable plan violation (no bodily fluid) and one slightly too-neat closing line. The voice is consistent, the imagery mostly lands, and the emotional register is exactly right. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Chapter 4 – 12 days before I’m too old for this shit and the ocean knows it. The Malia rolls dead slow through a mat of fish so thick the prop barely bites water. Midnight, no moon, stars gone somewhere else. The dead ones glow, faint green, like someone spilled glowstick juice across the whole Pacific. I can see my own shadow on their bellies. I cut the engine an hour ago because why burn diesel when there’s nowhere to go. The silence is total. No engine thump, no slap of waves against the hull, no night birds. Just the wet sound of fish breathing where they shouldn’t. I lie on my back across the fishbox, boots off, soles of my feet burning from the sun all day. The bodies under me are still warm. They give a little when I shift, like a waterbed full of rot. The smell is thick enough to chew—sweet and chemical and ashamed. My dick’s been half-hard for an hour and I don’t know why. Fear, maybe. Or the way the dead mahi keep brushing against the back of my calves like they’re trying to hold hands. I unzip anyway. Old habit when the world tilts: jerk off so at least something still works the way it’s supposed to. I spit in my palm, taste salt and blood from where I bit my tongue earlier. Start slow, eyes closed, thinking of Leilani the last time she let me fuck her in the fish hold, both of us stinking of skipjack and plumeria, her telling me to hurry before Ipo woke up from her nap. The fish move under me while I do it. A slow rolling wave that matches my stroke. I open my eyes and one of them—a big aku, maybe the same one from thirty days ago—has drifted close enough that its mouth is right by my ear. It opens and closes in perfect time with my hand. I laugh. Sounds like crying. “Little late for that, yeah?” I tell it. My voice is hoarse from swallowing diesel fumes and regret. “Should’ve asked while you were still swimming.” The aku’s eye reflects the glow from its own dead skin. Milky but bright. It watches me finish. I come hard, shameful, streaking across its scales like I’m marking territory nobody’s gonna fight me for. After, I don’t clean up. Just lie there panting, feeling the sperm cool between my belly and a dead fish. The glow gets brighter. The whole ocean is lit from underneath now, soft radioactive green, like the time Uncle Rudy took us night-diving for kona crab and the phosphorescence was so thick we could read the depth gauge by it. Only this isn’t plankton. I sit up slow. My skin sticks to the fish and makes a wet peeling sound when I move. Everywhere I look, the dead ones are facing me. Thousands of them. Tens of thousands. Mouths all opening and closing at the same speed, slow, like a heartbeat you can see. I crawl to the rail on hands and knees. The water itself is glowing. Not the surface—down deep. Green light rising up through blackness, layer by layer, until the whole water column is lit like a shitty aquarium. And in that light I can see shapes moving. Not fish. Bigger. Longer. Things braided together out of plastic bags and fishing line and six-pack rings, things that used to be trash and now have purpose. One of them brushes the hull. The boat lurches. I grab the rail to keep from falling overboard and my hand comes away wrapped in monofilament that’s warm like vein. I look down and the aku is still there, floating right below me. Its mouth is moving again, but this time there’s sound. Not in the air—inside my skull. Pressure behind my eyes. It says my daughter’s name. Ipo. Clear as the day she was born, when the nurse handed her to me slippery and screaming and perfect. I lean over the rail until my forehead touches the water. The glow burns my eyes. “Say it again,” I whisper. The fish all open their mouths at once. The sound comes from everywhere and nowhere, wet and gentle and ancient. Ipo. Ipo. Ipo. I slide down the rail until I’m sitting in fish guts and my own cooling come, tears mixing with snot, saying her name back to them like a prayer or a curse. They listen. Twelve days left and the ocean already knows what I lost.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a genuinely unsettling piece of work that largely succeeds on its own brutal terms. Let me examine it carefully. **Voice and Execution** The first-person present-tense voice is consistent and convincing. Jonah reads as a real person—the prose has the texture of someone who thinks in short, declarative bursts punctuated by longer, exhausted sentences when the weight gets too heavy. "The smell is thick enough to chew—sweet and chemical and ashamed" is a strong line. The rhythm shifts appropriately between panic and exhaustion as the plan demanded. **The Masturbation Scene** This is the chapter's central gamble and it mostly pays off. The plan called for Jonah to masturbate on the carpet of dead fish "because it's something to do while the stars disappear one by one"—a gesture of nihilistic normalcy against cosmic horror. The execution goes further: the fish moving in rhythm with his hand, the aku watching, the ejaculation onto scales "like I'm marking territory nobody's gonna fight me for." This is genuinely transgressive and purposeful rather than gratuitous. It establishes Jonah's particular brand of shame—he doesn't stop, he doesn't look away, he finishes and lies in it. That's character work. The line "I come hard, shameful, streaking across its scales like I'm marking territory nobody's gonna fight me for" is the chapter's best sentence. It's ugly and precise and earns its ugliness. **The Daughter Reveal** The pivot to Ipo's name being spoken by the fish is effective. The plan mentioned he "talks to the dead fish the way he used to talk to his daughter" but that's Chapter 7—here the ocean speaks her name unprompted, which is actually more powerful than the plan suggested. The detail "Clear as the day she was born, when the nurse handed her to me slippery and screaming and perfect" is the chapter's most human moment and it lands because it's earned by everything preceding it. **Problems and Weaknesses** The chapter has some forced moments. "Things braided together out of plastic bags and fishing line and six-pack rings, things that used to be trash and now have purpose" is a bit on-the-nose—it telegraphs the big reveal (the ocean thinking with our trash) too explicitly for a story that's supposed to let it leak out through taste and shame. The plan said "the big reveal is never stated cleanly; it leaks out through taste, memory, and shame." This sentence states it fairly cleanly. "The glow gets brighter. The whole ocean is lit from underneath now, soft radioactive green, like the time Uncle Rudy took us night-diving for kona crab and the phosphorescence was so thick we could read the depth gauge by it." The Uncle Rudy comparison is good—it grounds the supernatural in memory. But "soft radioactive green" is a slight contradiction in terms that doesn't quite work. "The wet sound of fish breathing where they shouldn't" is a strong image. "Like a waterbed full of rot" is effective. "Monofilament that's warm like vein" is excellent—spare and wrong in exactly the right way. The ending—"Twelve days left and the ocean already knows what I lost"—is the chapter's weakest line. It's too summarizing, too clean, too much like a chapter-ending thesis statement. The plan's stylistic rules said no sentence that would look good on a poster; this one comes close. It tells us what we've just been shown. **Consistency with Profile and Plan** Jonah is consistent with his profile: the guilt, the drinking (implied), the daughter he's lost contact with, the superstition, the physicality. The chapter follows the plan closely—dead fish carpet, lying on his back, masturbating, stars disappearing. The plan mentioned "the stars disappear one by one" but the chapter handles this more atmospherically ("stars gone somewhere else") which is actually better. The bodily fluid requirement is met (semen, tears, snot, spit). The prose reads like wet hands on a rocking boat. The dialogue is rare and half-swallowed. **Overall Assessment** This is strong, committed work that takes real risks and mostly lands them. The transgressive content serves the story's themes of shame, loss, and the body's persistence in the face of horror. The weaknesses are real but minor: one moment of over-explanation, one too-clean closing line. The chapter does what it sets out to do with considerable skill. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Chapter 5 – 7 days before I am six thousand metres down and the ocean is chewing on my bones. The sub is a coffin with windows. One-person aluminum sphere, three inches of glass between me and pressure that can turn steel into tinfoil. I named her Golondrina once, when I still thought names mattered. Now she’s just the thing keeping me from becoming red mist. Lights off to save battery. I run dark, the way whales do when they’re hunting. The only glow is the faint green coming through the porthole, the same sick colour that’s been leaking up from the trench for days. I’ve been descending for three hours. Slow, polite, like I’m visiting someone’s grave and don’t want to disturb them. The plastic layer starts at four thousand metres now. It used to be a thin snow of bags and nurdles. Now it’s thick. Braided. Moving. I turn the forward flood on for thirty seconds. Enough to see and regret. It’s alive. Shopping bags woven into cables as thick as my thigh, pulsing slow. Bottle caps for scales. Fishing nets knotted into tendons. A child’s plastic sandal (size maybe six) caught in the weave, still bright blue, still smiling with that stupid cartoon shark. The whole mess is breathing. Not metaphor. Actual peristalsis, slow waves rolling through trash that learned rhythm. I kill the light. Sit in the dark and feel my heartbeat try to match it. The tapping starts. Three short, three long, three short. SOS. Then my name. M-A-R-I-S-O-L In perfect Morse, tapped against the hull by something that has no fingers. I scream. The rebreather scrubs it clean, turns it into a flat hiss. I punch the bulkhead until my knuckles split. Blood floats in perfect spheres around my face like red planets. The tapping stops. Silence again, thicker now. I cry the way I haven’t since the whale took my hearing. Sobbing that comes from the gut, ugly, animal. The kind that makes you vomit if you don’t stop. I don’t stop. I let it wreck me. Because I know what this is. We fed it. Every straw, every lid, every bag that said Thank You Have A Nice Day. We fed it for fifty years and it learned our shape. Learned hunger. Learned language from the shit we threw away. It’s not alien. It’s us. Digested. The green glow brightens. Something huge moves past the porthole—slow, deliberate, curious. I catch a glimpse of what might have once been a humpback’s jawbone wrapped in yellow rope, teeth replaced by bottle caps sharpened to points. It pauses. Looks in. Its eye is a traffic light. Red, yellow, green. All three lit at once. It knows me. I press my forehead to the glass. Blood from my knuckles smears it. “Say it,” I whisper. My voice sounds like someone else’s. “Say my fucking name.” The jawbone opens. No sound. Just pressure. The same pressure I felt twenty-five days ago when the sky sat on the ocean. But this time it has words. They come inside my skull, gentle, like a lover who’s been waiting years to speak. You left us here. I scream again. This time the rebreather can’t keep up. I taste copper and plasticiser. My nose bleeds hard, twin streams that float and merge into one fat drop that drifts toward the ceiling. I reach for the ascent trigger. My hand stops halfway. Because the thing outside is crying. Not metaphor. Actual tears—clear fluid leaking from the traffic-light eye, mixing with the plastic cables like it’s trying to wash itself clean. I watch it cry for a long time. Then I do the worst thing I’ve ever done in my life. I open the external speaker. The one I use to play whale song to calves. I play the recording I’ve never played for anyone. The one I made the night my father left, age nine, standing on the balcony in Tijuana screaming for him to come back until my throat bled. Age nine. Miami. 1995. The sound fills the trench. My child voice, raw and broken, calling Papi over and over. The braided thing stops crying. It presses closer until the hull groans. It sings back. Not my voice. My father’s. The exact timbre, the exact Cuban accent, saying mija like he used to before he disappeared into America and never looked back. I curl into a ball inside the sphere and let the tears come until there’s nothing left but salt. Seven days left and the ocean knows every name I ever lost. It’s practising.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkable piece of sustained tension and visceral imagination. Let me assess it carefully across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The prose rhythm is genuinely accomplished. The author follows through on the stated intention of "short sentences when panicked, long drowning ones when exhausted." The opening "I am six thousand metres down and the ocean is chewing on my bones" is earned rather than decorative—it establishes physical reality and psychological state simultaneously. The shift between clipped declarative sentences during the tapping sequence and longer, more exhausted constructions during the emotional breakdown is technically controlled. The central image—plastic waste that has learned to think, to speak, to remember—is executed with restraint. The author resists explaining it. The shopping bags woven into cables, the bottle caps for scales, the child's sandal "still smiling with that stupid cartoon shark"—these details accumulate into something genuinely unsettling without ever becoming expository. The cartoon shark sandal is particularly good: specific, mundane, and horrifying in context. The bodily fluid requirement (blood from knuckles, nosebleed, tears) is met without feeling checklist-y. The blood floating in "perfect spheres like red planets" is one of the few metaphors that might be accused of prettiness, but it's grounded in the physics of zero-pressure environments and earns its place. The father's voice moment is the chapter's emotional climax and it lands. The decision to play the childhood recording—the nine-year-old screaming for her father—and have the ocean respond in her father's voice is genuinely devastating. It follows the plan's logic (the ocean learned from what we threw away, including our grief) without stating it. The reveal that the ocean knows "every name I ever lost" and is "practising" is chilling and thematically coherent. The character is consistent with the profile: Marisol's compulsive recording, her relationship with her absent father, her tendency to push past breaking points, her use of the whale-song speaker—all track with the established character sheet. The detail about her father leaving when she was nine, set in Tijuana (the profile says she was born in Tijuana), is consistent. WEAKNESSES: "It's us. Digested." is the chapter's most poster-ready moment—exactly the kind of thing the author's own plan said to burn. It's a clean, quotable statement of the theme that does the reader's interpretive work for them. It's the one place where the prose tells rather than shows. "You left us here" as the ocean's first communication is slightly on-the-nose. The thematic content is correct but the phrasing is almost too clean, too articulate for something that supposedly learned language from discarded plastic. The plan called for the reveal to "leak out through taste, memory, and shame" rather than be stated. This line states it. The traffic-light eye is a strong image but "Red, yellow, green. All three lit at once" risks tipping into the kind of sci-fi visual shorthand that the story is otherwise avoiding. It's not quite purple prose but it's close to a poster image. The rebreather "scrubbing" the scream clean is a nice detail, but "turns it into a flat hiss" is slightly imprecise—rebreathers don't work quite that way, and the story is otherwise careful about physical reality. The ending line "Seven days left and the ocean knows every name I ever lost. It's practising." is strong but the second sentence ("It's practising") is doing heavy thematic lifting in a way that's slightly too neat. The chapter would be marginally stronger ending on "every name I ever lost" alone. OVERALL: This is high-quality literary fiction executing a difficult tonal register with considerable skill. The failures are minor and the successes are substantial. The chapter follows its plan closely, maintains character consistency, and advances the story's central horror with genuine craft. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Chapter 6 – 2 days before The tide forgot us. I wake up because the roosters are screaming at nothing. No surf sound, no slap of water under the house. Just hot wind and the smell of reef left out in the sun too long. I walk outside in my cousin’s basketball shorts and nothing else. The lagoon is gone. Not low tide gone. Gone gone. Sand and coral stretching out white and cracked all the way to the old Japanese wreck, two kilometres out. Even the deep channel is dry, just a ditch full of flapping fish and dying eels. Fish everywhere. Big ones, small ones, parrotfish the colour of candy, triggerfish with their stupid teeth still trying to bite the air. They cover the reef flat like a carpet you could lie on and never touch bottom. Some still twitch. Most don’t. Their eyes are cooking in the sun, turning milky, but every few minutes a wave of gills opens and closes all at once, like the whole reef is practising breathing. I step down from the stilts. The coral cuts my feet instant. Sharp as glass. Blood comes quick, bright red on white coral, pretty almost. I keep walking. Halfway out I find the parrotfish. The big one that ate my rice twenty days ago. He’s on his side, mouth still crusted with old grains. When he sees me his tail flips once, weak, like he’s trying to wave. I sit next to him. The coral bites my ass but I don’t care. “Hey, fatty,” I say. My voice sounds too loud without water to carry it. His mouth opens. No sound. Just shaping my name the way he did before. Luz. Luz. Luz. I pick him up. He’s lighter than last time, dried out, but still warm from the sun. I hold him against my chest like a baby. His scales stick to my skin. “You said you were hungry,” I tell him. “I brought more rice. It’s old now. Got weevils. You mind weevils?” He doesn’t answer. His eye finds mine and stays there. All around us the reef fish start moving their mouths at the same speed. Open. Close. Open. Close. Like they’re learning a new word together. I look toward the horizon. The ocean is still there, way out, a dark line under the sky. But between here and there is just this huge dead plate of coral and bodies. The water pulled back and never came home. Mom’s voice behind me, hoarse from wine and sleep. “Lúcia, get back here! You’ll burn!” I don’t turn around. I keep walking toward the drop-off, carrying the parrotfish. Blood from my feet leaves prints that fill with tiny crabs instant. At the edge where the reef falls into deep blue nothing, I sit. The parrotfish starts twitching harder. His gills flare red. He’s suffocating in air and still trying to talk. I put my mouth right next to his ear hole, the little flap behind the eye. “I’m listening,” I whisper. “Say it. I’m not scared.” Thousands of fish mouths open at once. I feel it before I hear it. Pressure behind my eyes, same as before, only stronger now, like someone squeezing my brain gentle. The parrotfish uses my own voice inside my head. My voice from when I was six, the day Dad left on the boat and never came back. I’m hungry, Luz. But it’s not talking about rice. It’s talking about everything we took. I start crying without sound. Tears run down my neck and drip onto the parrotfish’s scales. He drinks them. I swear he does. His mouth keeps moving against my collarbone, slow, like kissing. I look out at the empty sea bed stretching forever. The sun is brutal. My skin already hurts. I lie down on my back in the middle of all the dying fish and hold the parrotfish on my chest. We stay like that all day. Me burning, him drying, both of us breathing the same hot air. When night comes the stars come out wrong. Too bright. Too many. Like someone poked extra holes in the sky. The fish keep practising. Gills flaring together. One big heartbeat made of millions of small ones. I fall asleep with coral cutting into my back and the parrotfish’s mouth still moving against my skin, shaping a word that isn’t ready yet. Two days left and the ocean pulled out so it could look us in the eye when it finally speaks.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong execution of the plan's intentions, and largely succeeds on its own terms. Let me work through it carefully. **Voice and Style** The prose maintains the "rocking boat with wet hands" quality the plan demanded. Sentences are short and percussive when the action is immediate ("Fish everywhere. Big ones, small ones, parrotfish the colour of candy, triggerfish with their stupid teeth still trying to bite the air."), and longer when exhaustion or grief sets in. The rhythm is controlled and mostly earns its effects. The chapter avoids the forbidden words and poster-ready sentences. "Blood comes quick, bright red on white coral, pretty almost" is the kind of ugly-beautiful observation that fits the voice without tipping into self-congratulation. **Character Consistency** Luz is rendered faithfully to her profile. The cruelty-tinged intelligence ("You said you were hungry. I brought more rice. It's old now. Got weevils. You mind weevils?"), the barefoot reef-walking, the relationship with the parrotfish from Chapter 3, the dead father, the mother calling from behind—all of these cohere with the established character. The detail of her using her six-year-old voice from the day her father left is genuinely affecting and consistent with her backstory. The mother's cameo ("Lúcia, get back here! You'll burn!") is brief, half-swallowed, and wrong for the moment in exactly the way the plan specified. **Following the Chapter Plan** The plan called for: tide never coming back, walking the exposed reef for kilometres, dead coral crunching like bones, fish on the flats twitching with gills opening and closing in unison like they're practising a word. All of these are present. The coral cutting her feet is a nice addition that earns the bodily fluid requirement (blood) organically. The chapter also advances the thematic throughline—the fish are "practising a word"—without stating the reveal cleanly. **Emotional Engagement** The chapter is genuinely moving in places. The parrotfish reunion, the detail of it drinking her tears, the child lying down among dying fish and burning in the sun—these land. The use of her father's absence as the emotional substrate for the fish's hunger is well-constructed. "I'm hungry, Luz. But it's not talking about rice. It's talking about everything we took." This is the chapter's best moment: the reveal leaks through grief and childhood memory rather than exposition. **Weaknesses and Forced Elements** A few moments strain. "Like someone poked extra holes in the sky" for the stars is a simile that feels slightly too cute, too whimsical for the register. The parrotfish drinking her tears is a striking image but risks tipping into the precious—it's close to the line. "Two days left and the ocean pulled out so it could look us in the eye when it finally speaks" is the chapter's closing line before the final image, and it's the weakest sentence in the piece: it explains the metaphor rather than letting it breathe, and it comes close to the "poster-ready" quality the plan explicitly forbade. It tells us what to think about the tide's withdrawal rather than trusting the imagery to do that work. The phrase "shaping a word that isn't ready yet" at the very end is better—it earns its place. **Purple Prose / Forced Poetry** The prose is mostly disciplined. "The coral cuts my feet instant" has a nice pidgin-inflected rhythm. "Blood from my feet leaves prints that fill with tiny crabs instant" is vivid and specific. The chapter avoids most purple prose traps. The "kissing" simile for the parrotfish's mouth is borderline—it's intimate in a way that could read as overwrought—but in context of a 12-year-old's perspective it just about works. **Coherence** The chapter is coherent and internally consistent. The timeline (two days before the speech) is maintained. The escalation from Chapter 3's fish-feeding to this more desperate, grief-saturated encounter is logical. **Overall** This is a well-executed chapter that honors the plan's intentions and the character's voice. Its flaws are minor: one explanatory closing line, one or two images that approach preciousness. It does not reach masterpiece level but it is genuinely accomplished work that avoids most of the pitfalls the plan warned against. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Chapter 7 – the day before I haven’t slept in four days and the fish are better company than people ever were. I’m sitting on the bow of the Malia with my legs hanging over, heels drumming on the hull like I’m still a kid waiting for Dad to finish talking story with the other captains. Except Dad’s been dead twenty-two years and the only ones listening now are the fish. They float right up to the boat, patient. Thousands of them. Eyes cooked white by the sun but still looking. Mouths opening and closing in that same slow rhythm that matches my pulse when I’m drunk enough to feel it in my teeth. I got a warm Primo beer balanced on my belly and a cigarette dangling from my lip. The beer tastes like piss and pennies. Everything tastes like pennies now. “Hey, you remember that time I pissed on the electric eel?” I ask the closest mahi. Its head is half-rotted, one eye gone, but the other one finds me. “Fourteen years old. Thought I was hot shit. Woke up speaking in tongues and shitting myself for a week.” The mahi’s mouth keeps moving. No judgment. I laugh. Sounds like gravel in a blender. “You’re better listeners than my ex-wife,” I say. “She at least told me to shut up.” I take a long pull of beer. Belch. It echoes across the dead water, comes back smaller, like even the air is tired. The fish drift closer. One bumps my heel gentle, like a cat asking to be let in. I reach down and scoop it up. A small opakapaka, maybe two pounds, belly already bloating. I hold it in both hands like a baby bird. “You ever have a kid?” I ask it. It opens its mouth against my thumb. I feel the tiny teeth. “Yeah, me too. Little girl. Used to fall asleep on my chest while I cleaned fish. Smelled like salt and milk and everything good. Now she won’t even read my texts.” The fish twitches once. Its guts slip out a little, warm and wet on my wrist. “I know,” I say. “I fucked it up. Don’t need you to tell me.” I tip the beer bottle and pour a little into its mouth. The liquid runs out the gills instant. Waste of good beer but whatever. Night comes fast when there’s no clouds. Sky goes from orange to black like someone pulled a plug. The fish start glowing again, that same sick green. Under the water it’s brighter now. I can see shapes moving deep, slow, patient. Things made of net and bottle and bone, weaving themselves tighter. I talk all night. Tell them about the first time I killed a man—accidental, fistfight over a woman who wasn’t worth it, his head hit the curb wrong. Tell them about the night Ipo was born and how I cried harder than Leilani. Tell them about the shark that took my toe and how I laughed while it chewed because I was high on adrenaline and young enough to think pain made you tough. The fish listen. Every time I stop talking, one of them bumps the hull. Gentle. Reminder. Keep going, Jonah. We’re here. By 3 a.m. I’m hoarse and weeping and the beer’s long gone. I’m lying on my back again, same as twelve days ago, only this time I’m not jerking off. Just holding the dead opakapaka against my chest like it’s my daughter and I’m trying to keep her warm. “I’m sorry,” I whisper to it. To all of them. “I didn’t know. I swear I didn’t know.” The glowing under the water gets brighter. The shapes are closer now. I can feel them through the hull, slow heartbeat matching mine. The fish in my arms opens its mouth one more time. This time I hear it clear. Not words. Just feeling. Like a hand on the back of my neck, gentle but firm. We know, Jonah. We always knew. I fall asleep with dead fish for a blanket and the ocean rocking me like it used to rock Ipo when she had nightmares. Tomorrow it speaks for real. I’m not ready. But I’m not scared anymore either.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 7 is a strong piece of work that largely delivers on the plan's ambitions. Let me examine it carefully. VOICE AND STYLE: The prose does read like wet hands on a rocking boat. The rhythm shifts appropriately—short punches when Jonah is confessing, longer sentences when he's exhausted and drifting. "Sounds like gravel in a blender" is a solid, earned image. "Sky goes from orange to black like someone pulled a plug" works. The beer tasting like "piss and pennies" connects to the recurring copper/penny motif across chapters. These are mostly landing. FOLLOWING THE PLAN: The plan called for Jonah drinking warm beer, talking to dead fish the way he used to talk to his daughter, telling them about the electric eel story, and the fish listening. All of this is present. The plan also specified "at least one bodily fluid per chapter"—we get the fish guts on his wrist, the beer, the weeping. The plan is followed faithfully. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Jonah's voice is consistent with his profile—the guilt about Ipo, the accidental killing (which is a new detail but fits the "carries guilt like extra weight" description), the superstition, the self-awareness about his failures. The detail about Ipo falling asleep on his chest smelling like "salt and milk and everything good" is genuinely affecting and specific. The confession about the accidental manslaughter is a bold addition that deepens him without contradicting the profile. WEAKNESSES: The ending is where the chapter stumbles. "We know, Jonah. / We always knew." is exactly the kind of thing the plan said to avoid—it's almost poster-worthy, it's clean and redemptive in a way that undercuts the stated intention of "no redemption, no hand-holding." The fish communicating in italicized, grammatically complete, emotionally reassuring sentences is a significant tonal violation. The plan specified the speech is "not triumphant" and that the reveal "leaks out through taste, memory, and shame"—but here we get a moment of comfort, of being known and forgiven. "I'm not scared anymore either" is similarly soft, almost hopeful, which contradicts the gut-punch ethos. The line "I fall asleep with dead fish for a blanket and the ocean rocking me like it used to rock Ipo when she had nightmares" is the most egregious purple prose moment—it's sentimental in a way that feels earned emotionally but is too neat, too beautiful, too poster-adjacent. The plan explicitly said to burn anything that would look good on a poster. "Tomorrow it speaks for real. / I'm not ready. / But I'm not scared anymore either." This ending is the weakest part. It's too tidy, too resolved. It gives Jonah a kind of peace that the plan explicitly wanted to deny. The chapter was supposed to wreck him differently, not comfort him. DIALOGUE: The dialogue to the fish is mostly good—the electric eel story, the confession about Ipo, the beer poured into the gills. These feel authentic to Jonah's voice. The fish "responding" in italics is a misstep. CREATIVITY: The detail of pouring beer into the dead fish's mouth and watching it run out the gills is genuinely strange and good. The accidental manslaughter confession is unexpected and adds dimension. The fish bumping the hull as a "reminder" is effective without being over-explained. OVERALL: A chapter that is 75% excellent and 25% self-sabotaging. The ending violates the plan's core ethos by offering comfort and resolution where there should be dread and unease. The italicized fish speech is a significant tonal error. But the body of the chapter—the confessions, the physicality, the specific grief about Ipo—is genuinely accomplished work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 7 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 10 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 12 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Chapter 8 – day of the speech Marisol – 09:17:31 UTC I’m in the galley of Esperanza III, elbow-deep in a can of cold Spam, eating it with my fingers because the last clean spoon went overboard three days ago. The raft rocks wrong—no swell, no rhythm, just a slow tilting like the planet forgot how to spin. My mouth tastes like metal and yesterday’s vomit. The hydrophones are still screaming silence. I lick grease from my thumb and look out the porthole. The water is black glass and glowing from below, bright enough to read by. I can see my face in the reflection—eyes red, skin grey, hair hacked short with the dive knife because I couldn’t stand it touching my neck anymore. Something taps the hull once. Soft. Curious. I wait for the second tap. It never comes. Instead the glow flares white and every hair on my body stands up. Luz – same second I’m lying on my back on the dead reef, skin blistered and peeling, parrotfish dried to leather on my chest. The sun is a white coin directly overhead. I haven’t moved since yesterday. My piss ran out hours ago. My tongue is swollen and tastes like coral cuts. The fish around me are flat now, cooked, paper-thin. Their mouths are still open. Waiting. I’m singing under my breath, the shark song Grandma taught me, voice cracked to nothing. The light changes. Not the sun moving. Something under the reef flares, bright as magnesium. My shadow burns black into the coral. The parrotfish’s mouth moves one last time against my skin. Dry scales rasp like sandpaper. I feel it coming. Pressure behind my eyes, same as always, only now it’s huge. Like the whole ocean is squeezing my skull gentle. Jonah – same heartbeat I’m asleep on the deck of the Malia, cheek stuck to a dead mahi’s belly, drool and snot mixing with fish slime. Gulls finally gave up and left. Even they know it’s over. The glow wakes me. Green-white, searing. Coming from everywhere and nowhere. I sit up slow. My back cracks like old wood. Every fish mouth on the ocean opens at once. Millions. Billions. A wet clicking chorus louder than anything I ever heard. The pressure hits me behind the eyes, soft but absolute, like a hand covering my face. Then the voice. It is not loud. It is five words long, spoken inside every skull that has ever tasted salt, all at once, in whatever language we first learned shame in. It is childish. It is ashamed. It is true. We’re sorry we waited. And every one of us (Marisol choking on Spam in the galley, Luz burning alive on the reef, Jonah covered in guts and tears, every kid licking salt off their fingers on every shore, every old woman who ever cried into the sea) hears it in our mother’s voice, or our child’s, or our own voice at seven years old. The glow peaks and dies. The fish mouths close. The ocean goes dark. Marisol drops the Spam can. It clangs once and is silent. Luz sits up. The parrotfish crumbles to dust on her chest. Jonah looks at the empty water where the fish were and laughs until he pukes. No one answers the apology. There is nothing left to say. Then they spoke.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is the climactic chapter of the story, and it largely delivers on the brutal, unromantic vision laid out in the plan. Let me assess it carefully. **Strengths:** The tripartite structure works well—cutting between Marisol, Luz, and Jonah at the exact same moment creates genuine tension and a sense of cosmic simultaneity without feeling gimmicky. The chapter honors the plan's instruction for "rapid cuts" and executes it cleanly. The sensory grounding is strong throughout. Marisol eating Spam with her fingers because the last clean spoon went overboard, Jonah's cheek stuck to a dead mahi's belly with drool and snot mixing with fish slime, Luz's tongue swollen and tasting like coral cuts—these are specific, unglamorous, and consistent with the story's aesthetic. The bodily fluid requirement is met (drool, snot, piss, vomit referenced). The five-word sentence "We're sorry we waited" is genuinely effective. It's childish, ashamed, and true as promised. The plan called for "the worst possible first word a dying ocean could say to its murderer" and the apology—rather than accusation—is a sophisticated inversion. An apology from the dying to the killer is more damning than any accusation. This is well-conceived. The ending—"Then they spoke"—lands as promised, and its placement after the apology has already been delivered creates a strange temporal loop that is appropriately disorienting. The ocean spoke, and then they spoke. The sentence functions as both conclusion and indictment. The detail that the voice is heard "in whatever language we first learned shame in" and "in our mother's voice, or our child's, or our own voice at seven years old" is the chapter's strongest moment. It's specific enough to feel true and universal enough to land. **Weaknesses:** The parenthetical list—"(Marisol choking on Spam in the galley, Luz burning alive on the reef, Jonah covered in guts and tears, every kid licking salt off their fingers on every shore, every old woman who ever cried into the sea)"—is the chapter's most amateurish moment. It reads like the author couldn't trust the reader to make the connection and felt compelled to spell it out. The "every old woman who ever cried into the sea" is particularly weak—it's the kind of line the plan explicitly said to burn ("any sentence that would look good on a poster"). This is close to poster territory. "Jonah looks at the empty water where the fish were and laughs until he pukes" is good. "Luz sits up. The parrotfish crumbles to dust on her chest" is effective. But "Marisol drops the Spam can. It clangs once and is silent" is slightly too neat, too cinematic in a way that feels borrowed from film rather than earned from prose. The plan called for the reveal to "leak out through taste, memory, and shame" and to "never be stated cleanly." The chapter mostly honors this, but "We're sorry we waited" is actually quite clean—perhaps too clean. The plan's ambiguity about what exactly the ocean is saying is somewhat collapsed by the directness of the apology. That said, the apology itself is ambiguous enough (sorry for what? for dying? for speaking? for existing?) that it partially satisfies the "leaks out" requirement. Luz's section is the weakest of the three. "Parrotfish dried to leather on my chest" and "My piss ran out hours ago" are good, but "skin blistered and peeling" and "voice cracked to nothing" are generic. The plan had her "knee-deep in dead reef water holding her tablet like a Bible"—the tablet is absent here, which is a minor deviation from the plan but also loses a character-specific detail (her relationship with the tablet and whale-song translations is central to her profile). The phrase "bright as magnesium" is a solid simile. "Green-white, searing" works. But "the planet forgot how to spin" is slightly overwrought—it's the kind of cosmic reach that doesn't quite land because it's imprecise. The plan specified Jonah should be "asleep on his deck surrounded by gulls that won't eat the fish anymore"—the gulls appear briefly and are handled well. The plan had Marisol "hungover in the raft galley eating cold Spam from the tin"—executed faithfully. **Overall assessment:** This is a strong chapter that mostly delivers on its ambitious plan. The five-word apology is genuinely surprising and thematically resonant. The prose maintains its salt-crusted, unglamorous register throughout. The weaknesses are real—the parenthetical list is a significant stumble, and Luz's section is underdeveloped compared to the other two—but they don't sink the chapter. The ending works. The darkness is earned. The chapter avoids most of the things the plan said to burn, with one or two near-misses. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 13 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is an ambitious, multi-perspective first-contact story told through three characters: Jonah (a Hawaiian fisherman), Marisol (a marine researcher), and Luz (a young girl from a Pacific island). The story spans 30 days leading to the moment the megastructures/ocean entities finally speak. Let me assess it carefully across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The prose voice is genuinely distinctive, particularly for Jonah. The opening chapter establishes a raw, masculine, self-destructive voice with real texture: "the way a battery smells when you bite it as a kid to see if it's still good" is an excellent, specific, earned image. The sensory detail throughout is often exceptional — the dead fish smelling like "coins, pennies, the inside of my daughter's mouth when she had braces," the blood that's "too dark, almost purple." These are not generic poetic gestures; they feel observed. The three-character structure works reasonably well. Each voice is differentiated: Jonah is coarse and grief-soaked, Marisol is scientific and traumatized, Luz is young and spiritually open. The story wisely uses these different registers to approach the same phenomenon from different angles. The central conceit — that the "alien" entity is actually humanity's own waste, digested and given consciousness — is genuinely creative and thematically resonant. Chapter 5 (Marisol in the submersible) is the strongest chapter, where this revelation lands: "It's not alien. It's us. Digested." This is earned and powerful. The final message "We're sorry we waited" is a clever inversion — the entity apologizing rather than humanity, suggesting the ocean/waste-creature has more grace than the humans who created it. This is a genuinely surprising and thematically coherent choice. WEAKNESSES: The masturbation scene in Chapter 4 is the most problematic element. While the author is clearly going for raw authenticity, the scene crosses into gratuitous territory that doesn't fully justify itself narratively. The fish "matching his stroke" and the ejaculation onto scales reads as shock value rather than genuine character revelation. The emotional beat it's trying to hit (Jonah's isolation, his grief, his broken sexuality) could be achieved without this level of explicit detail. It risks alienating readers without proportionate payoff. The "forced poetry" problem appears intermittently. "The ocean doesn't answer. It just keeps pressing down, slow and patient, like it's been waiting years for me to notice it's already won" — this is the kind of line that sounds profound but is actually somewhat empty. Similarly, "My hands have stopped shaking. That's how I know I'm really scared" is a cliché reversal that appears in countless thrillers. Marisol's chapters are the weakest. Her voice is less distinctive than Jonah's or Luz's, and her backstory (the whale injury, the father who left) feels more schematic. Chapter 5's revelation about her father's voice coming from the entity is emotionally manipulative in a way that feels slightly unearned — we haven't spent enough time with her relationship to her father for this to land with full force. The pacing is uneven. Chapters 1-3 build excellently. Chapter 4 stalls with the masturbation scene. Chapter 5 recovers strongly. Chapters 6-7 feel somewhat repetitive — both Luz and Jonah are essentially doing the same thing (lying with dead fish, having emotional conversations with them). The final chapter's convergence is effective but the actual "speech" — five words — feels slightly anticlimactic given the buildup, though one could argue this is intentional restraint. The dialogue is sparse but generally effective. The conversations with fish are appropriately one-sided. The few human interactions (Uncle Rudy, Luz's mother) are brief but functional. Some metaphors don't land: "The sun comes up red and mean, like it's pissed off at the night for something" — the personification is too casual to be striking. "My reflection looks up at me from four thousand metres down and I swear it blinks first" — this is physically impossible and the impossibility isn't doing enough work to justify the image. The prompt mentions "megastructures" — the story somewhat sidesteps this, focusing on the ocean/waste entity rather than traditional megastructures. The fish dying and then speaking is faithfully rendered, but the megastructure element is only gestured at (the "bruise" in the sky in Chapter 2). This is a minor prompt-faithfulness issue. The Luz chapters are the most tender and original. Her voice is the most surprising — a young Pacific Islander girl communicating with fish through song, using a broken whale app that speaks in her grandmother's voice. This is genuinely inventive and emotionally resonant without being manipulative. The ending works. The convergence of all three characters in the final chapter, the simultaneous moment of contact, and the entity's apology rather than threat or demand — this is a genuinely creative and emotionally satisfying resolution. The final line "Then they spoke" echoing the prompt is a nice structural choice. OVERALL ASSESSMENT: This is a strong piece of creative writing with genuine ambition, distinctive voices, and a thematically coherent central idea. Its weaknesses are real — the gratuitous Chapter 4, some uneven pacing, Marisol's relative thinness as a character, and occasional forced metaphors — but they don't undermine the overall achievement. The piece demonstrates real craft and originality, particularly in the Luz chapters and the central revelation about the entity's nature. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 14 Nuanced Characters: 13 Pacing: 12 World Building: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Coherent: 14 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 7 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 8 Unearned Transformations: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 13
Avg Final Item Score: 68.9/100
+ Darkly Comic British YA Fiction — Midnight Blood (Chapter Avg: 72.4 | Final: 59.6)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Darkly Comic British YA Fiction Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: The text came just after midnight: 🩸🩸🩸
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Darkly Comic British YA Fiction Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: The text came just after midnight: 🩸🩸🩸 -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming Prompt core: a text of exactly three blood-drop emojis arriving just after midnight. British YA, darkly comic tone. 8 chapters, ~1,000 words each, so roughly 8,000 words total – a novella in miniature. Possible directions (no filtering yet, just throwing paint at the wall): 1. Menstrual-code gone feral The three drops are the established SOS among a tight-knit group of sixth-form girls at a grim northern comprehensive: “I’ve started and I’m dying, bring painkillers/chocolate/solace.” This time it’s from Mia, the queen bee who vanished three weeks ago after a house party. Everyone assumed she’d run off with some older lad. Now the text has come, no words, just 🩸🩸🩸. The girls descend into escalating paranoia: is it really her? Is it a prank? Is it a confession? Dark comedy from the absolute filth of teenage girl banter clashing with genuine dread. 2. Literal vampire cult at a private school All-girls boarding school in the Cotswolds, very Brideshead-meets-Buffy. The blood drops are an invitation from the secret society that actually does drink blood (but ironically, with artisanal gin). Protagonist is a scholarship girl from Hull who thinks it’s all hilarious LARPing until she’s in too deep and someone actually dies. Comedy from class warfare and how posh girls can make even cannibalism boring. 3. Murder-mystery prank that stops being a prank Group of lads running a viral “horror experience” account on TikTok. They stage elaborate scares. One night they send 🩸🩸🩸 to the group chat as the kick-off for their biggest stunt yet. Except one of them actually turns up dead the next morning, staged exactly like the prank they’d planned. Whodunnit where every single suspect has motive, opportunity, and an alibi video. 4. Werewolf puberty metaphor (but make it nasty) North Manchester estate. Protagonist is 16, late bloomer, thinks he’s finally getting his first period (he’s trans). The blood drops text is him panicking in the Tesco loos. Turns out the blood is not his – it’s the guy in the next cubicle who’s just been stabbed. Dark comedy from the collision of queer coming-of-age awkwardness with sudden extreme violence. 5. Necromancer WhatsApp group A group chat mysteriously added to by a dead classmate. Every time someone opens the three blood drops, something awful happens to them exactly 7 days later. Very Japanese-horror, but everyone’s northern and takes the piss relentlessly even while bricking it. 6. Organ-legging Deliveroo The blood drops are the confirmation code from a dark-web app that delivers emergency pints of blood (O-neg, natch). Protagonist accidentally orders one while pissed. Now very real gangsters want paying, and the only currency they accept is a kidney. Comedy from the absolute incompetence of British youth trying to do serious crime. 7. The Curse of the Crimson Tide Every girl in Year 11 starts synching up. One night every single one of them gets the same text at the same time: 🩸🩸🩸. Mass hysteria. The school tries to cover it up with a “gas leak.” Comedy from teachers who are even more clueless than the kids, plus one extremely tired school nurse who’s seen it all. 8. Actual cannibal Shia LaBeouf (British version) Reclusive celebrity living in a gated mansion outside Leeds starts texting random local teens 🩸🩸🩸. Turns out he’s just really committed to his new concept album and wants to sample real screaming. Escalates until the kids kidnap him for ransom in Greggs reward points. Characters I keep circling back to: - Narrator: deadpan, sarcastic, slightly traumatised Northern or Brummie voice (first person or close third). - Core friend group of four, each with a clear comic function but also real damage. - One adult who is useless in the exact way only British authority figures can be. - A villain who is both terrifying and pathetic. Settings that feel juicy: - Rain-soaked comprehensive in Greater Manchester or Sheffield - Crumbling coastal town in Yorkshire or Lincolnshire - Grotty house-shares in Fallowfield or Hyde Park, Leeds - Night buses, Greggs at 2 a.m., abandoned retail parks Tone targets: Skulduggery Pleasant meets In Bruges meets This Is England, but everyone’s 17 and terminally online. Violence is sudden and shocking, but the reaction is pure gallows banter. Nobody says “this is just like a film”; they say “this is proper fucked, innit.” # Reflection The menstrual-code idea (Direction 1) keeps elbowing everything else out of the way. It’s inherently British (the sheer mortification, the weaponised banter, the sacred tampon run), inherently YA, and lets the dark comedy come from character rather than gimmick. Crucially, it starts funny and mundane, then can rot into something genuinely horrifying without ever losing the voice. The slow reveal that the missing girl isn’t missing – she’s dead, or worse – can be drip-fed across eight chapters without feeling rushed. The vampire boarding-school one is tempting for class satire, but risks being too “aesthetic” and not grimy enough for the tone I actually want. The prank-gone-wrong version is solid and very marketable, but I’ve seen it done (The Dares, One of Us Is Lying, etc.). I want something where the premise itself feels like it could only happen to British teenagers who communicate entirely in emojis and casual suicidal ideation. The trans-werewolf-puberty one is raw and could be brilliant, but might be too emotionally delicate to hit the “darkly comic” mark without punching down – and I’m not sure I’m the one to write it safely in this format. The organ-legging Deliveroo concept is objectively hilarious but probably peaks too early; hard to sustain over eight chapters without becoming straight farce. So the menstrual-code-gone-feral keeps winning because: - The opening image is instantly funny and relatable to half the population. - The stakes can escalate from “hilarious inconvenience” to “we have almost certainly seen a dead body.” - The friend-group banter writes itself. - It lets me be absolutely vile about teenage girlhood in a way that feels loving rather than cruel. - It’s inherently shareable – readers will either cackle in recognition or be horrified they recognise it. Still deliberately not locking anything in. But that’s the direction that keeps making me laugh in the exact dark way the brief wants.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This will be a brutally funny, northern, working-class British YA horror-comedy told in close-first-person by 17-year-old Morgan “Morgs” Brennan from Wythenshawe, Manchester. Voice: deadpan, viciously observant, Northern-Irish mum energy mixed with Derry Girls filth and the casual nihilism of End of the F***ing World. No Americanisms, no tidy morals, no “and then we called the police.” Core concept locked in: 🩸🩸🩸 is the ancient, sacred bat-signal the girls have used since Year 9 whenever someone’s period arrives like a war crime and they need emergency supplies/smuggling out of class. It is never, ever used for anything else. Until the night Mia “Queen of Everything” Iqbal (missing three weeks, presumed eloped with a 23-year-old DJ from Leeds) sends it at 00:03. The story will chart the four remaining members of the friendship group descending into total chaos over one extended, rain-soaked October weekend as they try to work out whether Mia is: - taking the absolute piss, - trapped somewhere, - dead, - or something worse none of them have the vocabulary for. Tone targets: laugh-out-loud grotesque one paragraph, genuine stomach-drop dread the next. Violence, when it arrives, will be sudden, wet, and underlit by the glow of a cracked iPhone screen. No redemption arcs, but real love between the girls that expresses itself almost entirely through savagery. Explicit choices: - All dialogue must sound like it was recorded on the top deck of the 103 bus at 3 p.m. on a Wednesday. - Sex is awkward, periods are disgusting, adults are catastrophically useless. - No sympathetic police officers. Ever. - At least one major character will vomit from stress. Probably more than one. - The phrase “proper bleeding” will be used both literally and figuratively. - The final reveal must be worse than murder, but still believable within the rules of a grim northern estate. Things I will avoid: - “Found family” speeches - Mental-health rep that feels like a TED Talk - A killer who monologues - Any suggestion that sixth-form college is in any way glamorous # Chapter Planning Chapter 1 – The Text (Friday night/Saturday 00:03) Introduction of Morgan, voice, the group chat history, the sacred law of 🩸🩸🩸. Establishing the four survivors: Morgs (narrator, designated driver of the friend group, feral caretaker), Shaz (tiny, angry, vape wizard), Leigh-Anne (beauty YouTuber in training, moral compass made of glitter), and Priya (quiet, posh-ish, secretly the most deranged). Mia’s disappearance retold through brutal group-chat screenshots. The text arrives. Immediate escalation from “she’s taking the piss” to “pack a bag, we’re going to Leeds.” Chapter 2 – The Night Bus to Hell (Saturday 2 a.m.–5 a.m.) Journey on the replacement bus service because Northern Rail is a hate crime. Drunk lads, a woman eating cold doner meat straight from the foil, Shaz threatening to bottle someone for looking at Priya funny. Morgs stealing a full English from the 24-hour McDonald’s for “emergency rations.” Increasingly deranged theories about where Mia is. First proper scare: they find Mia’s fake ID in the pocket of a hoodie left on the bus. Chapter 3 – The Abandoned House, Harehills (Saturday late morning) They track the last known location from Find My iPhone to a boarded-up terraced house scheduled for demolition. Breaking in. The place is squatsville: needles, an actual dead pigeon in the bath, and Mia’s blood-soaked school jumper nailed to the living-room wall like a horror art installation. Polaroids of the girls sleeping scattered on the floor. Someone has been watching them for weeks. Chapter 4 – The Most Normal Day in the World (Saturday afternoon) They bottle it and go home because it’s Leigh-Anne’s little sister’s christening. Surreal comedy chapter of trying to act human while covered in someone else’s blood and dread. Morgs hiding the jumper in the church font. Priest accidentally blesses it. Shaz live-tweeting the whole thing. Group chat blowing up with people thinking it’s an ARG. Chapter 5 – The Second Text (Saturday 23:59) Another 🩸🩸🩸, this time with a 3-second voice note: just Mia breathing, then a wet sound like someone biting into an apple. Phone tracked to a Travelodge off the M62. The girls steal Morgs’ mum’s knackered Micra and drive there in their christening outfits because none of them thought to change. Chapter 6 – Room 207 (Sunday 1 a.m.–2 a.m.) The room is paid for until Monday. Key still works. Inside: Mia’s phone on the bed, screen cracked, battery at 1%. The bathroom is an abattoir. Someone has tried to clean it with Domestos and failed. The girls finally, properly lose their minds. Leigh-Anne starts stress-painting her nails with the blood “for evidence.” Priya discovers the en-suite mirror has “YOU LEFT ME” written in lipstick on the inside. Chapter 7 – The Boyfriend (Sunday morning) They track down the 23-year-old DJ, Reece, living in a caravan behind his mum’s house in Halton Moor. He’s terrified; says Mia left him weeks ago after “getting into some proper weird occult shit with these older girls from college.” Shows them a video on his phone: Mia and two unrecognisable older women in balaclavas cutting symbols into their arms and laughing. The footage ends with Mia looking straight at the camera and mouthing “tell Morgs I’m sorry.” Chapter 8 – The Estate at Home (Sunday night/Monday predawn) They realise it’s been here all along. Final confrontation on the patch of wasteland behind the flats where they used to drink White Ace as kids. The big reveal: Mia isn’t dead. She’s recruited. She’s been bleeding herself slowly for weeks to “ascend” or some mystical anorexia bollocks two art-school witches sold her. She wants the others to join her or die. The girls choose neither. Brutal, messy, northern ending involving a scaffolding pole, a Lidl bag-for-life, and the immortal line: “You’ve had your period, Mia. Now have your fucking closure.” Story ends at 4:12 a.m. on Halloween morning with the survivors sitting on the curb sharing a contraband cigarette, covered in blood that’s a mixture of all of theirs now, quietly making a new pact: if any of them ever sends 🩸🩸🩸 again, the others are legally required to kill them on sight.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
### How a real human (specifically a feral northern teenage girl who’s been awake for 48 hours on Monster and regret) would actually approach this story A human with the right damage would not start with a plan. They would start with a voice in their head that won’t shut up, usually at 3 a.m., and they’d open their Notes app and just vomit 400 words of Morgan absolutely leathering someone in the group chat. Then they’d fall asleep on the toilet. The next day they’d read it back, cackle, hate themselves, add another 200 words of Shaz threatening to glass a bus driver, and only THEN realise there’s a story in it. The plot would arrive third or fourth, dragged in backwards like a wheelie bin on fire. They would not care about “darkly comic British YA market trends.” They’d be trying to make their mates wheeze into their pints. The horror would be an accidental by-product of describing something real that also happens to be horrifying (like the smell of a Travelodge bathroom after someone’s tried to bleach away three weeks of blood). They’d break every single “rule” we’ve politely listed: - Dialogue would have 40% more swearing and 60% less sense. - Half the sentences would be fragments. - There’d be random tangents about the price of Freddos in 2013 or the specific way Leigh-Anne’s mum says “Pakistan” that makes Priya want to set herself on fire. - At least two chapters would end mid-sentence because the writer got distracted and started an argument on Twitter. - The ending would feel slightly unfinished on purpose because nothing ever actually ends, does it? ### Typical LLM tells that would absolutely murder this piece 1. The Safety Valve LLMs love to signal “I know this is dark but look, the characters still have empathy.” We’d get a scene where Morgan has a little cry about how much she loves Mia. A human would have Morgan projectile-vomit instead and then call Mia a “self-dramatic silicone-titted vampire” while doing it. 2. The Democracy of Suffering LLMs distribute trauma evenly so nobody feels picked on. A human would pick one girl and absolutely brutalise her for eight chapters while the others just watch in horrified fascination. 3. The Redemption Reflex Even when we say “no redemption arcs,” LLMs sneak them in via subtext. Mia would get a tragic backstory. Her cult involvement would be “a cry for help.” A human would make Mia’s motive completely pathetic (she wanted cheekbones sharp enough to cut glass and thought blood fasting was the cheat code). 4. Clean Violence LLM violence is choreographed. A human’s violence is the kind where someone swings a scaffolding pole, misses, hits a wheelie bin, and accidentally concusses themselves with the ricochet. 5. Thematic Neatness We’ve already got “closure” in the last line. A human would end with someone saying “anyone got a tampon?” and then the battery dies. 6. Voice Consistency LLMs keep the narrator witty for 8,000 words. A real teenager’s voice collapses into full SMS speak when tired, then suddenly turns eerily lucid at 4 a.m. when the adrenaline hits. ### Specific pitfalls already creeping into our plan - Chapter 4 (the christening) is screaming “look how surreal and contrasted!” It’s clever-clever. A human would make it boring on purpose: two hours of small talk about napkins while Morgan is hiding a bloody jumper in her tights and trying not to scream. - The big reveal in Chapter 8 is currently too cinematic. “Ascend,” “mystical anorexia,” art-school witches in balaclavas; it’s giving Netflix limited series. A human would make the cult something genuinely embarrassing like an MLM for sage smudging and vaginal steaming that went wrong when they started doing blood rituals to “manifest a BooHoo Man sponsor.” - We have named chapters. No seventeen-year-old on earth would name chapters. They’d be like: chapter 3 chapter 3 again cos i deleted it harehills smells like death and monster munch - The pacing is balanced. That’s the biggest tell. Real teenage life is 90% waiting for a bus that never comes and then suddenly everything happens in six minutes while you’re trying to open a bottle of Frosty Jack’s with your teeth. - Priya is “quiet, posh-ish, secretly the most deranged.” That’s an LLM telling us she’s interesting instead of showing it. A human would just have her silently eat an entire tube of Pringles in 40 seconds while staring at a dead pigeon and we’d all understand. ### Ways to inject more human DNA without changing the plot yet 1. Let the prose get drunk. When they’re tired, sentences should disintegrate. Spelling mistakes in narration. Random capitals. “leeds is a shithole and i’m cold and priya keeps doing that thing where she pretends she’s not crying but her nose is running like a tap.” 2. Weaponise boredom. Chapter 4 could be 800 words of Leigh-Anne’s auntie asking if Morgan has a boyfriend yet while Morgan is literally holding a carrier bag with someone’s blood crust in it. 3. Make the banter actually hurtful. Not funny-ha-ha hurtful. Like, genuinely cruel in a way that makes you wince and then laugh because it’s too real. Shaz tells Priya her dad only pays for her to go to sixth form because he’s embarrassed she’s thick. Silence. Then Leigh-Anne changes the subject to lash extensions. 4. Let them be wrong constantly. They spend three chapters convinced Mia’s been sex-trafficked. Turns out she’s been in her mum’s loft the entire time live-streaming herself cutting symbols into her thighs for £4.99 a minute on OnlyFans: The Occult Edition. 5. Wrong genre references. No Buffy, no Skulduggery Pleasant. They reference things like “this is just like that episode of Jeremy Kyle where the girl had her boyfriend’s name tattooed on her fanny,” or “my cousin’s mate swears this happened on the Isle of Man and they made a Netflix doc but it got taken down.” 6. Physical grossness turned up to eleven. Someone pisses themselves a little bit on the night bus and they all just pretend it didn’t happen. Someone else uses a sanitary towel as a makeshift plaster for a head wound. The Travelodge room smells like copper and Lynx Africa because the killer tried to mask the blood smell with teenage boy deodorant. 7. Let the horror be mundane. The scariest moment isn’t the blood writing on the mirror. It’s finding Mia’s Spotify Wrapped from three weeks ago and realising she listened to “Weightless” by Marconi Union 47 times in a row. That’s when they know she’s properly lost it. Do not fix any of this yet. Just sit in the discomfort of how tidy and “good” the current plan feels. A human would be ashamed of how competent it is. We want shame. Shame is authentic.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This is not a book. This is a 999 call that someone accidentally left on voicemail and we’re all just listening to it unravel. Voice: Morgan Brennan, 17, Wythenshawe. She narrates like she’s texting you at 4 a.m. after six WKDs and a family-sized bag of regrets. No chapters with titles. No epigraphs. No clever little metaphors unless she’s taking the piss. Sentences will sometimes give up halfway through. Sometimes they’ll sprint for three pages without a full stop because that’s what panic feels like. Plot is now just one single weekend that refuses to end: Friday night to Monday morning, Halloween weekend 2025. Everything happens in the rain or under flickering streetlights that smell like chips and piss. The three blood drops 🩸🩸🩸 are still the period SOS. That rule is sacred and will be defended with teeth. Mia has not been kidnapped. She has not been murdered (yet). She has, however, gone properly, spectacularly mental in the way only a girl who’s been told she’s special her whole life can. She’s been living in her mum’s loft for three weeks, cutting herself for TikTok occult clout, convinced that if she bleeds enough she’ll “ascend” or get abs like Emily Ratajkowski or some other lie the internet sold her. The horror is not supernatural. The horror is that it’s all real and all pathetic and all love. We will never explain why Mia did it. We will only watch her friends try to drag her back from the edge using nothing but savage loyalty, Tesco carrier bags, and the threat of extreme violence. There will be no redemption. There will be survival, barely, and it will taste like cold chips and iron. Explicit promises: - At least one person throws up in every chapter after Chapter 2. - No police. Not once. They are a myth. - The phrase “you’re bleeding, you soft shite” will be used as a term of endearment. - Someone will weaponise a tampon. Someone else will use a sanitary towel as a gag. - The final confrontation will be three girls in soaked primark pyjamas beating the absolute shit out of their former best friend with a Scooby-Doo umbrella and pure despair. - The story ends exactly where it started: in Morgan’s bedroom, battery on 1%, rain against the window, someone crying so hard they can’t breathe, and a new message in the group chat that nobody dares open. # Chapter Planning (now just raw bullet-points because that’s how Morgan would do it) Chapter 1 bedroom 00:03, phone buzzes, 🩸🩸🩩, instant chaos in the gc, screenshots of every time they’ve used it before, morgan’s mum shouting up the stairs about noise, shaz already outside in her slippers with a kitchen knife “just in case”, they all pile into the house, haven’t slept, decide they’re going to leeds right now because that’s where mia’s phone last pinged, chapter ends with them on the night bus eating cold mcdonald’s and priya quietly bleeding through her jeans because karma Chapter 2 night bus replacement service, absolute state of it, some lad pukes in the aisle and it rolls downhill towards them, leigh-anne live-streaming for “content”, shaz threatening to deck the driver, morgan finds mia’s vape in the seat crack and nearly starts crying, they get off in harehills at 4 a.m. because that’s where find my says she is, chapter ends with them staring at a boarded-up house and realising none of them brought a torch Chapter 3 break into the house, it’s rank, someone’s been living there, mia’s school bag in the kitchen covered in mouldy monster munch, bathroom looks like a crime scene but it’s just period blood and dometos, polaroids of all five of them asleep pinned to the wall with drawing pins through their eyes, priya finds mia’s diary page that just says “i want to be empty” over and over, they leg it when they hear footsteps upstairs that definitely aren’t a cat Chapter 4 saturday daytime, they’re back home because leigh-anne has her cousin’s christening and you can’t miss that or you’re dead to the family, morgan still has the polaroids in her bra, spends the whole reception in the church toilets trying to scrub blood off her hands that isn’t even hers, shaz gets hammered on prosecco meant for the adults and tells the priest mia’s been “fucking about with satan”, priest thinks it’s hilarious northern banter, chapter ends with morgan hiding in the disabled toilet ringing mia’s phone and it starts ringing from inside the church font Chapter 5 23:57 another 🩸🩸🩸 and a voice note: mia just breathing wetly for six seconds then whispering “come get me i’m cold”, location pings to the travelodge on the m62, they steal morgan’s mum’s micra still wearing christening dresses and primark heels, priya drives because she’s the only one who passed her test, they stop at services and shaz buys 24 pack of monster and a family-size dairy milk with coppers, chapter ends pulling up outside room 207 and the door is already open Chapter 6 room 207 is worse than anything they imagined, mia’s been living there three days, walls covered in period blood finger painting, she’s skeletal, naked, rocking in the bath full of cold water and sanitary towels, keeps saying “i’m nearly there i’m nearly clean”, leigh-anne tries to hug her and mia bites her tit, proper bites, blood everywhere again, they wrap mia in the bedsheets like a burrito and carry her out while she screams about “the becoming”, morgan knocks her out with the kettle Chapter 7 sunday 6 a.m. back in wythenshawe, mia locked in morgan’s bedroom with the wifi password changed and the window nailed shut, the girls take turns sitting outside the door with a kitchen knife, mia alternates between sobbing and laughing and trying to bargain (“if you let me bleed one more time i’ll be perfect i promise”), shaz falls asleep and mia tries to hang herself with a dressing gown cord, priya finds her in time and punches her so hard in the face her nose explodes, nobody even flinches anymore Chapter 8 halloween night, they realise they can’t keep her like this, can’t take her to hospital because social services, can’t let her go because she’ll die, so they do the only thing left: they drag her to the wasteland behind the flats where they used to get off with lads, make her sit in the mud in her pants, and take turns screaming every single thing they never said to her face for the last ten years, leigh-anne slaps her until her hand bleeds, shaz pisses on her “for luck”, morgan kisses her on the mouth just to see what it tastes like now it’s all blood and teeth, mia finally breaks and cries like a human, they all pile on her in the rain crying and hitting and hugging until none of them know where one ends and the other begins, last line is morgan’s phone buzzing in her pocket at 04:44, screen cracked, one new message from mia’s phone: 🩸🩸🩸, battery dies before anyone can open it, fade to black on them sitting in the mud sharing a soggy cigarette, none of them brave enough to check if it was sent before or after they dragged her out
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Morgan “Morgs” Brennan (narrator, 17) Background: Lives in a two-bed flat above the bookies on Rowlandsway with her mum who works nights at the Amazon warehouse and her little brother who’s eleven going on thirty.
