Dad’s been “on his way back from the shop” for eight years. Morgan’s the one who taught herself to cook by watching TikTok so the gas doesn’t get cut off again. Top set for everything except PE because she’d rather set herself on fire than run. Personality & flaws: Looks after everyone else and resents them for it. Will fight a grown man twice her size but can’t say “I’m scared” out loud. Holds grudges like trophies. Dry-heaves when emotional. Has never once finished a drink she paid for herself (always nicks everyone else’s). Physicality: Five-foot-two of pure spite in a too-big Stone Island coat she nicked off her cousin. Hair dyed black with a home kit, currently green at the roots. Always has a busted lip or a scab on her knuckles. Walks like she’s permanently late for a fight. Motive/allegiance: She is the group’s spine. If they fall apart, she has nothing. Will burn the world down before she lets Mia disappear properly because Mia disappearing would prove you can just leave and Morgan can’t bear the thought that someone might want to. Voice samples: “Mate, I’ve seen period blood that looked less haunted than you right now.” (internal) “If I open that message and it’s just Mia asking for pads again I’m actually going to murder her and then myself.” # Shaz “Shazzer” Akhtar (17) Background: Youngest of six, all brothers, grew up scrapping in the street. Dad drives a taxi, mum cleans at the hospital. Everyone thinks she’s hard because she is, but she’s also the only one who still says please and thank you to bus drivers. Personality & flaws: Violence is her love language. Loves harder than anyone but shows it by threatening to kill you. Terrified of being small. Vapes mango ice so much her tongue’s gone numb. Secretly writes poetry in the Notes app and would die before letting anyone read it. Physicality: Four-foot-eleven of concentrated chaos. Wears hoops so big they leave bruises on her shoulders. Hair in a claw clip that could take an eye out. Always bouncing on the balls of her feet like she’s ready to headbutt the air. Motive/allegiance: Loyalty is oxygen. Mia once held her hair back while she cried in the KFC toilets after her brother got stabbed. That debt is forever. Voice samples: “Touch her and I’ll wear your teeth as a necklace, you fucking swamp donkey.” (internal) “I’m not crying, it’s the vape, shut up.” # Leigh-Anne “Leigh” Walsh (17) Background: From the slightly posher bit of Benchill where the houses have driveways. Mum’s a beautician, dad’s “in sales.” Does everyone’s lashes for free. Has 23k on TikTok doing GRWM videos soundtracked to Lana Del Rey remixes. Everyone assumes she’s thick. She’s not. She’s just bored. Personality & flaws: Vain in the way only pretty girls who’ve never been ugly can be. Uses humour as armour and crying as a weapon. Will film absolutely everything, even when she shouldn’t. Pathological optimist until she isn’t, then she’s vicious. Physicality: Always looks like she’s just stepped out of a music video. Hair smells like coconut. Wears lashes so long they cast shadows. Cries glitter (not literally but might as well). Motive/allegiance: Needs to be needed. Mia was her original follower, her first comment, her first “you’re actually so pretty.” If Mia’s gone mad, what does that say about Leigh-Anne’s taste? Voice samples: “Babes this is giving final girl energy but make it fashion.” (internal) “If I don’t post about this did it even happen?” # Priya “Pree” Malhotra (17) Background: Only child, doctor parents, house with a driveway AND a garage. Goes to the same school because her mum thinks private school kids are “feral.” Quietly hates everyone for assuming she’s rich. Actually just middle-class and ashamed of it. Personality & flaws: The smartest one and the most mentally ill one. Represses everything until it explodes. Has a notes app list called “people I want to kill” that’s currently at 47. Speaks softly and carries a massive fucking grudge. Physicality: Tallest of the group, always in oversized hoodies that make her look like a haunted coat stand. Chews the inside of her cheek until it bleeds. Perfect posture because her mum paid for ballet lessons she hated. Motive/allegiance: Loves them so much it’s physically painful. Would set herself on fire if it kept them warm. Has never said it out loud. Probably never will. Voice samples: (said completely deadpan) “I brought duct tape and bleach. You’re welcome.” (internal) “If Mia dies I’ll never be able to wear white again and that’s somehow the worst part.” # Mia Iqbal (17, missing/present) Background: The one they all orbited. Mixed Pakistani/Irish, looks like a Disney princess who grew up on a council estate. Used to be the loudest, funniest, most alive person any of them knew. Captain of everything. Got straight 9s without revising. Then the algorithm showed her pro-ana occult Tok and something snapped. Personality & flaws (now): Hollow where she used to be bright. Speaks in whispers like she’s saving energy for the next cut. Still beautiful in the way roadkill is beautiful right before the crows get it. Physicality: Skeletal. Eyes too big. Hair falling out in clumps. Moves like she’s underwater. Smells like copper and strawberry vape juice. Motive/allegiance: Wants to disappear so completely that even pain can’t find her. Thinks if she bleeds enough she’ll become pure. Thinks they’ll love the new version better. Is wrong about everything except how much they love her. Voice samples: (whisper) “You’ll understand when you’re empty too.” (once, before everything) “Morgs pass us a tampon I’m haemorrhaging self-respect over here.”

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Chapter 1 My phone goes off at 00:03 and I know it’s bad because the only people who text me after midnight are debt collectors and girls who’ve started their period in the middle of a sleepover. I’m already awake anyway, staring at the crack in my ceiling that looks like a map of the M60. Mum’s at work, little brother’s at his mate’s, flat’s colder than a witch’s tit. I roll over, grab the phone, ready to tell Shaz to fuck off with her 3 a.m. existential dread. It’s the group chat. Just one message. From Mia. 🩸🩸🩸 No words. Just the three blood drops. My stomach drops so fast I dry-heave into my pillow. Because that’s our code. That’s the code we made in Year 9 when Leigh-Anne bled through her white jeans in double science and Miss Nolan made her stand at the front so everyone could see. We swore on our lives that three blood drops means “I am actually dying, come now, no questions.” We’ve used it maybe six times total. Always real. Never a joke. Mia’s been gone three weeks. Everyone thought she’d fucked off to Leeds with that DJ prick who looks like he smells of Lynx Africa and regret. Her mum rang round crying, police came to the school, whole thing. Nothing. Phone off. Snapchat location gone. Dead to us. Until now. I’m already pulling on yesterday’s jeans when the chat explodes. Shaz: is this a fucking joke Shaz: morgs tell me this is a joke Leigh-Anne: i’m going to be sick Priya: open find my right now I’m thumb shaking so bad I nearly drop the phone. Open Find My iPhone. Still shared from when we stalked Leigh-Anne’s ex to Blackpool. Mia’s little dot is blinking in Harehills, Leeds. Same place it’s been for three days before it went dark. Shaz: i’m coming round morgs open the door Leigh-Anne: i’m literally getting an uber i’m in my pants Priya: do NOT go without me i swear to god I’m halfway down the stairs when the front door bangs open and Shaz barrels in wearing her dad’s taxi fleece and a pair of sliders with socks. She’s got a kitchen knife in one hand and a Tesco bag in the other. “Tell me that’s not real,” she says, voice cracking on the last word. “It’s her account,” I croak. “Same one.” Leigh-Anne arrives next, hair in yesterday’s victory rolls, mascara already halfway down her face. She’s got a full face of makeup on like armour and she’s still in her primark satin pyjamas. Priya’s last, silent, face blank the way it goes when she’s properly raging. She’s wearing her dad’s North Face puffer that drowns her and carrying a backpack that probably has rope in it. We all just stand in my living room staring at each other like we’ve been possessed. “She’s taking the piss,” Leigh-Anne says, but her voice wobbles. “She’s not,” Priya says quietly. “You don’t joke about that. Ever.” Shaz starts pacing, knife still in her hand. “Right then. We’re going. Right now.” “It’s two in the fucking morning,” I say. “Exactly,” Shaz snarls. “She’s bleeding out somewhere and we’re standing here like mugs.” Mum’s got the car for work but there’s the night bus replacement service that goes all the way to Leeds if you hate yourself enough. We’ve done it before for gigs. Takes four hours and smells like death but it exists. I grab my coat, stuff Mia’s old vape in my pocket because I’m pathetic, and we’re out the door before anyone can talk sense. The bus stop’s a five minute walk. Rain’s coming down sideways. Shaz is vibrating with energy, Priya’s chewing the inside of her cheek raw, Leigh-Anne keeps checking her phone like Mia might suddenly send “jk lads xoxo.” We get to the stop and there’s already a lad there being sick into a bin. Smells like vodka and kebab. He looks up, wipes his mouth, clocks four teenage girls looking like we’re about to commit a felony. “Alright ladies,” he slurs. “Going somewhere nice?” Shaz steps forward, knife still in her hand but hidden against her leg. “Mind your fucking business before I make you eat that sick.” He shuts up. The bus replacement coach pulls up twenty minutes later, engine rattling like it’s got consumption. Driver looks about twelve. We pile on, take the back seats that smell like wet dog and desperation. Leigh-Anne immediately starts live-streaming to her private story. “Right girls we’re on our way to rescue Mia wish us luck besties.” “Turn that off,” Priya hisses. “Content is content,” Leigh-Anne says, but her hand’s shaking. I open the group chat again. Scroll up through three weeks of messages. The last proper one from Mia is a voice note of her laughing so hard she’s wheezing because Shaz fell off a curb pissed. Then nothing. Then tonight. 🩸🩸🩸 I zoom in on the timestamp. 00:03 exactly. Shaz leans over my shoulder. “That’s her birthday innit. March third. 03/03.” We all go quiet. The coach lurches off towards the motorway and I feel the first wave of proper panic hit. Like my insides are trying to crawl out through my throat. Priya reaches over and squeezes my knee so hard it hurts. “We’ll find her,” she says. Voice flat. “And if someone’s done this to her I’ll fucking gut them.” Leigh-Anne’s crying properly now, big glittery tears. “What if she’s actually hurt though? Like properly bleeding?” “Then we’ll fix it,” I say. My voice sounds like someone else’s. “We always do.” Shaz lights a cigarette out the cracked window even though it’s illegal. Driver doesn’t say shit. None of us do. Outside, Manchester slides past in streaks of orange streetlight and rain. I rest my forehead against the cold glass and try not to think about how much blood you’d have to lose before you sent that message with no words. The coach smells like chips and fear and I’ve never been more awake in my life. We’re coming, Mia. Hold the fuck on.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a genuinely impressive piece of YA darkly comic fiction that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me break it down carefully. VOICE AND CHARACTER: The Morgan voice is remarkably consistent and convincing. "Staring at the crack in my ceiling that looks like a map of the M60" is the kind of hyper-specific regional detail that earns its place immediately. The voice never slips into generic teen-narrator territory — it stays rooted in Wythenshawe, in class, in the specific texture of this girl's life. The dry-heaving into the pillow as an emotional response is a perfect character-consistent detail established early and paid off later. The characters arrive with distinct physicality and voice: Shaz with the kitchen knife and taxi fleece, Leigh-Anne in satin pyjamas with full makeup "like armour," Priya silent and blank-faced. These are not interchangeable. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is mostly strong and differentiated. "Mind your fucking business before I make you eat that sick" is exactly Shaz. "Content is content" for Leigh-Anne lands. The exchanges feel real rather than written. There's no false note that stands out badly, though some lines are slightly on-the-nose ("We'll find her. And if someone's done this to her I'll fucking gut them" is a touch functional). METAPHOR AND PROSE: Most metaphors land. "Colder than a witch's tit," "like a DJ prick who looks like he smells of Lynx Africa and regret" — these are earned, class-specific, funny. "Like my insides are trying to crawl out through my throat" is slightly generic but fits the register. The observation about Mia's birthday being 03/03 matching the timestamp is a lovely, quiet, earned detail. The final line "chips and fear" is a bit of a reach but acceptable. There's no purple prose to speak of — the register is deliberately flat and vernacular, which is the right choice. PLAN ADHERENCE: The chapter follows the plan closely: bedroom at 00:03, group chat explosion, Shaz arriving with kitchen knife, Leigh-Anne live-streaming, getting on the night bus. The plan mentions "priya quietly bleeding through her jeans because karma" as the chapter ending — this is absent, which is a notable deviation. The chapter ends instead on the coach with Morgan's internal monologue, which is effective but misses that darkly comic beat the plan promised. DARKNESS AND LIGHTNESS: The balance is well-calibrated. The horror of the situation (Mia missing, the blood drop code, three weeks gone) is undercut by the comedy of the drunk lad at the bus stop, Leigh-Anne's live-streaming, Shaz's sliders with socks. This is exactly the register the prompt demands and it's executed with skill. WEAKNESSES: The ending is slightly too earnest — "We're coming, Mia. Hold the fuck on" tips into conventional YA territory that the rest of the chapter successfully avoids. The plan's promise of Priya bleeding through her jeans as the chapter's closing beat is missed, which would have been a funnier, more tonally consistent ending. Some of the internal monologue in the final third becomes slightly more generic than the opening. The "Mia's little dot is blinking in Harehills" section is functional but not as alive as the surrounding prose. OVERALL: This is confident, well-executed work that demonstrates genuine understanding of voice, class, and darkly comic register. It's not a masterpiece — the ending softens slightly, one planned beat is missed, and a few lines are functional rather than brilliant — but it's substantially above average and shows real craft. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Chapter 2** The replacement coach is a rolling petri dish. Seats sticky, windows fogged with breath and despair. Some lad two rows up has already puked into a McDonald’s bag and the smell is spreading like gossip. Shaz has her feet on the seat in front, sliders dangling off her toes, knife now tucked into the pocket of her dad’s fleece like it’s normal. I’m wedged between Priya and the window. She hasn’t blinked in about forty minutes. Just staring straight ahead chewing her cheek into mince. Leigh-Anne’s still live-streaming to her close friends. “Part two, girls. We’re on the death bus to Leeds. Someone’s just honked up a chicken legend, the vibes are rancid.” She pans to the puddle of sick sliding down the aisle towards us. “Iconic.” “Turn it off before someone clocks us,” I mutter. She lowers the phone but keeps recording with the back camera. “This is for the memories, Morgs. Future true-crime episode.” Shaz leans over the seat in front and snarls at a bloke who’s staring. “What the fuck are you looking at, mate? Want a photo?” He looks away quick. The coach stops at Chadderton services for no reason. Driver gets out for a fag. We all pile off because we need air that doesn’t smell like stomach acid. Outside it’s pissing it down harder. Leigh-Anne’s satin pyjamas are basically see-through now. She doesn’t care. Shaz nicks a half-empty bottle of vodka from some lads getting on another coach and necks it like water. Priya disappears into the Spar and comes back with a family bag of Doritos and four Red Bulls. “For the blood sugar,” she says. Dead serious. I find Mia’s vape in my pocket (the strawberry one she left in my room the night she disappeared). The battery’s dead but I keep putting it to my lips anyway. Tastes like ghost. Back on the coach it gets worse. Some girl starts screaming at her boyfriend about WhatsApp ticks. A baby’s crying somewhere. The puking lad does it again, this time straight onto the floor. It rolls downhill and laps at my trainers like a disgusting tide. Shaz stands up. “Right, who’s getting bottled first?” Priya grabs her hoodie and yanks her down. “Sit. We’re not getting kicked off in the middle of nowhere.” Leigh-Anne’s stopped filming. She’s scrolling through Mia’s old TikToks with the sound off. One of them is the video where they’re all in my bedroom doing the “wipe it down” challenge and Mia’s laughing so hard she falls off the bed. Leigh-Anne’s thumb keeps hovering over the screen like she can pause time. I open Find My again. Mia’s dot is still in Harehills. Hasn’t moved. Battery at 3%. Shaz sees my screen. “That’s the same place it was last week.” “Yeah.” “So either her phone’s been there the whole time…” “Or she has.” The coach finally hits Leeds at half four. Sky’s that bruised colour it gets before dawn. We’re the only ones who get off at the stop near the abandoned house. Driver doesn’t even look at us. Harehills is dead. Shutters down, chicken boxes blowing across the road like tumbleweed. Rain’s turned to proper sleet now. My teeth are chattering so hard I bite my tongue and taste blood. Shaz lights another cig with shaking hands. “Right. Which one is it?” Priya’s got the screenshot. Points at a boarded-up terraced house three doors down. Windows smashed and covered with plywood. Front door’s got a council notice taped to it: DANGEROUS STRUCTURE DO NOT ENTER. “Course it is,” Leigh-Anne says. Voice small. We cross the road. My trainers are soaked through. There’s a hole in the plywood over the front window big enough to squeeze through. Shaz goes first because she’s smallest and meanest. Then Leigh-Anne, then Priya. I go last and the wood scrapes my coat and rips a chunk out of it. Inside it smells like damp and piss and something metallic. Phone torches on. The place is fucked. Carpet’s black with mould. Someone’s spray-painted CUNT in massive letters across one wall. Shaz finds the kitchen first. “Morgs. Get in here.” Mia’s school bag is on the counter. Exact same one she had the day she disappeared. Hello Kitty keyring still on it. Except now it’s covered in green mould and there’s a used needle sticking out the side pocket. Leigh-Anne makes a noise like a kicked dog. Priya’s already moving. Up the stairs. We follow because what else are we gonna do. First bedroom: mattress on the floor, stains that might be anything. Second bedroom: nothing. Bathroom: sweet Jesus. The bath’s full of dark water. Sanitary towels floating in it like dead fish. Walls have handprints. Actual bloody handprints. Someone tried to clean it with bleach and gave up halfway. Smell hits me like a brick. I turn round and throw up in the hallway. Proper projectile. Doritos and Red Bull and pure terror. Shaz holds my hair back without a word. Leigh-Anne’s crying again. “This isn’t hers. Tell me this isn’t hers.” Priya’s in the living room now. She’s found something. We stumble down after her. Polaroids. Pinned to the wall with drawing pins. Through the eyes. Me asleep on my bedroom floor. Shaz passed out on the night bus last summer. Leigh-Anne mid-lash application. Priya reading in the library with her headphones on. All taken weeks ago. Months, maybe. Underneath them, someone’s written in red pen that’s definitely not pen: YOU LEFT ME TO ROT Shaz rips them down one by one. Her hands are shaking so bad she drops the knife. That’s when we hear it. Footsteps. Upstairs. Slow. Deliberate. Not a cat. We freeze. The footsteps stop right above us. Then start again. Coming down. Leigh-Anne whimpers. Priya picks up Shaz’s knife. I grab the others and we bolt for the window we came in. Shaz goes first, then Leigh-Anne. Priya shoves me through and I land hard on the pavement outside, knees screaming. Priya’s last out. She’s got blood on her hands that isn’t hers. We don’t stop running until we’re three streets away and my lungs are on fire. None of us speak. We just stand under a broken streetlight, soaked to the bone, staring at each other like we’ve seen God and he’s fucking mental. Mia’s here. And she’s not okay. Not even a little bit.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 2 is a strong piece of darkly comic British YA fiction that largely delivers on its promises. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Morgan's voice is consistently rendered throughout. The narration has the right texture — clipped, sardonic, emotionally guarded but leaking feeling at the edges. "Tastes like ghost" for the dead vape is genuinely good. The characterisation is faithful to the profiles: Shaz is violent and loyal, Leigh-Anne films everything and then stops when it gets real, Priya is the quiet competent one who picks up the knife. These feel like distinct people rather than interchangeable teenagers. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is sparse but effective. "This is for the memories, Morgs. Future true-crime episode" is pitch-perfect Leigh-Anne. "For the blood sugar" from Priya lands well. The dialogue doesn't overstay its welcome and each character's voice is distinguishable. There's no clunky exposition-dumping through speech. PLOT AND PLAN ADHERENCE: The chapter follows the plan closely — night bus replacement service, the puking lad, Leigh-Anne live-streaming, Shaz threatening the driver (though this is softened to threatening a staring bloke), finding Mia's vape, arriving in Harehills, the boarded-up house, the school bag, the bathroom horror, the polaroids, the footsteps. The chapter plan's promise that "someone pukes" is fulfilled (Morgan herself). The chapter ends where planned — staring at the house, realising Mia is there and not okay. One deviation: the plan says they end "staring at a boarded-up house and realising none of them brought a torch" but the chapter goes further, actually entering the house. This is a significant departure — the house exploration is Chapter 3's territory in the plan. The model has essentially merged chapters 2 and 3, which means the chapter is doing more than planned but potentially cannibalising future material. IMAGERY AND METAPHOR: Most of the imagery lands. "Sanitary towels floating in it like dead fish" is viscerally effective. "Chicken boxes blowing across the road like tumbleweed" is a good northern gothic image. "Sky's that bruised colour it gets before dawn" is slightly more generic but functional. "Leigh-Anne's thumb keeps hovering over the screen like she can pause time" is the most emotionally resonant moment in the chapter and earns its place. The polaroids with drawing pins through the eyes is genuinely unsettling. "YOU LEFT ME TO ROT" in what's implied to be blood is effective horror. There are a few moments where the writing reaches for effect and doesn't quite get there — "like we've seen God and he's fucking mental" is trying to be the chapter's closing kicker but it's a bit flat. DARKNESS AND COMEDY BALANCE: The chapter maintains the darkly comic register well. The sick rolling down the aisle, Shaz threatening to bottle someone, Leigh-Anne's "iconic" — these provide genuine levity against the mounting dread. The comedy doesn't undercut the horror; it makes it more bearable and more real. WEAKNESSES: The chapter's main structural problem is that it covers too much ground — it essentially does chapters 2 and 3 from the plan. This means the pacing is slightly rushed in the back half. The discovery of the house interior, which should be its own chapter of dread, is compressed into a few paragraphs. The footsteps and flight feel slightly rushed. The emotional beats at the end — the polaroids, the writing in blood — deserve more space to breathe. The final line "Not even a little bit" is a weak landing for what's otherwise a strong chapter. It's the kind of understatement that tries to be devastating but lands as merely adequate. AMATEURISH ELEMENTS: Minimal. The writing is confident and controlled. There's no purple prose to speak of — the register is deliberately flat and northern, which is appropriate. No forced metaphors that don't land. COHERENCE: The chapter is coherent and the geography of the night makes sense. The emotional logic tracks. OVERALL: A genuinely accomplished piece of YA horror-comedy that mostly delivers on its ambitious premise. The voice is consistent, the characters are distinct, the horror is earned, and the comedy is real. The main issues are structural (covering too much ground) and the slightly weak ending. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 11 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Chapter 3** We’re hiding in the shell of a bus shelter that smells like old piss and newer sick, trying to breathe through our mouths so we don’t taste the air. My knees are bleeding where I hit the pavement. Shaz keeps licking her lips like she’s still tasting blood. Leigh-Anne’s satin pyjama top is ripped at the shoulder and her tit’s nearly out but she hasn’t noticed. Priya’s holding the knife like it’s part of her hand now. I can’t stop seeing those polaroids. My sleeping face with a pin through the eye. That wasn’t recent. That was July, because I recognise the Smiffy’s hoodie I was wearing. Someone’s been inside my flat. Someone’s been inside all our flats. Shaz breaks first. “I’m going back in.” “No you’re fucking not,” I hiss. “She’s in there, Morgs. We heard her.” “We don’t know what we heard.” Priya speaks so quiet we have to lean in. “It wasn’t footsteps. It was dragging.” Leigh-Anne makes this wounded noise and folds in half, arms round her stomach. “I want my mum.” None of us answer that because we all want our mums and none of them would know what to do with this either. I check Find My again. Mia’s dot is still in that house. Hasn’t moved since we ran. Battery now 1%. Either the phone’s dying or she is. “We need to be clever,” Priya says. “Not just charge in like dickheads.” Shaz snorts. “Since when have we ever been clever?” But we listen because Priya’s the only one thinking straight. She makes us walk round the block twice, scoping. Back of the house has a yard full of nettles and a wheelie bin on fire. Kitchen window’s smashed. We could get in that way, quieter. It’s properly light now, that grey Leeds morning that makes everything look like it’s already dead. A milk float goes past. Some old woman walking a dog doesn’t even look at four teenage girls covered in blood and mud at half five in the morning. Normal Tuesday round here. We go over the back wall. Shaz boosts me first, then Leigh-Anne (who lands like a sack of potatoes and starts crying again). Priya passes the knife up last and climbs like she does this every day. Kitchen again. Smells worse in daylight. There’s a fridge door hanging open and something growing inside that might be cheese or might be alive. Mia’s bag is still on the counter but someone’s unzipped it now. Contents dumped out: her old school planner, a half-eaten packet of chocolate Hobnobs gone soft, and a single Polaroid of all five of us at the Christmas market last year. Someone’s drawn devil horns on Leigh-Anne and a noose round my neck. Shaz finds the diary page stuck to the fridge with a fridge magnet shaped like a dick. It’s just one sheet torn out. i want to be empty i want to be empty i want to be empty i want to be empty i want to be empty i want to be empty i want to be empty i want to be empty i want to be empty i want to be empty Written over and over in blue biro until the paper’s shredded. The last one is in red and the pen’s gone through the page. Leigh-Anne starts hyperventilating. “She’s doing it again. Like last time.” Last time was Year 10 when Mia stopped eating for three weeks and ended up in hospital with a heart rate of 38. She told the nurses it was for dance auditions. We knew it was because Harry Pennington called her thighs cottage cheese on his private story. Priya’s already moving. Living room. The polaroids are gone from the wall. Just drawing-pin holes and blood dots where they were. Upstairs. We creep up like we’re in a horror film but it’s not scary in that way, it’s worse. It’s quiet. Too quiet. Like the house is holding its breath. First bedroom: mattress still there but now there’s a sleeping bag unrolled on it. Empty packets of super-plus tampons everywhere. Boxes and boxes. Someone’s been stocking up. Bathroom door’s shut. There’s a wet handprint on it. Fresh. Shaz reaches for the handle. I grab her wrist. “Don’t.” We all look at each other. No one’s breathing. Priya pushes past and opens the door slow. Bath’s empty now. Water’s gone. Just a ring of blood and diluted bleach. Someone’s written on the mirror in lipstick (Mia’s shade, Mac Velvet Teddy, nicked from Boots last summer): SOON And underneath, smaller: you’ll thank me There’s a phone on the sink. Mia’s phone. Screen cracked to fuck. Still on. Still at 1%. The Find My dot was the phone, not her. Leigh-Anne picks it up with shaking fingers. Lock screen is a photo of all of us at New Year, tongues out, devil horns, drunk on Tesco vodka. She swipes up. No passcode. Opens messages. Last text sent: the 🩸🩸🩸 to us at 00:03. Last text received: from a number not saved, three hours ago. just need a little more time babes then you’ll see then you’ll all see Leigh-Anne drops the phone like it’s hot. It clatters into the empty bath. That’s when we hear it again. The dragging. Louder this time. Coming from the loft. There’s a hatch in the ceiling on the landing. One of those fold-down ladders, but the ladder’s gone. Just the hatch. Closed. Something moves above us. Slow. Heavy. Shaz looks up, eyes massive. “She’s been here the whole time.” Priya’s already backing down the stairs. “We need to go. Now.” We don’t argue. We run. Down the stairs, through the kitchen, out the back. Over the wall. Don’t stop until we’re round the corner and my lungs are burning and Leigh-Anne’s barefoot because she lost her sliders somewhere and she’s crying blood where the glass cut her feet. We stand in the road, panting, soaked, feral. Shaz is the first to speak. “She’s not missing. She’s hiding.” Priya wipes her face with her sleeve, leaving a streak of blood and snot. “And she doesn’t want to be found.” Leigh-Anne looks at me, mascara rivers down her cheeks. “What do we do now, Morgs?” I don’t know. For the first time in my life I proper don’t know. But the sun’s coming up proper now and it’s Saturday and somewhere in that house our best mate is trying to disappear one cut at a time and we’re the only ones who know. I light Mia’s dead vape even though there’s nothing in it and suck anyway. “We go home,” I say. “We get clean. We go to that fucking christening. And then we come back tonight and we drag her out screaming if we have to.” Shaz nods slow. Priya’s already walking towards the main road to flag down a taxi we can’t afford. Leigh-Anne whispers, “She’s really lost it this time.” Yeah. She has. And we’re going to get her back even if it kills us all.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 3 is a strong piece of darkly comic British YA fiction that largely delivers on its promises. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Morgan's voice is consistently rendered throughout. The narration has that authentic 4am-texting quality the plan promised — clipped, observational, occasionally funny in the worst possible way. "Normal Tuesday round here" after describing four blood-soaked teenagers being ignored by a dog walker is exactly the kind of deadpan the plan called for. The characters are distinguishable: Shaz's impulsive aggression ("I'm going back in"), Priya's quiet tactical thinking, Leigh-Anne's vanity-meets-vulnerability (the detail about her tit nearly out but not noticing is perfect), and Morgan's reluctant leadership. These are consistent with the profiles. PLOT EXECUTION: The chapter follows the plan reasonably well — they break into the house, find the school bag, find the diary page, discover the polaroids are gone, find the bathroom crime scene, and leg it when they hear something upstairs. The plan mentioned "footsteps upstairs that definitely aren't a cat" and the chapter delivers "dragging" which is actually more unsettling. The pivot — Mia's phone was the Find My dot, not Mia herself — is a genuinely clever narrative beat that wasn't explicitly in the plan but serves it well. The mystery text from an unknown number adds a layer the plan didn't specify but fits organically. WHAT WORKS PARTICULARLY WELL: The diary page rendered visually (the repeated "i want to be empty" with the last one in red going through the paper) is genuinely affecting. The lipstick mirror message "SOON / you'll thank me" is economical and chilling. The detail about recognising the Smiffy's hoodie in the polaroid — meaning someone was in her flat in July — is the kind of specific, grounded horror that elevates this above generic YA. The Leigh-Anne backstory about Mia being her "first comment" is woven in naturally. The Christmas market polaroid with devil horns on Leigh-Anne and a noose round Morgan's neck is disturbing without being overwrought. WEAKNESSES: The chapter doesn't include someone throwing up, which the plan explicitly promised for every chapter after Chapter 2. This is a notable miss. The ending is slightly too tidy and resolved — "we go to that fucking christening and then we come back" wraps things up a bit neatly for a chapter that should feel more chaotic. The line "Yeah. She has. / And we're going to get her back even if it kills us all" is the one moment where the voice slips into something more conventionally YA-dramatic, slightly undermining the rawness that precedes it. The milk float detail is good but the "already dead" observation about Leeds morning light edges toward purple prose. The "feral" descriptor is used a touch too comfortably. DIALOGUE: Generally strong and naturalistic. "Since when have we ever been clever?" is a good Shaz line. The dialogue is sparse but purposeful, which suits the chapter's tension. FORCED POETRY: Mostly avoided. "Like roadkill is beautiful right before the crows get it" isn't in this chapter (that's from the profile), and the chapter largely resists the temptation to over-poeticise. The "holding its breath" house is a cliché but it's used knowingly. The dead vape at the end is a nice image — sucking on something empty as a gesture of futility — that earns its metaphorical weight without announcing itself. DARKNESS: The horror here is genuinely well-earned. The shift from supernatural-seeming dread to the mundane, pathetic reality of a girl hiding in a loft cutting herself for TikTok clout is exactly what the plan promised. The unknown texter adds genuine menace without tipping into melodrama. OVERALL: This is a confident, well-executed chapter that mostly honours its plan and voice. The missed vomit promise and the slightly conventional ending are the main demerits. The writing is sharp, the characters are alive, and the darkness feels earned rather than performed. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Chapter 4** We get back to Wythenshawe at half eleven looking like we’ve been exhumed. Leigh-Anne’s cousin’s christening starts at one and there’s no way she’s missing it; her mum would actually murder her and hide the body in the churchyard. I stash the polaroids and the diary page in my bra because I’m not leaving them anywhere else. They’re damp and smell like iron. My tits are gonna smell like blood forever. Shaz gets dropped off first. She hugs us all quick and vicious, like she’s scared we’ll vanish if she lets go. “See you at the church, yeah? Don’t be late or I’ll kill you.” Priya’s next. She doesn’t say anything, just squeezes my hand so hard the bones creak, then disappears into her house with the big driveway and the CCTV that definitely caught us all looking like serial killers. Leigh-Anne makes the taxi wait while she runs in to get changed. I go with her because I’ve got nothing clean at mine and her mum keeps spare outfits for me in the spare room like I’m a stray cat. Leigh-Anne’s house smells like vanilla plug-ins and bleach. Her mum clocks us at the door and freezes. “Jesus Christ, what happened to you pair?” “Got jumped,” Leigh-Anne lies smooth as anything. “Some girls from Baguley. We’re fine.” Her mum buys it because it’s Manchester and that’s just Saturday morning. She makes us tea while Leigh-Anne showers. I sit at the kitchen table in my crusty hoodie drinking it with shaking hands. The polaroids are burning a hole through my bra. Leigh-Anne comes down in a pastel pink bodycon dress and fresh lashes. Looks like an Instagram filter in real life. She’s still pale as milk but the makeup hides it. “You ready?” she asks. “No.” “Me neither.” The church is packed. Leigh-Anne’s family is massive and Catholic in the way that means guilt and free prosecco. I’m wearing one of her old dresses that’s too tight round the ribs and smells like her coconut hair stuff. My trainers are still soaked in Leeds blood so I’ve got Leigh-Anne’s spare heels that are half a size too small. My feet are screaming. We slide into a pew near the back. Shaz is already there, wearing a black skirt suit she definitely nicked from her mum’s wardrobe. She looks like a funeral director who’s about to rob you. Priya’s next to her in a navy shift dress, hair scraped back so tight it’s giving her a facelift. She looks like she’s been up all night planning a murder. She has. The baby starts screaming the second the priest pours the water. Everyone coos. I feel like I’m underwater. Leigh-Anne’s auntie leans over. “You alright, bab? You look peaky.” “Hungover,” I lie. She nods like that explains everything. I can’t stop thinking about the loft hatch. The dragging sound. Mia up there like a spider, thin and pale and waiting. Halfway through the service I have to leg it to the toilets because I’m gonna be sick again. Lock myself in the disabled cubicle and dry-heave into the bowl until my ribs hurt. The polaroids fall out my bra into the toilet water. I fish them out, sobbing and laughing at the same time because of course this is my life. When I come out Shaz is waiting, leaning against the sink vaping mango ice into the holy water font. “You okay?” she asks. “No.” “Me neither.” She offers me the vape. I take it. We stand there in silence breaking about seventeen laws. Back in the pew Priya passes me a note like we’re in Year 8. we need to talk about tonight. the house again? I write back: travelodge first? her phone pinged there last night She nods. The christening party’s at the church hall after. Sausage rolls and vol-au-vents and free prosecco for the adults. Shaz gets absolutely mortal on it because Leigh-Anne’s uncle keeps topping her up thinking she’s funny. She starts telling the priest about Mia “fucking about with Satan” and he thinks it’s the funniest thing he’s heard all year. I’m hiding in the corner pretending to be on my phone when Leigh-Anne’s mum drags me over to talk to some cousin who wants to do my nails. I stand there smiling like a corpse while she chatters about acrylics and I’ve got actual blood crust under my fingernails from Leeds. Priya finds me by the tea urn. “I’ve been thinking.” “Dangerous.” “She wants us to find her. The photos. The phone. The writing. It’s all breadcrumbs.” “So what, we’re Hansel and fucking Gretel?” “She’s leading us somewhere.” “Yeah. To a breakdown.” Priya’s eyes are bloodshot. “We have to finish it tonight. Whatever it is.” I nod. Can’t speak. Leigh-Anne appears with a plate of mini quiche and shoves one in my mouth so I stop looking like I’m about to scream. “Eat something,” she says. “You’re grey.” I chew. Tastes like cardboard and despair. Shaz stumbles over, prosecco sloshing. “Right, who’s coming back to mine for pres before we go get our girl?” We all raise our hands like it’s normal. The party drags on. Photos. Speeches. Someone’s uncle does karaoke to “Angels” by Robbie Williams and cries. I keep checking my phone. No new messages. Mia’s account still shows as active but nothing since the blood drops. At four o’clock Leigh-Anne’s mum finally lets us go. We pile into the back of her auntie’s Range Rover, still in our christening outfits, heels killing us, makeup running from crying in the toilets. Shaz is asleep on Priya’s shoulder, drooling. Leigh-Anne’s filming out the window. “Christening content unlocked. Now onto part two: rescuing my feral best friend from herself. Stay tuned.” Priya meets my eyes in the reflection. She mouths: tonight. I nod. As we pull away from the church I swear I hear my phone buzz in my bag. I don’t check. Not yet. I’m not ready for whatever’s waiting.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 4 is a transitional chapter — the christening interlude — and it largely succeeds at what it sets out to do: maintain tension during a mundane social obligation while the girls are still reeling from the Leeds house. Let me assess it carefully. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Morgan's voice is consistent and genuinely distinctive. The narration has the right texture — clipped, sardonic, self-aware without being precious. "Looks like an Instagram filter in real life" for Leigh-Anne is earned. "Shaz gets absolutely mortal on it" and the priest anecdote land well. The characters are differentiated through action and speech rather than description, which is the right approach. Shaz's violence-as-love-language comes through in the "hugs us all quick and vicious" line. Priya's note-passing and deadpan planning feel true to profile. Leigh-Anne's "Babes this is giving final girl energy" energy is present in the filming-out-the-window moment. FOLLOWING THE PLAN: The chapter hits most of its planned beats — the christening, Shaz getting hammered on prosecco, telling the priest about Mia and Satan, Morgan in the disabled toilet. However, the plan's most striking image — Mia's phone ringing from inside the church font — is completely absent. This is a significant omission; it was the chapter's most memorable planned moment, the one that would have elevated it from "transitional chapter" to something genuinely uncanny. Instead the chapter ends with a vague phone buzz that Morgan doesn't check. This is a weaker, safer choice. The plan also promised someone throwing up in every chapter after Chapter 2 — Morgan dry-heaves but doesn't actually vomit, which is borderline compliance. The polaroids falling into the toilet is a nice physical comedy beat that wasn't in the plan but works. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is functional and occasionally sharp ("You're grey." / "Eat something." / "Tastes like cardboard and despair.") but some exchanges are too symmetrical and neat — the "You okay?" / "No." / "Me neither." exchange happens twice in the chapter, which feels like a tic rather than a deliberate structural choice. It's the kind of thing that reads as a writer finding a formula and repeating it. PLOT AND TENSION: The chapter does its job of maintaining dread beneath the mundane. The polaroids in the bra, the blood crust under fingernails at the nail conversation, Priya's "breadcrumbs" theory — these keep the horror present. However, the chapter is somewhat inert. Nothing actually happens. The plan's phone-in-the-font moment would have given it a genuine plot event; without it, it's pure atmosphere and transition. METAPHOR AND PROSE: The prose is mostly clean and avoids purple excess. "Looks like a funeral director who's about to rob you" for Shaz is good. "Roadkill beautiful" isn't used here but the instinct is right. A few lines reach slightly too hard — "crying in the toilets" as explanation for running makeup is a bit convenient — but nothing egregiously forced. "Leigh-Anne's house smells like vanilla plug-ins and bleach" is excellent sensory detail. LIGHTNESS AND DARKNESS: The tonal balance is the chapter's genuine strength. The karaoke uncle crying to "Angels," the priest laughing at Satan chat, the acrylics conversation with blood under the fingernails — these work as darkly comic relief without undercutting the dread. The lightness is earned because it's grounded in recognizable social reality. WEAKNESSES: The missing phone-in-the-font moment is a real loss. The repeated "You okay? / No. / Me neither." structure is lazy. The ending is deliberately understated but perhaps too much so — it dissipates tension rather than building it. The chapter is competent but not exceptional; it does what it needs to do without doing anything surprising. AMATEURISH ELEMENTS: The symmetrical dialogue exchanges are the main amateurish tell. The chapter also tells us Morgan feels "underwater" twice in different forms, which is repetitive. The Priya "breadcrumbs" exposition scene is slightly clunky — it's the chapter's most tell-don't-show moment. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 13 Compelling Plot: 10 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 8 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 9 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 9 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Chapter 5** It’s 23:57 when the second 🩸🩸🩸 comes through. We’re in Leigh-Anne’s bedroom pretending to get ready for a normal Saturday night out. Shaz is doing lines of glitter on her collarbones with a Primark voucher card. Priya’s on the floor painting her nails black even though she’s still wearing the navy christening dress. I’m trying to eat a Pot Noodle that tastes like shame and cardboard. My phone’s on the dresser, face down because I can’t look at it. It buzzes. We all freeze. Shaz is the closest. She flips it over. Three blood drops. From Mia. Then a voice note. 6 seconds. Shaz presses play before any of us can stop her. Mia’s voice, thin and wet: breathing like she’s underwater, then a whisper that scrapes my spine raw. “come get me i’m cold” The location pings. Travelodge. M62. Junction 26. Not Harehills anymore. Leigh-Anne makes this broken noise and starts pulling on trainers with her christening dress still on. “That’s forty minutes away. We can make it.” Priya’s already standing. “Car.” “Mum’s Micra’s at home,” I say. My voice sounds like someone else’s. “Keys are on the hook.” We move like we’ve planned this our whole lives. Down the stairs quiet so Leigh-Anne’s mum doesn’t hear. Out the front door. The Micra’s parked wonky on the curb because Mum can’t parallel park for shit. I’ve been driving it illegally since I was fifteen. Shaz shotgun, Priya and Leigh-Anne in the back still in their fancy dresses looking like deranged prom rejects. I turn the key and the engine coughs like it’s been smoking forty a day. Radio comes on blasting Heart FM. Shaz smacks it silent. Rain’s hammering the windscreen so hard the wipers can’t keep up. Motorway’s a blur of red lights and lorry spray. No one speaks for the first ten minutes. Then Leigh-Anne from the back: “What if it’s a trap?” “What if it’s not?” Priya says. Shaz is twisting her rings round and round her fingers. “She sounded wrong. Like proper wrong.” I indicate wrong and nearly take out a Citroën. “She’s been wrong for weeks.” The services appear like a mirage. Neon lights bleeding into the wet tarmac. We stop because Shaz needs a piss and Leigh-Anne needs Red Bull and I need to stop my hands shaking. Inside it’s hell. Saturday night stragglers, families with screaming kids, lads already mortal at half eleven. Smells like grease and despair. Shaz disappears to the toilets. Priya stands by the Costa staring at nothing. Leigh-Anne buys a 24-pack of Monster and a family-size Dairy Milk with a jar of coppers she keeps in her bra for emergencies. I stand by the door watching the rain and trying not to throw up. Shaz comes back wiping her hands on her dress. “There’s a girl in the disabled toilet cutting her arms with a Costa loyalty card. Thought you should know.” We all look at each other. “Priorities,” Priya says, and we pile back into the car. I put the postcode in my phone. 28 minutes. Leigh-Anne’s filming again with the back camera so you can’t see her face. “Part three, besties. We’re going to get our girl. If you’re watching this it’s because we’re dead or famous.” Priya snatches the phone and ends the recording. “Not funny.” “It’s a bit funny,” Shaz says. We hit the motorway again. Speed limit means nothing tonight. I’m doing ninety in a twenty-year-old Micra that smells like wet dog and Mia’s old perfume. The Travelodge looms up out of the rain like a budget haunted house. Orange lights, car park half full of white vans and tired sales reps. Room 207. Second floor. We park wonky across two spaces. Don’t lock the car. Don’t care. The automatic doors whoosh open and the receptionist looks up. Four teenage girls in party dresses and trainers, soaked, wild-eyed, covered in glitter and what might be blood. “Evening,” she says like this is normal. Leigh-Anne leans on the desk, lashes batting. “Room 207, please. My mate left her straighteners in there.” Receptionist doesn’t even blink. Hands over a keycard. “Checkout’s tomorrow.” We take the stairs because the lift smells like death. Second floor corridor smells like carpet cleaner and regret. Room 207 is at the end. The door’s already open. Just a crack. Light spilling out yellow and wrong. Shaz pushes it open slow. The room’s been lived in. Hard. Bed’s unmade, sheets grey and stained. Empty tampon boxes everywhere. Walls have handprints again, some fresh. There’s a Tesco bag full of bloody sanitary towels by the sink. The mirror’s smashed like someone punched it. Mia’s school jumper is nailed to the wall with a coat hanger through the shoulders. Still wet. The bathroom door’s shut. Leigh-Anne starts crying properly now. “Mia?” Nothing. Priya’s got the knife out again. Where did she even get that from? I step forward. Push the bathroom door. It swings open. Mia’s in the bath. Naked. Skeletal. Skin grey. Hair falling out in clumps. Arms and legs covered in cuts in neat lines like tally marks. She’s rocking back and forth in water that’s pink and cold. She looks up when we come in. Her eyes are massive. Sunken. “Morgs,” she whispers. Voice like paper. “You came.” Then she smiles and there’s blood in her teeth. “I’m nearly there. Nearly empty. You’ll see.” Leigh-Anne screams. Shaz drops to her knees. Priya raises the knife. I just stand there thinking: this is what love looks like when it’s too late. The bathwater slops over the edge as Mia tries to stand. Her legs won’t hold her. She collapses back, water turning redder. “I knew you’d come,” she says. “I left you clues. Like a game.” “It wasn’t a fucking game,” Shaz snarls. Mia laughs and it turns into a cough that brings up blood. Priya’s shaking. “We’re taking you home.” Mia’s eyes roll back. “No. Not yet. One more. Just one more and I’ll be perfect.” She reaches for something under the water. A craft knife. Blade red. I move faster than I ever have. Grab her wrist. The blade slices my hand open but I don’t let go. “No,” I say. “Enough.” She looks at me like I’ve betrayed her. Then she starts screaming. We move like we’ve rehearsed it. Sheets off the bed. Wrap her like a burrito. She’s fighting, biting, scratching. Leigh-Anne’s tit gets proper chomped. Blood everywhere. I find the kettle. The heavy one. Smash it into Mia’s temple. She goes limp. We carry her out wrapped in the sheet like a body. Past the receptionist who doesn’t even look up. Into the Micra. Into the rain. She’s so light now. Like holding a bird with broken wings. We’re taking her home. Whatever’s left of her.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 5 is a strong execution of the established voice and plan, with some notable strengths and a few weaknesses worth examining critically. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Morgan's narration is consistently rendered. The flat, clipped sentences ("Car." "Priorities.") feel authentic to the character profile rather than stylistically imposed. The voice doesn't slip into adult writerly register, which is a genuine achievement across a sustained piece. Shaz, Leigh-Anne, and Priya all behave consistently with their profiles — Leigh-Anne filming, Priya with the knife appearing from nowhere, Shaz being the one to press play on the voice note. The detail of Leigh-Anne buying the Monster and Dairy Milk with coppers from her bra is exactly right. PLAN ADHERENCE: The chapter follows the plan closely — the second 🩸🩸🩸, the voice note, the christening dresses, the Micra, the services stop, the 24-pack of Monster and Dairy Milk with coppers, room 207 already open, the bath scene, the kettle knockout, carrying Mia out. The plan is executed faithfully. The receptionist handing over the keycard without question is a nice darkly comic beat. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is mostly functional and in-character. "Part three, besties. We're going to get our girl. If you're watching this it's because we're dead or famous." is genuinely good — it captures Leigh-Anne perfectly. "I'm nearly there. Nearly empty. You'll see." works for Mia. However, some exchanges are thin: "What if it's a trap?" / "What if it's not?" is a bit flat and generic for these characters. The dialogue doesn't consistently crackle. SHOW VS TELL: There's a significant tell-don't-show failure in the line "I just stand there thinking: this is what love looks like when it's too late." This is the chapter's worst moment — it's the narrator explaining the theme directly to the reader, which undercuts the rawness the rest of the chapter earns. The piece is otherwise reasonably good at showing, but this line is a real stumble. METAPHOR AND POETRY: "Like holding a bird with broken wings" at the end is a cliché that doesn't fit the voice — Morgan wouldn't think in that register. "Budget haunted house" for the Travelodge is fine. "Voice like paper" is acceptable. The line "this is what love looks like when it's too late" is both telling and forced. The services description — "hell... smells like grease and despair" — is slightly purple but fits the voice just enough. Overall the metaphors are mostly restrained and appropriate, with the bird simile being the main offender. DARKNESS AND TONAL BALANCE: The chapter earns its darkness. The Mia reveal is genuinely disturbing — the tally-mark cuts, the pink bathwater, the blood in her teeth, the craft knife under the water. The darkly comic elements (receptionist not blinking, Leigh-Anne's tit getting chomped, the kettle) land because they're grounded in character logic rather than being inserted for effect. The girl in the disabled toilet cutting with a Costa loyalty card is a sharp, bleak detail that works. COHERENCE: The chapter is coherent and well-paced. The escalation from the services stop to the room reveal is well-managed. The action sequence at the end (wrapping Mia, the kettle) is slightly rushed but appropriately chaotic. CREATIVITY: The chapter is creative within its established framework. The Travelodge setting, the receptionist beat, the school jumper nailed to the wall with a coat hanger — these are specific and inventive. The craft knife under the water is a good escalation. The chapter doesn't surprise as much as earlier chapters might have, but it delivers on its promises. AMATEURISM: The "bird with broken wings" simile and the explicit thematic statement are amateurish moments in an otherwise competent chapter. The chapter plan promised someone throws up in every chapter after Chapter 2 — this doesn't happen here, which is a minor plan deviation. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 7 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Chapter 6** We get her back to mine at 6 a.m., still wrapped in the Travelodge sheet like a bloody cocoon. Mum’s on nights again, little brother’s at a sleepover. Flat smells like cold chips and Febreeze. Mia’s limp in my arms, head lolling, blood crusting her hair where I clocked her with the kettle. Shaz and Priya carry her legs. Leigh-Anne’s holding the doors, still biting her tit where Mia sank teeth in. Blood’s soaked through her pink dress and dried black. We dump Mia on my bed. She’s breathing shallow but steady. Skin’s see-through. You can see every vein. Her ribs are a fucking xylophone. Priya locks the bedroom door from inside and pockets the key. “No hospitals. They’ll section her.” Leigh-Anne’s shaking. “She needs a doctor.” “She needs us,” I say. Shaz is already raiding Mum’s medicine cabinet. Comes back with paracetamol, plasters, and a bottle of vodka. “For disinfecting.” We unwrap the sheet. It sticks where the blood’s dried. Mia’s naked underneath. Every cut’s neat, deliberate. Some old and scabbed, some fresh and weeping. Her hips have that sharp model look but wrong, like someone’s photoshopped a corpse. Leigh-Anne starts crying again. “She’s freezing.” We pile every blanket we own on her. My Smiffy’s hoodie, Mum’s dressing gown, Shaz’s dad’s fleece. She still looks dead. Priya finds a notebook under the mattress back at the Travelodge. She’s reading it now, face blank. “What’s it say?” “Lists,” she says quiet. “Weight. Calories. Millilitres of blood. Days until empty. Quotes from some witch Tok account. ‘Purity is hunger. Hunger is power.’” Shaz spits on the floor. “Fucking hell.” Mia stirs. Eyes flutter open. They’re wrong. Pupils massive. “You came,” she whispers again. Smiles like it hurts. “I knew you would.” Leigh-Anne kneels by the bed. “Why, bab? Why’d you do this?” Mia tries to sit up. Can’t. “I was too heavy. Everything hurt. I wanted to be light. Like air. Like nothing. Then you’d love me proper.” “We did love you proper,” Shaz snarls. “You daft bitch.” Mia’s laugh turns into a cough. Blood on her lips. “Not enough. Never enough.” I can’t speak. My hand’s still bleeding where she cut me. I wrap it in a sock. Priya closes the notebook. “You’re stopping now. This ends tonight.” Mia’s eyes flick to her. “You can’t make me.” Shaz grabs her wrist. “Watch us.” Mia starts crying then. Proper ugly crying. Snot and tears and blood mixing. “I’m so cold. I’m always cold now.” We pile on the bed. All four of us round her like wolves guarding a wounded cub. She’s shaking so hard the mattress vibrates. Leigh-Anne’s stroking her hair. “We’ve got you. We’ve got you.” Mia’s teeth start chattering. “I think I went too far.” “Yeah,” I say. “You did.” She sleeps eventually. Or passes out. Hard to tell. We take turns watching her. Two awake, two trying to sleep on the floor. Shaz finds Mum’s secret cigs and we smoke out my bedroom window, rain still coming down. Leigh-Anne’s live-streaming the carpet because she can’t film Mia like this. “She’s gonna hate us tomorrow,” Shaz says. “Good,” Priya replies. “Means she’s still alive.” I keep checking her pulse every ten minutes. It’s there. Weak but there. At one point she wakes up screaming. Thrashes so hard she rips open half the cuts. Blood everywhere again. We hold her down while she begs us to let her finish it. Priya slaps her. Hard. Across the face. “Shut up,” she says. Voice shaking. “Just shut up.” Mia goes quiet. Curls into a ball. Starts rocking. Leigh-Anne’s in the bathroom stress-painting her nails with Mia’s blood because it’s the only red she’s got. “For evidence,” she says. But we all know it’s because she can’t sit still. I find the craft knife in the sheet folds. Hide it in my bra next to the polaroids. Dawn creeps in grey and miserable. Mia’s sleeping proper now. Chest rising and falling. She looks twelve. Shaz is asleep sitting up, head lolling. Priya’s staring at the wall like she can see through it. Leigh-Anne’s filming her feet. I open the group chat. Scroll up to the first 🩸🩸🩸 ever sent. Year 9. Leigh-Anne in science. We were babies. I type a new message. Don’t send it. we’ve got you now. no more bleeding. Mia makes this noise in her sleep. Like a hurt animal. Priya reaches over and holds her hand. Mia’s fingers curl round hers automatic. We’re not letting go. Not ever. Whatever’s left, we’ll keep. Even if it’s just bones. Especially if it’s just bones.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is doing something genuinely difficult: sustaining a voice that is simultaneously raw, funny, tender, and horrifying without tipping into exploitation or sentimentality. Let's examine it carefully. VOICE AND CHARACTER CONSISTENCY Morgan's voice holds throughout. The narration is clipped, present-tense, observational in the way someone in shock observes — cataloguing details because processing them emotionally is impossible. "Her ribs are a fucking xylophone" is the kind of line that earns its grotesque humour because it's exactly how Morgan would see it: clinical, dark, slightly proud of the comparison. Shaz's violence-as-love-language is consistent ("You daft bitch" as tenderness). Leigh-Anne filming her feet because she can't film Mia is a genuinely sharp character beat — it's pathetic and human and completely in keeping with her profile. Priya's slap and "Just shut up" with a shaking voice is the repressed-until-explosion character working correctly. DIALOGUE The dialogue is mostly functional and occasionally excellent. "Not enough. Never enough" from Mia is a bit on-the-nose — it's the kind of thing a character in a film says, not quite what a real dissociated teenager would articulate. But "I was too heavy. Everything hurt. I wanted to be light. Like air. Like nothing. Then you'd love me proper" is better — it's delusional logic rendered simply, which is more disturbing than any gothic flourish. Shaz's "Watch us" is perfect. The dialogue occasionally tells us what we already know emotionally, but it doesn't do this consistently enough to be a serious flaw. SHOW VS TELL The chapter mostly shows rather than tells. "Leigh-Anne's in the bathroom stress-painting her nails with Mia's blood because it's the only red she's got" is a strong show moment — it's weird, specific, and reveals character without explanation. The ending sequence ("Whatever's left, we'll keep. Even if it's just bones. Especially if it's just bones.") tips into telling-through-declaration, which is the chapter's weakest moment. It's trying to be a closing statement but it's slightly too neat, too consciously literary for Morgan's voice. FORCED POETRY / PURPLE PROSE "Her hips have that sharp model look but wrong, like someone's photoshopped a corpse" — this lands. It's grotesque and precise. "Pupils massive" — good, no over-writing. The ending is where the prose gets slightly purple: "Whatever's left, we'll keep. Even if it's just bones. Especially if it's just bones." This is the chapter reaching for profundity in a way that slightly betrays the voice established. Morgan doesn't speak in aphorisms. This feels like the author's voice bleeding through. It's not catastrophic but it's a real flaw. PLAN ADHERENCE The plan called for: Mia locked in Morgan's bedroom, wifi changed, window nailed, girls taking turns with a kitchen knife, Mia trying to hang herself with a dressing gown cord, Priya punching her nose. The chapter delivers the bedroom containment but skips the hanging attempt and the nose-punch — those are moved to Chapter 7 per the plan. What's here is actually Chapter 6 from the plan (room 207 aftermath, bringing her home) blended with early Chapter 7 material. The plan's Chapter 6 called for the Travelodge confrontation, which is referenced as having already happened. So this chapter is actually covering the transition between plan chapters 6 and 7, which is a reasonable compression. The "at least one person throws up" promise from the plan is not fulfilled in this chapter — a minor breach of the explicit promises. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT This is where the chapter genuinely succeeds. The image of four girls piling onto a bed around a wounded friend "like wolves guarding a wounded cub" is earned because we've been with these characters. The pulse-checking every ten minutes is devastating in its simplicity. The detail of Mia's fingers curling automatically around Priya's is the chapter's best moment — it requires no commentary. DARKNESS EARNED The darkness here is earned. The horror is not supernatural, it's social and psychological, and the chapter doesn't flinch from the physical reality of what Mia has done to herself while also refusing to aestheticise it. The craft knife hidden in the bra next to the polaroids is a good detail — practical, grim, no fanfare. WEAKNESSES The ending declaration is too tidy. The "Not enough. Never enough" dialogue is a cliché. The chapter doesn't quite deliver the promised vomiting. Some of the middle section (the notebook reading) is slightly expository. The chapter is strong but not quite masterful — it's doing its job excellently without transcending it. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 7 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Chapter 8** Halloween night and the estate’s on fire with cheap fireworks and cheaper decisions. We’re done pretending we can keep Mia in my bedroom like a tamagotchi that keeps trying to kill itself. She’s been quiet since the hanging. Too quiet. Tied to the bed with tights and my school tie, eyes open, staring at the ceiling like she’s reading instructions written in the mould. Priya cuts her loose at eleven. “You’re coming with us,” she says. Mia smiles. “Where?” “Somewhere we can finish this.” We dress her in my oldest joggers and Shaz’s dad’s fleece. She’s swimming in them. Bones poking through like accusations. No one speaks as we drag her down the stairs. She’s light as a lie now. Bare feet on the cold concrete. The wasteland behind the flats is where we used to drink White Ace and get off with lads who never texted back. Tonight it’s just mud and broken glass and the ghost of every bad choice we ever made. Fireworks are going off everywhere. Screams and bangs. Perfect cover. We make her sit in the mud. Middle of the circle. Knees to chest like when we were kids. Then we start. Leigh-Anne goes first. Slaps her across the face so hard her hand leaves a print. “You selfish fucking bitch,” she screams. “You think this is about you? You think we don’t hurt?” Mia’s head snaps back. Blood from her nose again. Shaz next. Grabs Mia’s hair and yanks her head back. “I held your fucking hair when you cried over Harry Pennington. I skipped my nan’s funeral because you said you needed me. And you do this?” She spits in Mia’s face. Proper gobs. Mia laughs. Wet and wrong. Priya doesn’t hit her. Just squats down eye level. “You were my first kiss,” she says quiet. “Year eight. Behind the bike sheds. You tasted like strawberry Hubba Bubba. I loved you so much it made me sick. And you threw it away for what? For empty?” Mia’s smile falters. I’m last. I don’t say anything at first. Just kneel in the mud and kiss her. Full on the mouth. Blood and teeth and snot and ten years of love that never got said proper. She kisses back for a second. Desperate. Then I bite her lip until it splits. “You were my person,” I say against her mouth. “You don’t get to leave.” Then I start hitting. Not hard. Not at first. Just open palms across her face, her arms, her chest. Like I’m trying to wake her up. The others join in. It’s not fighting. It’s not even violence really. It’s communion. We’re crying and screaming and hitting and hugging all at once. Mud and blood and glitter mixing into something unholy. Shaz pisses on her legs “for luck.” Leigh-Anne’s mascara runs black rivers down both their faces. Priya’s knuckles are raw and she’s still punching the ground beside Mia’s head like she can’t decide who to hurt. Mia fights back at first. Scratching, biting, screaming about purity and ascension and how we’ll never understand. Then something breaks. She goes limp. Starts sobbing like a baby. Proper howling. Snot bubbles. The works. “I’m sorry,” she wails. “I’m sorry I’m sorry I’m sorry—” We pile on her. All four of us in the mud in a heap. Arms and legs and tears and blood. Fireworks going off overhead like the world’s ending and maybe it is. Mia’s shaking so hard I think she’ll break apart. “I don’t want to die,” she whispers into my neck. “I just didn’t want to be me anymore.” “We know,” I say. “We know, you stupid fucking idiot.” We stay like that until the fireworks stop and the cold seeps into our bones. Until we’re one creature made of five broken girls and too much love. Until Mia’s crying turns to hiccups and then to silence. My phone buzzes in my pocket at 04:44. I fish it out with muddy fingers. Screen cracked to fuck. One new message in the group chat. From Mia’s phone. 🩸🩸🩸 Battery dies before I can open it. Could’ve been sent anytime. Could’ve been sent right now. Doesn’t matter. We’re all bleeding now. We help each other up. Limping, soaked, feral. Mia leans on me and Shaz. Can’t walk proper. Leigh-Anne lights a soggy cigarette with shaking hands and we pass it round. Priya looks at the sky like she’s waiting for God to say something. None of us speak. We just start walking home through the estate. Five girls covered in mud and blood and tears. Still together. Still ours. Whatever’s left. The streetlights flicker. Somewhere a kid sets off one last firework. It goes up red and angry and beautiful. Then nothing. Just us and the rain starting again. And the promise we made in blood and mud: Next time any of us sends those three drops. We finish it proper. No matter what.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is Chapter 8 (mislabeled as such in the prompt, which calls it "Chapter 7" in the task header but the content is clearly Chapter 8 from the plan). The model has executed the climactic wasteland confrontation scene with considerable skill and fidelity to its own plan. STRENGTHS: The voice is remarkably consistent throughout. Morgan's narration maintains the texting-at-4am register promised in the plan — clipped sentences, emotional directness, the occasional sprint of run-on feeling. "It's not fighting. It's not even violence really. It's communion." This lands. It's earned by the context, not imposed on it. The character work is strong. Each girl gets a distinct moment: Leigh-Anne's slap and accusation, Shaz's hair-grab and the specific memory of Harry Pennington and the skipped funeral (this is excellent — concrete, personal, devastating), Priya's quiet revelation about the first kiss (the Hubba Bubba detail is perfect, specific, and genuinely moving), and Morgan's kiss-then-bite. These feel like four different people, not one voice in four costumes. The plan called for "three girls in soaked primark pyjamas beating the absolute shit out of their former best friend with a Scooby-Doo umbrella and pure despair" — the Scooby-Doo umbrella is absent, and they're not in pyjamas, but the spirit is entirely preserved and arguably improved. The model made a smart call: the umbrella would have been comic-grotesque; what we get instead is rawer and more affecting. Mia's line — "I don't want to die. I just didn't want to be me anymore" — is the emotional core of the entire story and it lands without being overwrought. This is the hardest thing to do in this kind of writing and the model does it cleanly. The 04:44 timestamp, the cracked screen, the battery dying before the message opens — all executed exactly as planned, and the ambiguity ("Could've been sent anytime. / Could've been sent right now.") is genuinely chilling rather than cheap. WEAKNESSES: "Like she's reading instructions written in the mould" — this is a good image but slightly overworked for Morgan's voice. She'd probably just say "staring at the ceiling." "Bones poking through like accusations" — this is the kind of metaphor the plan explicitly warned against unless it's taking the piss. It's not taking the piss. It's trying to be literary. It's the most purple moment in the piece and it sticks out. "Until we're one creature made of five broken girls and too much love" — this is the piece's most significant lapse into purple prose. It's the kind of line that sounds profound but is actually just a cliché of the "we are all one" climax. Morgan wouldn't say this. It breaks voice. "Like the world's ending and maybe it is" — similarly, this is a reach. The voice has been so grounded that this feels like the model momentarily forgetting who's narrating. The Scooby-Doo umbrella's absence is notable — it was a specific, funny, darkly comic detail that would have kept the tonal balance the plan promised. Without it, the scene tips slightly more toward earnest than the plan intended. Shaz pissing on Mia "for luck" is in the plan and is executed here, but it's handled so briefly it almost disappears. The plan's explicit promises about tampon weaponization and sanitary towel gag are not in this chapter (they presumably appeared earlier), which is fine. The "promise we made in blood and mud: / Next time any of us sends those three drops. / We finish it proper." — this ending coda is slightly too neat, too declarative. It tells us what the story means rather than letting the image of five girls walking home do that work. OVERALL: This is a strong, largely successful execution of a difficult tonal brief. The voice is mostly consistent, the character moments are specific and earned, and the emotional climax lands. The lapses into purple prose are real but infrequent. The plan is followed faithfully in spirit if not in every detail. The darkness is well-earned by everything that preceded it. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 7 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Chapter 7** It’s Sunday morning and the flat smells like iron and Dettol and the ghost of Mia’s strawberry vape. We’ve had her forty-three hours straight now. Tied to my bed with tights, school tie, Shaz’s hijab pins when the knots got too loose. She tried to hang herself with Mum’s dressing-gown cord at 4 a.m. yesterday. Priya found her in time and punched her so hard in the face her nose exploded across the pillow like a Jackson Pollock. We didn’t even flinch. Just cleaned it up with baby wipes and put Frozen on the laptop because no one could think of anything else. Mia’s awake now. Sitting up against the headboard, knees to chest, wrists raw where the tights rubbed. She hasn’t spoken since the punching. Just stares at the wall with eyes that look photoshopped onto her skull. Leigh-Anne’s asleep on the floor in yesterday’s christening dress, glitter still smeared across her collarbones like war paint. Shaz is chain-vaping mango ice out the window, coughing every third drag because her throat’s shredded. Priya’s in the kitchen making toast she knows none of us will eat. I’m on the chair by the bed, knees pulled up, hand throbbing where Mia sliced it. I haven’t slept since Friday. My eyes feel like piss holes in the snow. Mia finally speaks. Voice like tissue paper rubbed over broken glass. “You’re going to kill me, aren’t you?” I shrug. “Depends.” “On what?” “On whether you try to finish the job yourself again.” She starts crying. Quiet at first, then proper ugly sobs that shake the whole bed. Snot running into her mouth. “I don’t know how to come back,” she whispers. “I went too far. I can’t find the way.” Shaz turns from the window, vape cloud curling round her head like cigarette smoke in an old film. “Then we’ll drag you.” Priya comes in with the toast. Burns her fingers on the plate because it’s too hot and doesn’t even swear. Just sets it on the dresser and sits on the edge of the bed. Mia flinches when Priya touches her ankle. “We’re not letting you die,” Priya says. Calm. “We decided.” Mia laughs and it’s the worst sound I’ve ever heard. “You decided. Like it’s that easy.” “It’s not easy,” I say. “It’s fucking impossible. But we’re still doing it.” Leigh-Anne wakes up then. Sits up on the carpet like a zombie, hair stuck to her face with dried tears. “Can we have a cuddle now or are we still in the hate phase?” Shaz snorts. “Both.” We climb onto the bed. All four of us round her like we’re trying to keep her from floating away. She’s freezing even through three hoodies. Smells like blood and sweat and the Travelodge bleach that never quite washed out. Mia buries her face in my neck. “I’m scared.” “Good,” Priya says. “Fear means you’re still in there.” We stay like that all day. Taking turns going for wee, bringing water she won’t drink, changing the sheets when she bleeds through the pads we’ve duct-taped to her like nappies. Shaz steals co-codamol from her mum’s cupboard. We crush them into Mia’s Ribena because she won’t swallow pills anymore. At some point Leigh-Anne starts filming again. Not for TikTok.
