Leaves voicemails in that flat, expensive voice. “Mr. Rowan, tick-tock.” “Mr. Rowan, I’m growing impatient.” “Mr. Rowan, do you need a reminder of what happens to men who disappoint me?” I delete them all. Wednesday I tail Julian to his studio, a warehouse off Sandy with a busted roll-up door. Inside it smells like cat piss, turpentine, and the ghost of every man who’s ever cried in there. Canvases lean against the walls like drunks. One of them is half-finished—my police jacket, the one that disappeared from evidence after my 2001 solicitation bust, painted huge on raw canvas. My name is still on the collar in faded Sharpie. Julian’s turned the tear in the elbow into a mouth. He’s working in jeans and nothing else, paint smeared across his chest like war paint. When he sees me he doesn’t stop brushing. Just smiles that used-up smile. “Took you long enough,” he says. I set the camera bag on a crate. “Thought I’d bring you the proofs in person.” He wipes his hands on a rag that used to be a T-shirt. “Show me.” I unzip the bag, pull out the contact sheets. He flips through them slow, expression never changing. Councilman, bartender, some guy I don’t even recognize in the back of a Lyft. When he gets to the ones from the studio—him on his knees for me, paint on his back, my hand in his hair—he lingers. “These are good,” he says finally. “You’ve got an eye.” “Burn them,” I say. My voice sounds like someone else’s. He looks up. “You’d do that?” “I get paid either way.” He studies me for a long minute, then tosses the sheets onto a table covered in thinner and brushes. Steps close. Smells like linseed oil and sex we haven’t had yet. “You’re shaking,” he says. “Shut up.” He kisses me instead. Soft at first, then not. I back him into the nearest canvas, a half-finished fake Diebenkorn that probably costs more than my life. Paint smears across his shoulder blades. I drop to my knees, mouth on him through denim until he’s cursing in Spanish and English and something that isn’t either. We end up on the drop cloth, jeans around ankles, frantic. He rides me facing away so he can watch us in the cracked mirror propped against the wall. When he comes he bites his own wrist to stay quiet and I follow right after, saying his name like a prayer I don’t believe in. After, he lights two cigarettes, hands me one. We lie there naked on a ten-thousand-dollar forgery and watch the smoke curl. “You could just not turn them in,” he says to the ceiling. I take a drag. “Yeah.” “That easy?” “No,” I say. “But I’m thinking about it.” He turns his head, looks at me with those green eyes that have seen every room I’m afraid to go into. “You’re not what I expected,” he says. “Same.” He reaches over, stubs his cigarette out on the canvas. Leaves a perfect brown circle right over the signature. “Evelyn’s gonna cut my hands off when she finds out I fucked the help,” he says, almost cheerful. “Let her try.” He laughs, soft. “Look at you, playing hero.” “Not a hero,” I say. “Just a man who finally found something he wants more than money.” He goes quiet. Then: “Don’t say shit like that unless you mean it.” I roll on top of him again. Kiss the track marks, the burn scar, the place where his heart is beating too fast. “I mean it,” I say against his skin. He wraps his legs around me like he’s trying to keep me from floating away. Outside, the rain keeps falling. Inside, two idiots keep pretending tomorrow doesn’t exist.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 4 is a strong piece of noir-inflected queer romance that largely delivers on its plan and the broader project's ambitions. Let me work through it carefully. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Calder's voice is consistent and well-maintained throughout. The flat, tired narration ("I delete them all," "Yeah," "No") feels earned rather than performed. The detail of touching the Folgers tin instead of reaching for coffee is the kind of small, precise observation that builds character without announcing itself. Julian's physicality—"jeans and nothing else, paint smeared across his chest like war paint"—is rendered efficiently. The line "He rides me facing away so he can watch us in the cracked mirror" is both sexually specific and characterologically revealing: Julian's narcissism, his need to witness himself, his distance even in intimacy. These are consistent with the profiles. The Evelyn voicemails are economical and effective. Three lines, three escalating threats, all deleted. This is good compression. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is mostly strong. "You've got an eye" is a nice double-meaning. "Don't say shit like that unless you mean it" lands. The exchange "You could just not turn them in" / "Yeah" / "That easy?" / "No" is excellent—it does a lot of work in very few words. The weakness is the final exchange: "I mean it" followed by "He wraps his legs around me like he's trying to keep me from floating away" tips slightly toward the sentimental. The line "Just a man who finally found something he wants more than money" is the chapter's weakest moment—it's a little too clean, too declarative, too much like a line from a lesser noir. It tells us what we should be feeling rather than letting the scene generate it. PLOT AND PLAN ADHERENCE: The chapter follows its plan closely. The studio, the half-finished canvas, the contact sheets, the sex on the drop cloth, Julian's "Burn them," Calder's "I get paid either way"—all present. The plan specified "They both know he's lying" and the chapter delivers this through action rather than statement, which is the right call. The police jacket detail (from Chapter 5 in the plan) appears here instead, which is a slight deviation—the plan had this discovery in Chapter 5—but it works better here dramatically, giving Julian's knowledge of Calder's past a concrete, visual form. SEXUAL CONTENT: The sex scene is handled with the right register—urgent, specific, not elegant. "Mouth on him through denim until he's cursing in Spanish and English and something that isn't either" is good. The detail of Julian biting his own wrist to stay quiet is excellent—it connects to his self-harm history without being heavy-handed. METAPHOR AND PROSE QUALITY: The prose is mostly clean. "Canvases lean against the walls like drunks" is a solid simile that fits the world. "A prayer I don't believe in" is slightly worn but functional. "Two idiots keep pretending tomorrow doesn't exist" as a closing line is weaker than the chapter deserves—it's a little too knowing, too winking, and echoes the Chapter 3 ending's structure ("two men who ruined each other") in a way that starts to feel like a tic. The plan's chapter endings tend toward this kind of summarizing couplet, and it's the project's most consistent weakness. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT: The chapter is genuinely engaging. The tension between Calder's professional obligation and his desire is rendered through action and restraint rather than internal monologue, which is the right choice. The moment where he drops to his knees is earned by the preceding tension. The post-coital conversation has real weight. CREATIVITY: The police jacket turned into a canvas with Calder's name on the collar is a genuinely inventive detail—it makes Julian's foreknowledge concrete and gives the betrayal theme a visual anchor. The "tear in the elbow into a mouth" is a strong image. WEAKNESSES: The closing two lines are too neat. "Just a man who finally found something he wants more than money" is the kind of line a character in a movie says, not a man like Calder. The chapter's emotional arc resolves slightly too cleanly for a story that promises irresolution. The plan said "They both know he's lying"—the chapter gestures at this but doesn't quite land the ambiguity with full force. Overall this is accomplished, disciplined work that mostly earns its darkness and its desire. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 5 – Teniente Hasn’t Eaten Since Thursday Teniente is sitting on the desk staring at the empty bowl like it personally betrayed him. I’ve been home exactly forty-three minutes in the last four days and every one of those minutes was spent sleeping in my clothes with Julian’s smell still on me. The cat’s food dish is a crime scene of dust and judgment. I open a can of tuna, the cheap stuff in oil. He attacks it like I’ve been starving him for months instead of five days. I watch him and feel something close to parenthood. My phone finally dies while I’m trying to delete another voicemail from Evelyn. The last one I heard was just breathing and the click of a lighter. Message received. I’m pouring yesterday’s coffee into a mug that says WORLD’S OKAYEST DAD (gift from my ex-wife, naturally) when the door opens without a knock. Julian steps in wearing a paint-spattered hoodie and the kind of tired that lives behind the eyes. He smells like cold air and the clove cigarettes he switches to when he’s nervous. In his left hand is a paper bag from the Vietnamese place on 39th. In his right is a canvas wrapped in brown paper. He sets the food on the desk, pushes Teniente’s bowl aside without asking. The cat hisses once and decides the tuna is more important. “Figured you weren’t eating,” Julian says. He doesn’t look at me yet. “I eat.” “You eat like a raccoon in a dumpster. There’s a difference.” He unwraps the canvas. It’s the police jacket painting, the one from the studio. Except now the tear in the elbow has been sewn up with thick red thread that looks like a scar or a scream. “I finished it,” he says. “Thought you should have it.” I take it. The paint is still tacky in places. My name on the collar is outlined in gold leaf now, like a medal or a target. “Evelyn knows,” he says quietly. “She had someone follow me to the studio. Saw us.” I set the canvas against the wall. My hands are steady until they aren’t. “She called?” “She sent a picture.” He pulls out his phone, shows me. It’s grainy, taken from across the street: me on my knees in the studio doorway, Julian’s hands in my hair. Timestamped yesterday. My stomach flips. “She wants to meet tonight,” he says. “Alone. Says if I don’t show she starts with my fingers and works her way up.” I open the drawer, take out the .38 I haven’t carried since I left the force. Check the cylinder out of habit. Six rounds, all old but willing. Julian watches me. “You’re not coming,” he says. “Watch me.” “No.” He steps close, puts his hand over the cylinder so I can’t close it. “This is mine to finish.” “Bullshit.” “Look—” That word again. “I married her. I took her money. I let her think she owned me. You don’t get to bleed for my mistakes.” I slam the cylinder shut. The sound makes Teniente jump. “You think I’m doing this for you?” I say. “I’m doing it because I’m too fucking old to start over again without you.” He laughs, cracked and wet. “You’re an idiot.” “Yeah,” I say. “But I’m your idiot.” He kisses me hard, tasting like cloves and desperation. When he pulls back his eyes are shining. “I have to go alone,” he says. “But I’m coming back. Afterward we disappear. You, me, the cat if you want. Anywhere but here.” I want to argue. Instead I set the gun on the table between us like an offering. “Take it,” I say. He looks at it a long time. Then shakes his head. “I’m done letting other people pull triggers for me.” He reaches past the gun, cups my face, kisses me softer this time. His thumbs wipe away wet I didn’t know was there. “Feed your cat,” he says against my mouth. “I’ll be back before sunrise.” He leaves the painting and the food and takes nothing but the clothes he’s wearing. I stand there until the pho is cold and the rain is just static on the window. Then I pick up the gun again, open the cylinder, and dump the rounds into my palm. They’re warm from sitting in the drawer. Teniente jumps onto the desk, knocks the empty bowl to the floor. It doesn’t break. I sit down heavy. The painting watches me like it knows something I don’t. I wait. The clock ticks like a heartbeat that forgot how to stop.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 5 is a strong entry in this planned noir romance, and it largely delivers on the promise of the writing plan and character profiles. Let me assess it carefully across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter opens with Teniente as a moral witness—the cat's empty bowl as "a crime scene of dust and judgment" is a genuinely good line that earns its metaphorical weight without straining. The detail of the WORLD'S OKAYEST DAD mug is exactly the kind of lived-in specificity the plan called for, and it lands without announcement. Calder's voice is consistent throughout. The flatness, the self-awareness without self-pity, the way he processes emotion through action (checking the cylinder "out of habit") all feel true to the character profile. "Too fucking old to start over again without you" is the kind of confession that works precisely because it's framed as selfishness rather than romance—that's sophisticated emotional writing. Julian's entrance with Vietnamese food and the finished canvas is well-staged. The detail of the tear in the jacket sewn with "thick red thread that looks like a scar or a scream" is one of the chapter's best moments—it's doing real symbolic work without being heavy-handed. The dialogue is generally strong. "You eat like a raccoon in a dumpster" is perfectly Julian. The "I'm your idiot" exchange risks sentimentality but is saved by Julian's cracked laugh and the immediate pivot to tension. The "Look—" verbal tic from the character profile is deployed correctly. WEAKNESSES: The chapter plan specified: "Calder pulls his gun, sets it on the kitchen table between them, and they stare at it for a long time. They end up fucking on the table instead. The gun stays where it is." The chapter deviates significantly here—there's no sex scene, and the emotional resolution is quite different. Julian announces he's going to meet Evelyn alone, which isn't in the plan at all. The plan had Evelyn threatening Calder, and Julian revealing the forged police jacket canvas (which does appear, but differently). The chapter invents a new plot development (Evelyn's surveillance photo, Julian going to meet her alone) that isn't in the plan and somewhat softens the confrontational energy the plan promised. The ending is the chapter's weakest section. "The clock ticks like a heartbeat that forgot how to stop" is exactly the kind of line the writing plan explicitly warned against—it's reaching for profundity and landing on cliché. "The painting watches me like it knows something I don't" is similarly weak, a tired personification. These closing lines undercut what was otherwise disciplined prose. "His thumbs wipe away wet I didn't know was there"—Calder crying is a significant character moment (the profile notes he "hasn't cried since 2003 and is stupidly proud of it"), but it's handled too quickly and without the weight it deserves. The plan's instruction to avoid redemption arcs is slightly violated by the tenderness of this ending. The Evelyn threat ("starts with my fingers and works her way up") feels slightly melodramatic and underwritten—we're told about it rather than feeling its weight. The chapter runs shorter in emotional complexity than the plan's ambition suggested. The plan's version had the gun on the table as a standoff that becomes sex—a much more charged, morally complex scene. What we get instead is more conventionally romantic, which works against the stated intention of "no redemption." OVERALL: A competent, often excellent chapter that stumbles at its ending and deviates from its own plan in ways that reduce rather than enhance the story's moral complexity. The voice is consistent, the details are sharp, but the final beats reach for poetry and find cliché instead. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Characters Consistent with Profile: 14 Followed Chapter Plan: 9 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 6 – The Recording I Never Play Back He comes back at 4:03 a.m., soaked to the bone, smelling like the river. I’m still sitting at the desk with the gun in pieces in front of me, cleaning it for the third time because my hands won’t stay still. Teniente is asleep on the police-jacket canvas like it’s the warmest thing in the room. Julian closes the door soft, leans against it. No lights on except the streetlamp bleeding orange through the blinds. His hoodie is gone. Shirt torn at the collar. Lip split. A bruise blooming under his left eye like a rotten peach. I stand up so fast the chair rolls back and hits the wall. “I’m okay,” he says before I can speak. Voice hoarse. “She just talked.” I cross the room, touch the bruise with the pad of my thumb. He flinches, then leans into it. “Talked with her fists?” I ask. “With pictures. With lawyers. With the video she’s had since the first night at the motel.” He laughs, wet. “Turns out the clerk owes her money. Whole place is wired.” My stomach drops through the floor. “She played it for me,” he says. “You coming on my back like a fucking firehose. Sound up. My face when I—” He stops, swallows. “She asked if I thought you’d still want me when the whole city saw what a whore I am.” I pull him against me hard enough to hurt. He buries his face in my neck and shakes. “I told her I was leaving,” he whispers. “Told her I’d give her the paintings, the accounts, everything. Just let me walk.” “And?” “She laughed. Said I could walk, but not clean. Said she’d make sure every gallery from here to Miami knew my hands were dirty. Said she’d make sure you lost your license, your gun permit, maybe your freedom. Said planting evidence on a child killer was a cute story until someone looked close.” I feel the rage start low in my spine, the same red hum I felt the night I slid that knife into evidence. Julian pulls back, cups my face. His palms are ice. “Look,” he says, and I hate that word now. “There’s one way out. One.” He reaches into his pocket, pulls out a flash drive on a piece of string like a necklace. “Everything,” he says. “Offshore accounts, the forgeries she’s been laundering money through, the councilman, the judge, her sister’s overdose she paid to keep quiet. All of it. I’ve been copying it for months.” I stare at the drive. “You leak this, she goes down for twenty years,” he says. “We disappear. Mexico, Portugal, wherever. Just us.” I take the drive. It’s warm from his body. “That easy?” I ask. “No,” he says. “But easier than watching her cut pieces off both of us slow.” I slip the string over my neck. The drive settles against my chest like a bullet I asked for. He kisses me, tasting like blood and rain and the last chance we’re ever gonna get. We end up on the floor because the desk is covered in gun parts and cold pho. Clothes half-off, desperate. He rides me slow, eyes open, watching my face like he’s trying to memorize it before someone takes it away. When he comes he bites my shoulder so hard I taste copper. I follow right after, saying his name like a confession. After, we lie tangled on the rug that smells like cat and old bourbon. He traces the dentures with his tongue when I kiss him, like he likes the click. “I knew who you were the first night,” he says into the dark. “At the bar. Evelyn showed me your file. Told me to seduce you if you got too close. I was supposed to feed you fake affairs, keep you distracted.” I go still. “I couldn’t do it,” he says. “Not after you looked at me like I was something worth keeping.” I roll us so I’m on top. Pin his wrists above his head. “You played me.” “Yeah,” he says. “And you played yourself harder. We’re even.” I kiss him until we’re both hard again. Fuck him a second time against the desk, the flash drive swinging between us like a pendulum. Later, he falls asleep with his head on my thigh. I sit there in the half-dark and take out my phone. Old flip phone, still has a recorder app from 2008. I hit record. “Tell me again,” I whisper. He stirs, doesn’t open his eyes. “Tell you what?” “Tell me you knew. Tell me the plan. Tell me you used me.” He smiles, sleepy, cruel, beautiful. “I knew, Calder. I used you. I’d do it again. Come with me anyway.” I let it run ten more seconds. Then I stop it. I sit there a long time staring at the file: JULIAN_0403.mp3 I highlight it. My thumb hovers. I press delete. Are you sure? the screen asks. Yes. File gone. I pull him up into my lap, wrap my arms around him like I can keep the world out. He nuzzles my neck, already half-dreaming. Tomorrow I’ll call in sick to the rest of my life. Tonight I just hold him and listen to the rain try to wash the city clean and fail.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is doing a great deal of work and doing most of it well. Let me examine it carefully. VOICE AND CHARACTER CONSISTENCY Calder's voice is maintained with discipline. The flat, tired narration ("My stomach drops through the floor," "the same red hum I felt the night I slid that knife into evidence") stays true to the profile. The dentures detail appearing during the intimate scene ("He traces the dentures with his tongue when I kiss him, like he likes the click") is a genuinely unexpected and humanizing touch—it's the kind of specific, unglamorous detail that separates real characterization from pastiche. Julian's dialogue is consistent with his profile: the "Look—" tic appears, the self-aware cruelty ("I used you. I'd do it again. Come with me anyway") lands with the right weight. Evelyn, though absent, is rendered effectively through Julian's account of her. The detail about the motel being wired, the clerk owing her money, the video—this is the kind of cold, methodical cruelty the profile promises. "She asked if I thought you'd still want me when the whole city saw what a whore I am" is a strong line that reveals Evelyn's particular brand of violence. FOLLOWING THE CHAPTER PLAN The plan called for: Julian admitting he knew who Calder was from the first night, saying he wanted the divorce to hurt Evelyn, offering Calder nothing but "come with me anyway," Calder recording it then deleting it, and uncertainty about whether he's still that kind of cop. The chapter delivers all of this, but adds the flash drive subplot and the physical confrontation backstory (Evelyn showing Julian the video) which enriches rather than contradicts the plan. The recording/deletion sequence is executed exactly as planned and is the chapter's strongest moment—the phone asking "Are you sure?" and Calder pressing "Yes" is economical and effective. DIALOGUE The dialogue is largely strong. "You played me." / "Yeah. And you played yourself harder. We're even." is excellent—it's the kind of line that earns its cleverness because it's true and both characters know it. "Come with me anyway" lands as promised. The weaker moment is Julian's exposition dump about Evelyn's crimes ("Offshore accounts, the forgeries she's been laundering money through, the councilman, the judge, her sister's overdose she paid to keep quiet") which is functional but feels like plot delivery rather than character speech. It's the chapter's most mechanical passage. METAPHOR AND PROSE QUALITY The prose largely avoids the "rain like tears" bullshit the plan explicitly forbids. "A bruise blooming under his left eye like a rotten peach" is the most overtly poetic line and it works—it's specific, slightly ugly, not romanticized. "The flash drive swinging between us like a pendulum" is a bit on-the-nose symbolically but not egregiously so. "Tomorrow I'll call in sick to the rest of my life" is the kind of line that could tip into purple but is saved by its wry self-awareness. The ending—"listen to the rain try to wash the city clean and fail"—is the weakest moment, a noir cliché that the plan explicitly warned against. It's not terrible, but it's the one place where the prose reaches for atmosphere and grabs something generic instead. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT AND EARNED DARKNESS The chapter earns its darkness. The sex scenes are handled with the unglamorous urgency the plan demands—"desperate," "half-off," "bites my shoulder so hard I taste copper"—without becoming pornographic or romanticized. The emotional core—Calder deleting the recording—is genuinely moving because it's shown through action rather than interiority. We don't need Calder to explain what deleting that file means; the "Are you sure? / Yes." exchange does it all. The revelation that Julian knew from the start is handled well. It could have been a melodramatic betrayal scene, but instead it's absorbed into intimacy, which is more disturbing and more true to these characters. COHERENCE AND PLOT The flash drive as an exit strategy is a solid plot addition. The chapter advances the story meaningfully while deepening both characters. The Evelyn threat (the video, the evidence about planted knife) raises stakes credibly. One slight issue: the chapter plan says Julian "offers Calder nothing, no future, no money"—but the flash drive and the "Mexico, Portugal" plan is actually offering quite a lot. This is a deviation from the plan, though arguably a more interesting one. WORD COUNT DISCIPLINE The chapter appears to be within the 950-1050 word target, consistent with the plan's stated discipline. OVERALL ASSESSMENT This is accomplished genre fiction with genuine literary ambition. The voice is consistent, the characters are lived-in, the darkness is earned, and the structural choice to have Calder delete the recording rather than keep it is the right one. The weaknesses are minor: one clichéd ending line, one expository dialogue passage, and a slight deviation from the "offers nothing" plan element. These are small costs for a chapter that otherwise delivers exactly what it promises. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17
