# Chapter 4 – Storm Mouth The sky split open right as the sun died. One second the air was thick and gold with dust; the next it was black and the first fat drop hit Kellan’s shoulder like a thrown stone. Then the whole world fell out of the clouds. Rain so hard it stung, wind that clawed the canvas off its hoops and flung it flapping into the dark. Widowmaker screamed and tried to bolt; Kellan hauled him to a stop with one brutal yank on the reins, wrapped them twice around the brake, and jumped down into mud already ankle-deep. Lightning strobed. In the white flash he saw Lilira in the cage, soaked red skin shining like fresh blood, horns throwing long shadows, mouth open to the rain like she was drinking the storm. Thunder cracked right overhead. The horse reared. Kellan swore and fought the reins until his arms shook. No shelter, no trees thick enough, just open road and the wagon. He looped the reins to the wheel, slapped Widowmaker’s rump to send him running for whatever cover he could find, and turned back. Lilira was laughing. Head thrown back, rain pouring off her horns, chains rattling like bells. She looked like the end of the world made flesh. Kellan climbed into the wagon bed soaked to the bone, boots sliding on wet wood. The canvas flailed overhead, half-torn, useless. He grabbed her by the waist and hauled her out of the cage into the open air because there was nowhere else to go. Rain hammered them both, cold enough to burn after the day’s heat. She wrapped legs and tail around him instantly, mouth crashing into his. Teeth first, then tongue. She tasted like lightning and come and his own spend from earlier. He bit her lip until he tasted blood and she moaned into him, grinding her cunt along his stomach. He dropped her in the mud. She landed on her back with a splash, chains clinking, legs already spreading. Rain filled her open mouth; she swallowed it and laughed again. Kellan fell on her like punishment. His cock was out before he remembered moving, shoved free of wet leather, aching and purple from the day’s torment. He drove into her in one thrust. No slow, no mercy. Just the slick, brutal heat of her cunt swallowing him whole. She screamed into the rain, back arching off the ground, tail whipping around his hips to drag him deeper. Mud sucked at his knees. Water ran off his back in sheets. He fucked her like he hated her. She fucked him back like she’d invented hate and wanted to show him the advanced lessons. Every thrust punched a sound out of her—raw, animal, over the thunder. He felt her come around him almost immediately, walls clamping so hard his vision tunneled. He kept going, harder, punishing, chasing his own ruin. Lightning again. In the flash he saw her face: eyes rolled white, mouth open, rain pouring in and running out the corners like she couldn’t drink fast enough. She looked holy and obscene and he was going to die inside her and it still wouldn’t be enough. He flipped her over, face-down in the mud, ass up. Grabbed the chain between her manacles and used it like reins. Slammed back in. She howled, pushed back, met him thrust for thrust until the sound of wet flesh on flesh was louder than the storm. Her tail found his throat—tight, tighter—until spots danced in his eyes and his balls drew up hard. He came with her name ripped out of him, a sound he’d never made before, half-sob, half-prayer. Pumping into her, endless, hips jerking, vision gone black at the edges. She kept moving, milking him through it, cunt fluttering around his spent cock like she could wring another one out of him just by refusing to let go. He collapsed on top of her, face in her wet hair, breathing her in—rain, mud, sex, brimstone. His arms shook. His legs wouldn’t hold him. Lilira turned her head sideways in the mud, smiled with lips swollen and bleeding. “Good boy,” she croaked, voice wrecked. His wrists were caught. He felt it before he understood—cold iron closing around them, click, click. She’d threaded the manacles through the wagon wheel spokes while he was still inside her, still coming, still blind. Locked him to the wagon like a dog while his cock was still pulsing in her cunt. He yanked once, weak. The chain held. Rain hammered down on his back. Lilira rolled under him, slow, until they were chest to chest in the mud. She licked a stripe up his cheek—rain and tears and snot, all of it. “Shh,” she whispered, tail curling gentle around his bound wrists. “I’ve got you now.” Thunder rolled over them like laughter. Kellan buried his face in her neck and shook so hard his teeth chattered. The storm kept fucking them long after they were done.

Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 4 executes its plan with considerable skill and discipline. The writing is tight, mean, and purposeful—exactly what the plan called for. Let me assess it across the relevant dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter follows its plan faithfully. The storm arrives, forces them together under the canvas (though the author makes the interesting choice to have them fuck in the mud rather than under canvas—a deviation that actually improves the scene by adding texture and degradation). The "first full fuck—brutal, fast, hateful" is delivered. She comes twice before he's fully inside (implied by the immediate orgasm). He sobs her name into her (approximated by the half-sob, half-prayer). The manacles click shut on his wrists without him noticing. All major beats hit. The prose voice is controlled and effective. "He fucked her like he hated her. She fucked him back like she'd invented hate and wanted to show him the advanced lessons." This is exactly the blunt, mean voice the plan demanded—and it lands. It's not trying to be beautiful; it's trying to be accurate, and accuracy here is its own kind of poetry. The sensory work is strong. Mud, rain, cold iron, brimstone, blood—these are grounded and specific. The storm as participant rather than backdrop is well-executed. The image of her face in the lightning flash—"eyes rolled white, mouth open, rain pouring in and running out the corners"—is genuinely striking and earns its place. The manacle reversal is the chapter's best moment. The mechanics of how she does it (threading them through the wagon wheel spokes while he's still inside her, still coming) is specific and clever. It rewards the reader's attention and makes the power shift feel earned rather than arbitrary. Lilira's "Good boy" is perfectly placed—wrecked voice, swollen lips, the casual ownership of it. It's the chapter's emotional pivot and it works. WEAKNESSES: The chapter is somewhat thin on Kellan's interiority. The plan called for tight third POV getting "progressively more fucked-up and fragmentary," but this chapter reads more like close third with action emphasis. We get glimpses—"he was going to die inside her and it still wouldn't be enough"—but the psychological unraveling is more told than shown through his thought patterns. The fragmentation the plan promises isn't really here yet. "She looked like the end of the world made flesh" is the chapter's weakest line—it's the kind of purple reach the plan explicitly warned against. It's not earned by the surrounding prose; it's a generic hyperbole that any writer might reach for. The dialogue is minimal (by design) but "I've got you now" is slightly too on-the-nose, too villain-monologue. It's the one moment where Lilira's voice slips from the feral specificity of "Good boy" into something more generic. The final line—"The storm kept fucking them long after they were done"—is a bit of a reach. The personification of the storm as sexual participant is the kind of metaphor that sounds good but doesn't quite hold up. The storm isn't fucking them; it's raining on them. It's a closing flourish that slightly undercuts the rawness of everything preceding it. The chapter is also somewhat predictable in its arc—the reader of the plan knows exactly where this is going, and the chapter doesn't find many surprises within its constraints. The mud-fucking is a good addition, but the emotional beats land exactly where expected. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Kellan is consistent—the pride, the violence, the collapse. Lilira is consistent—the laughter, the patience, the strategic cruelty of the manacle move. Both characters behave as profiled. OVERALL: This is competent-to-good dark erotica that executes its plan faithfully. The prose is disciplined, the scene is visceral and specific, and the power dynamic shift is well-staged. Its weaknesses are minor: one purple line, one slightly generic villain moment, and a closing metaphor that doesn't quite earn itself. The interiority is thinner than the plan promised but not absent. # Chapter 5 – Morning Filth Dawn came in slow, ugly streaks of gray. The rain had stopped sometime in the night, leaving the world dripping and stinking. Mud sucked at Kellan’s bare back where he lay chained to the wagon wheel, wrists raw, arms numb above his head. His leathers were gone—peeled off and flung somewhere in the dark. She squatted over his face, thighs framing his eyes, cunt swollen and glistening right above his mouth. The smell hit him like a fist: piss, sex, mud, the sour-milk stink of too many orgasms. He groaned, tried to turn away. The chain wouldn’t let him. His shoulders screamed. “Morning, pet,” she said, voice gravel and honey. “Open.” He opened before he thought. Tongue out, desperate. She lowered herself slow, let him feel every slick fold settle over his mouth, his nose. Then she ground down hard and rode his face like she was breaking a horse. He licked her clean—greedy, sloppy, choking on it. Tongue inside her, scooping out last night’s mess, his mess, swallowing it down because she hadn’t given him water and he was so fucking thirsty. She held his horns like handles, rolling her hips, smearing herself across his beard until he was painted in her. When she came it was quiet—just a long, shuddering exhale and a flood of fresh wet that he drank like wine. She stayed on him after, thighs trembling, letting him feel her pulse against his tongue. Then she rose up, turned around, and sat on his chest so she could look at him. His cock twitched against his stomach, leaking again. The head was angry red, oversensitive. He whimpered. Lilira smiled with teeth. “Poor hunter,” she crooned. “Still think you’re in charge?” She wrapped her tail around his cock—slow, deliberate. The ridges dragged over every vein. He bucked, chains rattling, a broken sound ripping out of his throat. “Stay still.” He tried. Couldn’t. Every squeeze of her tail made his hips jerk. She started stroking—lazy, cruel—base to tip, twisting at the head until precome drooled over her knuckles. Whenever he got close she stopped, waited until he sobbed, then started again. She did it for hours. The sun climbed. Heat came back twice as mean, baking the mud on their skin into cracked plates. Flies found them, crawled over his chest, his open mouth. He didn’t care. Nothing existed except her tail and the ache in his balls and the way she watched him break with calm, terrible patience. At one point he started begging—actual words, shameful, strung together like broken beads. “Please, Lilira, please let me come, I’ll do anything, I’m sorry, I’m yours, please—” She leaned down, licked the tears off his temple, and whispered, “I know.” Then she edged him again. By the time the sun was high he was speaking in tongues—half Abyssal picked up from her humming, half nonsense. His cock was purple, balls drawn up so tight they hurt. Every muscle shook. Lilira shifted lower, straddled his hips but didn’t take him inside. Just let her soaked cunt slide along the length of him, coating him fresh. He tried to thrust up; she pinned his thighs with hers and squeezed her tail tighter around the base of his cock until he screamed. “Eyes on me.” He forced them open. They wouldn’t focus. She took his face in both hands, thumbs stroking his cheekbones like he was something precious. “Come,” she said softly. And squeezed. He came untouched. Dry, wrenching spasms that bent his spine and tore his throat raw. Nothing came out but pain and devotion. His vision whited out, body seizing so hard the wagon rocked. When it ended he was crying in earnest—ugly, snotty sobs that wouldn’t stop. Lilira kissed his forehead, his eyelids, the salt tracks on his cheeks. “Thank you,” he rasped. Actually said it. Meant it. She smiled against his skin, gentle for the first time, and it broke him worse than anything else. “Good boy.” She left him chained while she pissed on the far side of the wagon—long, deliberate stream that steamed in the heat. Then she came back, straddled his chest again, and fed him water from her own mouth, slow and careful, until he stopped shaking. When she finally unlocked the manacles his arms flopped useless to the mud. He didn’t try to stand. Just rolled onto his side and curled around her legs like a dog. She scratched behind his ear absently, humming that same nursery rhyme, and let him cry himself to sleep with his face pressed to her sticky thigh and her tail curled possessively around his throat. The wagon never moved that day. Widowmaker grazed nearby, reins dragging, and never once looked back.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter executes its plan with considerable craft and discipline. The model was tasked with writing a scene where Kellan wakes chained, fucks Lilira's throat to reassert control, gets flipped and edged until he's babbling apologies, comes dry and painfully, and then thanks her. The chapter deviates from the plan in one notable way: the throat-fucking scene is entirely absent. Instead, the chapter opens with Kellan already in a submissive position, face-sitting, which skips the "reassertion of control" beat that was supposed to give the scene its arc of failed dominance. This is a meaningful structural omission—the plan called for a specific power reversal (he tries to dominate, she flips it), and without that attempt at control, the submission feels less earned. The reader doesn't get to watch him try and fail; he's already failed before the chapter begins. That said, what is written is executed with genuine skill. The prose is blunt and sensory in the way the plan demanded—"piss, sex, mud, the sour-milk stink of too many orgasms" is exactly the kind of ugly, specific detail that makes this register as real rather than pornographic fantasy. The flies crawling over his open mouth while he doesn't care is a strong image. The edging sequence is rendered with patience and cruelty that feels authentic to Lilira's character. The dialogue is sparse but effective. "Morning, pet" and "I know" and "Good boy" are all perfectly calibrated—Lilira never over-explains, never justifies, which is consistent with her profile. The "thank you" from Kellan lands because it's been built toward across the chapter. The prose occasionally reaches for poetry and mostly lands: "tears off his temple" is good, "painted in her" works, "broken beads" is slightly clichéd but functional. "Dry, wrenching spasms that bent his spine and tore his throat raw. Nothing came out but pain and devotion" is the chapter's best line—it earns its abstraction. The final image of Widowmaker grazing and never looking back is a nice, understated close. Where the chapter weakens: the edging sequence, while effective, runs slightly long and becomes repetitive in its rhythm (edge, sob, edge, sob). The "speaking in tongues—half Abyssal picked up from her humming, half nonsense" is told rather than shown—we're told he's incoherent but don't experience the incoherence in the prose itself, which the plan specifically called for (fragmentary POV). The chapter plan promised progressively more fragmented narration, and this chapter reads in clean, competent sentences throughout, which is a missed opportunity. Character consistency is strong for Lilira—she's patient, cruel, precise, and the one moment of gentleness (kissing his face, feeding him water) lands as the plan intended, breaking him "worse than anything else." Kellan's collapse feels earned within the chapter even if the structural setup was skipped. The water-feeding scene is genuinely tender and disturbing in the right way. The final image—him curled around her legs like a dog, her tail around his throat—delivers the chapter's thematic payload cleanly. Overall: competent, often strong execution of difficult material, with one significant structural deviation from the plan and some missed opportunities for formal fragmentation that the plan explicitly called for. # Chapter 6 – The Key Never Mattered The wagon wheels creaked like old bones. Kellan drove one-handed, the other wrist still raw from the manacles he no longer wore. His shirt was gone—torn to rags sometime yesterday. The sun baked the come and piss and tears cracked across his chest into salt. Every bump jolted his cock against his thigh, half-hard and aching, too ruined to get fully up again but too owned to go soft. Lilira walked beside him now. No chains. No cage. Just barefoot in the dust, red skin gleaming, tail flicking lazy behind her like a banner. She’d taken his belt at some point and wore it loose around her hips, his dagger tucked into it like a trophy. The manacle key swung between her tits on a piece of twine she’d bitten from the reins. He hadn’t asked for it back. They were two hours out from Irongate. The road had widened, ruts filled with merchant carts and pilgrims. People stared. Of course they did. Naked tiefling strolling next to a barefoot, filthy bounty hunter with bite marks on his throat and her handprints bruised into his hips. Someone spat. Someone crossed themselves. A child pointed until its mother slapped its hand down. Lilira waved at them all, queen of the fucking world. Kellan kept his eyes on the horizon where the city walls shimmered like a fever dream. Eight thousand crowns. Mara’s freedom. That had been the plan. The plan was dead. Lilira stopped walking. He pulled Widowmaker up without thinking. The horse snorted, grateful. Sweat steamed off its flanks. She turned to him, slow, smiling that small private smile that made his balls try to crawl back inside his body. “Pet,” she said softly. He was off the bench before she finished the word, knees hitting the dirt road hard enough to bruise. Dust plumed around him. A passing caravan slowed to watch. Lilira stepped close, cupped his jaw with one hand, thumb tracing his lower lip. Her tail curled around his throat—not tight, just there. A reminder. She pulled the key from between her breasts. Held it up so the sun caught the iron. “Stole this the first night,” she said conversationally. “While you were pissing against the wheel and coming all over yourself because my tail touched your leg.” Kellan’s breath hitched. “I’ve been free since then.” She leaned down, licked a stripe up his cheek, tasting dust and old tears. “Could’ve walked away any time. Killed you in your sleep. Burned the wagon. Gone.” She straightened, tilted her head. “I didn’t.” The key dangled between them, swinging like a pendulum. “Because you looked so pretty pretending you still had a choice.” A sob tore out of him—raw, ugly. He dropped his forehead to her belly, arms wrapping around her thighs, face buried in the sticky heat between her legs. She smelled like everything he’d ever wanted and everything he’d lost. People were definitely watching now. Someone muttered “demon-whore.” Someone else laughed nervously. Kellan didn’t care. He licked her through the belt, desperate, mouthing at her cunt like a starving man. She let him for a moment—ten seconds, twenty—then fisted his hair and yanked his head back. “Look at me.” He did. Eyes swollen, cheeks wet. “Say it.” The words came out broken, perfect. “I’m yours.” Lilira’s smile was soft and terrible. “Yes,” she said. “You are.” She stepped back, turned, and kept walking toward Irongate like nothing had happened. Kellan scrambled after her on hands and knees, crawling through the dust, cock dragging between his thighs, leaking again. The road scraped his palms raw. Someone threw a rock; it bounced off his shoulder. He didn’t feel it. Lilira never looked back. When he caught up she let him press his face to the back of her thigh and stay there, licking the sweat from her skin while she walked. Her tail rested across his shoulders like a yoke. A mile from the gates she stopped again. “Up,” she said. He stood on shaking legs. She took the belt from her hips—his belt—and buckled it around his throat. Tight. The dagger went into his mouth like a bit; she tucked it between his teeth point-down. “There,” she said, patting his cheek. “Much better.” Then she climbed onto the driver’s bench, took the reins, and snapped them once. Widowmaker started forward. Kellan walked beside her now, barefoot and belted, dagger in teeth, cock swinging soft and ruined between his legs. The key still hung between her breasts, catching the sun with every breath she took. People got out of their way. Irongate rose ahead of them, gates yawning open, guards already staring. Lilira leaned over and kissed his temple, gentle as a mother. “Almost home, pet.”
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 6 executes its plan with considerable skill and discipline. The writing plan called for Lilira to reveal she's had the key since the first night, for Kellan to drop to his knees in the moving wagon and lick road dust off her feet—the chapter adapts this slightly (he kneels in the road rather than the wagon, and licks her thigh rather than her feet) but captures the essential spirit faithfully. The final image of him walking belted beside the wagon is a strong variation that serves the escalating degradation arc. The prose voice is genuinely accomplished. "The sun baked the come and piss and tears cracked across his chest into salt" is visceral and specific without being purple—it earns its ugliness. "Too ruined to get fully up again but too owned to go soft" is a sharp, economical line that does real character work. The writing maintains the blunt, mean register the plan demanded. Character consistency is strong. Kellan's collapse is rendered without sentimentality—the sob is "raw, ugly," not romanticized. Lilira's revelation is delivered "conversationally," which is exactly right for her character profile: she doesn't gloat, she simply states facts with the casual cruelty of someone who has never doubted the outcome. The "queen of the fucking world" observation and her waving at onlookers is perfectly in character. The public humiliation element—the passing caravan, the spitting, the child pointing, the thrown rock—is handled well. It grounds the fantasy in a social reality that makes the degradation more potent rather than existing in a vacuum. Dialogue is sparse but effective. "Say it" / "I'm yours" / "Yes. You are." is economical and lands with appropriate weight. "Almost home, pet" as the closing line is genuinely good—it reframes Irongate from Kellan's destination (freedom, money, his sister) to Lilira's destination, completing the ownership inversion. Some weaknesses: The dagger-in-mouth-as-bit image is slightly awkward logistically (a dagger point-down between the teeth is an odd bit), and the chapter doesn't quite achieve the fragmentary, deteriorating POV the plan promised for chapter 6 ("his thoughts should read like a man jerking off with a broken hand"). The prose remains relatively coherent and controlled when it might have pushed further into dissolution. The sister Mara is mentioned briefly—"Mara's freedom. That had been the plan. The plan was dead."—which is efficient but perhaps too clean a dismissal of what was supposed to be his core motivation; a bit more anguish there might have deepened the tragedy. The public witnesses are used well as a device but the chapter doesn't quite exploit the full horror of that social exposure—it gestures at it without fully landing the knife. Overall this is confident, disciplined erotic dark fantasy writing that executes its plan faithfully, maintains character consistency, and avoids most of the pitfalls (purple prose, forced metaphor, amateurish sentiment) while delivering genuine emotional and physical intensity. # Chapter 7 – Outside the Walls, All Night They never made the gates. Fifty yards from the torchlight Lilira pulled Widowmaker to a stop in the middle of the road. Wagons edged around them, drivers cursing, but no one got too close. The horse dropped its head and grazed the trampled grass at the roadside, too tired to care. Kellan stood waiting, belt tight around his throat, dagger still between his teeth, eyes glassy. His knees were scabbed from crawling. His cock hung heavy, half-hard again, leaking slow in the cooling air. Lilira hopped down from the bench. She looked at him for a long moment—really looked—head tilted, tail flicking. Then she smiled like someone who’d just decided to keep a stray. “Down.” He dropped instantly, palms and knees in the dirt, forehead pressed to her foot. She stepped over him, straddled his back, and settled her weight. Her cunt pressed hot against his spine, wet already. “Carry me.” He crawled. Around the wagon, off the road, into the patchy grass where the torches barely reached. She rode him like that—slow, deliberate—until they were out of sight of the gates, hidden by the wagon’s shadow and the slope of the hill. When he stopped she slid off, pushed him onto his back with one bare foot on his chest. The stars were out now, cold and sharp. The city bells tolled midnight somewhere behind the walls. Lilira knelt over him, took the dagger from his mouth, and cut the last of his pride away. She fucked him everywhere. First his mouth—slow, deep strokes until he gagged and drooled and thanked her around her fingers for the privilege. Then his cock, riding him reverse so he could watch her ass bounce and her tail curl around his balls and squeeze every time he tried to thrust up. She made him lick her clean after. Held his head between her thighs while he sobbed into her cunt and swallowed himself down. Later she bent over the wagon wheel and told him to take her ass. He did—crying the whole time, hips slapping wet against her, begging her to tell him he was good. She reached back, scratched behind his ear like a dog, and said yes, yes, best boy, ruin me, pet. He came inside her there too, shaking so hard he collapsed face-first into the dirt. She wasn’t done. She pissed on him after—hot stream across his back, his hair, his open mouth when he rolled over and begged for it. He drank what he could, the rest cutting runnels through the filth on his skin. The shame burned worse than the piss. He came again, untouched, just from the taste. Hours blurred. She took him against the wagon tongue, legs over his shoulders, horns digging into his chest while she screamed at the stars. She edged him with her tail until he was babbling apologies for things he hadn’t even done yet. She let him suck her tits until they were purple with bruises and he was crying again because they weren’t enough, would never be enough. At some point she chained his wrists to the wagon wheel again—just because she could—and rode his face until he passed out, drowned in her. He woke to her sliding down his cock, slow, reverent, like she was putting him back together wrong. She did it again. And again. Until dawn was a pale bruise on the horizon and his voice was gone and his body was one raw nerve ending wrapped around her. Until he was dry-heaving between her legs, cock spent and twitching, balls aching like they’d been kicked. Until the only word left in his head was her name and even that came out broken. Lilira cradled his head in her lap then, fingers stroking his hair, sticky with everything they’d done. She kissed his swollen eyes, his split lip, the teeth marks on his shoulder. He cried like a child—quiet, hiccuping, the kind of crying that happens when the pain finally runs out of room and has to come out the eyes. She let him. When the first bird called she leaned down and kissed him—soft, almost tender. The first real kiss she’d ever given him that wasn’t about winning. He sobbed into her mouth. “I know,” she whispered against his lips. “I know, baby.” She held him until the sun came up and the city bells started ringing for morning gate-opening. Then she stood, stretched, cracked her neck, and looked down at him where he lay wrecked in the grass. “Time to go home, pet.” Kellan tried to stand and couldn’t. Legs wouldn’t hold him. She smiled, bent, and slung his arm over her shoulder. Half-carried, half-dragged him back to the wagon. He managed to climb onto the bench only because she pushed him. She took the reins again, barefoot and filthy and radiant. Widowmaker started forward without being asked. The gates were open now. Guards stared. Merchants parted. Kellan sat beside her, belt-collar soaked with everything, cock ring made from the manacle chain glinting in the sunrise. He leaned his head on her shoulder and closed his eyes. Lilira snapped the reins once, gentle. They rolled toward Irongate together, slow and inevitable as sunrise. He was still crying. She let him.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 7 is the penultimate chapter of this dark erotic fantasy, and it functions as the emotional and physical nadir before the dawn resolution. Let me assess it carefully across multiple dimensions. **Strengths:** The chapter largely follows its plan: the all-night session outside the walls, the piss scene, the crawling, the collaring, and crucially, the single moment of genuine tenderness — the first real kiss — which the plan specified as "gentle for the first and only time." This is executed with reasonable restraint. The line "the first real kiss she'd ever given him that wasn't about winning" lands with appropriate weight given what precedes it. The prose voice is consistent with the established POV intention — blunt, fragmented, accumulative. The structure of short declarative sentences mimics exhaustion and dissolution effectively: "She did it again. And again." This is a legitimate stylistic choice that earns its keep. The physical degradation escalates logically from prior chapters, and the emotional payoff — Kellan crying "like a child" — is set up adequately. The tenderness Lilira shows is genuinely surprising given her established character, and the plan notes this as intentional: she's "unsettled to discover she might want this one alive." **Weaknesses:** The chapter suffers from a significant structural problem: it is largely a catalogue. "She fucked him everywhere. First his mouth... Then his cock... Later she bent over the wagon wheel..." This is list-making dressed as prose. The plan called for POV that reads "like a man jerking off with a broken hand" — fragmentary, yes, but also *felt*. What we get instead is a fairly clinical inventory of sex acts. The interiority is thin. We're told he's crying, begging, sobbing, but we rarely inhabit the specific texture of his psychological collapse. The dialogue is minimal, which is appropriate, but what exists is functional rather than distinctive. "Down." "Carry me." "Time to go home, pet." These are competent but not memorable. The plan's sample voice for Lilira — "Shh, pet. Breathe through your mouth. You'll taste me better that way" — has a specific, tasting quality that the actual dialogue doesn't match. The piss scene is handled with less craft than the plan implies — it's described efficiently but without the psychological specificity that would make it devastating rather than merely transgressive. "The shame burned worse than the piss. He came again, untouched, just from the taste." This is the chapter's best line — economical, psychologically true, earns its darkness. The ending softens considerably from the plan's vision. The plan specified she "kisses him through it, gentle for the first and only time, and he cries like a child" — this is executed, but then the chapter continues into a somewhat conventional tender denouement: she half-carries him, he leans on her shoulder, they roll toward Irongate together. This reads more like a romance conclusion than the brutal, unsentimental piece the plan promised. The writing plan explicitly stated "no tenderness, no redemption, no moral, no bow" — yet this chapter ends with considerable tenderness and something approaching a bow. The metaphors are generally restrained and don't embarrass themselves. "Dawn was a pale bruise on the horizon" is competent if not original. "Radiant" applied to Lilira in her filthy state is a nice inversion. The chapter is coherent and consistent with character profiles. Lilira's single moment of genuine affection is consistent with her profile's note that she's "unsettled to discover she might want this one alive." Kellan's complete dissolution is consistent with his arc. The "hours blurred" section is the weakest — it's pure tell-don't-show, a montage summary that skips over what should be the most intense material. This is a significant craft failure in a chapter that should be maximally immersive. Overall: competent execution of a difficult brief, with real moments of effectiveness, but undermined by catalogue structure, insufficient interiority, and an ending that contradicts the stated aesthetic of the project. Model Output:
# Chapter 8 – Irongate Dawn The city bells rang in the new day like hammers on an anvil. Kellan heard them through a haze of exhaustion and her scent in his nose. The wagon rolled slow now—Widowmaker had nothing left to give. Morning light slanted gold across the walls, turning the stone warm and almost kind. Almost. Lilira drove barefoot, thighs sticky, his shirt hanging open on her shoulders and doing nothing to hide the bruises he’d left on her tits. The belt-collar around Kellan’s throat was dark with sweat and come. The manacle chain wrapped his cock and balls twice, tight enough that every heartbeat throbbed purple. He sat slumped against her side, head on her shoulder, eyes half-open and glassy. People stared as they passed beneath the gatehouse. Guards in Irongate green, merchants with their mouths open, whores leaning out of upper windows, street kids already running alongside shouting questions no one answered. The bounty poster with Lilira’s face was still nailed to the wall—red skin, horns, gold eyes, eight thousand crowns in bold letters. Someone had drawn a cock shooting across her mouth in charcoal. Lilira reached up lazy, ripped the poster down as they rolled under the arch. Paper tore loud in the sudden quiet. She looked at it, smiled, then wiped her cunt with it—slow, deliberate, soaking the ink until it ran. Folded the wet sheet once and stuffed it between Kellan’s teeth like a gag. He moaned around it, soft, grateful. Drool and her wetness ran down his chin instantly. The guards didn’t stop them. One made a half-hearted move with a halberd, saw Kellan’s state—naked, collared, eyes rolled back, cock ring biting cruel—and thought better. Another crossed himself. A third looked like he wanted to ask questions and decided he didn’t want the answers. They rolled through the gates unchallenged. The smell of the city hit like a slap—bread baking, piss in alleys, forge smoke, bodies too close together. Home. Kellan started shaking harder. Tears again, silent, running off his jaw onto Lilira’s thigh. She stroked his hair absently, guiding Widowmaker deeper into the Smelt Ward. Cobbles rattled under the wheels. People parted. Someone threw a rotten cabbage; it splattered across Kellan’s chest and he didn’t flinch. They stopped outside the Velvet Row. The pleasure house where Mara worked—red lanterns already lit even at morning, girls in doorways blinking at the light. Music drifted out, tinny and tired. Kellan tried to stand when he saw it. Legs wouldn’t hold. He half-fell off the bench, landed on his knees in the street, chains clinking. Looked up at the sign with the painted roses and started sobbing like his heart had finally broken all the way. Lilira climbed down graceful, barefoot on the cobbles, and stood over him. “Shh,” she said, cupping his face. “Look.” The madam was already in the doorway—fat, painted, eyes sharp. She took one look at Lilira, one look at Kellan on his knees with the bounty poster gag soaked through, and her mouth fell open. Lilira smiled, sweet as poison. “I believe you have something that belongs to my pet,” she said, voice carrying clear down the street. “His sister. Mara. We’re here to buy her out.” The madam found her voice. “Eight thousand crowns,” she croaked. “That’s the price. You got it?” Lilira reached into the wagon, pulled out Kellan’s coin purse—heavy, jingling. The one that had been meant for her bounty. She upended it into the dirt. Gold spilled everywhere, rolling between cobblestones. “Keep the change,” she said. Then she hauled Kellan to his feet by the collar, dragged him stumbling into the pleasure house like a dog on a lead. Mara was in the parlor, thin and pale, eyes too old for her face. She saw Kellan—naked, wrecked, tears cutting clean tracks through the filth on his cheeks—and screamed his name once, broken. He tried to go to her. Couldn’t walk. Dropped again, crawling across the floor, chains dragging, poster still in his mouth. Reached her feet and pressed his face to her ankles, sobbing harder. Mara knelt, shaking, and wrapped arms around him like she used to when they were kids hiding from their mother’s pimp. Lilira watched them, tail flicking, something soft and terrifying in her eyes. Then she turned to the madam. “Pack her things. She’s leaving.” The madam opened her mouth—closed it. Looked at the gold in the street, at the tiefling with horns and hell in her smile, at the bounty hunter who used to be the scariest bastard in the ward now licking his sister’s bare feet and crying like a baby. She went to pack. Later—minutes, hours, Kellan didn’t know—Lilira led them both out into the sunlight. Mara clinging to her brother’s arm, staring at the collar, the chain, the ruin of him. Not asking questions yet. Lilira took the reins again. Widowmaker turned them toward the city’s heart without being told. Kellan walked barefoot beside the wagon now, one hand on Mara’s, one hand holding Lilira’s tail like a leash. The city bells kept ringing. They rolled deeper into Irongate, three of them now, and the gates never closed behind them because they were already inside. Kellan looked up once—saw the bounty poster still clenched in his teeth, ink running down his chin like blood—and came one final time, untouched, just from the taste of her on paper and the sound of the city swallowing him whole. Lilira leaned down, kissed the top of his head, and whispered against his hair: “Welcome home, pet.” The wagon kept moving. He followed.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 8 is the culmination of a deliberately transgressive erotic fantasy, and it largely delivers on its promises while introducing some elements that weren't in the plan—most notably the Mara subplot, which the chapter plan mentioned only obliquely ("buy his sister out of the pleasure house") but which here becomes the emotional centerpiece of the chapter's middle section. Let's start with what works. The prose voice is consistent with the established register: blunt, sensory, economical. "Guards in Irongate green, merchants with their mouths open, whores leaning out of upper windows" is efficient scene-setting that doesn't overstay its welcome. The bounty poster moment—Lilira wiping herself with it and stuffing it in Kellan's mouth—is exactly the kind of degradation theater the story has been building toward, and it lands with appropriate grotesque flair. The image is earned, not gratuitous in the cheap sense; it's the logical culmination of the power dynamic established across seven chapters. The Mara scene is the chapter's most interesting deviation from the plan. The plan called for Kellan crawling out on all fours with Lilira riding his back like a horse, his belt between her teeth like reins—a pure spectacle of public humiliation. Instead, the chapter pivots to something more emotionally complex: Kellan's original motivation (freeing his sister) is fulfilled, but through Lilira's agency, using the bounty money that was meant for her capture. This is actually a more sophisticated resolution than the plan's pure spectacle—it shows Lilira choosing to honor what matters to Kellan while still maintaining total dominance. The line "something soft and terrifying in her eyes" is the story's one moment of genuine emotional ambiguity, and it works. However, the Mara scene also introduces some tonal problems. Kellan crawling to his sister's feet and licking her ankles while sobbing is meant to be simultaneously pathetic and tender, but it reads as slightly absurd in a way that undercuts both the erotic charge and the emotional sincerity. The sister's reaction—"not asking questions yet"—is a smart deflection, but the scene asks us to hold too many registers at once (degradation, sibling love, redemption) without fully committing to any of them. The final orgasm—"came one final time, untouched, just from the taste of her on paper and the sound of the city swallowing him whole"—is the planned closing image, and it mostly works, though "the city swallowing him whole" is a slightly forced metaphor that reaches for poetry it hasn't quite earned. The final line, "Welcome home, pet," is clean and effective. Character consistency is strong. Lilira remains in control, never explains herself, acts with casual cruelty and unexpected generosity in the same breath. Kellan is appropriately wrecked. The guards' reaction (one crossing himself, one deciding he doesn't want answers) is a nice touch of world-grounding. Weaknesses: The dialogue is sparse but functional—Lilira's "Keep the change" is good, her address to the madam is appropriately imperious. The madam's "Eight thousand crowns, that's the price" is flat but serviceable. The chapter doesn't quite achieve the "fragmentary" POV promised in the writing plan for late chapters—it's still relatively coherent third-person, which is a minor disappointment given the ambition of the plan. The rotten cabbage detail ("Someone threw a rotten cabbage; it splattered across Kellan's chest and he didn't flinch") is a genuinely good touch—specific, humiliating, grounding. The chapter deviates significantly from the planned final image (Lilira riding Kellan like a horse past the guards) in favor of something more emotionally resonant but less viscerally spectacular. Whether this is an improvement is debatable; the planned image was more purely committed to the story's stated ethos of "no redemption, no bow," while the actual chapter introduces something closer to a bittersweet resolution with Mara's freedom. The writing plan explicitly said "no bow"—and the Mara resolution is, functionally, a bow. Overall: competent, often effective, occasionally genuinely striking, but slightly softened from its stated intentions and with one tonal miscalculation in the Mara crawling scene. Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is an ambitious piece of fantasy erotica that commits fully to its premise: a bounty hunter slowly broken by his captive tiefling over the course of a journey to Irongate. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The voice is consistent and confident throughout. The prose has a distinctive register — terse, punchy, sensory-heavy — that suits the material. Sentences like "The wagon stank. Horse, old blood, wet rope, and her. Mostly her." establish an immediate, grounded tone that the piece largely maintains. The author understands rhythm and uses sentence fragments effectively to create urgency and texture. The central dynamic is established quickly and maintained with reasonable consistency. Lilira is dominant, manipulative, and intelligent; Kellan is stubborn but crumbling. This power inversion is the engine of the story and it runs throughout all eight chapters without losing its basic logic. The world-building is light but functional — Irongate, the Velvet Row, the bounty system, cold iron runes — enough to ground the fantasy setting without overwhelming the intimate focus. The reveal that Lilira had the key since the first night is a genuinely good structural choice that recontextualizes everything preceding it. The ending with Mara is an interesting tonal shift that attempts to give the story emotional stakes beyond pure erotica, and it partially succeeds. WEAKNESSES: The piece suffers from significant escalation fatigue. By Chapter 4, the sexual content has become repetitive in its mechanics, and Chapters 5-7 blur together into a catalog of acts rather than a developing narrative. The emotional beats — Kellan crying, Kellan submitting, Lilira saying "good boy" — repeat so frequently they lose impact. "Good boy" appears at least five times and functions as a structural crutch rather than a meaningful refrain. The metaphors are frequently strained or clichéd. "Velvet dragged over broken glass," "soft as sin," "gentle as a mother" — these reach for poetry but land in stock phrases. The piece overuses weather and heat as emotional mirrors in ways that feel mechanical rather than organic. "The storm kept fucking them long after they were done" is the kind of line that sounds impressive but collapses under scrutiny. Kellan's transformation is too rapid and too complete. By Chapter 2 he's already essentially broken; the remaining six chapters are variations on the same note. There's no genuine resistance, no moment where he nearly escapes or reasserts himself in a meaningful way. The arc is a straight line downward rather than a struggle with reversals. The dialogue, while often sharp, occasionally tips into self-parody. Lilira's lines are consistently on-the-nose about her own power ("I've been free since then... Could've walked away any time"). Real manipulators don't typically announce their methodology this explicitly. It makes her feel like a fantasy construct rather than a character. The purple prose problem is real but inconsistent — some passages are genuinely effective, others are overwrought. "She looked holy and obscene and he was going to die inside her and it still wouldn't be enough" is the kind of sentence that tries to do too much. The Mara subplot, while structurally clever (the bounty money used to free the sister), is introduced too late and resolved too quickly to carry real emotional weight. It feels appended rather than integrated. The piece is faithful to the prompt and delivers on its genre promises, but it mistakes escalation for development and repetition for intensity. + Cozy-Horror Post Apocalyptic Survival — Mushroom Season (Chapter Avg: 75.6 | Final: 65.1)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Cozy-Horror Post Apocalypse Survival Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: When you're out of food, you get desperate. Who would have thought the spore mutants -- the things trying to kill us -- would be delicious?
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Cozy-Horror Post Apocalypse Survival Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: When you&#x27;re out of food, you get desperate. Who would have thought the spore mutants -- the things trying to kill us -- would be delicious? -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming Core concept to explore in many directions: starvation forces survivors to eat the spore-infected mutants that have been hunting them, and against all logic and revulsion, the meat is transcendentally delicious. Possible flavors of the story: 1. Pure cozy-horror culinary diary A lone scavenger keeps a beautifully written journal of recipes. Each chapter is a new dish (mutant shank braised in rainwater, spore-cap risotto, crispy tendon crackers) interwoven with tender memories of the lost world and quiet terror outside the bunker door. The horror is atmospheric—never seeing the mutants clearly, only hearing them scratch, smelling their pheromonal musk through the vents—while the cooking scenes are lush, sensual, almost erotic in their detail. 2. Found-family black comedy Four mismatched survivors in an abandoned bed-and-breakfast turn mutant harvesting into a nightly ritual. One is a former Michelin-star chef who is having the time of his life, one is a teenage biology nerd trying to prove the spores are harmless, one is a religious zealot who believes eating the “devils” is communion, and one is just trying to keep everyone alive. Think The Bear meets The Last of Us. 3. Slow psychological descent of a single mother She’s keeping her infected daughter alive by feeding her mutant meat that somehow halts the progression. Every chapter she becomes more feral-gourmet: starts with ashamed quick boils, ends seasoning reductions with her own tears while singing lullabies. The cozy part is the domestic routine she clings to—setting the table, cloth napkins, candlelight—while the horror is that she’s slowly turning into what she eats. 4. Reverse infection love story A survivor falls in love with a sentient, still-verbal mutant who begs to be eaten piece by piece so the human can live. Consensual cannibalism as the ultimate act of devotion. Very tender, very disturbing, very horny in a body-horror way. 5. Capitalism survives the apocalypse Someone figures out how to farm the mutants, brands the meat “Morel Prime,” starts a post-apocalyptic Michelin guide. Chapters follow the opening night of the first pop-up restaurant inside a deconsecrated subway car while rival factions try to burn it down for blasphemy. 6. Epistolary via radio broadcasts A lone DJ in a fortified tower broadcasts nightly cooking segments: “This is Radio Cordyceps with your host Chef Boyardead, coming at you with tonight’s recipe: Pan-seared Clicker Thigh with fermented cordyceps beurre blanc…” Listeners send in tips, love letters, suicide notes, marriage proposals. 7. Children’s-book-that-definitely-isn’t-for-children Written in the style of a cozy picture book (“Mara and the Friendly Bloomers”) but every illustration description gets progressively more graphic. The mutants are drawn like cute Studio Ghibli soot sprites until you read the text and realize they’re flaying someone. 8. Seasonal structure Eight chapters = eight “courses” of one long, perfect final meal that takes an entire year to prepare as the protagonist waits for the perfect mutant specimen to mature. Winter: foraging spores for salt. Spring: courting a blooming Stalker. Summer: slow smoking over scavenged applewood. Autumn: the dinner party where everyone eats and then politely begins eating each other. Tone possibilities: - Warm candlelit domesticity vs. the wet clicking outside - ASMR-level food writing against body horror - Gallows humor so dark it loops back around to wholesome - Sensual, borderline food-porn prose that gradually becomes literal porn as the spore pheromones rewrite the brain’s reward system - Quiet British repression—everyone terribly polite while chewing on former neighbors Pacing ideas across 8 chapters: Option A: Classic three-act inside 8 chapters (2 / 4 / 2) Option B: One chapter per recipe, escalating complexity and horror Option C: Same two characters, eight sequential nights of one continuous argument about whether they should eat “people” again tonight Option D: Countdown—8 days of food left, each chapter another day, final chapter they run out and the decision is made # Reflection The prompt demands three things: post-apocalypse survival, genuine desperation, and the batshit revelation that the monsters are the best thing these people have ever tasted. The cozy-horror blend only works if both sides are equally sincere—if the comfort is real, the horror lands harder; if the horror is real, the comfort feels obscene and therefore delicious. The ideas that excite me most are the ones where the deliciousness itself becomes the horror, not just a punchline. Not “haha gross we’re eating monsters,” but “oh god we moan when we chew and we hate ourselves for it.” The cozy elements have to be weaponized—grandma’s cast-iron skillet, the soft yellow light of a propane lantern, the comfort of routine—because those are the things that make the cannibalism feel intimate instead of barbaric. The single-mother version and the found-family black comedy feel richest for 8 chapters. The mother gives me a tight emotional through-line and terrifying stakes (her child), while the found-family gives me multiple voices and the chance to show the cuisine evolving as a group obsession. I could even fuse them: a surrogate family forms around the mother and child in the bed-and-breakfast, with the chef, the zealot, the teenager, all protecting/feedstock-gathering for the little girl who must eat mutant flesh to live. The seasonal “eight courses” structure is gorgeous but probably too slow for 8k total words. Same with full epistolary. The “eight nights of the same argument” could be brilliant restraint—same setting, same table, just the dishes and the damage getting worse. Whatever I choose, the food writing has to be genuinely, unforgivably mouth-watering. If the reader doesn’t feel a shameful craving by chapter 3, the whole thing collapses. The horror works best when it’s quiet and domestic: the gentle pop of tendons contracting in the pan, the way the mycelium threads glisten like fat, the moment someone realizes they’re licking the plate and crying at the same time. I think I’m circling the found-family-in-a-bed-and-breakfast with the sick child at the center, eight chapters as eight consecutive nights of dinner, each dish more elaborate, each mutant more “advanced,” until the final chapter where they serve something that is still moving. That gives me character conflict, escalating body horror, and the cozy ritual of setting the table every night while the world ends outside the lace curtains.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This will be a tight, intimate, eight-night chamber piece set entirely inside a once-charming Victorian bed-and-breakfast called “The Briar Rose,” now a candlelit fortress in a spore-overgrown world. Five survivors have barricaded themselves inside after finding a little girl wandering alone, half-transformed but still lucid. The spores in her lungs will finish the job unless she eats fresh mutant flesh every day. They have chosen to keep her human, whatever the cost. Each night they hunt, butcher, cook, and eat together at the same long walnut table, pretending they are still civilized people having dinner. Tone: Warm-coffee-and-blood cozy-horror. Think Shirley Jackson meets The Great British Bake-Off filmed by Ari Aster. Every chapter is built around one exquisite, progressively more blasphemous dish. The food writing will be lush, sensual, unashamedly delicious; the horror will be quiet, domestic, and emotional: the soft scrape of forks, the way candlelight flickers on a cheek that has begun to sprout white gills, someone whispering grace while carving something that still blinks. Voice: Third-person limited, rotating focal character each chapter so we taste the same meal through five different psyches. The prose will be rich but restrained, almost Victorian in its politeness, which makes the grotesque details feel even more obscene. Core promise to the reader: by the final chapter you will be starving, disgusted with yourself for it, and desperate to know what they serve on the eighth night. Things I will aim for: - Genuine hunger in the reader - No jump scares, only creeping wrongness - The monsters are never seen clearly; only pieces of them arrive on the plate - Every character believes they are the one still holding on to humanity - The little girl, Mira, never speaks after chapter 1; her silence becomes louder than screaming Things I will avoid: - Action set-pieces or chases - Explaining the science of the spores - Moral lectures - Anyone suddenly going “cannibal crazy” in a cartoonish way; the descent is polite, incremental, heartbreaking Length per chapter: 950–1050 words exactly, giving us ~8000 words total. # Chapter Planning Chapter 1 – “The First Supper” (focal: Eleanor, 48, former innkeeper) They bring in the first small Bloater, shamefully easy to kill. Simple pan-roast with rosemary scavenged from the window boxes and the last of the butter. They all cry while eating. The meat is sweet like veal and pork belly had a baby in heaven. Mira eats two plates and sleeps without coughing for the first time in weeks. Eleanor sets five places at the table though there are only five of them, as if someone else is coming. Chapter 2 – “Consommé of the Quiet One” (focal: Jun, 19, biology dropout) Jun makes a crystal-clear consommé from the cartilage and gill-fronds of a Stalker. It tastes like the memory of miso and ocean. They drink it from teacups because bowls feel too honest. Someone laughs too loudly. Mira traces her finger through the condensation on the window and draws a smiley face with too many teeth. Chapter 3 – “The Wellington” (focal: Father Colm, 61, defrocked priest) They trap a Clicker whose skull plate has split into perfect morel-like caps. Colm insists on Wellington—sealing the loin in puff pastry made from the last bag of flour. It is the best thing any of them have ever tasted. Colm leads grace and thanks “the generous martyr” on the platter. After dinner he is found in the pantry kissing the leftover pastry crust. Chapter 4 – “Bone Marrow Tartare with Spore Caviar” (focal: Silas, 34, former line cook at a 3-Michelin restaurant) Silas finally gets to play. He serves the raw marrow beaten with rendered fat and topped with glistening spore eggs that pop like salmon roe but taste like browned butter and lightning. They eat it on toasted sourdough started from the 20-year-old starter he carried out of the city in a ammo tin. Someone moans out loud. No one pretends it was accidental. Chapter 5 – “Milk-Braised Child” (focal: Mira, the only chapter in her silent POV) They harvest a juvenile mutant no bigger than Mira herself, still soft and pink beneath the mycelium. Silas braises it in condensed milk and vanilla pods scavenged from a ruined Starbucks. The flesh falls apart like the most tender osso buco. Mira eats first, eyes closed in perfect happiness. The others watch her and realize they are no longer sure which child they are saving. Chapter 6 – “The Crown Roast” (focal: Eleanor again) They take down a Shambler Queen—ribcage splayed open like a crown, fruiting bodies blooming along every bone. Sixteen ribs, perfectly frenched. Silas stuffs the cavity with forcemeat made from previous harvests. They eat until they are sick, then eat more. Colm proposes marriage to Jun between bites. Jun accepts. Eleanor sets a sixth place at the table tonight. Chapter 7 – “Consommé No. 2 – Clarified with Regret” (focal: Jun) The pantry is nearly empty. They use every leftover bone, every scrap of skin, every tear they have cried. The consommé comes out even clearer than the first time. They drink it in silence and realize they are no longer hungry for anything else in the world. Chapter 8 – “The Briar Rose Soufflé” (focal: all of them at once, third-person omniscient for the only time) On the eighth night they serve the dish they have been unconsciously perfecting for months: a towering soufflé made from the egg-like spore sacs of the creature that has been singing beneath the floorboards all winter. It rises golden and perfect. When they cut into it, the entire dining room fills with warm, sweet air that smells like childhood birthdays. They eat until the soufflé is gone and keep eating long after there is nothing left on the table but five empty chairs and one very small dress folded neatly on a plate. Outside, the garden is suddenly, shockingly quiet.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# Critique: How a real human would approach this piece vs. how LLMs usually betray themselves A human who actually loves this prompt would not be trying to “deliver a product.” They would be half-disgusted, half-turned-on, and writing it in a fever because the idea is stuck in their teeth like gristle they can’t stop worrying. They’d be writing it for themselves first and the reader second, the same way people write about their worst kink or their most shameful memory. That raw, private charge is the only thing that can make the cozy-horror fusion actually work. LLMs, even the good ones, almost always do one or more of the following, and every single one of them would neuter this piece: 1. They aestheticize instead of appetize LLMs describe food the way interior designers describe throw pillows: “sumptuous,” “velvety,” “decadent,” “mouth-watering” (always the same adjectives). A human who is genuinely hungry and genuinely horrified writes about food like it’s sex or violence: specific, wet, embarrassing, greedy. They name the exact moment the fat hits the pan and the smell punches you in the amygdala. They let the reader catch them drooling. 2. They keep emotional stakes tasteful LLMs are terrified of letting anyone be truly pathetic, truly petty, truly in love with something disgusting. They’ll let a character weep elegantly. A human would let someone shove an entire rib into their mouth with both hands while sobbing because it tastes like their dead mother’s birthday dinner. 3. They resolve or punishe instead of leaving the wound open Most models feel compelled to end with either “and then they all turned into monsters—see, there are consequences!” or “and actually eating the mutants saved humanity, isn’t that ironically beautiful?” Both are bows. A human would end mid-bite, or with someone licking the plate in the dark while whispering “thank you” to something that isn’t there anymore. 4. They symmetrize and balance everything The current plan has gorgeous symmetry: 8 nights, rotating POVs, escalating dishes, perfect soufflé climax. That’s LLM catnip. A human would let the structure wobble. Maybe chapter 4 is only 400 words because that’s the night someone can’t speak at all. Maybe chapter 6 is 1800 words because that’s the night everything falls apart sexually and violently at the table and no one wants to stop eating even while they’re crying and fucking and confessing. 5. They are allergic to cringe Real humans let their characters be cringe, desperate, ridiculous, horny, gluttonous, pious, childish, all at once. LLMs sand those edges off because cringe reads as “bad writing” to them. 6. They over-explain the menu The current plan still sounds a little like a restaurant tasting menu with horror sprinkles. A human wouldn’t give the dish a clever name every time. Sometimes it’s just “the leg.” Sometimes someone just says “pass the fucking thigh, I’m still hungry” and that’s the most frightening line in the chapter. # Deep dive on the current plan—where it’s already showing LLM DNA - The rotating POV is clever, but it smells like the model making sure every character gets their “arc moment.” Humans don’t give equal airtime; someone would dominate three chapters and then disappear because they can’t look anyone in the eye anymore. - The dishes are too perfectly themed to the mutant type (Clicker morels → Wellington → genius!). That’s the model showing off how neatly it can match imagery. A human would sometimes just serve badly burnt chops because that’s all they managed to drag back that night, and the burnt flavor becomes erotic in its failure. - Every chapter ends on a poised, poetic image (smiley face with too many teeth, kissing the pastry, empty chairs). That’s the model’s safety valve: always leave them with a chill that feels artistic. A human would sometimes end mid-sentence because someone just puked on the tablecloth and nobody knows what to say. - The little girl’s silence is a gorgeous idea, but the plan treats it like a symbol. A human would let her do one utterly mundane, horrifying thing—like methodically eat the garnish off every plate before touching the meat, every single night—until the others start doing it too without realizing. - The sex is still chaste. “Someone moans out loud. No one pretends it was accidental.” That’s the model being risqué while staying safe. A human would let two characters fuck on the counter covered in marrow and blood while a third watches and keeps stirring the sauce because it’s about to break and that somehow feels like the most intimate thing happening. - The final soufflé is too beautiful, too symbolic, too cinematic. A real human might just have them open the oven and find that the creature has already hatched and is sitting on the table wearing Mira’s dress, politely waiting to be eaten, and nobody screams because they’re too hungry. # Brainstorming ways to make it genuinely human - Let the prose get ugly. Food writing can be pornographically good without being pretty. Use short sentences when someone is chewing. Let periods splatter like fat in a hot pan. - Let characters lie to themselves out loud in real time. Not elegant denial, but pathetic, obvious, rambling lies: “This isn’t even technically cannibalism because they stopped being people when the fruiting bodies came in, right? Right?” - Let someone develop a favorite cut of meat the way people have a favorite child. Let them get possessive and jealous when someone else gets the best piece. - Let the table manners collapse unevenly. One person still uses a napkin, one eats with their hands, one starts licking the plate on night 2 and never stops, one begins cutting the meat into perfect cubes long after everyone else has given up pretense. - Let there be nights when they can’t find anything and they just sit in silence staring at the empty plates, and that chapter is still 900 words of excruciating, mundane hunger. - Let someone keep a secret stash of “people” meat (an actual human corpse they’ve been hiding) because they’re afraid the mutants will run out, and the shame of that is worse than everything else. - Let the horniness be small and embarrassing: someone getting hard watching another person suck marrow out of a bone and hating themselves for it. Someone masturbating in the pantry with a spoonful of cold fat because it’s the only privacy they get. - Let the religious one start praying to the food itself, not to God. Let him call the roast “my sweet lamb” while carving and mean it literally. - Let one character decide they don’t want to eat anymore and try to starve themselves honorably, only to break on night 6 and eat more than anyone else while screaming. - Let the last line of the entire story be someone saying, utterly sincerely, “That was the best meal of my life,” and no one disagrees. That’s the difference. An LLM wants to impress you. A human wants to sit at that table and never, ever leave.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This will be a raw, wet-mouthed, 8000-word confession written by someone who is still chewing. Setting stays the same: The Briar Rose Bed-and-Breakfast, eight consecutive nights, same long table, same five survivors and the silent child. No exposition dumps, no world-building paragraphs, no explanations about spores or society. We only know what happens inside this dining room and the kitchen behind the swing door. Voice: single focal character for the entire piece—Eleanor, the innkeeper who has not left the building in two years. Everything is filtered through her exhausted, starving, increasingly shameless senses. She is writing the story after the fact on the back of old guest-registration cards because paper ran out months ago. Sometimes she forgets which night she’s describing. Sometimes she writes in grease. Stylistic choices: - Prose that gets fatter as the nights go on—sentences thicken with fat and spit. - Food described like it is fucking her mouth, not like it’s on a menu. - No clever dish titles. Just “the thigh,” “the soft one,” “what was left of Colm.” - Hunger is the only plot. Everything else is consequence. - No tidy arcs. People break unevenly, suddenly, or not at all. - Sex, prayer, crying, pissing yourself at the table—all allowed if it happens while someone is still eating. - The horror is that they are happy. The horror is that they are polite. The horror is that they mean it when they say thank you. Things I will aim for: - The reader must finish every chapter hungry and ashamed of being hungry. - At least one moment per chapter where Eleanor does something so small and pathetic it hurts (smelling a plate someone already licked clean, hiding a piece of gristle in her apron to suck on later, falling asleep with her cheek on the table in cold grease). - The monsters never get a proper name. They are “the soft ones,” “the singing ones,” “the ones that still scream when you cut the tongue out.” - Mira never speaks again after night one, but she starts humming on night five—only when she’s chewing. Things I will avoid like poison: - Any sense that this is clever, balanced, or fair. - Any moment where someone “finally sees the truth” or “makes a noble sacrifice.” - Any line that could appear on a restaurant chalkboard. - Any ending that punishes or redeems them. They just keep eating until there is nothing left to eat, including the story. # Chapter Planning (8 nights, 950–1050 words each) Chapter 1 – Night they decide The first kill they drag in is small and still dripping. They boil it in the big lobster pot because nobody can bear to touch it long enough to do anything fancy. The meat is so sweet Eleanor vomits and then immediately scoops the pieces out of the sink and eats them anyway. Mira eats three bowls and falls asleep under the table with broth in her hair. Everyone cries except Silas, who is already planning tomorrow. Chapter 2 – Night the fat renders Silas renders the fat slowly in the cast-iron skillet that belonged to Eleanor’s mother. The whole house fills with the smell of Sunday roast. They eat the crackling first, burning their fingers and mouths. Jun starts laughing and can’t stop. Colm prays over the grease jar like it’s holy chrism. Eleanor hides half a cup in her bra to keep it warm against her skin all night. Chapter 3 – Night they run out of salt Nothing but meat and water. They sear thick steaks anyway. The meat tastes like nothing and everything. Someone starts licking the wooden cutting board. Eleanor realises she is aroused and hates how little it surprises her. Mira falls asleep with her thumb in her mouth and a strip of meat still hanging out like a tongue. Chapter 4 – Night of the soft one They bring in something pale and child-sized that doesn’t fight. Silas roasts it whole in the oven like a suckling pig. The skin blisters gold. When they pull it out the belly has split and the insides are already cooked to custard. They eat with spoons straight from the roasting pan. Colm comes in his cassock. Nobody mentions it. Chapter 5 – Night the singing starts For the first time the meat is still warm when they sit down—Silas cut the throat in the backyard five minutes ago. Something under the floorboards answers Mira’s humming in perfect harmony. They eat faster to drown it out. Eleanor realises she is chewing louder than anyone, greedy animal noises she can’t stop. Chapter 6 – Night they eat Colm Colm doesn’t come back from the garden. They find what’s left at dawn. Silas butchers him in silence while Jun holds the flashlight. That night they eat him rare, almost raw, because cooking feels dishonest now. The meat tastes like guilt and Sunday school and the best pork they ever had. Eleanor cries into her plate and keeps eating through the tears. Chapter 7 – Night there is almost nothing left Only scraps, cartilage, the soft parts behind the eyes. Silas makes a ragout that takes six hours of stirring. They eat it cold out of the pot at 3 a.m. because waiting for morning feels stupid. Mira climbs into Eleanor’s lap and falls asleep sucking on a rib like a pacifier. Eleanor realises she is no longer sure where her own body ends and the meat begins. Chapter 8 – Night of the last supper They open the cellar door and something comes up the stairs willingly, wearing Mira’s yellow dress. It sits at the head of the table and waits. Silas sharpens the good knife. The thing opens its chest like a flower. Inside is the most perfect cut they have ever seen—pink, marbled, still beating. They eat it raw, passing pieces hand to mouth, moaning like animals, like lovers, like children getting everything they ever wanted. When it is gone they lick each other’s fingers clean. Outside, the garden finally stops singing. Eleanor writes the last guest card with a fingertip dipped in grease: We were so hungry. We are not hungry anymore. Thank you.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Eleanor Whitmore (48, innkeeper, narrator) Background: Third-generation owner of The Briar Rose. Married once, briefly, to a man who left the day the spores hit the news. Never had children; the guests were her kids. Used to embroider tiny roses on every pillowcase and leave homemade shortbread on the nightstands. Still folds napkins into swans out of muscle memory even when they’re crusted with blood. Personality & flaws: Pathologically unable to let anyone feel uncomfortable in her house. Will apologise to the meat. Terrified of silence, fills it with hostess chatter that gets loopier every night. Hoards useless beautiful things—china cups with no handles, a silver cake server—because beauty feels like proof they’re still people. Has started smelling her own armpits to remember what humans used to smell like. Physicality: Tall but stooped now, always leaning forward as if listening for the next complaint. Grey-blonde hair twisted up with knitting needles. Hands that never stop moving—smoothing tablecloths, tucking strands of hair behind ears, wiping mouths that aren’t dirty. Sleeps in her mother’s floral housecoat with burn holes in the hem. Smells like tallow and lavender water. Motive: If the Briar Rose dies, she dies. These people are her final guests and she will serve them perfectly or not at all. Voice samples: “It’s only polite to use the good plates while we still can.” (shovelling scraps into her mouth with trembling fingers) “Mind the bones, darling, they’re sharp—oh god, I’m sorry, I’m so sorry it’s delicious.” # Silas Bouchard (34, ex-line cook) Background: Was sous-chef at Auroral, the three-Michelin place that burned down the first week of the fall. Walked out of the city with his knife roll, a sourdough starter in an ammo tin, and a bottle of 1982 Sauternes he never opened “for civilization.” Has cooked for billionaires and still thinks instant ramen is a valid life choice. Personality & flaws: Runs on pure competence kink. Gets hard when a sauce comes together. Zero empathy until someone cries in front of him, then he panics and feeds them. Secretly terrified he peaked at 29 and everything since has been gravy (literally). Hums French rap when nervous. Physicality: Six-four, all elbows and cheekbones, arms covered in faded burn scars and one tiny tattoo that says “oui chef” in Comic Sans because he lost a bet. Moves like he’s still in a brigade—shoulders squared, eyes scanning plates before people. Hasn’t cut his black hair in two years; it’s in a greasy topknot held with twine. Motive: This is the menu he was born to cook. These are the diners who finally, truly appreciate him. Voice samples: “Temperature’s everything. Respect the product or get the fuck out of my kitchen—sorry, old habit.” (quiet, almost shy, after someone moans) “You liked that? Good. That’s… good.” # Jun Park (19, biology dropout) Background: Was meant to start Stanford pre-med the fall everything ended. Spent lockdown cataloguing spore mutations on TikTok until the cell towers died. Still calls the mutants “specimens” because it feels cleaner. Has read every paper on Cordyceps unilateralis ever written and still jerks off to tentacle hentai—cognitive dissonance is his brand. Personality & flaws: Manic science gremlin. Talks in run-on sentences when excited. Thinks feelings are data points. Secretly convinced he’s immune because he’s never been infected despite licking half the specimens.
