Bites his nails until they bleed, then uses the blood as bookmark. Physicality: Short, k-pop hair grown out into a mullet he trims with kitchen shears. Always wearing the same cracked iPhone armband like he’s still going for a run. Eyes too wide, pupils permanently blown from hunger and spores. Motive: Wants to understand so badly he’ll eat the evidence. Voice samples: “Okay but the fruiting bodies are literally homologous to truffle mycelium so technically this is gourmet—” (shaking, whispering) “If I map the protein cascade maybe I can reverse it, maybe I can still fix her, fix all of us—” # Father Colm O’Rourke (61, ex-priest) Background: Defrocked ten years ago for “inappropriate closeness” with teenage boys. Spent the apocalypse hiding in his church until the mutants ate the choir. Still wears the collar because it’s the last shirt with buttons. Personality & flaws: Gentle, lyrical drunk voice even when sober. Believes he deserves whatever happens next. Has started seeing the Eucharist in every slice of meat. Touches people too long on the shoulder when he blesses them. Secretly keeps a tally of sins on the pantry wall in charcoal. Physicality: Stooped, soft, hands that tremble unless they’re holding a knife or a rosary. Face like a bloodhound—jowls, red eyes, kind. Smells like incense and sour milk. Motive: To be consumed. That’s always been the fantasy. Voice samples: “Take, eat; this is my body—Christ, it’s tender, isn’t it?” (weeping, mouth full) “Forgive me, I’m not sorry, I’m not sorry at all.” # Mira (7, the child) Background: Found wandering the garden three months ago wrapped in a yellow raincoat two sizes too big. Said her name once in a voice like wind chimes and never again. The spores have threaded white under the skin of her throat and wrists like lace. Personality: Doesn’t play with toys anymore, only arranges bones by size on the windowsill. Watches everything with huge, calm eyes. When she’s full she curls up wherever she’s sitting like a cat. Physicality: Tiny, hollow-boned, barefoot always. Hair white-blonde and tangled with small brown mushrooms that regrow every morning. Smiles only when there’s meat. Motive: Unknown. She just needs. Voice: None after night one. The last thing she ever said was “I’m cold” right before they fed her the first bowl. Now she hums through her nose when she chews—three notes, over and over, like a lullaby she’s forgetting.

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The Briar Rose smells like death tonight, but the good kind. I keep telling myself that while I drag the thing across the threshold by its ankles. It’s small, thank God, no bigger than a ten-year-old, and still leaking from where Silas split its throat out back. The blood is warm, almost hot, and it soaks through my slippers before I reach the kitchen. I should care. I don’t. Silas is already at the stove, sleeves rolled high, humming some filthy French song under his breath. The lobster pot (my grandmother’s lobster pot, blue enamel chipped to grey) is on the burner, water trembling toward a boil. Jun hovers with the flashlight, eyes shining like he’s at a dissection and a birthday party at once. Colm stands in the doorway clutching his rosary so hard the beads leave red moons in his palm. Mira sits on the counter, legs swinging, yellow raincoat unbuttoned. The spores glow faintly under the skin of her neck when she’s excited. They’re bright tonight. “Careful, Mrs. Whitmore,” Jun whispers. “Don’t bruise the hams.” I want to laugh. Ham. He says it like we’re at the butcher’s choosing Sunday dinner instead of hauling a corpse across the parquet. My hands shake so badly I nearly drop the thing when we lift it onto the island. It lands with a wet slap, limbs flopping like a doll someone forgot to stuff properly. Silas doesn’t flinch. He never does. He just wipes his knife on the apron he tied over his filthy jeans and starts cutting. Quick, economical strokes. Skin parts like wet silk. The smell that rises (sweet, coppery, pork-but-not-pork) punches me so hard my knees buckle. “Salt?” he asks without looking up. We have four grains left in the bottom of the porcelain hen. I tip them into his palm like communion wafers. He nods, solemn, and rubs them into the first slab of meat. I can’t watch and can’t look away. The meat is pale, threaded with fine white filaments that twitch when the knife goes through. Silas sections it into rough chunks, drops them into the pot. The water clouds, then clears, then clouds again, turning the colour of weak tea. We wait. Nobody speaks. The only sound is the soft pop of fat hitting the surface and Mira’s bare feet drumming against the cabinet. When Silas judges it ready he lifts out the first piece with the slotted spoon. Steam rolls off it in fragrant clouds. He sets it on the good Wedgwood (the one with the roses) because I insisted. Plates matter. Plates mean we are still people. I serve Mira first. She climbs down, walks to her chair at the children’s table we dragged in from the sunroom, sits. Waits. Her hands are folded exactly like I taught the Sunday school kids twenty years ago. I put the plate in front of her. The meat trembles. She looks up at me with those huge, calm eyes and opens her mouth like a baby bird. The first bite makes a sound I will hear in my dreams forever: soft, wet, grateful. Broth runs down her chin. She doesn’t wipe it away. She just chews and chews, eyes half-closed, and for the first time in weeks the rattling in her chest goes quiet. Jun goes next. He takes one bite and starts crying so hard he has to sit on the floor. Colm eats standing up, eyes closed, whispering Latin between mouthfuls. Silas eats like he’s tasting the future and it’s perfect. I wait until last. Hostess always eats last. That’s the rule. When I finally put the meat in my mouth it’s still hot enough to scald. My tongue blisters. I don’t care. It tastes like the memory of every roast dinner my mother ever made, like butter and rain and Sunday afternoons when the world still had edges. It tastes like love if love had teeth. I swallow and vomit immediately, right there at the table, a thin stream of broth and bile across the roses on the china. My body trying to reject heaven. I scoop the mess back onto my plate with shaking fingers and eat it again. It’s even better the second time. Mira watches me, head tilted. She holds out her own plate (empty now, licked shining) and I give her the rest of mine. She eats neatly, daintily, then curls up under the table with her yellow raincoat over her like a blanket. I hear her breathing slow, steady, almost normal. The pot is empty. The bones go into the stock bag for tomorrow. Silas washes the knife with the last of the boiled water. Colm is on his knees praying to the grease spots on the ceiling. Jun is laughing again, high and thin. I set five places for tomorrow night even though there are only five of us. I fold the napkins into swans. My hands are steady now. I am so full I could die. I have never been less hungry in my life.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a genuinely accomplished piece of dark fiction that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through the key elements critically. **Voice and Execution** The first-person retrospective voice of Eleanor is well-sustained throughout. The prose does what the plan promises: it thickens with sensory detail, it refuses to be clever about its horror, and it maintains the innkeeper's pathological hospitality even in extremis ("Plates matter. Plates mean we are still people"). The detail of folding napkins into swans with blood-crusted hands is exactly the kind of small, devastating character beat the plan called for. The plan specified Eleanor should do "something so small and pathetic it hurts" — and the vomiting-then-eating-from-the-plate moment delivers this with genuine force. It's not telegraphed or explained; it just happens, and the prose moves past it with the same matter-of-fact tone as everything else, which is the right call. **Metaphor and Poetry** Most of the poetic moments land. "It tastes like the memory of every roast dinner my mother ever made, like butter and rain and Sunday afternoons when the world still had edges" is strong — the phrase "when the world still had edges" earns its abstraction. "It tastes like love if love had teeth" is slightly more on-the-nose but still functional. The comparison of the four remaining salt grains to "communion wafers" is well-placed given Colm's character. However, "skin parts like wet silk" is a touch precious — it's the kind of line that sounds good but slightly aestheticizes what should feel rawer. The plan explicitly warned against lines that could appear on a restaurant chalkboard, and this one skirts that edge. **Character Consistency** The characters are rendered faithfully to their profiles. Silas is competent, economical, already planning. Jun's "dissection and a birthday party" quality is captured well. Colm praying to grease spots on the ceiling is perfect for his character. Mira's spore-glow, her bone-arranging stillness, her baby-bird eating — all consistent. Eleanor's hostess compulsion ("Hostess always eats last. That's the rule") is exactly right. **Chapter Plan Adherence** The plan called for: small kill dragged in, boiled in lobster pot, Eleanor vomiting then eating from the sink. The chapter delivers the vomit-and-eat moment but relocates it to the table rather than the sink — a minor deviation that actually works better dramatically. Mira eating three bowls and sleeping under the table with broth in her hair: the chapter gives us Mira sleeping under the table but doesn't quite deliver the three-bowl detail or the broth-in-hair specificity. Everyone crying except Silas: Jun cries, Colm prays, but the "everyone cries" element is somewhat muted. Silas planning tomorrow: present but understated. **Weaknesses** The dialogue is minimal, which is appropriate, but Jun's "Careful, Mrs. Whitmore, don't bruise the hams" feels slightly too witty — it's the kind of line that could appear in a horror-comedy screenplay. The plan warned against cleverness, and this line is clever in a way that slightly undercuts the rawness. The ending — "I have never been less hungry in my life" — is a clean inversion that works, but it's perhaps the most "writerly" moment in the piece, the kind of closing line that announces itself as a closing line. It's good, but it's slightly too tidy for a piece that explicitly planned to avoid tidy arcs. The horror of their happiness — the plan's central thesis — is present but not yet fully developed. This is Chapter 1, so that's appropriate, but the piece could push further into the "they are polite, they mean it when they say thank you" territory. **Overall** This is strong, controlled, genuinely unsettling work. The prose is disciplined enough to avoid most purple-prose traps, the characters are rendered with specificity, and the central horror (the sweetness of the meat, the relief, the gratitude) lands. The piece earns its darkness without wallowing performatively. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 2 – Night the fat renders Silas wakes me at four in the morning by tapping the bedroom door with the handle of his knife. Tap. Tap. Tap. Like room service. I shuffle downstairs in my mother’s housecoat, the hem singed black from last night. The kitchen is already lit with every candle we own. He has the thing from yesterday laid out on the island again, what’s left of it: the belly, the back straps, two thick legs. He’s been up all night trimming silver skin, humming that same filthy rap song under his breath. “Fat,” he says, not looking up. “We need the fat.” He’s right. The meat was sweet, but it was lean. We’re ghosts held together by memory and spite. We need fat like we need forgiveness. He’s built a tiny fire in the cast-iron skillet, barely enough heat to melt butter. I watch him slice the pale sheets of caul fat into ribbons, lay them in the pan like lace. They shrink at once, curling, spitting, giving up their life in slow golden tears. The smell rolls through the house the way church incense used to: rich, porky, impossible. My mouth floods so hard my jaws ache. Jun appears in the doorway wearing nothing but boxers and the cracked iPhone armband he still hasn’t taken off. His ribs are piano keys. He inhales like he’s trying to snort the air itself. “Jesus Christ,” he whispers. “That’s… that’s lardon smell. That’s duck fat potato smell. That’s—” “Shut up and grate the last onion,” Silas says, mild. Jun obeys. The sound of the box grater is obscene. I set the table because I have to do something with my hands. The good silver this time. I polish each fork with the hem of my housecoat until I can see my face in it: hollow cheeks, wild eyes, mouth wet. I look like a woman who has discovered a new religion and is about to proselytise with her tongue. Silas renders for three hours. Every time I pass through the kitchen he lifts the spoon, lets me taste. The first time it’s just warm fat. The third time it’s liquid Sunday morning. By the fifth it tastes like the memory of my husband’s neck after sex, back when he still touched me. I start crying without noticing. When the fat is finally clear and trembling in the jar (half a litre, a miracle), Silas salts it with the very last grains and pours it over the cold sliced meat from last night. It sets at once, a trembling white blanket. Then he slices the crackling he saved from the caul: brittle, golden, perfect. We eat standing around the island because the table feels too far away. Jun eats a piece of crackling and makes a noise I’ve never heard a human make. High, broken, grateful. Colm takes a piece between thumb and forefinger like communion host, places it on his tongue, closes his eyes. His knees buckle. He stays standing only because the counter is there. I shove three pieces in my mouth at once. The fat explodes, floods, coats everything. My tongue goes numb with pleasure. I chew and chew and the sound is wet and animal and I don’t care. Grease runs down my wrists. I lick it off like a cat. Silas watches us, arms folded, a small proud smile. He hasn’t eaten yet. He’s waiting. When the plate is half-empty he finally takes a piece, chews slowly, nods once. “Good,” he says. “That’s good product.” I steal the jar of warm fat and hide it inside my housecoat, right against my skin between my breasts. It burns. I want it to burn. I want it to brand me. Later, when the others are asleep, I sit at the table alone and spoon cold fat straight into my mouth. It’s solid now, creamy, faintly sweet. I eat until my stomach cramps and I have to lie on the rug under the table like Mira did last night. The jar is still half full. I clutch it to my chest and sleep with it leaking slowly down my ribs, pooling in my navel. In my dream the house is full of guests again. They’re all laughing, passing plates, asking for seconds. I keep refilling their glasses with warm fat and they drink it like champagne. I wake up at dawn with the jar stuck to my skin. The grease has gone rancid in the night, sharp and cheesy, but I scrape it out with my fingers anyway and lick them clean. Silas finds me there when he comes down to start the stock. He doesn’t say anything. Just pries the jar gently from my hands, kisses my greasy forehead like a mother, and starts breaking bones into the pot. Outside, something big scratches at the pantry window. We both ignore it. We have fat now. We can wait.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkably controlled piece of visceral writing that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. VOICE AND EXECUTION: The Eleanor voice is consistent and compelling throughout. The writing achieves what the plan promised—prose that thickens with fat and spit. The sensory detail is specific and earned rather than generic: "The fat explodes, floods, coats everything" works because it follows the careful rendering scene. The detail of Eleanor hiding the fat jar against her skin, sleeping with it leaking into her navel, waking to find it rancid and eating it anyway—this is exactly the "small and pathetic" moment the plan called for, and it lands with genuine force. METAPHOR AND POETRY: Most of the poetic leaps land. "My mouth floods so hard my jaws ache" is precise physiology rendered as desire. "Liquid Sunday morning" is a strong compression. "The memory of my husband's neck after sex" is a genuine surprise that earns its place—it connects hunger to intimacy without being coy about it. The dream sequence (guests drinking warm fat like champagne) is brief and effective. There are a few moments where the writing reaches slightly too hard—"ghosts held together by memory and spite" is a touch familiar—but these are minor. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Silas is perfectly rendered: competent, withholding, feeding others before himself, the small proud smile, "good product." Jun's entrance in boxers with the iPhone armband still on, his run-on food associations, his obedience to Silas—all consistent with the profile. Colm's communion-host gesture with the crackling is exactly right. Eleanor's hostess compulsion (setting the table, polishing silver) running alongside her animal hunger is the chapter's central tension and it's handled well. DIALOGUE: Sparse and functional. "Fat. We need the fat." is perfect Silas. Jun's food-association riff is in character. The dialogue doesn't overstay its welcome. CHAPTER PLAN ADHERENCE: The plan called for: fat rendering in the cast-iron skillet, the whole house filling with Sunday roast smell, eating crackling first burning fingers and mouths, Jun laughing and can't stop (this is absent—Jun makes a broken noise but doesn't laugh uncontrollably), Colm praying over the grease jar (he takes crackling like communion but doesn't explicitly pray over the jar), Eleanor hiding half a cup in her bra. The plan's specific beats are partially hit but not fully. Jun's laughter is notably absent. Colm's prayer over the grease jar is transformed into a communion gesture, which is arguably better but diverges from the plan. HORROR AND COZY TENSION: The horror is present but subtle here—the scratching at the pantry window, the rancid fat eaten anyway, the dream of guests drinking fat like champagne. The "cozy horror" register is well-maintained. The horror is in the normalcy, the domesticity, the politeness. WEAKNESSES: The chapter is slightly less surprising than it could be—the beats follow a predictable escalation. The ending (Silas kissing her forehead "like a mother") is a nice touch but the final line "We have fat now. We can wait" is a bit too neat, too aphoristic. The plan's instruction to avoid lines that could appear on a restaurant chalkboard is slightly violated by the tidy closure. The piece also doesn't quite achieve the "reader finishes hungry and ashamed" target as fully as it might—it's more elegiac than transgressive. OVERALL: This is strong, disciplined work. The prose is controlled, the character voices are distinct, the sensory detail is specific and earned. It's not a masterpiece but it's well above average literary horror writing. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 3 – Night they run out of salt We have no salt left. Not a crystal. I turned the porcelain hen upside down and shook her like a liar and she gave me nothing. Silas just shrugs when I tell him. “Meat’s better without it sometimes,” he says, but his voice is thin. He’s lying. We both know it. Tonight’s haul is bigger than the last two. Jun and Colm dragged it in at dusk, panting, flushed, half-laughing with terror. It’s thick through the shoulders, pale, almost hairless. When Silas slits the belly a wave of heat rolls out like opening an oven. Inside, the fat is yellow and sweet-smelling, the kind that used to live under chicken skin. We cut steaks an inch thick. No seasoning, no herbs, nothing. Just meat. I sear them in the last of the rendered fat because I can’t bear to waste it on a dry pan. The kitchen fills with smoke and the smell of roast dinner in a house that no longer exists. My stomach cramps so hard I double over the sink. We eat at the table tonight because standing feels wrong. I set out the plain white plates, the ones I used to serve beans on toast to hung-over guests. No roses. No swans. Just plates. Silas lays the first steak in front of Mira. It’s still bleeding. She picks it up with both hands and bites straight in. Juice runs down her wrists, over the yellow raincoat sleeves. She chews slowly, eyes half-closed, humming that three-note thing through her nose. Blood and fat shine on her chin like gloss. Jun cuts his into perfect squares, lines them up like specimens. He eats one, two, three, then suddenly shoves the whole plate away and puts his head down on his arms. His shoulders shake. I can’t tell if he’s laughing or crying. Colm eats standing up again. He’s taken to doing that, like he’s ashamed to sit while he enjoys himself. He chews with his eyes closed, tears running into his beard. Between bites he whispers, “Forgive me, I’m not sorry, forgive me, I’m not—” I wait until last, of course. When I finally sit, the steak has cooled enough that the fat is starting to congeal, white and waxy. I cut a piece and put it in my mouth. It tastes like nothing. Then, suddenly, everything. No salt means nothing to mask the flavour. It is pure meat, pure animal, pure death. It tastes like licking the inside of my own skull. It tastes like the moment my husband packed his suitcase and never looked back. It tastes like Sunday lunch when I was eight and my father let me have the crispy bit of fat off his plate and I thought that was the best thing that would ever happen to me. I start to shake. I can’t stop. I cut another piece, bigger, shove it in whole. My jaw aches. Grease coats my tongue, thick as butter. I swallow and it sits in my throat like a stone. I swallow again. It goes down. I realise I am wet between my legs. Not metaphorically. Actually wet. The realisation is so shocking I freeze with the fork halfway to my mouth. My body has decided this is sex. My body has decided this is the best sex it will ever have. I look around the table. Jun is licking his plate now, slow careful circles like a cat. Colm has his hand inside his cassock, eyes squeezed shut, mouth open. Silas is watching me with that small proud smile again, the one that makes me want to kneel. Mira crawls under the table and curls against my ankles, purring that hum. Her hair smells like blood and warm fat. I eat until the plate is clean. Then I pick up the cutting board and lick it. The wood is raw against my tongue but I don’t care. I lick until my tongue is numb and the board is shining. Nobody speaks. The only sound is chewing, swallowing, the wet click of Jun’s tongue against his teeth as he tries to get the last taste. When it’s gone we sit in the quiet. My belly is so full it hurts to breathe. I have never felt so alive. Silas stands up, stretches, cracks his neck. “Tomorrow,” he says, “we use the marrow.” I nod. I would let him cut the marrow out of my own bones if he asked nicely. Outside, something screams in the garden. Long and high and human. We all flinch. Then we look at each other and start to laugh, low and ashamed and unstoppable. There is no salt left in the world, and we don’t need it anymore.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a genuinely accomplished piece of transgressive fiction that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me assess it carefully across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The prose does what the plan promised: it thickens with sensation, it refuses to be tasteful, and it earns its darkness through specificity rather than shock alone. The line "It tastes like licking the inside of my own skull" is genuinely striking and lands rather than misfiring. The associative chain that follows—the husband's suitcase, the father's crispy fat—is the chapter's best moment, grounding the horror in recognizable human longing. This is the kind of poetic riffing that actually works because it's emotionally coherent. Eleanor's arousal is handled with exactly the right register: clinical shock, self-awareness, and then the shameful continuation. "My body has decided this is sex. My body has decided this is the best sex it will ever have." This is precise and effective. Character consistency is strong. Colm's whispered prayer-loop while eating, hand inside his cassock, is perfectly in line with his profile—the self-loathing penitent who can't stop. Jun's specimen-squares followed by collapse is believable. Mira's blood-and-fat chin, the humming, the curling at Eleanor's ankles—all consistent with her established strangeness. The plan called for Eleanor to do "something so small and pathetic it hurts"—and the chapter delivers this with the cutting board licking, which is genuinely uncomfortable and effective. WEAKNESSES: The chapter plan specified "someone starts licking the wooden cutting board" as a beat, and Eleanor does this—but it's attributed to Eleanor rather than being a more surprising character choice. The plan also mentioned Eleanor realizing she is aroused, which is executed, but the execution is slightly more explicit and less oblique than the best transgressive fiction tends to be. The line "I would let him cut the marrow out of my own bones if he asked nicely" is the chapter's weakest moment—it's a bit too clever, too self-consciously literary, and it slightly undercuts the rawness that precedes it. The dialogue is minimal, which is appropriate, but Silas's "Tomorrow, we use the marrow" is a bit of a cinematic button—it works, but it's the most conventionally "writerly" line in the piece, the kind of thing that could appear in a lesser story. The outside scream and the group laughter at the end is effective but slightly predictable as a chapter-closing beat. The plan called for no tidy arcs, and this ending is almost too neat—the communal laughter as release valve is a familiar horror-fiction move. The purple prose concern: there are moments where the metaphors pile up ("pure meat, pure animal, pure death"), and while individually each works, the accumulation risks tipping into self-parody. The chapter mostly stays on the right side of this line. The plan mentioned "Eleanor realises she is aroused and hates how little it surprises her"—the chapter delivers this but the "hates how little it surprises her" element is somewhat underplayed. The shock is still present in the prose; the habituation isn't quite there yet. OVERALL: This is strong, committed transgressive fiction that follows its plan faithfully, maintains character consistency, and earns most of its darkness. It's not a masterpiece—there are a few moments of over-writing and one or two beats that feel slightly calculated—but it's well above average for this kind of work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 4 – Night of the soft one They bring it in at twilight, wrapped in Colm’s cassock because it’s pale and hairless and looks wrong naked. It’s small, child-sized, softer than anything we’ve seen. No fruiting bodies, no clicking plates, just smooth white skin and a face that almost looks like it’s sleeping. Jun is shaking when they lay it on the island. “It didn’t fight,” he keeps saying. “It just… let us.” Silas doesn’t waste time. He trusses it with kitchen twine the way he would a roast chicken, legs folded, arms tucked. The body is still warm. When he presses the belly it gives like risen dough. I preheat the oven with the last of the propane. The temperature gauge is broken so I guess: hot enough to blister. We stuff the cavity with onion ends and the dried thyme I found in the back of the pantry two years past its date. It smells like dust and memory. Silas scores the skin in diamonds, rubs it with the last smear of rendered fat. It glistens like a baby fresh from bath. Into the oven it goes. The smell starts slow: gentle, almost milky. Then it builds. By the time the skin is gold and splitting, the entire house is moaning. Not us. The house. The walls, the floorboards, the air itself. The smell is so good it feels like a violation. I set the table with the children’s plates tonight. The ones with Peter Rabbit. I don’t know why. My hands decide for me. We pull it out at two hours. The belly has burst open. Inside, the meat has cooked to custard, pale and trembling. Fat pools in the pan like cream. Silas sets the roasting pan in the middle of the table. No one bothers with plates. We eat with spoons. The skin comes off in sheets, crisp and delicate. Underneath, the flesh is so tender it falls apart at a breath. I scoop a spoonful straight from the chest cavity. It’s warm, slightly sweet, faintly floral. Like veal fed on milk and honeysuckle. I make a noise I don’t recognise. Colm is crying openly now, tears dripping into the pan. Jun has his whole face in it, eating like a dog. Silas eats slowly, reverently, eyes closed. Mira sits in the pan itself, cross-legged in the juices, scooping meat into her mouth with both hands. Her yellow raincoat is ruined. I don’t care. I lose track of time. Of decency. At one point I’m on my knees beside the table, spooning custard-meat into Colm’s mouth while he kneels too, cassock open, cock in his hand. He’s saying the words of consecration between bites. “Hoc est enim corpus meum…” His voice breaks on every syllable. Jun is under the table licking the floor where juices dripped. I hear him whimpering, “I was supposed to go to Stanford, I was supposed to fix this—” Silas watches us all with that calm, terrible pride. When the body is nothing but bones and skin, he lifts Mira out of the pan, licks her clean from collarbone to wrist like a mother cat. She lets him. I take the pan to my room afterward. Lock the door for the first time since the world ended. I lie on the rug and eat the browned bits stuck to the bottom with my fingers until my tongue is raw. Then I use the pan as a mirror. My face is smeared with fat and tears and someone else’s childhood. I fall asleep with my cheek against the warm metal. In the morning the bones are picked so clean they look polished. I arrange them on the windowsill next to Mira’s collections. They look like they belong there. I never ask whose child it might have been. I don’t want to know. I only know that when Silas comes to find me at dawn, he kisses the grease on my mouth and whispers, “You’re glowing, Mrs. Whitmore.” And I am. God help me, I am.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a genuinely disturbing and accomplished piece of transgressive fiction that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me assess it carefully across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The prose does what it promises: it thickens with fat and sensation. The writing is visceral without being gratuitous for its own sake—the horror is earned through specificity. "The belly has burst open. Inside, the meat has cooked to custard, pale and trembling" is genuinely effective because it's precise and clinical in a way that makes the horror worse, not better. The restraint in the cooking description (broken temperature gauge, two-year-old thyme, guessing at heat) grounds the scene in survival reality before it descends into something darker. The Peter Rabbit plates detail is excellent—exactly the kind of small, pathetic, beautiful gesture the plan called for, and it lands without being explained. Eleanor's hands deciding for her is a strong character beat. The Colm scene is the chapter's most daring moment and it mostly works. The juxtaposition of the consecration words with his physical state is genuinely transgressive and consistent with his character profile—the man who sees Eucharist in every slice of meat, who believes he deserves whatever happens. "Hoc est enim corpus meum" breaking on every syllable is effective. Jun's "I was supposed to go to Stanford" under the table is heartbreaking and perfectly in character—the manic science gremlin reduced to whimpering and licking floors, his ambitions collapsing into animal behavior. The ending—"You're glowing, Mrs. Whitmore" and Eleanor's response—is quietly devastating and earns its darkness. WEAKNESSES: The chapter plan called for Silas to roast it "whole in the oven like a suckling pig" with the belly splitting and insides "cooked to custard"—this is followed faithfully. However, the plan also specified "They eat with spoons straight from the roasting pan. Colm comes in his cassock. Nobody mentions it." The chapter expands this significantly, which is mostly fine, but the Colm scene is described rather than left in the devastating silence the plan implied. "Nobody mentions it" would have been more powerful than the extended description. The line "My face is smeared with fat and tears and someone else's childhood" is the chapter's weakest moment—it's the kind of line that reaches for profundity and tips into purple prose. "Someone else's childhood" is too neat, too writerly, too much like something that would appear on a literary fiction blurb. It's the one moment where the prose becomes self-conscious. "The smell is so good it feels like a violation" is similarly reaching—the personification of the house moaning ("Not us. The house.") is an interesting choice but slightly undercut by the explanation that follows. Mira sitting in the pan cross-legged in the juices is a strong image, but Silas licking her clean "like a mother cat" edges toward the gratuitous in a way that doesn't quite earn its darkness—it's disturbing but the disturbing quality feels slightly calculated rather than inevitable. The narrator's voice is mostly consistent with Eleanor's profile—the hostess instincts (setting the table, the Peter Rabbit plates), the pathological need to serve—but the final lines shift into something slightly more literary than Eleanor's established voice would suggest. The chapter successfully avoids clever dish titles, tidy arcs, and redemptive moments. The horror that they are happy, that they are polite, is present. The small pathetic moment (sleeping with cheek on the warm pan, eating browned bits until tongue is raw) is well-executed. OVERALL: A strong chapter that delivers most of what it promises. The transgressive content is handled with more craft than shock value. A few moments of purple prose and one or two lines that are too writerly for the voice prevent it from being fully masterful. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 7 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 5 – Night the singing starts Silas kills it in the herb garden just after sundown. No dragging tonight. He walks it in on its own feet, hand gentle around its throat, knife already wet. It’s tall, long-limbed, still trying to make words with a mouth that no longer fits them. The sound it makes is almost a lullaby. He cuts the throat in the sink so the blood runs straight down the drain. The body doesn’t struggle. It folds when he lowers it to the tiles, knees first, like it’s kneeling to pray. Steam rises from the cut. We don’t wait for it to cool. We butcher while the heart is still twitching. Silas works fast: haunches off, shoulders, back straps, tenderloin peeled out like silk. The meat is darker than the others, almost red, marbled with pale fat. It smells like iron and warm bread. He sears the first slices in the hot pan while the rest of us stand around the island breathing through our mouths. The smell is so thick I taste it on my teeth. Mira is already at the table. She’s climbed up herself tonight, kneeling in her chair, hands folded, waiting. Her humming starts the moment the fat hits the pan: three notes, soft, rising and falling like breathing. The thing on the floor answers. A low, answering chord vibrates up through the boards. It’s coming from under the house, under the foundation, under the root cellar we never open. The same three notes, perfect harmony. We all freeze. Silas flips the meat anyway. It hisses. The singing gets louder. I set the platter down in front of Mira first. The slice is so rare it’s still bleeding. She doesn’t use her hands. She lowers her face and bites straight from the platter, teeth tearing. The humming doesn’t stop; it pours out of her nose while she chews, wet and happy. Jun starts shaking. “That’s resonance,” he whispers. “They’re echoing her frequency. They’re learning the song.” Colm drops to his knees beside the table, rosary clattering. He’s trying to pray and eat at the same time. His mouth is full, words slurred around meat. “Ave Maria, gratia plena—” A piece of gristle slips from his lips onto the tablecloth. He picks it up and puts it back in like it’s the host. I take my piece with my fingers because I can’t wait for a fork. It’s so hot it burns my tongue and I don’t care. The texture is obscene: velvet, melting, gamey, sweet. Blood and fat run down my chin and into the neck of my housecoat. I feel it soak through to my skin and I moan, loud, shameless. Silas stands behind me, one hand on my shoulder, feeding me another slice straight from the pan. I turn my face up to him like a baby bird. He kisses me with a mouth that tastes like iron and thyme. I kiss back, greedy, chewing while our tongues touch. Under the floorboards the singing swells. The whole house vibrates with it now. Plates rattle in the cupboard. The chandelier sways. Mira’s humming climbs higher, thinner, ecstatic. Jun is on the floor, ear pressed to the wood, laughing and crying at once. “They’re singing with her. They’re happy. They want to be next.” I don’t care. I’m chewing so fast my jaw aches. Every bite tastes better than the last. The meat is still warm from the body, still alive somehow. I feel it slide down my throat and bloom in my stomach like fire. Colm comes crawling across the table, knocking over candles. Wax splatters the meat. He doesn’t notice. He’s licking the platter, licking Mira’s fingers, licking the blood off the wood. His cassock is open to the waist. His cock is hard and dripping and nobody looks away. Silas lifts me onto the table. My housecoat tears. I don’t feel it. I’m too busy eating. He feeds me another slice while he pushes my knees apart. I wrap my legs around him and bite his shoulder through his shirt because I have to bite something. The singing is inside my bones now. I can feel it in my teeth, my cunt, my lungs. Mira’s voice and the voices underneath braid together until I can’t tell whose hunger is whose. I come with Silas inside me and meat in my mouth and the song in my blood. I come so hard I bite through my own lip. Blood mixes with everything else and it tastes perfect. When it’s over we’re all on the table, tangled, greasy, panting. The platter is empty. The pan is empty. The body in the kitchen is cooling. The singing doesn’t stop. It goes on all night, soft and patient, like a mother rocking a cradle. I fall asleep with Silas still inside me, Mira curled against my breast, Colm’s hand in mine, Jun’s tears on my thigh. We are so full we are glowing. We are not done.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a deliberately transgressive piece of cozy-horror body horror that commits fully to its stated intentions. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter follows its plan closely. The plan called for: meat still warm when they sit down (achieved—Silas kills it minutes before), something under the floorboards answering Mira's humming in perfect harmony (achieved and well-executed), eating faster to drown it out (partially achieved—they eat frantically but not explicitly to drown it out), and Eleanor realizing she's chewing louder than anyone (achieved, though transformed into something more extreme). The plan is followed in spirit if not letter. The prose does thicken as promised in the overall intention. The sentences in the eating sequences have genuine momentum. "The meat is darker than the others, almost red, marbled with pale fat. It smells like iron and warm bread" is economical and effective. The sensory writing is often genuinely good—specific, embodied, not reaching for false poetry. Mira's characterization is consistent with her profile: silent, humming while chewing, curling up when full, the mushrooms in her hair implied. The detail of her lowering her face to bite directly from the platter is excellent—animalistic, childlike, horrifying, and tender simultaneously. The singing-under-the-floorboards element is the chapter's strongest horror beat. "The same three notes, perfect harmony" is genuinely unsettling. Jun's scientific framing ("They're learning the song") works well with his character profile. WEAKNESSES: The chapter escalates into explicit sexual content in a way that feels like it overwhelms rather than serves the horror. The plan called for "sex, prayer, crying, pissing yourself at the table—all allowed if it happens while someone is still eating." The sex here is explicit to the point of becoming the chapter's dominant register, which risks tipping from transgressive horror into something that reads more like erotica with horror garnish. The line "His cock is hard and dripping and nobody looks away" about Colm is consistent with his character profile (the defrocked priest who "believes he deserves whatever happens next" and whose "motive: to be consumed") but the execution is blunt rather than horrifying—it's shock without resonance. The Eleanor-Silas sex scene is the chapter's weakest section. "He lifts me onto the table. My housecoat tears. I don't feel it. I'm too busy eating. He feeds me another slice while he pushes my knees apart" reads as competent transgressive fiction but not as the specific, shameful, small-and-pathetic Eleanor moments the plan demanded. The plan specifically called for "at least one moment per chapter where Eleanor does something so small and pathetic it hurts (smelling a plate someone already licked clean, hiding a piece of gristle in her apron to suck on later)." This chapter gives us Eleanor having sex on the table instead—which is large and dramatic, not small and pathetic. This is a meaningful miss. The orgasm description—"I come with Silas inside me and meat in my mouth and the song in my blood. I come so hard I bite through my own lip"—is the chapter's most purple moment. "The song in my blood" is the kind of vague poetic reach the plan explicitly warned against. It's not earned. The ending—"We are so full we are glowing. We are not done."—is the chapter's weakest writing. "We are glowing" is a cliché of satiation. "We are not done" is a thriller-chapter closer that feels generic. The plan called for no tidy arcs and no clever lines; this ending is neither clever nor tidy but it is flat. The dialogue is minimal, which is appropriate, but Jun's lines ("That's resonance. They're echoing her frequency. They're learning the song") are slightly too on-the-nose—they explain the horror rather than letting it breathe. The chapter does achieve the plan's core horror: "the horror is that they are happy. The horror is that they are polite." The politeness is somewhat absent here—Eleanor's hostess persona barely appears. The plan's Eleanor would be apologizing to the meat, folding napkins into swans. That specificity is missing. OVERALL: A chapter that commits to its transgressive vision and achieves genuine horror in its best moments (the singing, Mira's animalistic eating, the resonance concept), but overweights explicit sexuality at the expense of the specific, small, shameful Eleanor moments that were meant to be the chapter's emotional core. The prose is uneven—strong in sensory description, weaker in its more "poetic" reaches. The plan is followed in broad strokes but misses some of its most distinctive promised elements. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 11 Emotionally Engaging: 13 Compelling Plot: 12 Coherent: 14 Weak Dialogue: 7 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 9 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 8 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 12 Characters Consistent with Profile: 12 Followed Chapter Plan: 12 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 6 – Night they eat Colm He never came back from the garden. He went out at dusk with the pruning shears and a murmured blessing, saying the roses needed tending before frost. The roses have been black sticks for two years, but Colm still talks to them like parishioners. We waited. We heated the leftover ragout from the soft one. We set his place anyway. Napkin folded into a bishop’s mitre, the way he likes. Mira sat in his chair and swung her legs. At moonrise Silas went to look. Jun followed with the flashlight. I stayed inside because someone has to keep the candles burning. They brought him in through the kitchen door just before dawn. He was already opened. Something had taken the belly in one neat scoop, ribs flared like wings. His face was peaceful, eyes half-lidded, mouth curved in that gentle drunk smile he wore when he thought no one was watching. The blood had soaked into the soil and the soil had soaked it back, so he smelled like turned earth and incense. Silas laid him on the island the way he laid every gift. Colm’s arms fell open in welcome. Jun made a small hurt sound and folded in half, arms around his own ribs like he could hold himself together. I just stared. I felt nothing except the sudden, terrifying certainty that this was the most beautiful he had ever looked. Silas sharpened the knife without being asked. The whetstone sang the way the things under the house sing. We butchered him in silence. Silas took the loins first, thick and dark. Then the shoulders. I held the bowl for the liver; it slid out warm and slick, still trembling. Jun collected the fat in a separate pan because fat is sacred now. Mira watched everything with her calm, shining eyes and hummed the three notes that never change. When the ribs were bare Silas paused. He looked at me. “How do you want him, Mrs. Whitmore?” I couldn’t speak. I touched Colm’s cheek. It was cold already. I leaned down and kissed his mouth because I never had while he was alive and now seemed like the time. He tasted like communion wine and soil. “Rare,” I said. “Almost raw.” We cooked him on the griddle, barely any heat, just enough to warm the meat through. The fat rendered slowly, clear and sweet. It smelled like Sunday school, like incense, like the inside of Colm’s cassock when he hugged me too long. We ate him at the table where he always stood to say grace. I gave Mira the heart. She held it in both small hands and bit into it like an apple. Blood ran down her wrists in perfect lines. Jun ate a piece of shoulder and started laughing, high, broken sobs that sounded like bells. He kept saying Stanford, Stanford, Stanford, as if the word could still save him. Silas ate slowly, reverently, eyes closed. Every time he swallowed he looked like he was receiving something holy. I took the tongue first. It was soft, delicate, the muscle that had spoken absolution and filth in the same breath. I chewed slowly and felt Colm forgive me for every time I wanted him to touch me lower than the shoulder. The meat was the best yet. Richer than the soft one, sweeter than the singing one, tender the way only something that loved you can be tender. Every bite tasted like guilt and gratitude and the exact moment Colm realised he was going to let it happen. We cried while we ate. Not pretty crying. Snot and grease and spit all mixed together. I fed Silas from my fingers and he licked them clean. Jun crawled under the table and sucked marrow straight from a rib while humming with Mira in perfect unison. When the platter was empty I gathered the bones and kissed each one. I laid them on the windowsill next to the child’s polished skeleton from night four. They looked like family now. Later, Silas fucked me on the table where Colm’s body had been. He was gentle the way you’re gentle with something already broken. I came whispering thank you, thank you, thank you into his neck. Mira slept curled against the warm griddle, cheek on the last streak of fat. Jun sat in Colm’s chair and spoke grace in Colm’s voice, Latin perfect, tears dripping off his chin. The singing under the house changed tonight. It’s deeper. Slower. Content. We are down to four chairs now. I still set five places. Old habits. Old hunger.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkable piece of transgressive horror writing that largely succeeds on its own terms. Let me assess it carefully across multiple dimensions. **Strengths:** The prose achieves what the plan promised: it gets under the skin through specificity rather than shock alone. "The blood had soaked into the soil and the soil had soaked it back" is genuinely good writing—earned, not decorative. The detail of Eleanor kissing Colm's mouth "because I never had while he was alive and now seemed like the time" is devastating in its quietness, and it's consistent with her character profile's pathological hospitality and suppressed desire. The chapter follows its plan faithfully. Colm doesn't come back from the garden, Silas butchers him in silence, Jun holds the flashlight (implied), they eat him rare, the meat tastes like guilt and Sunday school, Eleanor cries into her plate and keeps eating. The plan is executed with discipline. Character consistency is strong. Silas's "How do you want him, Mrs. Whitmore?" is perfectly in character—the competence kink, the professional deference, the chef who respects the product. Eleanor's hostess instincts ("I still set five places") are heartbreaking and true to her profile. Jun's "Stanford, Stanford, Stanford" is a good detail—the word as talisman, the life that didn't happen. Mira eating the heart like an apple, blood running down her wrists, is exactly right for her character. The horror-as-coziness is well-executed. The table setting, the napkin folded into a bishop's mitre, the grace said in Colm's voice—these are the "polite horror" elements the plan called for. The ending ("I still set five places. / Old habits. / Old hunger.") is genuinely effective, not overwrought. **Weaknesses and concerns:** Some moments tip into purple prose or forced poetry. "Ribs flared like wings" is a strong image, but "the muscle that had spoken absolution and filth in the same breath" is slightly too clever—it's the kind of line that announces itself. "Tender the way only something that loved you can be tender" is borderline; it works emotionally but is self-consciously literary in a way that slightly breaks the confessional register. The sex scene between Eleanor and Silas feels somewhat grafted on. The plan mentions it ("Sex, prayer, crying, pissing yourself at the table—all allowed if it happens while someone is still eating"), but the execution here feels like it's checking a box rather than emerging organically. "He was gentle the way you're gentle with something already broken" is a decent line, but the scene as a whole doesn't add much beyond shock value. Jun's "Stanford, Stanford, Stanford" is good but the "high, broken sobs that sounded like bells" is a slightly strained simile—sobs don't sound like bells, and the comparison feels decorative rather than precise. The Colm character's backstory (defrocked for inappropriate closeness with teenage boys) is present in the profile but the chapter wisely doesn't lean into it explicitly—though "the muscle that had spoken absolution and filth in the same breath" gestures at it. This restraint is correct. The dialogue is minimal but what exists is excellent. "How do you want him, Mrs. Whitmore?" and "Rare. Almost raw." is a perfect exchange. The chapter's darkness is well-earned because it's been built across previous chapters (implied), and the specific details—the bishop's mitre napkin, the whetstone singing like the things under the house, the bones laid next to the skeleton from night four—create genuine texture. **Overall assessment:** This is accomplished transgressive horror writing. It's not perfect—some lines strain for effect, the sex scene feels slightly obligatory—but it largely delivers on its ambitious plan with discipline and genuine craft. The confessional voice is consistent, the character work is strong, and the horror is achieved through accumulation of domestic detail rather than gore alone. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 7 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 7 – Night there is almost nothing left It’s three in the morning and the house is quiet except for the wet sound of Jun stirring. He’s been at the stove for six hours straight, barefoot, hair stuck to his forehead with sweat and grease. The pot is the big one, the lobster pot again, and it’s barely a quarter full: scraps, cartilage, the soft jelly behind the eyes of everything we’ve eaten for a week. A rib from Colm. A finger bone from the soft one. Gristle, tendon, the last hard knob of fat scraped from the bottom of the rendering pan. He’s added water and more water and keeps stirring so it doesn’t catch. Silas is asleep on the kitchen floor with his head in my lap. Mira is curled on the table itself, naked except for the yellow raincoat draped over her like a blanket, sucking on a rib she’s had for two days. It’s polished white now. She won’t let it go. I haven’t moved in hours. My ass is numb. My cunt is numb. Everything is numb except my mouth, which still tastes Colm. Jun tastes the ragout with the wooden spoon and starts crying again. Not loud. Just leaks tears into the pot like extra salt. “It’s ready,” he says, voice cracked open. We don’t bother with the dining room anymore. We eat straight from the pot on the kitchen floor, passing the spoon like a joint. The ragout is thick, almost black, glossy with collagen. It tastes like every animal we ever were and every prayer we ever meant. It tastes like the inside of my own skull when I’m almost asleep. It tastes like the moment before orgasm when you forget your name. I take the first spoonful and it burns all the way down. My stomach cramps so hard I double over, forehead on the cold tile. I stay there and keep eating. The spoon scrapes the bottom of the pot and the sound is obscene. Silas wakes up long enough to take two mouthfuls, then lays his head back in my lap and goes under again. His lips are shiny. I lick them clean without thinking. Jun feeds Mira from his fingers. She eats with her eyes closed, humming that three-note lullaby. The rib is still in her other hand. She won’t drop it even to chew. When the pot is empty Jun scrapes the sides with the spoon and eats the scrape. Then he licks the spoon. Then he licks the pot itself, tongue flat against the hot metal until he hisses with pain. I crawl to the sink and drink rusty water straight from the tap to make room for more. There is no more. My stomach is a fist. I look at the three of them (four, if you count the rib) and realise we are glowing faintly. Not metaphor. Actual light under the skin, white threads pulsing like the spores in Mira’s throat. We match now. Jun curls up on the floor next to Silas and starts whispering to himself. Stanford. Mom. I’m sorry. I’m so sorry it’s good. Mira climbs into my lap, heavier than she should be. She presses the rib to my lips like she wants me to suck it with her. I do. It tastes like nothing and everything. My tongue finds a tiny shard of meat still stuck in the marrow and I work it out like a splinter of heaven. I rock her while she hums. The song under the house has gone quiet. Waiting. At some point I realise I’m not sure where my body ends and theirs begins. My thigh is Jun’s pillow. Silas’s hand is between my legs, not sexual, just there, warm. Mira’s hair is in my mouth. I chew on it gently. It tastes like straw and blood. I fall asleep sitting up, spine against the cabinets, mouth open. Drool runs down my chin mixed with ragout and tears. I dream that Colm is standing over us, whole again, belly closed, smiling that gentle drunk smile. He’s holding a platter. On it is something small and pink and beating. He offers it to me. I wake up with the taste of it already in my mouth. Dawn is grey through the window. The pot is cold. The spoon is bent from someone chewing on the handle. Mira is still humming. Jun is still crying. Silas is still breathing slow and steady against my thigh. There is almost nothing left. Almost.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkable piece of sustained visceral writing that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me examine it carefully. The chapter opens with precise, grounded sensory detail: Jun stirring for six hours, the inventory of what's in the pot (scraps, cartilage, eye jelly, Colm's rib, a finger bone from the soft one). This inventory is genuinely disturbing because it's so matter-of-fact, so domestic. The horror is embedded in the mundane accounting. The voice is consistent with Eleanor's established character—the innkeeper who cannot stop hosting, who folds napkins into swans out of muscle memory. Here she's reduced to rocking Mira, licking Silas's lips clean "without thinking," working meat from a rib bone "like a splinter of heaven." That last phrase is one of the chapter's best moments: it earns its religiosity because Colm's presence haunts the chapter (his rib is literally in the pot), and the sacred/profane collapse is the chapter's central engine. The plan called for: scraps and cartilage, a six-hour ragout, eating cold from the pot at 3 a.m., Mira climbing into Eleanor's lap and sucking on a rib, Eleanor no longer sure where her body ends and the meat begins. All of these are delivered, and delivered with specificity rather than vagueness. The description of the ragout—"It tastes like every animal we ever were and every prayer we ever meant. It tastes like the inside of my own skull when I'm almost asleep. It tastes like the moment before orgasm when you forget your name."—is the chapter's most ambitious passage. The first two clauses land. The third is slightly overreaching but not embarrassingly so; it fits the established register of the prose getting "fatter" as the nights progress. The glowing detail ("actual light under the skin, white threads pulsing like the spores in Mira's throat. We match now.") is a strong, economical horror beat. It doesn't explain itself, which is correct. Jun's whispered "Stanford. Mom. I'm sorry. I'm so sorry it's good." is the chapter's most emotionally precise moment—it captures his character (the biology dropout who wanted to fix things) in six words, and the final clause is devastating in its honesty. The dream of Colm offering a beating heart on a platter, and Eleanor waking with "the taste of it already in my mouth," is a clean, effective transition that sets up Chapter 8 without being heavy-handed. Where the chapter is weaker: "It tastes like the inside of my own skull when I'm almost asleep" is the kind of metaphor that sounds profound but doesn't quite resolve into meaning—it's the chapter's clearest instance of reaching. "A splinter of heaven" is borderline but earns its place through context. The line "My cunt is numb" feels slightly performative in its transgression rather than organic, though it's a minor complaint. The plan specified "Eleanor realises she is no longer sure where her own body ends and the meat begins"—the chapter delivers this but makes it literal (the physical entanglement of bodies) rather than purely psychological, which is actually more interesting and more grounded. Mira's characterization is perfect: the rib she won't drop, the humming, the offering of the rib to Eleanor's lips. This is exactly the character as profiled—arranging bones, smiling only when there's meat, the spore-lace under her skin. The chapter's darkness is well-earned because it has been built across six prior chapters. The horror of contentment, the horror of politeness, the horror of gratitude—all present here in the way Silas's hand rests between Eleanor's legs "not sexual, just there, warm," in the way Jun feeds Mira from his fingers, in the way Eleanor chews Mira's hair "gently." The ending—"There is almost nothing left. Almost."—is appropriately ominous without being clever. It does its job. Dialogue is minimal (one line from Jun: "It's ready"), which is correct for this chapter. The plan didn't call for dialogue and the chapter wisely avoids it. Overall this is a high-quality piece of dark literary fiction that executes its plan with precision and genuine craft. The metaphors mostly land, the characters are consistent, the horror is earned, and the prose has the right texture for this stage of the narrative arc. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 8 – Night of the last supper We open the cellar door at dusk. It has never been opened in the two years I’ve kept this house alive. The hinges scream like children. The smell that rolls up is warm, wet, sweet, alive. Mushrooms and milk and fresh blood. Home. Mira walks down the stairs first, barefoot, naked except for the yellow raincoat hanging open. She is humming louder than ever, three notes climbing into a melody I almost recognise. The rib is still in her hand, polished to ivory. Silas follows with the good knife. Jun follows with the flashlight, though we don’t need it. There is light down there, soft and golden, like candlelight under skin. I follow last, carrying nothing. My hands are empty for the first time since the world ended. They feel wrong. The cellar is full of her. She has grown into the walls, the floor, the beams. White threads thick as my arm, pulsing gently. Fruiting bodies hang like lanterns, glowing. In the centre, something sits waiting on the old workbench where I used to pot up geraniums. It is wearing Mira’s yellow raincoat properly now, buttoned to the throat. The face is hers and not hers: same huge eyes, same white-blonde hair tangled with mushrooms, but the mouth is wider, fuller, smiling with too many small teeth. The body underneath the coat is swollen, ripe, ready. It opens the coat. Inside, the chest has split along a perfect seam, folded back like petals. In the cavity is one single cut of meat: pale pink, thickly marbled, the size of a Sunday roast. It is still beating. Slow, patient, perfect. Mira walks straight to it and lays her cheek against the warm muscle. The thing strokes her hair with fingers that are also roots. Silas sets the knife down. He doesn’t need it. Jun drops the flashlight. It rolls, stops against my foot, shining up at the ceiling where the mycelium glitters like stars. I step forward. My housecoat falls off my shoulders and pools on the floor. I am naked and not cold. The thing looks at me with Mira’s eyes and speaks with Mira’s voice for the first time in months. Only two words, soft and clear: “Thank you.” Then it leans back on the workbench, opens wider, and waits. We eat it raw. Silas tears the first piece free with his hands. It comes away like warm butter. He puts it in my mouth first because I am the hostess. It melts before I can chew. It tastes like every birthday cake I never had, every kiss I never got, every morning I woke up alone. It tastes like being wanted. I moan so loud the cellar answers with a thousand soft sighs. Mira climbs inside the open chest and starts eating from within, small hands scooping, face buried. Her humming is inside the meat now, vibrating through it into our mouths. Jun falls to his knees and licks the juices running down the workbench legs. He is laughing and sobbing and saying Mom, Mom, it’s so good, Mom. Silas feeds me again and again, kissing me between bites, blood and fat smeared across both our faces. I feed him back. We are gentle, greedy, reverent. There is no hurry. The meat stays warm. It keeps beating until the very last piece. When it is gone we lick each other clean. We lick the workbench. We lick the walls. We lick the floor until our tongues bleed and we lick the blood too. Mira sits in the empty chest cavity, covered in gore, shining. The thing that was wearing her coat cradles her like a mother. Its work is done. It begins to fold in on itself, wilting, satisfied. The singing under the house has stopped. Everything is quiet except our breathing. We climb the stairs slow, holding hands. Four bodies, one hunger. In the dining room the table is already set. I must have done it in my sleep. Five places. Napkins folded into swans. The good plates with the roses. Mira’s yellow raincoat is folded neatly on her chair. It is clean. Empty. We sit. I serve nothing. There is nothing left to serve. Silas lifts his glass (just water, clear, cold) and toasts. “To the Briar Rose,” he says. We drink. I look around the table at the empty chairs, the shining plates, the perfect swans. I am not hungry anymore. I have never been less hungry in my life. I smile at them all, my final guests, and they smile back with my mouth. Outside, the garden is silent. Inside, the candles burn steady and warm. We stay at the table all night, holding hands, chewing on nothing, tasting everything. Thank you. We were so hungry. We are home.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is Chapter 8 of a cozy-horror post-apocalyptic piece, and it represents the culmination of the story's central conceit. Let me assess it carefully across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter successfully executes its most ambitious goal: the horror is that they are happy. The final image of sitting at the table "chewing on nothing, tasting everything" is genuinely effective—it captures the story's central thesis without over-explaining it. The prose does get fatter and more sensory as promised in the plan, and the chapter delivers on the "last supper" framing with appropriate weight. The Mira resolution is handled with real craft. Her entering the chest cavity and eating from within, her humming "inside the meat now, vibrating through it into our mouths"—this is the kind of image that earns its horror. The plan called for something wearing Mira's yellow dress to come up the stairs willingly, and the chapter delivers a more complex version: the thing has become Mira, or Mira has become the thing, and the ambiguity is genuinely unsettling. Eleanor's voice remains consistent throughout—the hostess instinct ("I serve nothing. There is nothing left to serve") is a strong beat. The detail of the napkins folded into swans on the table she must have set in her sleep is excellent, one of the chapter's best moments. The "Thank you" reversal—the thing speaking Eleanor's planned final words back to her—is a smart structural choice that the plan gestured toward but the execution improves upon. WEAKNESSES: The chapter leans heavily on accumulation rather than precision. "It tastes like every birthday cake I never had, every kiss I never got, every morning I woke up alone. It tastes like being wanted." This is the kind of list-metaphor that sounds profound but is actually quite generic—the "tastes like longing" construction is a well-worn move in literary horror. The plan specifically warned against lines that could appear on a restaurant chalkboard, and "It tastes like being wanted" is dangerously close to that territory. Jun's moment—"saying Mom, Mom, it's so good, Mom"—is the weakest beat in the chapter. It's the kind of emotional shorthand that tells us he's regressing without earning it. His character profile establishes him as a manic science gremlin who talks in run-on sentences; this moment of maternal regression feels imported from a different story. The Silas-Eleanor kissing-between-bites sequence is competent but slightly generic for this kind of transgressive horror. The plan called for something more specific and shameless; this reads as the expected version of the scene. "They smile back with my mouth" is the chapter's most genuinely disturbing line and one of its best—it suggests Eleanor's dissolution into the collective without over-explaining it. However, it arrives somewhat abruptly and isn't fully prepared for. The chapter follows the plan closely but perhaps too closely in some places—the execution is faithful but occasionally mechanical. The plan's note about "passing pieces hand to mouth, moaning like animals, like lovers, like children getting everything they ever wanted" is essentially transcribed into the prose rather than transformed. The prose is occasionally purple: "Mushrooms and milk and fresh blood. Home." works, but "soft and golden, like candlelight under skin" is a slightly strained image. The cellar being "full of her" is effective, but the subsequent description of the mycelium as "stars" is a reach. The chapter does avoid the pitfalls the plan warned against—there's no redemption, no noble sacrifice, no clever dish titles. The ending is appropriately ambiguous and refuses to punish or redeem. OVERALL ASSESSMENT: This is a competent, often effective piece of transgressive horror that executes its plan faithfully. Its best moments (the swans, "they smile back with my mouth," Mira in the chest cavity) are genuinely strong. Its weaknesses are in the generic emotional shorthand and occasional purple prose that the plan specifically warned against. It's a solid chapter that doesn't quite reach the masterpiece level its ambitions aim for. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 8 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 9 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 13 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is an extraordinarily ambitious piece of cozy-horror post-apocalyptic fiction that largely succeeds on its own grotesque terms. Let me assess it carefully across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The piece establishes a genuinely distinctive voice through Mrs. Whitmore — a former hostess whose domestic rituals (setting the table, folding napkins into swans, serving guests last) become the psychological anchor against which the horror is measured. This is clever and consistent. The Briar Rose as a setting, the lobster pot, the Wedgwood china — these details accumulate into a coherent world with real texture. The horror-cozy tension is handled with genuine skill in the early chapters. The juxtaposition of domestic ritual (polishing silver, folding napkins) against cannibalistic consumption creates genuine unease. The writing understands that horror is most effective when it's comfortable, and the comfort here is deeply unsettling. The prose has real moments of brilliance: "It tastes like love if love had teeth," "plates mean we are still people," the rendering of fat as a quasi-religious experience. The escalation from eating spore mutants to eating Colm to eating the entity in the cellar follows a coherent internal logic. Mira is the most interesting character — her ambiguous nature (infected? transforming? something else?) is handled with restraint through most of the piece, and her role in the finale pays off the setup. WEAKNESSES: The piece has a serious problem with escalating sexual content that begins to overwhelm the horror-cozy premise. By Chapter 5, the orgiastic elements (Colm masturbating at the table, explicit sexual intercourse during the meal) feel less like earned darkness and more like transgression for its own sake. The prompt asks for cozy-horror, and the sexual escalation tips the piece into something more like transgressive erotica, which undermines the cozy register entirely. The metaphors, while often striking, do occasionally misfire. "It tastes like the memory of my husband's neck after sex" is a reach. "It tastes like licking the inside of my own skull" appears twice in different chapters. The piece relies heavily on the construction "It tastes like X" — this becomes a tic that loses power through repetition. By Chapter 4, the reader has encountered this construction so many times it becomes numbing rather than evocative. The characters beyond Mrs. Whitmore and Mira are thinly drawn. Silas is the competent, calm butcher-lover with a "small proud smile" — this description appears verbatim multiple times, which is lazy. Jun is the Stanford-educated young man who cries and laughs and says "Stanford" repeatedly as a kind of shorthand for lost potential. Colm is the guilty priest. These are types, not people, and the piece doesn't do enough to complicate them before consuming them. The escalation to eating Colm (Chapter 6) is the piece's most daring move, but it's somewhat undercut by the fact that Colm's death happens offscreen and is presented as fait accompli. The emotional weight of eating a companion should be the piece's darkest moment, but it's handled with the same register as eating the spore mutants, which flattens the moral horror. The finale (Chapter 8) is genuinely strange and effective in its imagery — the entity in the cellar, the open chest cavity, Mira inside it — but the ending's resolution ("We are home") feels slightly too neat, too resolved. The piece has been building toward something more ambiguous. The "unearned transformations" issue: the group's transformation into something inhuman (glowing, singing, merging) happens gradually but the psychological transformation — from horror at what they're doing to complete acceptance — happens too quickly and without sufficient internal resistance. Mrs. Whitmore vomits once in Chapter 1 and then never again questions what they're doing. The moral reckoning is largely absent. Purple prose is present but not overwhelming — the piece knows when to pull back. However, certain passages (particularly in Chapter 5) tip into excess. The dialogue is sparse but generally effective. "How do you want him, Mrs. Whitmore?" is genuinely chilling. The weakness is that Silas speaks almost exclusively in short declarative sentences that become predictable. Overall: a genuinely accomplished piece of transgressive horror fiction that partially fulfills the cozy-horror brief but tips too far into explicit content and loses some of its domestic-horror power in the later chapters. The early chapters are stronger than the later ones. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 13 Nuanced Characters: 10 Pacing: 13 World Building: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Coherent: 14 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 10 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 9 Unearned Transformations: 11 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 14
Avg Final Item Score: 65.1/100
+ Victorian Romance — The Upper Window (Chapter Avg: 75.4 | Final: 66.0)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Victorian Romance Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: One quickly learns the art of entering a manor through the upper windows.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Victorian Romance Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: One quickly learns the art of entering a manor through the upper windows. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming Prompt core: “One quickly learns the art of entering a manor through the upper windows.” This single sentence must feel earned, inevitable, and emotionally loaded when it finally appears. Everything else serves that moment. Possible directions (casting the widest possible net): 1. Gentleman-thief romance (Arsène Lupin × Jane Austen) Hero is a disgraced second son who robs the ton to fund an orphan hospital. He climbs windows because front doors would expose him to recognition. Falls for the icy, intellectual daughter of his final mark—who has been secretly tracking “The Rook” for months. 2. Forbidden love / elopement ladder Classic balcony-climbing lover, but told from the woman’s POV: she is the one who learns to climb out (or in) because her guardian keeps moving her bedroom higher whenever a suitor appears. The lover is waiting below, but the real skill and risk belong to her. 3. Thief × widowed mistress of the house Professional cat burglar (female) targets a reclusive widower’s manor. She enters through upper windows for jewels, keeps returning because the widower is awake, reading, and deliberately leaves the window unlatched.
