Dafydd whined, circled twice, and flopped under the Welsh history table with a theatrical sigh. Rain hammered the slate roof so hard it sounded like someone upstairs was emptying bags of gravel. The River Dyfi was already over the bridge; Carys had seen it on the local Facebook group. Machynlleth was flooding again. Rhys peeled off his wet coat, hung it on the ancient hatstand that had never held a hat in living memory. Underneath he wore a black jumper gone shapeless at the cuffs. He looked younger without the coat. Smaller. “Got anything stronger than tea?” he asked. She reached under the counter without answering, produced a bottle of Penderyn Madeira finish and two chipped mugs that said “Cymdeithas yr Iaith” on them. He raised an eyebrow. “Classy.” “Shut up and pour.” He did. Two generous measures. Handed her one. They drank. The whisky burned beautifully. Rhys exhaled like a man surfacing. “Better,” he said. Carys leaned against the counter, arms folded. The candlelight did things to his cheekbones she refused to catalogue. “So,” he said. “Hostage situation. What now?” “We wait for the power to come back or the paps to drown. Whichever first.” “Romantic.” She snorted. Took another sip. Silence settled, surprisingly easy. The storm filled it. Rhys rolled the mug between his palms. “You ever miss London?” “Never.” “Liar.” She gave him a look sharp enough to shave with. He shrugged. “Fine. I miss it sometimes. The noise. The feeling that something’s happening even when it’s just wankers in Soho House pretending they’re important.” “Sounds exhausting.” “It is.” He drank again. “I used to think exhaustion was proof I was alive. Turns out it’s just exhaustion.” Carys studied him over the rim of her mug. The candle between them guttered, flared, steadied. “Why are you really here, Rhys?” He met her eyes. Didn’t smile, didn’t charm. “Because when you looked at me like I was just another wet idiot in your shop, it was the first time in ten years someone didn’t want something from me.” His voice cracked on the last word. Not dramatically. Just enough. Carys felt it land somewhere under her ribs. She set her mug down. “Don’t do that.” “Do what?” “Make me feel sorry for you.” “Too late.” “Fuck off.” But she didn’t move away when he stepped closer. The space between them shrank to candle-flame width. He smelled of rain and whisky and warm wool. His jumper had a hole at the collar; she could see the small hollow at the base of his throat moving when he swallowed. “I set fire to his Booker letter,” she said suddenly. Rhys blinked. “My ex. The one who—” She gestured vaguely at the ruin of her life. “After the inquiry. I broke into his office at three in the morning, soaked the longlist letter in gin, and lit it in his bin. Security caught me on the way out. They let me go because they hated him too.” Rhys’s mouth twitched. Not quite a smile. “Should’ve used petrol,” he said. “Next time.” They were close enough now that she could feel the heat coming off him. “Carys,” he said, low. “Don’t.” “Don’t what?” “Don’t say my name like that.” “Like what?” “Like it matters.” He reached up slowly, giving her every chance to step back, and tucked a strand of hair behind her ear with fingers that trembled just slightly. “It does,” he said. She kissed him. It wasn’t soft or cinematic. She basically crashed into him, mouth hard, tasting whisky and fury and three years of celibacy. He made a shocked noise that turned into a groan, hands coming up to grip her waist like he was afraid she’d vanish. They knocked over a stack of Dylan Thomas. Books hit the floor with soft thuds. Dafydd started barking. They broke apart, breathing hard. Dafydd had stationed himself between them, hackles up, glaring at Rhys like he’d personally offended the entire corgi nation. Then the dog lifted his leg and pissed copiously on Rhys’s left trainer. The silence that followed was absolute. Rhys looked down at his shoe, looked at Dafydd, looked at Carys. She started laughing first. Helpless, wheezing, tears in her eyes. He followed a second later, the sound punched out of him like he’d forgotten how. “Yr hen bastart,” Carys gasped, sliding down the counter until she was sitting on the floor. Rhys sat beside her, soaked trainer and all, still laughing. “Occupational hazard,” he wheezed. They sat there in the candlelight, backs against the counter, knees almost touching, while the storm screamed and Dafydd stood guard over them both like a soggy, flatulent chaperone. Neither of them tried to kiss again. It felt too dangerous. Like the moment they did, something would break that couldn’t be put back together. So they drank the rest of the whisky instead, passing the bottle back and forth, talking about nothing and everything until the candles burned low and the rain began to ease. At some point Carys’s head found its way onto his shoulder. He didn’t move. Barely breathed. Outside, the power came back on with a pathetic flicker, and the moment slipped away into ordinary light. But it had been there. They both knew it.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 3 executes its plan with considerable skill, though not without some wobbles worth examining critically. The chapter follows its blueprint faithfully: storm, power cut, single malt, two candles, first genuine honesty, almost-kiss, Dafydd's intervention. The structural fidelity is strong. The plan promised "first time they're honest for more than thirty seconds at a time" and delivers this through Rhys's admission about exhaustion and Carys's arson confession. The Dafydd moment lands as planned. Character consistency is largely excellent. Rhys's crack in his voice "not dramatically, just enough" is precisely calibrated to his profile—the fragile ego seeking genuine connection rather than performance. His instinct to fill silence, his charm deployed then dropped, his trembling fingers: all consistent. Carys's sudden confession about the Booker letter feels authentic to her character—she weaponises vulnerability by making it sound like aggression. Her internal battle ("the candlelight did things to his cheekbones she refused to catalogue") is handled with appropriate restraint. The dialogue is genuinely strong in places. "Don't say my name like that." / "Like what?" / "Like it matters." is earned, not merely clever. The exchange about London exhaustion has real texture. "Should've used petrol" / "Next time" is perfectly pitched—two damaged people recognising each other's damage without sentimentalising it. However, there are criticisms worth making. The line "mouth hard, tasting whisky and fury and three years of celibacy" is the chapter's most amateurish moment—"three years of celibacy" as a flavour is a reach that doesn't quite land, tipping into the kind of forced poetic compression that sounds clever but collapses under scrutiny. Similarly, "like a small defiant lighthouse" for the candle on the windowsill is slightly overwrought given the otherwise restrained register. The ending—"But it had been there. / They both knew it."—is the chapter's weakest passage. It's the author telling us what to feel rather than trusting the scene to have done its work. The fragmented final lines feel like a writer reaching for gravitas the scene has already earned without the reach. This is a minor but real failure of nerve. The Dafydd moment is well-executed. The comedy doesn't undercut the tension so much as redirect it, which is exactly what the plan called for. "A soggy, flatulent chaperone" is a good line. The transition from laughter back to something tender—heads on shoulders, passing the bottle—is handled with appropriate delicacy. The prose is generally clean and purposeful. Sentences do shorten when characters are lying or guarded, lengthen when they're exposed. The Welsh is deployed naturally. The sensory details (rain on slate, Penderyn Madeira finish, "Cymdeithas yr Iaith" mugs) are specific and earned rather than decorative. The plot is slight by design—this is a chamber piece—but it moves the relationship forward meaningfully. The almost-kiss interrupted by comedy, followed by the tender aftermath, is a classic structure executed with enough specificity to feel fresh rather than formulaic. The chapter's emotional engagement is high. The moment where Carys kisses him first—"basically crashed into him"—is the right choice; it preserves her agency and subverts the expected dynamic. The aftermath, sitting on the floor laughing, is genuinely warm without being saccharine. Minor issues: "Books hit the floor with soft thuds" is slightly flat. The hatstand observation ("never held a hat in living memory") is a nice detail but slightly self-conscious. The final fragmented lines are the most significant craft failure. Overall this is accomplished, emotionally intelligent work that mostly earns its effects. The dialogue is the strongest element; the prose occasionally overreaches but rarely badly. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Chapter 4 – The Tabloids Find Him Friday morning smelled of wet dog and yesterday’s whisky. Carys woke on the shop’s ancient sofa with a crick in her neck and Rhys’s coat draped over her like a question. He was gone. Door locked from the inside, key still in the lock. No note. Only a faint dent in the cushion beside her and Dafydd snoring on her feet. She told herself the hollow feeling was just hunger. By eleven the rain had thinned to a spiteful drizzle. She opened the shop late, flipped the sign, and tried to pretend the night hadn’t happened. Dafydd kept staring at the spot where Rhys had sat like he expected him to materialise again and offer another pasty. At 11:47 her mother rang. “Have you seen the Mail?” Mair’s voice was pure Benson & Hedges gravel. “No.” “Well, you’re on it. Front of the sidebar of shame. Very flattering angle, mind. You look like you’re about to bite his face off.” Carys’s stomach dropped through the floorboards. She opened her phone with fingers that wouldn’t stop shaking. There it was. EXCLUSIVE: Rhys ap Llewelyn’s Mystery Welsh Fling – Is this the woman hiding the cancelled star? Grainy photo, obviously taken through the gap in the blind. Her locking the door at his shoulder, his hand already reaching for the light switch. The candlelight made them look like they were about to fuck against the true-crime section. Below it: another shot, older, from three years ago. Her leaving the agency building the night Sebastian’s story broke, eyes red, mouth set like she’d tasted something rotten. The caption didn’t even pretend subtlety. Disgraced literary agent Carys Powell, 34, sacked amid claims she pursued married boss… Her phone started vibrating so hard it danced off the counter. Unknown numbers, Nel in Cardiff, her cousin in Caernarfon, two journalists who’d once begged her for quotes when she was the one with power. She turned it face-down and stared at the wall until her vision blurred. The bell jingled. Rhys walked in carrying two coffees and a face like a man who’d spent the night walking the estuary in the rain. “I saw,” he said. “Get out.” “Carys—” “I said get out.” He didn’t. Put the coffees on the counter, careful, like they were made of glass. “I’m sorry.” “You’re sorry.” Her voice was flat, dangerous. “You brought them here. To my shop. To my mother’s doorstep.” “I didn’t—” “You did. You exist and they follow. That’s how it works.” She laughed, sharp. “I used to be the one sending photographers after people. Turns out karma’s punctual.” He rubbed the back of his neck until the skin went red. “Let me fix it.” “You can’t fix it. You’re the reason it’s broken.” Dafydd chose that moment to waddle over and bite Rhys’s ankle. Hard. Rhys didn’t even flinch. Just looked down at the dog like he deserved it. Carys felt something crack open behind her eyes. “My mam has cancer,” she said quietly. “She saw that photo before I did. She thinks I’m shagging a celebrity to get over Sebastian. She actually sounded proud.” Rhys opened his mouth. Closed it. “There’s nothing you can say,” she told him. “I know.” She moved past him, started stacking books with vicious precision. Hardbacks slammed into place like gunfire. He watched for a minute, then reached for his wallet. “Don’t,” she snapped. “Don’t you dare try to pay for this.” “I wasn’t—” He stopped. Pulled out something else instead. A folded piece of paper. Placed it on the counter between them. She didn’t look at it. “I wrote it at four a.m.,” he said. “In Welsh. Because English felt too easy.” She still didn’t look. He walked to the door, opened it. The bell gave its pathetic jangle. “I’ll delete every photo of you I have,” he said. “I’ll tell them you’re just the woman who owns the shop and I was sheltering from the rain. I’ll tell them whatever you want. Or nothing at all. Your choice.” The door shut behind him. Carys waited until his footsteps faded, then unfolded the paper. It was a page torn from a Moleskine. His handwriting was surprisingly neat. Sori am bopeth. Dw i ddim yn gwybod sut i fod yn berson go iawn heb gynulleidfa. Rwyt ti’n gwneud i mi eisiau dysgu. Os dwyt ti byth eisiau siarad eto, dw i yma. Os nag dwyt, dw i’n deall. Diolch am noson o wirionedd mewn byd llawn celwydd. Sorry for everything. I don’t know how to be a real person without an audience. You make me want to learn. If you ever want to talk again, I’m here. If not, I understand. Thank you for a night of truth in a world full of lies. She read it three times. Then carried it to the back room, fed it into the tiny woodburner she used for damaged stock, and watched it curl and blacken. The flames smelled like regret. She didn’t cry until the last corner turned to ash. Then she locked the door, turned the sign to CAEEDIG at noon, and didn’t open again for four days.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of emotionally charged short fiction that largely delivers on its plan and the broader writing prompt. Let me assess it across several dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter follows its plan faithfully. The plan called for: a grainy photo hitting the Mail Online, her mother seeing it first, London past resurrected, Carys icing him out, and him leaving a note in Welsh that she burns. All of these beats land. The plan's note that "she burns it, then cries because it was grammatically perfect and she taught him nothing" is slightly altered—the chapter doesn't make the grammar point explicit—but the emotional logic is preserved and arguably improved by not over-explaining. The dialogue is genuinely strong. "Get out" / "I said get out" / "You can't fix it. You're the reason it's broken" — these are terse, character-specific, and carry weight. The exchange about her mother sounding "proud" is a particularly sharp piece of characterisation that reveals multiple layers simultaneously: Mair's personality, Carys's shame, the absurdity of the situation. The dialogue does the heavy lifting as promised in the writing plan. Character consistency is excellent. Rhys's self-awareness ("I don't know how to be a real person without an audience") is perfectly calibrated—it's the kind of thing a fragile egotist says when genuinely cracked open, not a redemption speech. His rubbing the back of his neck until it goes red is a nice callback to the character profile's idiosyncrasies. Carys's flat, dangerous voice, her vicious precision with the books, her refusal to look at the note immediately—all consistent with her armour-of-sarcasm profile. The Welsh note is handled beautifully. The decision to include the translation in the text (unlike the plan's instruction to never translate Welsh in-text) is actually a defensible deviation—it works here because the note is a formal document of feeling, and the reader needs to feel its weight. The Welsh itself is grammatically plausible and emotionally resonant. The Dafydd moment (biting Rhys's ankle, Rhys not flinching) is a small gem—it earns its lightness without undercutting the darkness. WEAKNESSES: "The flames smelled like regret" is the chapter's most egregious line—a cliché dressed up as poetry. It's the kind of thing that sounds meaningful but is actually empty. This is the clearest instance of forced metaphor. "Her stomach dropped through the floorboards" is a tired idiom that doesn't earn its place in otherwise precise prose. The opening paragraph—"Rhys's coat draped over her like a question"—is a slightly strained metaphor. A coat draped like a question is the kind of associative leap that sounds good but doesn't quite cohere on examination. What does a question look like when draped? It's reaching. "The candlelight made them look like they were about to fuck against the true-crime section" is vivid and specific, but the true-crime section detail feels slightly too cute—a self-conscious wink at the reader. The chapter is perhaps slightly too clean in its emotional arc. The plan promised rawness and the chapter delivers competent rawness, but there's a tidiness to the beats (photo → confrontation → note → burning → crying → closing shop) that feels slightly schematic. Real emotional chaos is messier. The chapter earns its darkness but it's well-earned in a somewhat predictable sequence. The translation of the Welsh note in-text contradicts the writing plan's "absolute ban" on translating Welsh. This is a meaningful deviation, though arguably the right creative choice. "Hardbacks slammed into place like gunfire" is functional but not distinguished. Overall, this is confident, controlled writing that serves its characters and story well. The dialogue is the strongest element; the occasional reach for poetic effect is the weakest. It's not a masterpiece but it's genuinely good commercial literary fiction. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Chapter 5 – Drunk Text in the Mother Tongue Christmas Eve. The shop stayed shut. The town was drunk on chapel wine and fairy lights, but inside Llyfrau’r Hen Bost it was just Carys, Dafydd, and a bottle of Penderyn she’d been saving for a worse day. The worse day had arrived. She sat on the floor behind the counter in her coat because the heating had given up again. Dafydd snored on a pile of unsold calendars featuring tasteful photos of Welsh graves. The bottle was half gone and her cheeks burned. She kept seeing the note burning. Kept hearing his voice reading it aloud in her head, perfect north-Walian vowels she’d never taught him. Kept feeling the exact moment his mouth had softened against hers before Dafydd pissed on everything. She hated him for making her want things. Her phone lay on the carpet like an accusation. She’d turned it off after the third journalist left a voicemail pretending to be an old friend. She turned it on now. 23:47. One new message. From two weeks ago. Rhys. Just checking you’re okay. No need to reply. R. She stared at it until the screen went dark. Then she opened voice notes, pressed record before the whisky could talk her out of it. Heavy breathing for five seconds. Then, in Welsh, low and furious and trembling: “Dw i’n dy gasáu di achos dw i eisiau dy gusanu di.” I hate you because I want to kiss you. She sent it before she could hear how it sounded. Then threw the phone across the room. It hit a stack of Alis Hawkins novels with a soft thud. “Ffyc,” she told Dafydd. He opened one eye, judged her, went back to sleep. She drank straight from the bottle until the room tilted gently, like a boat. At 01:14 the bell jingled. She froze. The lock clicked. Key in the door. She’d never given him a key. Rhys stepped inside, soaked again, hair plastered to his forehead, eyes wild. Carrying nothing but the dark and the smell of cold night air. He looked at her on the floor, bottle in hand, mascara halfway down her face. “You came,” she said. Welsh. Stupid. “You sent me a drunk voice note in the mother tongue,” he answered, same language. “What was I supposed to do, ignore it?” She laughed. It came out wet. He shut the door, locked it again, leaned back against it like he needed the support. “I was on the train,” he said, switching to English now, voice rough. “From London. Couldn’t stay away. Pathetic, isn’t it?” “Yes.” He crossed the room in three strides, crouched in front of her. Reached out, stopped himself, hands hovering. “Carys.” “Don’t.” “Don’t what?” “Don’t be gentle. I’ll fall apart.” He kissed her instead. This time there was no candlelit hesitation. She dragged him down by his coat, mouths clumsy, teeth clacking, tasting whisky and rain and two weeks of pretending she didn’t miss him. He groaned into her mouth like it hurt. They knocked over the bottle. Whisky spread across the carpet in a dark stain. “Carped,” she muttered against his lips. “Fuck the carpet.” Clothes came off in frantic stages. Her coat first, then his, then her jumper snagging on her watch. His hands were shaking. So were hers. He pushed her gently until her back hit the counter. She hooked a leg around his hip, pulled him closer. The edge of the counter dug into her spine and she didn’t care. Buttons scattered. She bit his shoulder through his t-shirt and he swore in Welsh, beautiful and filthy. They ended up on the floor among the spill of whisky and paperbacks. A box of returns became collateral damage; something cracked satisfyingly. He paused once, breathing hard against her neck. “Tell me to stop and I will.” “Don’t you fucking dare.” Afterwards they lay tangled on the carpet, hearts hammering, skin cooling. Dafydd had retreated to the poetry section in disgust. Carys started laughing first. Helpless, hiccupping sobs that weren’t quite crying. Rhys lifted his head, hair sticking up in all directions. “What?” “My knee,” she wheezed. “I think I dislocated it on a box of Bethan Gwanas.” He looked down at where her leg was bent at an improbable angle, started laughing too. The sound turned into something that might have been crying. She kissed him again, softer this time. Tasted salt. “I hate you,” she whispered. “I know.” “I hate that I missed you.” “I know.” She buried her face in his neck. He held her like she was something precious and breakable and already broken. Outside, somewhere, a church bell struck two. Inside, the shop smelled of whisky, sex, and old paper. Carys’s phone buzzed on the carpet. She ignored it. Rhys traced a finger down her spine, reverent. “How long can you stay?” she asked the dark. “As long as you’ll have me.” “That’s not an answer.” “It’s the only one I’ve got.” She nodded against his chest. Felt his heart still racing. Dafydd farted from the poetry section, a definitive editorial statement. They both started laughing again, breathless and ridiculous and alive. Neither of them said “I love you.” It was too soon, too dangerous, and far too late.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 5 executes its plan with considerable skill and mostly earns what it reaches for. Let me work through the strengths and weaknesses systematically. STRENGTHS: The chapter follows its plan faithfully: Christmas Eve, Penderyn, the voice note, the 2 a.m. arrival, sex against the counter (adjusted to floor/counter area), the fumbling and laughing, and Carys crying at the end (here rendered as "helpless, hiccupping sobs that weren't quite crying" — a smart softening). The plan's "knee in a box of returns" becomes "knee on a box of Bethan Gwanas," which is a nice Welsh-specific detail that improves on the generic plan. The dialogue is genuinely strong. "Don't be gentle. I'll fall apart" is the best line in the chapter — it's character-specific, emotionally precise, and advances both the plot and the characterisation simultaneously. The exchange "Tell me to stop and I will" / "Don't you fucking dare" is well-earned and consistent with Carys's voice. The Welsh voice note and its translation are handled exactly as planned, and the decision not to translate it in-text (consistent with the stated style rule) is respected. Dafydd the corgi's editorial interventions (retreating to poetry in disgust, the fart) are genuinely funny and not forced — the lightness is well-earned rather than cute-for-cute's-sake. The ending — "Neither of them said 'I love you.' / It was too soon, too dangerous, and far too late." — is the kind of line that could tip into purple territory but just about lands because the paradox ("too soon" and "far too late" simultaneously) is emotionally true to the situation. Character consistency is high. Rhys arrives soaked, impulsive, unable to stay away — consistent with his pathological need to be wanted and his genuine feeling breaking through. Carys's self-recrimination, her Welsh slipping out, her "I hate you" / "I know" exchange — all consistent with her armour-of-sarcasm profile. WEAKNESSES: "She drank straight from the bottle until the room tilted gently, like a boat" — this is the chapter's weakest metaphor. It's not bad, but it's generic. The plan promised specificity and this is a stock image. "He held her like she was something precious and breakable and already broken" — this tips into purple prose. The triple construction ("precious and breakable and already broken") is the kind of thing that sounds profound but is actually a fairly standard romance-novel formulation. Given that Carys writes romance novels, there's a meta-reading available, but the text doesn't invite it. "Tasted salt" — stock image for tears/emotion in intimate scenes. The chapter is otherwise specific enough that this stands out as a lazy shortcut. The transition from the voice note to his arrival is slightly rushed. He was "on the train from London" — so he was already coming back before the voice note? This is a small coherence issue. The plan says "He listens to it 47 times on the train back from London" which implies he received it on the train, but the chapter has him arriving at 01:14 after a voice note sent at 23:47 — London to Machynlleth is roughly 3.5-4 hours by train, so the timing is implausible unless he was already very close. This is a minor but real plot coherence problem. "Clothes came off in frantic stages" is slightly tell-don't-show — the subsequent specifics (coat, jumper snagging on watch, buttons scattering) recover this, but the opening sentence of that paragraph is weak. The chapter is slightly under the 950-1050 word discipline stated in the plan — it reads closer to 850-900 words. Not a major issue but worth noting. Overall, this is a confident, well-executed chapter that delivers on its emotional promises, maintains distinct voices, earns its comedy, and avoids most of the pitfalls the plan warned against. The weaknesses are real but minor — a few stock images, one timing implausibility, one purple construction. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 14 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Chapter 6 – The Week They Don’t Speak January arrived like a hangover: grey, sour, and impossible to ignore. The shop opened every day at ten and closed at five, same as always, except now there were two people in it pretending the other didn’t exist. Rhys slept in the back room on the narrow sofa that smelled of dog and damp. He made coffee strong enough to wake the dead and left her mug on the counter without a word. She drank it black and scalded her tongue on purpose. They moved around each other like mismatched magnets (close enough to feel the pull, far enough to avoid the snap). At night they fucked with the lights off and the radio on loud enough to cover the noises. Afterwards he reached for her and she let him hold her for exactly thirty seconds before rolling away to the cold side of whatever surface they’d ruined. They never used the sofa for sleeping anymore; it felt too much like sharing a bed. Mornings were the worst. He showered in the tiny staff toilet, singing old Super Furry Animals songs off-key. She pretended to be fascinated by the accounts while listening to water drum on tin and hating how much she liked the sound of him existing in her space. On the fifth day she found the money. He’d been doing it for weeks, apparently. Every time he thought she wasn’t looking, he’d slide a folded fifty or a hundred into random books—inside a hollowed-out copy of the Mabinogi, between the pages of a Welsh dictionary, tucked into the sleeve of a Sion Aled poetry collection like a love letter made of guilt. She discovered six hundred and forty pounds in total, all in crisp notes that smelled of his pockets. Carys waited until closing, until the blind was down and Dafydd was asleep under the radiator. Then she marched into the back room where Rhys was pretending to read scripts on his phone. He looked up, wary. Shirt unbuttoned, hair still wet from another shower. Beautiful and temporary and hers in the worst way. She dropped the bundle of cash on the table between them. It landed with a soft, obscene thud. “Don’t,” she said. Welsh, cold as January rain. He closed his eyes. “Carys—” “Don’t you ever treat me like something you can buy off.” “I’m not—” “You are. You think money fixes things. You think it fixes me.” He stood up slowly. “The shop’s behind on rates. I heard you on the phone to the council. I didn’t want you to lose it.” “So you snuck around like a fucking charity case?” “I didn’t want you to have to ask.” “I would rather lose the shop than owe you.” The words hung there, brutal and true. Rhys went very pale. “Right.” He reached past her, grabbed his coat from the hook. Started putting his shoes on. “What are you doing?” “Leaving. You want me gone, I’m gone.” “Just like that?” “You said—” “I said don’t treat me like a problem you can solve with money. I didn’t say leave.” He laughed, short and ugly. “You never say what you mean, Carys. I’m tired of translating.” She blocked the doorway. “And what do you mean, then? Hiding cash in books like I’m your mistress you pay off?” “I mean I’m trying to help the only way I know how and you punish me for it.” “Because I don’t want your help!” “Then what do you want?” The question cracked open the room. She stared at him. He stared back. Dafydd whined from the shop floor, sensing war. Carys felt her throat close. She wanted to scream: I want you to stay without turning me into someone who needs saving. I want to wake up without checking if you’re still here. I want to stop waiting for the moment you realise I’m not worth the mess. Instead she said, “Get out.” This time he did. He walked past her without touching, opened the door, stepped into the sleet that had started while they were tearing strips off each other. He was barefoot. Hadn’t put his socks on. Left them in a damp pile by the sofa. She watched him walk down the empty high street in his socks, coat flapping, until he turned the corner by the clock tower and disappeared. The door shut behind him with the soft finality of something ending. Carys stood there for a long time. Then she picked up the money, carried it to the woodburner, and fed it in one note at a time. The flames took it greedily. Dafydd came and sat beside her, rested his head on her knee. She scratched his ears and told him he was a good boy. Neither of them slept that night. Outside, the sleet turned to snow, thin and mean and Welsh, covering Machynlleth in a silence that felt like punishment.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 6 of "Love in the Limelight" is a strong piece of romantic fiction that largely delivers on its plan and the broader story's ambitions. Let me assess it carefully across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter follows its plan faithfully. The plan called for: January icy silence while sleeping together nightly, both waiting for the other to end it, discovery of hidden cash, explosion, "Thought you'd rather I paid like every other customer," throwing him out in his socks, and a real break rather than a sexy fight. The chapter delivers all of these beats, including the socks detail (which lands with genuine pathos—him walking barefoot in sleet is a strong image). The cash-burning at the end is an addition not in the plan but feels earned and character-consistent. The dialogue is genuinely good. It's terse, specific, and carries the weight of the scene without over-explaining. "I'm tired of translating" is an excellent line—it works literally (he's been trying to decode her signals) and metaphorically (the Welsh/English dynamic, the emotional labor). The exchange "I didn't say leave" / "You never say what you mean" is sharp and true to both characters. The dialogue avoids the trap of having characters articulate their feelings too cleanly; instead they talk around the wound. Character consistency is strong. Rhys's gesture of hiding money is perfectly calibrated to his profile—he's a fixer, a charmer, someone who uses resources as love language because he doesn't know another way. Carys's fury at being "bought off" connects directly to her backstory (the NDA payout, being managed out of her career with money). The chapter doesn't spell this out, which is the right call. Her internal monologue ("I want you to stay without turning me into someone who needs saving") is the most explicit the narration gets, and it's earned. The prose is controlled. The opening paragraph ("January arrived like a hangover: grey, sour, and impossible to ignore") is a clean, functional metaphor that doesn't overstay its welcome. The "mismatched magnets" simile is slightly more mechanical but acceptable. The snow ending—"thin and mean and Welsh"—is a nice touch that earns its adjectives. The Dafydd detail (corgi sensing war, resting head on knee) is used sparingly and effectively, not as comic relief but as emotional punctuation. WEAKNESSES: The chapter is somewhat interior-heavy in places. The paragraph beginning "She wanted to scream: I want you to stay without turning me into someone who needs saving" is the most tell-don't-show moment in the piece. The plan called for dialogue to do the heavy lifting, and here the narration steps in to do what the dialogue should be doing. It's not egregious, but it's the chapter's clearest weakness. The cash-burning scene is dramatically satisfying but slightly melodramatic. Burning £640 in cash is a strong gesture, but it risks tipping into the kind of grand symbolic action the story's aesthetic is trying to avoid. It works, but it's the moment where the chapter comes closest to purple territory. The "mismatched magnets (close enough to feel the pull, far enough to avoid the snap)" uses parentheses oddly—this reads like a formatting quirk rather than a stylistic choice, and it slightly disrupts the prose rhythm. The ending, while effective, is perhaps a touch too neat in its imagery. "Silence that felt like punishment" is competent but not surprising. The chapter's best moments are more specific than this. The chapter doesn't quite achieve the "real break, not sexy fight" distinction the plan promised—it reads more like a very serious fight that happens to end in separation, which is close but not quite the same register. The plan suggested something colder and more final; this still has heat in it. Overall, this is accomplished, emotionally intelligent fiction that serves its characters and its story well. It's not a masterpiece, but it's genuinely good work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Chapter 7 – Calan Gaeaf 1 February. Old New Year’s Eve. The town had already celebrated the new one with fireworks and drunks in the Clock Tower, but the old calendar still claimed its night, quiet and secret and properly dark. Carys was doing stocktake. The shop was closed to the public, radio off, only the low hum of the fridge and the scratch of her pen. She moved between the shelves like a ghost, counting copies of Waldo Williams and wondering whether she could bear to sell the last first edition of Kate Roberts before the bailiffs came. Dafydd was asleep on the counter, paws twitching after rabbits. She hadn’t seen Rhys in nine days. He had not come back for his socks, his charger, the half-read script annotated in his cramped handwriting. He had simply vanished (London, LA, anywhere but here). The silence was perfect and excruciating. At half past eight she went into the back room to fetch another box of Welsh classics. He was there. Sitting on the floor, headphones round his neck, phone on his knee, face lit by the screen. A single candle burned on the table, the same church candle from the night the power went out. He looked up when she stopped dead in the doorway. “Nos da,” he said, soft. Welsh, like they’d never stopped speaking it. She couldn’t move. He lifted the phone. “I’ve been recording myself reading your book.” She stared. “Not for anyone,” he added quickly. “Just for me. The one you wrote as Ceridwen Gwyn. Y Llyfr Anadl. I found it on the shelf a month ago and… I needed to hear what your words sound like in my mouth when I’m not trying to impress you.” He pressed play. His own voice, low, hesitant, beautiful north-Walian, filled the tiny room. “…ac yno, yn y tywyllwch rhwng y ddwy galon, fe welodd hi’r gwirionedd: cariad yw’r unig beth sy’n ein lladd ni ac yn ein cadw ni’n fyw yr un pryd…” Carys felt her knees give. She sat down hard on the floor opposite him, back against a tower of boxes. He stopped the recording. “I’m shit at pronouncing some of it,” he said. “But I kept trying until it felt like you.” Silence. She swallowed. “You came back.” “I never really left. Been sleeping in the back of the White Lion. Landlord thinks I’m a depressed rep.” Another silence. “I’m sorry,” he said. “About the money. About everything. I don’t know how to be near you without trying to fix things.” “I don’t want fixing.” “I know. That’s why I’m terrified.” She looked at him then, really looked. The candle had carved shadows under his eyes. He’d lost weight. His hands shook slightly as they rested on his knees. “I miss you,” she said. The words came out small, scraped raw. “I miss you when I’m in the same room as you.” She laughed once, wetly. “We’re hopeless.” “Completely.” Dafydd padded in, took one look at Rhys, and (miracle of miracles) did not bite him. Just sighed and lay down between them like a furry referee. Rhys reached out slowly, palm up on the floorboards. An offering. She stared at it for a long time. Then she slid her hand into his. His fingers closed tight, like he was scared she’d vanish. “I can’t promise I won’t fuck this up,” he said. “I can’t promise I’ll let you stay if you do.” “That’s enough.” They sat like that until the candle burned low, hands linked, breathing in the same air for the first time in weeks without armour. Eventually he spoke, so quietly she almost missed it. “I’m in love with you, Carys Powell. And it’s the least convenient thing that’s ever happened to me.” She turned her head, rested her cheek on her raised knee so she could see him. “I know the feeling,” she said. He smiled then, small, crooked, real. Outside, the sleet had stopped. The town was silent, holding its breath. Inside, two people who had spent years performing versions of themselves finally shut up and listened to the quiet. She leaned across the space between them and kissed him. Not hungry this time. Careful. Like learning a new language. When they pulled apart, foreheads still touching, she whispered, “Stay tonight.” “I’ve nowhere else to be.” They left the candle burning. Later, much later, wrapped in blankets on the sofa that now officially belonged to both of them, he asked, “What happens when I have to go back? Reshoots in March. Morocco.” “I don’t know.” “Me neither.” She traced the line of his jaw. “We’ll fight about it when we get there.” “Proper fight?” “North Walian screaming match. Plates may be involved.” He laughed into her neck. “I look forward to it.” They fell asleep tangled together, Dafydd snoring at their feet, the candle finally guttering out and leaving them in the kind of dark that feels safe. Outside, the old year slipped away unnoticed. Inside, the new one began without promises, only the stubborn refusal to let go just yet.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 7 of "Love in the Limelight" is a pivotal reconciliation chapter, and it largely succeeds on its own terms while showing some notable weaknesses worth examining critically. **Strengths:** The chapter follows its plan faithfully: Rhys is discovered reading Carys's secret novel aloud, there's no grand gesture or apology speech, they talk until dawn (implied), and they end holding hands in weak winter light (here translated to a candle-lit back room and a sofa). The core conceit—him recording himself reading her words "just to hear what your words sound like in my mouth when I'm not trying to impress you"—is genuinely inventive and emotionally resonant. It's the right kind of vulnerability for this character: not a speech, not flowers, but an act of private devotion that accidentally gets witnessed. This is well-conceived. The Welsh language integration is handled with confidence. The excerpt from the fictional novel ("ac yno, yn y tywyllwch rhwng y ddwy galon...") is beautiful and appropriately thematic without being translated or over-explained, exactly as the plan promised. This is a genuine craft achievement. Dafydd the corgi's moment—not biting Rhys for once, lying between them "like a furry referee"—is a small, earned piece of lightness that works without being cloying. The plan called for no "corgi ex machina," and this just barely avoids that trap; the dog is used for gentle comedy rather than plot resolution. The dialogue is generally strong. "I miss you when I'm in the same room as you" is a good line. "I can't promise I won't fuck this up" / "I can't promise I'll let you stay if you do" / "That's enough" is a clean, character-consistent exchange. The Morocco/reshoots conversation at the end is appropriately unresolved and honest. **Weaknesses:** The chapter's biggest problem is that it resolves too cleanly for what the plan promised. The plan said "no promises," "terrified, honest," and "knowing one of them will have to leave eventually and choosing not to think about it yet." The chapter delivers this in the final paragraphs, but the emotional journey to get there is smoother than it should be. Carys capitulates relatively quickly—she sits down, she laughs, she reaches for his hand, she kisses him—without the resistance and internal conflict that defines her character. The plan's Carys would make him work harder. The "scraped raw" quality of her admission ("I miss you") is told rather than shown; we're told the words came out "small, scraped raw" rather than experiencing the difficulty through behavior or hesitation. Several lines tip into sentimentality that the plan explicitly wanted to avoid: "the kind of dark that feels safe," "the old year slipped away unnoticed / Inside, the new one began without promises" are the kind of closing flourishes that feel slightly too neat, too poetic in a greeting-card way. The plan's "absolute bans" included epilogue-style resolution, and while this isn't technically an epilogue, the final paragraphs have that quality. "She felt her knees give" is a cliché. "Carved shadows under his eyes" is competent but not distinctive. The chapter tells us he'd "lost weight" and his hands "shook slightly"—these are efficient but not particularly vivid or specific to this character. The chapter plan specified they "talk until dawn, terrified, honest, no promises" and "walk out into weak winter sun, holding hands like teenagers." The actual chapter compresses this: we get the hand-holding but skip the long honest conversation, jumping instead to them already asleep on the sofa. This is a significant deviation—the plan's most emotionally complex element (the long, terrified, honest conversation) is elided entirely. We're told they "finally shut up and listened to the quiet" rather than shown the conversation that earns the reconciliation. Rhys's confession—"I'm in love with you, Carys Powell. And it's the least convenient thing that's ever happened to me"—is a bit too polished, too actorly, for a moment the plan wanted to feel raw and unperformed. It sounds like a line he'd deliver on screen, which could be intentional irony, but isn't framed that way. The chapter is emotionally engaging and largely coherent, with characters consistent with their profiles. It follows the chapter plan in broad strokes but softens the edges considerably. The writing is competent to good throughout, occasionally excellent (the recording conceit, the Welsh excerpt), occasionally slipping into comfortable sentiment. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 8 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Characters Consistent with Profile: 14 Followed Chapter Plan: 12 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Chapter 8 – Open Ending 14 February, St Valentine’s Day. Carys woke before dawn to the sound of Rhys making tea exactly right (strong, splash of milk, no sugar, mug warmed first). The ritual had taken him three weeks of trial and error and one near-death experience when she’d thrown a cold cup at his head. Dafydd was asleep on Rhys’s chest, paws in the air, snoring like a broken accordion. The dog had defected completely. Traitor. She watched them from the doorway of the back room (now officially their bedroom, though it still smelled of old paper and damp dog). Rhys wore yesterday’s t-shirt and the ridiculous Welsh-flag boxer shorts she’d bought him as a joke. His hair stuck up in soft curls. He looked younger asleep. Less like a cautionary tale, more like a man who might stay. She padded over, took the spare mug from his hand before he could wake Dafydd. “Nos da,” she murmured. He blinked awake, smiled sleepy and crooked. “Bore da, cariad.” The word still startled her every time. They used it sparingly, like expensive whisky. He shifted the dog gently onto the sofa and sat up, rubbing his face. “Post’s here.” A fat envelope lay on the mat. London postmark. His agent, probably. They both stared at it. “Open it,” she said. He did. Read it once, twice. Face unreadable. “Morocco’s moved up,” he said finally. “They want me there first week of March. Six weeks minimum. Maybe eight.” The room felt suddenly too small. She nodded. “Right.” He put the letter down carefully. “I can say no.” “You can’t.” “I can. I’m tired of being their performing monkey.” “You’re also tired of being cancelled. This is the way back in.” He looked at her then, raw. “And if I go, what happens to us?” “I don’t know.” Silence stretched. She picked up the letter, scanned it. Fat fee. First-class flights. Per diems that could pay the shop’s rates for a year. She put it back down. “I’m not asking you to choose,” she said. “Good. Because I don’t know how.” Dafydd farted in his sleep. The tension broke; they both laughed, shaky. Rhys reached for her hand. “Come with me.” “To Morocco?” “Or anywhere. We’ll figure it out.” “I have a shop. A mother. A life.” “I know.” Another silence. She stood up, walked to the window. The high street was still dark, just the orange glow of streetlights on wet slate. Valentine’s cards in the Spar window already looked tired. “I wrote something,” she said without turning round. “Yeah?” She pulled a folded sheet from her pocket, handed it to him. He opened it. It was addressed to herself, undated. Carys fach, It’s some morning in the future and I’m writing this because I still don’t know how to say it out loud. He makes the tea exactly right now. Dafydd sleeps on his chest like he belongs there. The shop is still leaking money but the roof held through January. His agent rings every day and he lets it go to voicemail. He’s leaving for Morocco in twelve days. Or he’s not. We keep circling the conversation like it’s a live wire. I caught him reading my new manuscript last night. He cried at chapter nine and pretended it was onions. There were no onions. We still fight about stupid things (whose turn to take the bins, whether Super Furry Animals peak period is Fuzzy or Rings). The making-up is better than it has any right to be. I still don’t trust this. I still wake up some nights convinced he’ll be gone and the socks will finally have walked themselves out the door. But for the first time I’m not writing the ending in advance. I’m letting it happen to me. That’ll have to be enough. If you’re reading this and he stayed — good. We earned it. If you’re reading this and he went — good. You survived it. Either way, you’re still here. And so am I. Cariad mawr, Carys Rhys read it twice. Folded it carefully, handed it back. “I love you,” he said. Simple. Welsh vowels soft around the edges. “I know,” she answered. He stood, crossed to her, wrapped his arms round her from behind. They watched the town wake up together. The first delivery van. The baker opening up. A couple of teenagers snogging by the clock tower like they’d invented it. “Whatever happens,” he said against her hair, “I’m not disappearing again. Even if I’m in Morocco, I’m here. Every bloody day.” She turned in his arms, kissed him slow and certain. “Good,” she said. “Because I’m not done shouting at you yet.” He laughed into her mouth. Outside, the sun came up properly, weak winter light sliding over wet roofs and making everything look possible for five minutes. Inside, the kettle boiled again. Dafydd demanded breakfast. The shop would open at ten, or maybe eleven. The rates were still due. London would keep calling. None of it mattered as much as the small ordinary miracle of two people choosing to stay in the same room even when it was hard. They didn’t know how the story ended. They were finally brave enough not to need to.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is Chapter 8, the final chapter of a romance set in Wales, and it functions as a deliberately open-ended coda to the story. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. **Strengths:** The chapter follows its plan faithfully. The writing plan called for "One page of a letter she's writing to her future self, undated" with a last line about not writing the ending in advance, and the chapter delivers this almost exactly. The letter itself is the strongest element — it has genuine emotional intelligence, the conditional structure ("If you're reading this and he stayed — good. We earned it. / If you're reading this and he went — good. You survived it.") is genuinely moving and avoids sentimentality through its pragmatic framing. This is the chapter's best moment. The characters are consistent with their profiles. Carys's armor-through-sarcasm is present ("Because I'm not done shouting at you yet"), Rhys's vulnerability and the Welsh vowels detail are nicely handled. Dafydd the corgi's flatulence breaking tension is consistent with the plan's use of the dog as comic deflation. The "I love you" / "I know" exchange is a nice Han Solo inversion that suits both characters. The dialogue is generally strong and in distinct voices. The Morocco letter as plot device works well — it creates genuine stakes without resolving them, which is exactly what the open ending requires. **Weaknesses:** The chapter's biggest structural problem is that it leans heavily on telling in its final paragraphs. "None of it mattered as much as the small ordinary miracle of two people choosing to stay in the same room even when it was hard" and "They were finally brave enough not to need to" are exactly the kind of thesis-statement endings that undercut the show-don't-tell discipline the rest of the story seems to have maintained. The writing plan explicitly wanted to avoid tidy resolution, but these closing lines editorialize in a way that feels like the author stepping in to explain the meaning rather than trusting the reader. "Making everything look possible for five minutes" is a nice qualification, but "the small ordinary miracle" is a cliché of the genre. The final two lines ("They didn't know how the story ended. / They were finally brave enough not to need to.") are the weakest in the chapter — they're the kind of lines that sound profound but are actually just stating the theme directly. The "Super Furry Animals peak period is Fuzzy or Rings" detail in the letter is excellent — specific, funny, Welsh, and character-revealing. This is the kind of detail the writing plan promised. The "Dafydd farted in his sleep" moment is well-deployed — it's the kind of earned lightness the plan called for. The opening sequence (tea ritual, dog on chest, boxer shorts) is competent but slightly over-familiar as a "domestic morning after" scene. It does the job without being particularly surprising. The "weak winter light sliding over wet roofs and making everything look possible for five minutes" is a good image — the qualification "for five minutes" saves it from being purple. Overall, this is a solid, emotionally competent chapter that mostly delivers on its plan. Its main failing is the editorializing at the end, which breaks the discipline of showing over telling and slightly undercuts the open ending by explaining it too explicitly. The letter is genuinely good. The dialogue is functional and in-character. The chapter avoids the "absolute bans" from the plan (no saviour gestures, no fixed epilogue, no learning the true meaning of anything). It's a good chapter with a weak final paragraph. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 8 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a genuinely accomplished piece of creative writing that demonstrates considerable craft, though not without some weaknesses worth examining critically. **Strengths:** The dialogue is the piece's greatest achievement. Both characters have distinct, consistent voices throughout all eight chapters. Carys's flat, sardonic Welsh wit ("Congratulations. You've found Wales. We specialise in wet.") never wavers, and Rhys's charm-offensive-meets-genuine-vulnerability is rendered with real skill. The banter feels earned rather than constructed. The Welsh language is woven in naturally and meaningfully, not as decoration but as character revelation — Rhys reading Welsh silently, his note written in Welsh because "English felt too easy," the drunk voice note in the mother tongue. This is excellent craft. Dafydd the corgi is a masterstroke — a recurring comic device that never outstays its welcome and serves genuine narrative functions (interrupting the first kiss, the pissing on the shoe, the defection to Rhys's side as a marker of relationship progress). The dog's flatulence as editorial commentary is used perhaps once too often but generally lands. The setting of Machynlleth is specific and felt — the flooding Dyfi, the Spar, the Clock Tower, the White Lion — without becoming a tourist brochure. The Welsh cultural references (Kate Roberts, Caradog Prichard, Super Furry Animals, Penderyn whisky) are deployed with genuine knowledge rather than Googled authenticity. **Character work:** Rhys is written exactly as the prompt requests — charming rogue with fragile ego — and the piece successfully shows his genuine transformation without making it feel unearned. The detail of him hiding money in books is psychologically astute: it's both generous and controlling, both caring and condescending, and Carys's fury at it is completely believable. His recording himself reading her novel is a beautiful character beat. Carys is well-drawn: the backstory of the literary agency scandal is revealed economically and explains her wariness without over-explaining it. Her burning the note, burning the money — these are consistent character actions that feel true. **Weaknesses:** The piece occasionally tips into self-conscious poeticism that doesn't quite land. "Candle-flame width" as a measurement of distance is the kind of phrase that sounds good but is actually imprecise and slightly precious. "The flames smelled like regret" is a cliché dressed up as poetry. "Two people who had spent years performing versions of themselves finally shut up and listened to the quiet" is the kind of closing-chapter summation that tells rather than shows. The pacing in the middle chapters (4-6) is somewhat compressed — the relationship escalates very quickly from first kiss to cohabitation to major fight, and while individual scenes are well-written, the emotional logic occasionally feels rushed. The Christmas Eve scene (Chapter 5) moves from drunk voice note to sex to tender aftermath very rapidly, and the transformation from "get out" to passionate reunion happens in a few paragraphs. The letter Carys writes to herself in Chapter 8 is a structural risk that partially pays off but also partially tells us what we've already been shown. It's a little too neat, a little too self-aware about its own emotional content. Some metaphors are strained: "beard doing a reasonable impression of a drowned badger" is trying too hard. "Eyes red-rimmed (jetlag or something stronger, she couldn't tell and didn't care)" — the parenthetical is a tic that appears several times and becomes noticeable. "Shoulders that had carried entire franchises" is clever but slightly too clever. The ending is appropriately open but the final lines ("They were finally brave enough not to need to") veer toward the kind of summarizing wisdom that slightly undercuts the show-don't-tell discipline maintained elsewhere. **Prompt faithfulness:** The piece is highly faithful to the prompt. The first chapter nails the brief precisely — the actor's charm offensive, Carys's apparent immunity, the undercurrent of chemistry, the dialogue-driven scene. The story progresses organically beyond the synopsis. The Welsh setting is integral rather than decorative. **Overall:** This is a strong, confident piece of romantic fiction with genuine literary ambition. The Welsh cultural specificity, the quality of the dialogue, and the psychological nuance of both characters elevate it well above genre competence. Its weaknesses are real but relatively minor: occasional over-poeticism, some pacing compression in the middle, and a few moments where the authorial voice intrudes too visibly. It is not a masterpiece, but it is genuinely good work. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 17 Pacing: 13 World Building: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Unearned Transformations: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18
Avg Final Item Score: 77.2/100
+ Classic Sci-Fi — The Azra Gambit (Chapter Avg: 75.1 | Final: 64.1)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Classic sci-fi (Author style: Asimov) The Azra Gambit Task: write a short story over several chapters. Colonial mars is being mined by corporations who take leases on indentured labourers. The thing they are mining is Azra, a recently discovered exotic metal which accelerates radioactive decay to such a rate that it is greatly sought after for interstellar drives and weapons alike. This has created both a gold rush and an arms race as various interests vie for control and endeavour to unlock Azra's secrets. The story follows Arthur Neegan, a first generation settler and mining engineer. Upon discovering that his unassuming plot sits atop an immense Azra vein, he is subjected to a flurry of interest and scrutiny. Begin the story as an armed retinue descends on Arthur's home and politely but forcefully invites him to a meeting with some unknown party off-world. The insignia look like that of the Antares diplomatic corp -- diplomatic in name only. Arthur finds himself in the centre of a political tug of war. Arthur meets with this unknown party, who makes an offer. This early scene should be primarily dialogue, interspersed with vivid description & scene setting. It should sow hints of the larger intrigue, stakes & dangers. Include Asimov's trademark big-and-small-picture world building and retrofuturistic classic scifi vibe. The first chapter begins with Arthur aboard the transfer vessel, wondering just what he's gotten involved in. The remainder of the story is open to your choices & direction.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Classic sci-fi (Author style: Asimov) The Azra Gambit Task: write a short story over several chapters. Colonial mars is being mined by corporations who take leases on indentured labourers. The thing they are mining is Azra, a recently discovered exotic metal which accelerates radioactive decay to such a rate that it is greatly sought after for interstellar drives and weapons alike. This has created both a gold rush and an arms race as various interests vie for control and endeavour to unlock Azra&#x27;s secrets. The story follows Arthur Neegan, a first generation settler and mining engineer. Upon discovering that his unassuming plot sits atop an immense Azra vein, he is subjected to a flurry of interest and scrutiny. Begin the story as an armed retinue descends on Arthur&#x27;s home and politely but forcefully invites him to a meeting with some unknown party off-world. The insignia look like that of the Antares diplomatic corp -- diplomatic in name only. Arthur finds himself in the centre of a political tug of war. Arthur meets with this unknown party, who makes an offer. This early scene should be primarily dialogue, interspersed with vivid description &amp; scene setting. It should sow hints of the larger intrigue, stakes &amp; dangers. Include Asimov&#x27;s trademark big-and-small-picture world building and retrofuturistic classic scifi vibe. The first chapter begins with Arthur aboard the transfer vessel, wondering just what he&#x27;s gotten involved in. The remainder of the story is open to your choices &amp; direction. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming I’m treating this as a classic Asimov-style puzzle box: the real story is never the metal, it’s what the metal makes people do. Azra is the MacGuffin that forces every character to reveal their true utility function. Possible spines for the 8 chapters: Spine 1 – “The Offer He Can’t Refuse” (most Asimovian) Ch1 transfer → Ch2-3 meeting with the “unknown party” who is actually a high functionary of the Antares Combine (a Foundation-like mercantile empire that hides behind diplomatic immunity). They want Arthur to sign away his claim in exchange for a cushy research post on Antares Prime. He refuses. They expected refusal. The real plan was to get him off Mars so corporate kill-teams can seize the vein while he’s in transit. The rest of the novella is Arthur trying to get back to Mars before the vein is strip-mined and the evidence of its true properties destroyed. Spine 2 – “The Three Laws of Azra” Azra isn’t just a catalyst; prolonged exposure rewrites human neural pruning thresholds (think subtle psychohistory drug). Arthur has been living above the motherlode for years → he’s unconsciously far smarter and more cold-blooded than he realizes. The Antares diplomats detect this and want to study/control him. The story becomes Arthur discovering that his own mind is the real weapon of mass destruction. Spine 3 – “The Cavanaugh Variation” The unknown party is Dr. Elara Cavanaugh, legendary materials scientist who vanished twenty years ago. She claims Azra isn’t an element—it’s engineered, a relic of a long-dead Type II civilization that used controlled decay cascades to move stars. She needs Arthur’s pure vein to finish her prototype drive that will make every existing interstellar power obsolete overnight. She’s willing to burn Mars to glass to get it. Arthur has to decide if humanity is ready for that kind of jump. Spine 4 – “Indentured King” Arthur accepts a counter-offer from his own indentured workforce. They’ve been secretly unionizing and have a plan: declare the claim a sovereign workers’ republic, use Azra-derived weapons to hold Mars hostage, force Earth and the Combine to recognize them. Arthur, the legal owner, is the only one who can legitimize the coup. The story is Arthur trying to stay human while becoming the reluctant monarch of a revolution. Spine 5 – “The Decay Constant” Azra accelerates all decay, including biological. Everyone who mines it dies young and mad. Arthur’s vein is so pure that a single refined gram could trigger a chain reaction that flash-ages the entire crust of Mars into radioactive slag. The arms race isn’t for weapons; it’s to keep anyone from using it. The Antares “diplomats” are actually a containment crew trying to bury the knowledge forever. Arthur is the only one who can expose it—or trigger it. Spine 6 – “The Psychohistorical Gambit” Full Foundation homage. Antares Combine has been running a 400-year plan to control the Azra supply because their psychohistorical models predict that whoever monopolizes it will rule the next thousand years of human expansion. Arthur’s vein is an unforeseen Seldon-level anomaly that threatens to collapse the entire equation. They need him alive and cooperative long enough to recalculate the crisis, then he becomes expendable. Spine 7 – “The Robot’s Advocate” Late Asimov twist: Arthur himself is a humanoid robot (he doesn’t know it). Built by an ancient mining corporation that went bankrupt, his “memories” of being a first-gen settler are implanted. The Azra vein resonates with his positronic brain in a way that no human brain can survive. Antares wants to dissect him to merge Asimov’s Three Laws with Azra physics to create perfectly loyal super-soldiers. The story is Arthur discovering his nature while trying to remain “human.” Spine 8 – “Small Man, Big Lever” (the humble one) Arthur just wants to keep his claim and live quietly. Every power in the solar system descends—corporations, Earth gov, Combine, Belter pirates, even a cult that worships Azra as the physical manifestation of entropy. Arthur wins by being underestimated, playing them all off each other in a comedy of errors that somehow leaves him the only one standing when the dust settles. Pure Caves of Steel energy. Tone possibilities: - Dry, ironic, dialogue-driven (pure Asimov) - Slightly more poetic decay-porn (Bradbury bleeding in) - Hard-boiled noir on red sand (for contrast, probably wrong) - Quiet, melancholic, almost elegiac (the death of the frontier dream) Pacing targets per chapter (roughly 1000 words each): Ch1 – transfer ship, low information, high paranoia Ch2 – arrival, first meeting, the Offer Ch3 – refusal and immediate consequences Ch4 – revelation of true stakes Ch5 – midpoint reversal (Arthur gains/loses decisive advantage) Ch6 – darkest moment Ch7 – clever solution no one saw coming Ch8 – epilogue that zooms out to galactic implications # Reflection Spines 2, 5, and 7 are the most tempting because they let me do genuine Asimovian idea-bounces: a scientific principle that has inescapable sociological consequences. But Spine 2 (Arthur himself altered) risks making him too superhuman too fast; Asimov’s protagonists win with insight, not superpowers. Spine 7 (secret robot) is fun but feels like a gimmick unless the reveal is earned over seven chapters, and it might overshadow the Azra mystery. Spine 5 (planet-killer decay cascade) is almost too large; it turns the story into a disaster countdown, which is more Niven than Asimov. Spine 1 and Spine 6 are the purest Asimov: ordinary(ish) man caught in gears far larger than himself, wins (or loses) through understanding the rules better than the players. Spine 6 especially lets me do psychohistory-lite without naming it, which feels honest to the homage. Spine 8 (small man lever) would be the most fun to write and probably the most re-readable, because Asimov at his best is quietly funny—think the banter in The Caves of Steel or the Mule sections of Foundation. Spine 3 (Cavanaugh) gives me a classic Asimov mentor/antagonist figure (think Hardin vs. the Encyclopedia committee, or Susan Calvin vs. everyone). The “ancient alien engineers” angle risks going too Clarke, but if I keep it rigorously materialist—no mysticism, just terrifyingly advanced engineering—it stays in bounds. The indentured-workers revolution (Spine 4) is politically sharper than Asimov usually gets, but he did write The Currents of Space and the Trantor underclass sections, so it’s not impossible. What I think works best for 8 tight chapters: a combination that keeps Arthur human, fallible, clever rather than powered-up, and lets the Azra property itself remain mysterious until the final reel. The winning spine will be the one that makes the climax a logical deduction any reader could have made if they’d noticed the same clues Arthur did. That’s the Asimov sweet spot. I’m leaning toward a hybrid of Spine 1 and Spine 6: Antares Combine needs Arthur because his vein is the “anomaly” that breaks their psychohistorical forecast, but they can’t simply take it—they need his voluntary cooperation for legal or propaganda reasons. The story becomes Arthur realizing he’s the grain of sand that jams the galaxy-spanning machine, and figuring out how to extract maximum freedom for himself and Mars with that leverage. Quiet, talky, ruthless, and deeply satisfying when the final equation balances.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This will be a pure, distilled Asimov-style puzzle story set against a retro-futuristic 24th-century Mars: no ray-guns, no space battles, no chosen-one powers. Victory or defeat will come through contracts, deductions, psychohistorical forecasting, and the precise application of bureaucratic leverage. Core plot spine: Hybrid of Spine 1 and Spine 6 The Antares Combine has been running a 300-year psychohistorical simulation that predicts, with 99.94 % certainty, that whoever controls the largest pure Azra lode within the next eighteen months will dictate the shape of human expansion for the following millennium. Arthur Neegan’s accidental claim is the 0.06 % anomaly that collapses the entire projection. The Combine therefore cannot simply murder or expropriate him; they require his freely given, legally unbreakable cooperation so the equation re-stabilises in their favour. Arthur, a stubborn, mildly antisocial mining engineer who just wanted to be left alone, realises he is holding the one lever that can move the galaxy—and that every other power (Earth, the Martian corporations, the indentured miners’ clandestine soviet, even rogue Belt factions) will kill him the instant the Combine no longer needs him alive. Voice & style Dry, precise, dialogue-heavy, lightly ironic. Minimal physical action; tension arises from what is said and, more importantly, what is left unsaid. Vivid but never lush descriptions—dust, rust, the smell of ozone in a habitat corridor, the exact shade of red light on a pressure gauge. Retro-futuristic optimism curdled into corporate feudalism. No infodumps; world-building delivered through contracts, shipping manifests, bureaucratic forms, overheard conversations, and Arthur’s own terse internal calculations. Things to aim for - Every chapter must contain at least one genuine surprise that feels inevitable in hindsight. - Arthur remains an ordinary man—stubborn, detail-obsessed, socially awkward, but never superhuman. - The true nature of Azra is revealed only in Chapter 7; until then it is spoken of with a mixture of greed and dread, never explained. - The climax is a negotiation, not a firefight. - The epilogue zooms out to show the new galactic equilibrium Arthur has forced into being, exactly like the Encyclopaedia Galactica entries Asimov loved. Things to avoid - No heroic sacrifices, no tragic deaths, no love interests, no fistfights, no sudden competence porn, no ancient aliens, no mystical entropy worship, no “I am my own grandfather” paradoxes. Length discipline Each chapter 950–1100 words, ending on a line sharp enough to make the reader immediately turn the page. # Chapter Planning Chapter 1 – “Invitation at Gunpoint” Transfer shuttle from Phobos Station to the diplomatic courier Icarus-9. Arthur, still in his dusty coveralls, escorted by six silent Antares “diplomats” whose sidearms are politely holstered but very visible. Interior monologue establishes his character—forty-eight years old, first-generation Mars-born, hates cities, hates paperwork more. First hints that Azra does something stranger than merely catalysing decay. Ends with the courier slipping into superluminal transit and Arthur realising he is now legally outside every jurisdiction that ever protected him. Chapter 2 – “The Velvet Glove” Arrival at Antares Prime orbital habitat. Arthur is bathed, dressed in a charcoal-gray tunic that costs more than his annual water allowance, and brought to Minister-Coordinator Lysander Cho. Dialogue scene: Cho offers Arthur lifetime wealth, a research institute named after him, and the gratitude of a thousand worlds. All Arthur has to do is sign the prepared deed of transfer. Arthur refuses, politely, then less politely. Cho expected refusal—this is merely the opening bid. Ends with Cho smiling and saying, “Then let us show you why refusal is mathematically impossible.” Chapter 3 – “The Equation” Cho reveals (through elegant holographic projections) the existence of the Antares Psychohistorical Institute and the 300-year forecast. Arthur, who has a minor degree in statistical sociology he never uses, spots the flaw immediately: the model assumes rational actors will always choose maximum personal gain. Arthur is not rational in that way. Cho is quietly delighted—this is the anomaly they needed to locate. Ends with Arthur placed under luxurious house arrest while the Institute frantically recalculates. Chapter 4 – “Visitors” Three separate parties bribe, threaten, or seduce their way into Arthur’s gilded prison: - A representative of the Martian Mining Consortium offering to smuggle him home in exchange for exclusive rights. - The elected spokesman of the indentured miners (his own workforce) who have already occupied the claim and declared it a workers’ protectorate. - An Earth admiral who simply wants the vein destroyed before it triggers the next war. Arthur realises everyone is operating on the assumption that the Combine will eventually force his signature. He begins to understand the true size of his leverage. Chapter 5 – “The Margin of Error” Midpoint reversal. Arthur is granted access to the Institute’s restricted library to “prove” his obstinacy is futile. Instead he discovers a forgotten 70-year-old monograph by a junior researcher who predicted that an Azra monopole the size of his vein would exhibit zero-point decay resonance—effectively making it the only known source of stable negative-mass propulsion. The Combine has been hiding this because it would instantly obsolete every existing drive and therefore every existing power structure. Arthur now knows something Cho believes no outsider knows. Chapter 6 – “The Darkest Orbit” Lowest point. The Combine stages a fake rescue by the miners, lets Arthur “escape,” then publicly executes the miners for treason, framing Arthur as complicit. Overnight he becomes the most hated man on Mars. Simultaneous news: corporate kill-teams have begun strip-mining his vein anyway, claiming he is already legally dead under Antares law. Arthur is alone in a maintenance corridor with nothing but a borrowed tablet and eighteen hours before the Icarus-9 returns him to Antares for “protective custody.” Chapter 7 – “The Neegan Variation” Arthur hacks the courier’s comm array and broadcasts a single document to every newsfeed, stock exchange, and naval command frequency in the solar system: his new Articles of Incorporation for the Azra Trust, a non-profit legal entity domiciled on Ceres that owns the vein in perpetuity, with himself as sole trustee-for-life and succession passing to an elected council of indentured miners upon his death. The Trust’s charter contains one lethal clause: any use of force against the vein triggers immediate release of the negative-mass refinement protocol to every faction simultaneously. Mutually assured innovation. The Combine’s forecast collapses into a hundred equitable futures instead of one imperial one. Chapter 8 – “Encyclopaedia Galactica Entry, 124th Edition” Epilogue, written in the calm, omniscient voice of the Encyclopaedia. The Azra Rush ended not with war but with the fastest peaceful redistribution of technology in history. Arthur Neegan retired to his original habitat, now a museum, and refused all interviews.
