He is believed to have died in 2391 at the age of ninety-three, cause listed as “stubbornness.” The entry ends with the line: “Thus the Galaxy learned that the most dangerous substance in the universe is not Azra, but an honest man with nothing left to lose.”
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
### How a human writer (especially one who grew up on Asimov) would actually approach this piece A real human who loves classic Asimov would not start with a perfect 8-chapter skeleton. They would start drunk on the smell of the idea and half-terrified it’s too big for them. They would probably write Chapter 1 three different times, throw all three away, then finally write it at 3 a.m. while listening to old Vangelis tracks, and that fourth version would be the one that sings. They would mutter to themselves the whole time: “God, I hope this is half as clever as ‘The Last Question’ and not just a pale imitation.” They would keep a paper notebook full of scrawled contradictions: - “Arthur has to be boring but fascinating” - “Make the science sound hard but never explain it” - “Cho has to be charming and monstrous at the same time” - “No shootouts, but the reader must feel like someone is about to get shot every ten pages” Most importantly, a human would be haunted by the fear that the story is emotionally hollow. Asimov’s own Achilles heel was that his characters sometimes felt like chess pieces wearing lab coats. A human who knows this would be obsessed with giving Arthur at least one utterly irrational, embarrassing, human trait that has nothing to do with the plot and everything to do with making him real. ### Typical LLM tell-tales that would murder this particular story 1. Over-polished, bloodless prose LLMs love to write in that smooth, slightly elevated register that never risks sounding awkward. The result is dialogue that sounds like corporate memos and descriptions that feel like they were focus-grouped for maximum cinematic appeal. Asimov at his best is awkward, clipped, sometimes outright dowdy. He uses “said” 9 times out of 10. He repeats words. His sentences sometimes stumble. 2. Neatness addiction LLMs cannot resist tying every thread. They panic at loose ends. The current plan ends with a perfect Encyclopaedia Galactica bow and a quotable last line. A human would be perfectly happy to let the reader close the book thinking “Wait, but what actually happened to the miners’ kids?” and trusting that unease is part of the effect. 3. Character consistency as a fetish Arthur in the plan is flawlessly rational and always one step ahead by the end. That’s LLM comfort food. A human would give Arthur at least two moments where he does something completely stupid out of pride, loneliness, or sheer contrariness. 4. Dialogue as exposition dump wearing a hat The plan has long, flawless speeches where Cho explains psychohistory. That’s exactly how LLMs write “smart” characters. Real Asimovian dialogue is oblique, full of interruptions, people talking past each other, and sudden embarrassing silences. 5. Fear of boring the reader LLMs stuff every chapter with a revelation or reversal. Humans know that the most gripping Asimov stretches are sometimes twenty pages of two guys in a room arguing about grain shipments on Trantor. ### Deep critique of the current intention & plan through a human lens The plan is 95 % clever and 5 % soul-dead. Here are the specific places where it’s showing LLM symptoms: - Chapter 5 “The Margin of Error” is pure competence porn. Arthur wanders into the restricted library and instantly finds the one monograph that cracks the universe open. That’s LLM wish-fulfillment. A human would have him find the monograph by accident while looking for something else entirely—like porn, or old mining logs, or a recipe for Martian chili. - Chapter 7 climax is too clean. The “mutually assured innovation” clause is a brilliant game-theory move, but it arrives fully formed like Athena from Zeus’s forehead. Humans don’t solve galaxy-shattering crises with a single perfect legal document composed in a maintenance corridor in eighteen hours. They solve them messily, with typos, and with at least one clause they regret forever. - Arthur has no wounds. He ends wealthy, respected, and morally immaculate. That’s Disney-fication of Asimov. A human would make sure that by the end Arthur has betrayed someone he liked, or lost something he can never get back (maybe the miners who trusted him get screwed anyway, just less badly than they would have). - No humour that hurts. Asimov’s humour is often cruel or awkward (think of the Mayor’s aide in Foundation who keeps mispronouncing things). The plan has no cringe, no bathos, no moment where Arthur realises his flies have been open for the entire diplomatic reception. - The science is too pat. “Zero-point decay resonance → stable negative-mass propulsion” sounds impressive but is actually meaningless technobabble salad. Asimov would either leave it completely unexplained or ground it in one real physics footnote that quietly terrifies you. ### Brainstorming ways to make it genuinely human 1. Give Arthur one humiliating weakness Options (pick one or more): - He’s secretly addicted to 300-year-old Earth soap operas and cries at the endings. - He’s impotent from radiation damage and has never told anyone. - He’s terrified of open spaces because he grew up in habitats and has never seen a real sky. - He hoards physical books and smells them when he’s stressed. 2. Make the cleverness cost something visceral Let Arthur win, but let winning taste like ash. Maybe the price of his broadcast is that every friend he ever had on Mars now believes he sold them out. He gets to be right and still never gets to go home. 3. Let some mysteries stay mysteries Never explain exactly what Azra “really” does. Let three different characters give three mutually contradictory theories, all of them plausible, none of them confirmed. 4. Insert deliberate ugliness One scene where Arthur throws up from stress. One scene where Minister Cho picks his nose absent-mindedly during a negotiation. One scene where someone’s breath smells of recycled onions. 5. Allow Arthur to be wrong—spectacularly—in Chapter 6 Let him believe he’s orchestrating his own rescue, only to realise too late that he’s been the bait all along. Real humans do not have perfect information. 6. Replace the perfect Encyclopaedia entry with something messier End instead on a leaked memo, or a child’s school essay found centuries later, or a single line from Arthur’s private journal dated the day after victory: “I fixed the galaxy. Why do I still want to die?” 7. Let the language get grubby when emotions run high Arthur swears exactly once in the entire story, and it shocks everyone because he never swears. Or he calls Cho “you silk-suited bastard” under his breath and immediately regrets it because it’s the best compliment Cho has had all year. 8. Insert one chapter that is 70 % silence Chapter 6 could be almost entirely Arthur alone in a dark service tunnel, listening to the ship’s heartbeat and thinking about the fact that he will never again smell Martian dust the way it smelled the first time he stepped outside as a child. Do these things and the story stops feeling like an impeccable machine and starts feeling like something a lonely, stubborn, brilliant man bled onto the page at 3 a.m. because the idea wouldn’t let him sleep. That’s the difference between an LLM writing Asimov and a human writing Asimov.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This will be an Asimov story written by a human who is slightly afraid of it. Voice: plain, mid-century sentences that occasionally stumble when the character is upset. Dialogue that interrupts itself, repeats, circles, lies by omission. Descriptions only of things Arthur would notice: the exact shade of rust on a bolt, the smell of someone else’s recycled sweat, the way a diplomatic smile never reaches the eyes. No cinematic sweeps, no purple dust storms, no heroic silhouettes against twin moons. Arthur Neegan is forty-eight, balding, allergic to bullshit, hoards paper books, and has never once in his life managed to tell a woman he loved her. He is not clever in the way the Antares Combine is clever; he is clever the way a stuck valve is clever—by refusing to turn until the whole system backs up and someone finally notices the pressure. The science of Azra will stay half-shadowed. Everyone knows it makes decay run faster; nobody agrees on why or how much faster or whether that’s even the right question. By the end we will know one true thing about it and one only, and it will be quietly horrifying. The victory will be ugly, incomplete, and will cost Arthur the only home he ever wanted. There will be no quotable last line for posterity; there will be a man alone in a small habitat, staring at red dust that no longer legally belongs to him and wondering why winning feels exactly like losing. Tone targets: dry, awkward, occasionally cruel, and (this is crucial) sometimes genuinely funny in a way that hurts. Length discipline remains: 950–1050 words per chapter, no exceptions. # Chapter Planning Chapter 1 – “Dust on My Boots and Guns at My Door” Late afternoon, Arthur’s habitat, Tharsis. Six Antares “diplomats” in charcoal uniforms arrive with polite smiles and visible sidearms. They give him twenty minutes to pack. Arthur tries to refuse, tries to stall, tries to call his foreman—nothing works. Last thing he does before they seal the airlock is grab one battered paper copy of Foundation and Empire because it’s the only book he can reach. On the shuttle up to Phobos he realises the diplomats are afraid of him. He doesn’t understand why. Ends with the courier ship lensing into superluminal and Arthur throwing up in the polished steel toilet because he’s never jumped before. Chapter 2 – “Minister Cho and the Smell of Real Oranges” Antares Prime orbital. Arthur is scrubbed, shaved, and dressed in clothes that cost more than his dome. He meets Minister-Coordinator Lysander Cho in a room that smells faintly of actual Earth citrus—an obscene luxury. Cho is charming, reasonable, almost paternal. He offers Arthur everything except the one thing Arthur wants: to be left alone. Arthur refuses the deal three separate times, each refusal ruder than the last. Cho never stops smiling. Ends with Cho saying softly, “Then we will have to educate you,” and Arthur realising education is going to hurt. Chapter 3 – “The Library Where Nobody Reads” Arthur is installed in luxury quarters with access to the Institute’s restricted stacks. He spends three days pretending to sulk while actually reading everything he can find about Azra. He discovers conflicting papers, censored margins, one insane footnote from 2289 that was never followed up. He also finds the soap-opera streaming channel and binge-watches twelve episodes of Terra Nova Nights because he’s homesick and ashamed. Ends with Cho visiting at 3 a.m. to inform him that corporate kill-teams have already landed on his claim. “You are now the only obstacle between progress and a great deal of money.” Chapter 4 – “Three Visitors and One Lie” Three covert meetings in one Martian week: 1. Consortium lawyer offers rescue for 90 % of the vein. 2. Katya, his former shift foreman (someone he once almost kissed), tells him the indentured have occupied the claim and are holding it with jury-rigged mining lasers. She begs him not to sign anything. 3. An Earth admiral offers to nuke the site from orbit—“the only way to be sure.” Arthur lies to all three. He doesn’t know which lie is the worst. Chapter 5 – “The Footnote That Should Have Stayed Buried” Arthur finds the 2289 paper again. It isn’t about propulsion; it’s about entropy propagation. A vein the size of his will eventually accelerate the decay of the entire Martian crust. Timeframe: 80–120 years. The planet becomes uninhabitable, but slowly enough that no one will notice until it’s too late. The Combine has known for decades and has been trying to secure the vein precisely so they can control the countdown. Arthur realises every offer he’s received is about who gets to decide when Mars dies. Chapter 6 – “The Rescue That Wasn’t” The miners stage a daring raid to spring Arthur from the orbital habitat. He goes with them because Katya is there and he’s stupid about her. Halfway back to Mars the “rescue ship” docks with an Antares cruiser. It was a Combine operation from the start. Katya is shot in the stomach in front of him. The last thing she says is “You should have signed, you stupid bastard.” Arthur is returned to custody. The newsfeeds announce that Arthur Neegan helped orchestrate the massacre of loyal miners. He is now the most hated man on two worlds. Chapter 7 – “The Only Thing Left to Sell” Alone in a holding cell with nothing but the borrowed tablet they forgot to confiscate, Arthur does the only thing he still can. He records a twelve-minute confession: everything he knows, everything he guesses, the 2289 footnote, the countdown, the fact that the Combine needs his cooperation because any forcible seizure triggers accelerated decay within months instead of centuries. He attaches the full refinement protocol he reverse-engineered from fragments. Then he broadcasts it uncut to every open channel in the solar system with a single line header: “Do whatever you want with it. Just leave me out of it.” The broadcast ends with twelve seconds of dead air and the sound of him crying, because he can’t turn the microphone off fast enough. Chapter 8 – “Property of the Martian Trust” Epilogue, three years later. The claim is now administered by an elected council of former indentured. Mars is evacuating in orderly stages; no one is pretending anymore. Arthur lives in his original habitat, which is inside the exclusion zone and therefore empty except for him. He has enough water and air for twelve more years. He spends his days fixing things that don’t need fixing and reading the same paper copy of Foundation and Empire until the pages fall out. The last line of the story is Arthur speaking aloud to no one: “I saved them. They just didn’t want to be saved by me.” Then he closes the book, puts it carefully on the shelf, and goes outside to watch the dust blow across a planet that is already, very quietly, beginning to die.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Arthur Neegan Background First-generation Mars-born, 48 standard years. Father died in a blowout when Arthur was nine; mother followed six years later from untreated thyroid cancer because the clinic was owned by the company that held her indenture. Raised by an elderly assayer who taught him to read ore samples and paper books. Claimed his own quarter-section at twenty-six after paying off his parents’ inherited debt. Hasn’t left the Tharsis region in fifteen years. Personality & flaws Pathologically stubborn, socially awkward, sentimental about inanimate objects, emotionally constipated. Hoards paper books because they smell like the childhood he barely had. Terrified of intimacy; the closest he ever got to asking Katya out was leaving an extra thermos of coffee outside her bunk during night shift. Collects broken tools “because they might come in useful someday.” Hates waste more than he hates people. Physicality 5′10″, thinning rust-brown hair he cuts himself with shears, perpetual squint from decades under helmet lamps. Hands always cracked and stained with iron oxide. Moves like a man who expects every surface to be sharper than it looks—slow, deliberate, testing. Speaks softly, rarely raises his voice; when angry the volume drops further and the words get very precise. Motives & allegiances He only ever wanted to be left alone with his patch of dirt and the quiet certainty that no corporation would ever own him again. Now that same patch is killing the planet and everyone wants a piece of him. Loyal only to the memory of people who are dead and to the handful of indentured who once shared coffee with him at 0400. Everyone else can burn. Voice samples Internal: “Forty-eight years old and the only thing I’ve ever finished is a book someone else wrote. Good going, Neegan.” Spoken, to Cho: “You keep using the word ‘reasonable’. On Mars that word means ‘whatever keeps the air in the dome another week’. Your reasonableness smells like vacuum.” # Minister-Coordinator Lysander Cho Background Born on Antares Prime to old money, educated at the Combine’s most exclusive academy, topped the psychohistorical civil-service exam at twenty-one. Has spent thirty years steering policy with the serene confidence of a man who believes history is a tame animal. Has never set foot on a planet with unfiltered gravity. Personality & flaws Charming in the way a scalpel is charming—clean, precise, genuinely curious about pain thresholds. Mildly addicted to real citrus; keeps a single orange in his desk and peels it only when negotiations reach the interesting stage. Collects first editions the way other people collect enemies. Underneath the polish is a cold terror that the equations might one day be wrong and he will have no idea who he is without them. Physicality Tall, elegant, late fifties, silver hair always perfect. Moves like he’s being filmed in slow motion. Wears charcoal silk that never wrinkles. Smiles with his mouth but never his eyes; when genuinely amused the smile disappears entirely and he looks ten years younger and far more dangerous. Motives & allegiances Absolute loyalty to the Combine’s 300-year plan. Sees individual lives the way chess players see pawns—regrettable but necessary. Personally fond of Arthur the way a entomologist is fond of a particularly interesting beetle. Voice samples To Arthur, softly: “Mr Neegan, you are not refusing me. You are refusing mathematics. Mathematics is patient.” Internal, while peeling the orange: “He still smells of rust. How deliciously primitive.” # Katya Volkov Background Thirty-four, third-generation indentured from the Belt. Came to Mars on a ten-year metals contract when she was nineteen. Best explosives tech in the Tharsis basin; can set a charge that will crack a seam without bringing down the overhead. Once blew open a sealed corporate vault to rescue trapped miners and got an extra five years tacked onto her contract for “malicious destruction of property.” Personality & flaws Foul-mouthed, funny, fiercely protective, allergic to self-pity. Drinks too much recycled vodka, sings old Russian mining songs off-key when drunk. Has a soft spot for strays—human or otherwise. Trusts too quickly and regrets it slowly. Physicality Short, compact, muscular. Black hair in a practical braid that’s always coming undone. Scar across left cheek from a flying rock shard. Moves fast, like someone perpetually late for a fight. Laughs easily and loudly; when truly angry she goes very quiet and her hands stop moving. Motives & allegiances Wants the indentured to own the fruit of their own lungs and blood. Sees Arthur as a decent man who got handed a loaded gun and is too scared to pull the trigger. Would follow him into vacuum if he ever asked properly, which he never will. Voice samples To Arthur, half-laughing, half-furious: “You think being right is the same as being useful, you beautiful idiot.” Internal, while bleeding out: “Should’ve kissed him when I had the chance. Stupid, Katya. Always stupid.”

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The knock came at 14:37 local, three measured raps that sounded like someone testing whether the dome was hollow. Arthur Neegan was on his knees in the equipment bay, trying to coax a seized valve on the number-two pump. The knock repeated, polite but not waiting. He wiped his hands on the rag that lived in his back pocket and walked the short corridor to the airlock, boots leaving red prints on the gray decking. Through the porthole he saw six figures in charcoal uniforms, visors up, sidearms holstered but not hidden. The lead one raised a gloved hand in greeting that managed to look like a threat. Arthur thumbed the intercom. “I’m not expecting company.” “Mr Neegan,” the man said, voice smooth through the speaker, “we represent the Antares Diplomatic Corps. We have an invitation you will want to accept.” Arthur rested his forehead against the cool metal of the lock. He had known this was coming the moment the assay came back ninety-eight percent pure. He had simply hoped for another week, maybe two. Time enough to finish reading Foundation and Empire for the thirty-second time. He opened the lock. They stepped inside without waiting, bringing the smell of new fabric and shipboard disinfectant. The leader removed his gloves one finger at a time. “You have twenty minutes to pack personal effects. One duffel, fifteen kilos maximum.” Arthur looked past him at the others. None of them blinked. Their boots were clean. That bothered him more than the guns. “I’m not going anywhere.” The man smiled the way accountants smile when they tell you the numbers don’t lie. “Then we are authorised to carry you. It will be less dignified.” Arthur felt the old, familiar pressure behind his eyes, the same pressure he used to feel when the company doctor told his mother the treatment wasn’t cost-effective. He took one deliberate step back. “I’ll need my book,” he said. The man inclined his head. “One book.” Arthur walked to the shelf above the bunk, reached past the row of dog-eared paperbacks, and pulled out the one with the cracked spine. Foundation and Empire. The pages smelled of dust and the particular loneliness of long nights. He tucked it inside his coveralls, against his chest. They gave him exactly nineteen minutes. He spent them standing in the centre of the habitat he had patched and nursed for twenty-two years, memorising the exact shade of rust on the southwest bulkhead. Then they zipped him into an oversuit two sizes too large and marched him out. Outside, the wind was up. Red dust hissed against the visors. The shuttle waited fifty metres away, ramp down, engines already spooling. Arthur took one look back at his dome—low, ugly, perfect—and felt something inside him tear with a sound he would never forget. On board, they strapped him into a seat that smelled of someone else’s sweat. No one spoke. The shuttle lifted, kicking dust across the claim like a child knocking over blocks. At Phobos Station they transferred him to the diplomatic courier Icarus-9 without ever passing through public corridors. The crew wore the same charcoal. Someone offered him a bottle of water. He shook his head. The acceleration couch was too soft. Arthur clutched the book through the fabric and stared at the bulkhead. There was a hairline crack in the paint shaped like the Martian canal system they taught kids didn’t exist anymore. He watched it until the jump. Superluminal transit feels, to the uninitiated, like drowning in perfect silence. Arthur’s stomach turned inside out. The lights dimmed to emergency red. He lurched out of the couch, found the polished steel toilet, and vomited the morning’s recycled coffee and the last of his illusions about staying home. When he came back, one of the diplomats was holding out a towel. Charcoal, of course. Arthur wiped his mouth. “How long until we arrive?” “Forty-three standard hours.” Arthur sat down again, cradling the book. Forty-three hours. In forty-three hours the claim would already belong to someone else. He had seen the contracts; they all had forfeiture clauses for “abandonment.” He closed his eyes and smelled dust that wasn’t there anymore. The diplomat across from him finally spoke, voice gentle. “You should try to sleep, Mr Neegan. Minister Cho dislikes it when guests are untidy.” Arthur opened the book at random. Page 217. Salvor Hardin was saying something about violence being the last refuge of the incompetent. Arthur ran his thumb across the words until the paper threatened to tear. He did not sleep. He sat perfectly still, counting heartbeats the way his mother had taught him when the habitat pressure dropped and there was nothing to do but wait and see whose luck ran out first. After a while the diplomat stopped watching him. Arthur opened the book again and read the same paragraph over and over until the words stopped making sense. Somewhere beyond the hull, Mars fell away into the dark, taking everything he had ever been with it. He pressed his forehead to the cool metal wall and whispered the only prayer he knew. “Let me go home.” The ship did not answer.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that demonstrates genuine craft and a clear authorial voice. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. VOICE AND STYLE: The prose is admirably restrained. The Asimov influence is present but not slavish—there's a mid-century plainness to the sentences that occasionally opens into something more resonant. "The knock came at 14:37 local, three measured raps that sounded like someone testing whether the dome was hollow" is an excellent opening line: functional, precise, and quietly ominous. The retrofuturistic vibe is well-maintained throughout. CHARACTER: Arthur comes through clearly. The detail of grabbing Foundation and Empire, the rust-shade memorization, the whispered prayer—these are economical character strokes that feel earned rather than imposed. The plan called for him to be "allergic to bullshit" and that registers in his clipped responses and the internal observation about the diplomats' clean boots bothering him more than the guns. That's a genuinely good detail—it reveals character through perception rather than exposition. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is sparse but functional. "I'm not going anywhere" / "Then we are authorised to carry you. It will be less dignified" is clean and effective. The Salvor Hardin quote is a nice intertextual touch that doesn't feel forced. However, the dialogue is minimal enough that it's hard to fully evaluate—the chapter plan called for more of the "polite but forceful" dynamic to play out, and while it's present, it's compressed. METAPHORS AND POETRY: Most of the figurative language lands. "The man smiled the way accountants smile when they tell you the numbers don't lie" is excellent—specific, character-revealing, and not overwrought. "Something inside him tear with a sound he would never forget" is slightly weaker—the "sound he would never forget" is a cliché gesture toward interiority that doesn't quite earn its place. "Drowning in perfect silence" for superluminal transit is serviceable but not distinctive. The final line "The ship did not answer" is a bit on-the-nose, though it fits the tone. PLAN ADHERENCE: The chapter follows its plan closely. The six diplomats, the twenty minutes, the book grab, the Phobos transfer, the jump, the vomiting—all present. The plan mentioned Arthur realizing the diplomats are afraid of him, which is notably absent from the chapter. This is a meaningful omission since it was supposed to be a key beat that ends the chapter's middle section. The chapter ends with the vomiting as planned, but the "diplomats are afraid of him" insight never materializes. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT: The chapter is genuinely moving in places. The mother's treatment being "not cost-effective" is a devastating detail delivered with appropriate restraint. The habitat memorization scene works. The prayer at the end is simple and effective. The piece avoids sentimentality while still achieving genuine pathos. COHERENCE: The chapter is coherent and well-paced. The timeline is clear, the geography of the transfer is logical, and the emotional arc—from resistance to resignation—tracks naturally. WEAKNESSES: The "sound he would never forget" is a minor cliché. The missing "diplomats are afraid of him" beat is a structural gap. The final few paragraphs slightly over-explain the emotional state ("taking everything he had ever been with it" is a bit much after the more restrained "Let me go home"). The chapter is perhaps slightly too uniformly somber—the plan called for something "occasionally genuinely funny in a way that hurts," and there's almost no humor here, not even the dry, painful kind. OVERALL: This is accomplished work. It's not a masterpiece, but it's well above average—controlled, purposeful, and emotionally honest without being manipulative. The Asimov influence is present without being a pastiche. The character is vivid and consistent with the profile. The main failings are the missing "afraid of him" beat and the slight over-reach in the final lines. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Chapter 2 – “Minister Cho and the Smell of Real Oranges” The Icarus-9 docked so smoothly Arthur felt nothing at all. A gentle chime, a shift in the air pressure, and then the charcoal diplomats were unstrapping him as if he were fragile cargo. They walked him through corridors that smelled of money: polished metal, real wood accents, the faint green bite of living plants. Arthur kept his eyes on the deck. He didn’t want to see how the other half breathed. A woman in a pale gray uniform took custody of him at the habitat ring. She was small, brisk, and smelled faintly of soap that wasn’t recycled. Without asking she stripped the oversuit off him, handed him a towel the size of a bedsheet, and pushed him into a shower hot enough to scald. Arthur stood under the water until his skin went red and the smell of Mars finally left his hair. When he stepped out she had laid out clothes: charcoal trousers, charcoal shirt, charcoal socks. Everything fit perfectly. That scared him more than the guns had. She combed his hair with her fingers while it was still wet. “Better,” she said, not smiling. “Minister Cho dislikes the smell of desperation.” They walked him past windows full of stars and one narrow viewport that showed the curve of Antares Prime below, blue and white and impossibly clean. Arthur’s stomach cramped with something that wasn’t hunger. The room they left him in was larger than his entire habitat. Low table, two chairs, a bowl of actual oranges on a sideboard. The scent hit him like a fist. The door opened again and Lysander Cho walked in alone. He was taller than Arthur expected, silver-haired, wearing the same charcoal but softer, as if the fabric had been persuaded rather than cut. His smile was small and perfect. “Mr Neegan,” he said, voice warm, “you look almost civilised.” Arthur stayed standing. “I want to go home.” Cho peeled an orange with his bare hands. The smell filled the room, obscene and wonderful. “Of course you do. Home is a powerful concept. Regrettably, home is now the property of the Antares Combine, pending your signature on a small formality.” He separated a segment and offered it. Arthur didn’t move. “Please,” Cho said. “Real fruit. You may never have another.” Arthur took the segment because his hand moved before his brain caught up. The taste was so bright it hurt. Cho sat, gestured to the opposite chair. Arthur remained standing. “Let us be reasonable,” Cho said. “You are one man. You have a quarter-section of rust and regret. We are offering you laboratories, staff, a pension measured in planetary economies. All you must do is sign the transfer. You keep the mineral rights in perpetuity—merely leased to us for nine hundred and ninety-nine years. Your great-grandchildren will be wealthy beyond their ability to count.” Arthur put the orange segment on the table untouched after that first bite. “I don’t have great-grandchildren. I don’t even have children. I have a dome with a slow leak in sector four and a book with coffee stains on page one-seventeen. That’s enough.” Cho’s smile widened a fraction. “You are refusing mathematics, Mr Neegan.” “I’m refusing you.” “Same thing.” Cho ate his own orange segment slowly, savouring. “You believe you are protecting something. Principle, perhaps. Independence. The dignity of labour.” He licked juice from his thumb. “These are children’s toys. Out here we have learned that history does not care about children.” Arthur felt the words land like stones in his gut. “History didn’t care about my mother either. She still died.” “Yes,” Cho said gently. “And in the new history we are writing, no one will have to die like that again. Not if the Azra flows where it is meant to flow.” Arthur sat down at last, because his knees decided for him. “What happens if I keep refusing?” Cho placed a single sheet of paper on the table between them. It was thick, creamy, the kind of paper that cost more than water. At the bottom was a blank line and a pen that probably wrote in actual ink. “We continue educating you,” Cho said. “Education can be pleasant. It can also be less pleasant. Eventually you will sign, because every rational creature chooses the path of least pain. It is a law of nature.” Arthur looked at the paper, then at Cho’s hands—clean, manicured, never touched rock in their life. “You keep using the word reasonable,” Arthur said quietly. “On Mars that word means ‘whatever keeps the air in the dome another week’. Your reasonableness smells like vacuum.” Cho’s smile did not flicker, but something shifted behind his eyes, something almost like delight. “Forty-eight hours,” he said, standing. “Rest. Eat something that isn’t algae. Watch the stars if you like. In forty-eight hours we will talk again, and you will be ready to be reasonable.” He left without the paper. The orange scent lingered, cloying now. Arthur sat alone in the big room and listened to his heartbeat counting down someone else’s clock.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong piece of work that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me examine it carefully. VOICE AND STYLE: The Asimov-adjacent voice is well-maintained—plain, declarative sentences, dialogue that carries the weight of the scene, minimal ornamentation. The retrofuturistic feel comes through in the social dynamics and the bureaucratic menace rather than in gadgetry, which is the right call. The prose is controlled and the restraint is genuine rather than performed. CHARACTER WORK: Arthur is rendered convincingly. His stubbornness, his social awkwardness, his involuntary hand reaching for the orange before his brain catches up—these are small, precise character moments that feel earned. The detail about page 117 with coffee stains is exactly the kind of specific, lived-in detail the plan called for. Cho is appropriately scalpel-like: charming, patient, faintly predatory. The orange-peeling as a power move is a good choice—it's theatrical without being overwrought. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. "Your reasonableness smells like vacuum" is a genuinely good line that feels like it belongs to Arthur specifically. Cho's "You are refusing mathematics, Mr Neegan" lands well. The exchange about Arthur's mother is efficient and emotionally resonant without being melodramatic. The dialogue does real work—it reveals character, advances the power dynamic, and plants seeds of the larger intrigue. PLAN ADHERENCE: The chapter follows its plan closely. Arthur refuses three times (implicitly—the plan says three separate refusals, and while the chapter doesn't make this as explicit as three distinct beats, the refusals are present). The orange is there, the luxury contrast is there, Cho's final line about "education" is there. The chapter ends where it should. WEAKNESSES: A few moments strain. "The taste was so bright it hurt" is a slightly forced synesthetic leap—not terrible, but the kind of thing the plan warned against. "His stomach cramped with something that wasn't hunger" is a bit vague and reaches for profundity without quite earning it. The woman who combs his hair with her fingers is an interesting detail but her line—"Minister Cho dislikes the smell of desperation"—feels slightly too on-the-nose, too convenient as exposition. The chapter is perhaps a touch too smooth; the plan called for prose that "occasionally stumbles when the character is upset," and Arthur's voice here is more composed than the plan suggested it should be. The "forty-eight years old and the only thing I've ever finished" type of internal voice from the character profile doesn't appear—Arthur's interiority is somewhat muted in this chapter, which is a missed opportunity given the plan's emphasis on his specific internal register. WORLD-BUILDING: The Asimov big-and-small-picture balance is handled well. The obscene luxury of real oranges does more world-building work than paragraphs of exposition would. The 999-year lease is a nice satirical touch that feels both futuristic and historically resonant. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT: The chapter is engaging. The power imbalance is palpable, the stakes feel real, and Arthur's quiet defiance is sympathetic without being heroic. The ending—Arthur alone, counting down someone else's clock—is appropriately bleak and restrained. OVERALL: A competent-to-good chapter that executes its plan faithfully, maintains voice discipline, and delivers strong dialogue. Its weaknesses are minor: occasional reaching for effect, slightly muted interiority, and one or two lines that feel like they're trying too hard. It doesn't quite reach the level of genuinely surprising or transcendent writing, but it's solid, controlled work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Chapter 3 – “The Library Where Nobody Reads” They gave him a suite with a window that looked out on the galaxy’s slow turn. Arthur stood there for an hour, waiting for Mars to appear. It never did. After that he stopped looking out windows. The guards—always charcoal, always polite—escorted him to the Institute’s restricted stacks on the second day. The librarian was an old woman with steel-gray hair and eyes that had forgotten how to blink. She handed him a tablet and said, “Touch nothing with your fingers. Some of these volumes are older than your planet’s atmosphere.” Arthur almost laughed. Everything here was older than his planet’s atmosphere. He spent the first day pretending to read reports on Azra yield projections. The numbers were beautiful lies. On the second day he started searching for the cracks. The stacks were a maze of shelves that floated in zero-g, books and folios and crystal storage locked in magnetic cradles. Arthur drifted between them like a ghost who had forgotten where he was buried. The air smelled of dust and ozone and something metallic that might have been fear. He found the 2289 footnote at 03:14 ship time, wedged in a marginal note on a paper about decay catalysis thresholds. The author—someone named R. Singh, junior researcher, deceased—had written in handwriting so tiny it looked like insect tracks: “If vein purity exceeds 97.3 % and total mass > 4.8×10⁸ kg, cascade propagation becomes self-sustaining within crustal lattice. Time to irreversible thermal runaway: 80–120 standard years. Recommend immediate quarantine and denial of all extraction permits.” Underneath, in different ink, someone else had scrawled: “Denied—security classification Omega.” Arthur read it three times. The numbers matched his assay exactly. He felt the deck tilt under him though the habitat wasn’t moving. He copied the footnote by hand onto the inside cover of Foundation and Empire because the tablet wouldn’t let him screenshot restricted files. His handwriting looked drunk. After that he couldn’t sleep. The suite had a entertainment terminal with ten thousand channels. He found an ancient soap opera called Terra Nova Nights—episode 217, “Betrayal at Port Lowell”—and watched it straight through because the heroine had Katya’s eyes and the same stubborn set to her mouth. He cried twice. Once when the heroine’s brother died in a blowout, once when she kissed the wrong man because she thought the right one didn’t want her. Arthur hated himself for crying and watched three more episodes anyway. On the fourth night the door chimed at 03:00 exactly. Cho walked in without waiting for an answer. He was wearing a dressing gown the colour of deep space and carrying two glasses. “You look terrible,” he said, almost kindly. “Alcohol?” Arthur sat up on the couch, the book open on his lap like a shield. “I don’t drink.” “Tonight you do.” Cho set a glass in front of him. It was real whiskey, the colour of Martian sunset. “Your claim was seized at 18:00 Tharsis time yesterday. Corporate kill-teams landed with heavy equipment. They are stripping the overburden as we speak.” Arthur stared at the glass. The whiskey didn’t shiver; the habitat gravity was perfect. “How long until they hit the vein?” “Forty-two hours, perhaps less. They are motivated.” Cho sipped his own drink. “You are now the only obstacle between progress and a great deal of money. Sign the transfer and they stop. You can watch from orbit as your fortune is excavated safely.” Arthur picked up the glass. The smell made his eyes water. “You knew,” he said. His voice cracked on the second word. “About the cascade. Singh’s footnote. You’ve known for decades.” Cho’s expression didn’t change. “Knowledge is a heavy burden, Mr Neegan. Some of us are built to carry it.” Arthur drank the whiskey in one burning swallow. It tasted like defeat and wood smoke. “Another?” Cho asked. Arthur shook his head. “I need to piss.” He walked to the sanitary cubicle on legs that didn’t feel like his. Locked the door. Leaned over the sink and stared at his reflection until the face stopped looking familiar. When he came out Cho was gone. The glass was still there, empty. Arthur sat down again and opened the book to the page where he had copied the footnote. He read it until the words blurred. Then he did something he hadn’t done since he was nine years old and his father’s body was brought home in a bag. He put his head in his hands and cried without sound, shoulders shaking hard enough to crack ribs that weren’t broken. Outside the window, the stars kept their ancient indifferent watch.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of work that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me examine it carefully across multiple dimensions. VOICE AND STYLE: The Asimov-adjacent voice is well-maintained — plain, mid-century in cadence, with emotional restraint that makes the moments of feeling land harder. The prose avoids the trap of over-writing. Sentences like "The numbers were beautiful lies" and "He drifted between them like a ghost who had forgotten where he was buried" are economical and effective. The chapter plan called for "dry, awkward, occasionally cruel, and sometimes genuinely funny in a way that hurts" — the funny is largely absent here, which is a minor miss, but the dry and awkward land consistently. THE FOOTNOTE REVEAL: This is the chapter's central task — delivering the horror of the 2289 footnote — and it's handled with admirable restraint. The specificity of the numbers (97.3%, 4.8×10⁸ kg, 80–120 years) gives it scientific texture. The handwritten denial in different ink is a genuinely chilling detail. Arthur copying it by hand because the tablet won't screenshot restricted files is exactly the kind of small, practical, character-revealing detail that Asimov would approve of. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Arthur is well-rendered. His watching Terra Nova Nights because the heroine has Katya's eyes is quietly devastating and consistent with his emotional constipation. The crying without sound, shoulders shaking, is earned. Cho's entrance in a dressing gown "the colour of deep space" carrying two glasses is appropriately patrician and slightly theatrical. His line "Knowledge is a heavy burden, Mr Neegan. Some of us are built to carry it" is exactly the kind of smooth, chilling thing Cho would say. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is sparse but effective. Cho's lines are polished and menacing. Arthur's "I need to piss" as an exit line is perfect — crude, deflating, and entirely in character. The exchange is brief but does real work. The plan called for this chapter to be primarily about Arthur's research and discovery, with Cho's late-night visit as the capper, and the dialogue serves that structure well. WEAKNESSES: The chapter plan mentioned Arthur binge-watching twelve episodes and being "homesick and ashamed" — the chapter delivers this but slightly rushes it. The Terra Nova Nights section is good but could have been funnier or more specific in its humiliation. The plan also mentioned "one insane footnote from 2289 that was never followed up" — the chapter delivers this, but the discovery feels slightly too smooth. Arthur finds it almost too easily, and his reaction, while emotionally resonant, doesn't quite capture the intellectual vertigo of realizing the entire political situation is about planetary murder. The line "He felt the deck tilt under him though the habitat wasn't moving" is a competent but not exceptional way to convey shock. FORCED POETRY: There are a few moments where the metaphors strain slightly. "The air smelled of dust and ozone and something metallic that might have been fear" is a bit of a cliché — fear smelling metallic is well-worn. "The stars kept their ancient indifferent watch" as a closing line is serviceable but generic, the kind of cosmic-indifference gesture that feels borrowed rather than earned. The chapter plan specifically warned against "cinematic sweeps" and this closing image is exactly that kind of sweep. PLAN ADHERENCE: The chapter follows its plan closely. The three-day arc, the footnote discovery, the Terra Nova Nights binge, Cho's 3 a.m. visit, and the revelation about the kill-teams are all present. The ending with Cho's line "You are now the only obstacle between progress and a great deal of money" is delivered almost verbatim from the plan, which is appropriate. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT: The chapter's emotional climax — Arthur crying without sound after Cho leaves — is genuinely moving because it's been earned through restraint. The detail about not crying like this since his father's body came home in a bag is the right kind of biographical anchor. It doesn't over-explain; it trusts the reader. OVERALL: This is confident, controlled writing that serves the story's larger architecture. Its weaknesses are minor — a slightly generic closing image, the humor promised in the plan is mostly absent, and the discovery of the footnote could have been more disorienting. But the chapter does its essential work with skill and restraint. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Chapter 4 – “Three Visitors and One Lie” They moved him to a smaller suite, no windows, after the whiskey night. Arthur stopped counting days. The first visitor came disguised as a maintenance tech. He was a thin man with Consortium patches half-scraped off his coveralls and a face like a worried weasel. He knelt by the air vent, sonic screwdriver whining, and spoke without looking up. “Name’s Park. I can have you on a shuttle to Luna in six hours. From there you’re a ghost. All you do is sign over ninety percent of the vein to the Consortium. Ten percent is still more money than God needs. You’ll be home before they finish stripping the caprock.” Arthur sat on the edge of the bed, Foundation and Empire open but unread in his lap. “Home’s already gone,” he said. Park glanced at the door. “Not yet. There’s still time if you move fast.” Arthur thought of the kill-teams chewing through his overburden like rats through insulation. “Ninety percent,” he repeated. “Ten’s generous. Most claims get zero.” Park’s voice dropped. “They’ll kill you slow if you stay here, Neegan. I’ve seen the orders.” Arthur closed the book. “Tell them I’ll think about it.” Park’s eyes widened. “You’ve got maybe four hours before the next window closes.” “I said I’ll think.” Park left with the vent still half-fixed. Arthur listened to his footsteps fade and felt nothing. The second visitor came at what passed for evening. The door opened without a chime and Katya Volkov walked in wearing a stolen charcoal uniform two sizes too big. She had a bruise blooming across her cheekbone and blood on her knuckles. Arthur stood up so fast the book hit the floor. “Jesus, Katya.” “Don’t blaspheme, it’s bad luck.” She shut the door with her heel. “We’ve got the claim. Me and two hundred others. Barricaded the main shaft with the heavy haulers turned sideways. Mining lasers on tripods. We can hold maybe two weeks if they don’t use orbital strikes.” Her voice was hoarse. She smelled of cordite and sweat and the particular stink of recycled air pushed past its limits. Arthur’s mouth went dry. “You shouldn’t be here.” “Shut up.” She stepped close enough that he could see the tremor in her hands. “We’re not asking for your permission, Arthur. We took it. But it only stays ours if you don’t sign anything. Anything. You hear me?” He reached out, stopped himself before touching the bruise. “They told me the kill-teams already landed.” “They landed. We sent three of them home in bags. The rest are learning respect.” She tried to grin; it came out crooked. “Sign nothing. That’s all. Then when this is over maybe you’ll buy me a drink with your billions.” Arthur swallowed. “Katya—” “Don’t.” She backed toward the door. “Don’t say anything you’ll regret. Just don’t sign.” She was gone before he could find the words he’d never said anyway. The third visitor arrived wearing an Earth Navy uniform and the expression of a man who had stopped sleeping years ago. Admiral Rao sat without invitation and placed a small black cube on the table. “Neegan,” he said, “I’m here unofficially. In about nine hours an Earth cruiser is going to suffer a tragic navigation error and drop a kinetic rod on your coordinates. Nothing personal. We nuke the site, the Azra stays buried, and the balance of power doesn’t flip overnight. Everyone wins except you and a few thousand indentured who should have known better.” Arthur stared at the cube. It was warm to the touch. “Alternative,” Rao continued, “you sign transfer to Earth authority. We extract under controlled conditions, use the proceeds to buy out every indenture on Mars. Everyone goes free. You become the hero of the revolution. Posthumously, probably, but still.” Arthur picked up the cube. It weighed almost nothing. “You’d kill two hundred people to keep the peace?” “I’ve killed more for less.” Rao’s voice was flat. “This isn’t about people. It’s about centuries.” Arthur put the cube down carefully. “Get out.” Rao stood. “You’ve got nine hours. After that, physics makes the decision.” After he left, Arthur sat in the dark and lied to himself three different ways. He told himself he would sign with the Consortium to save Katya’s people. Then he told himself he would sign with Earth to save Mars from the Consortium. Then he told himself he would sign nothing and damn them all. None of the lies felt better than the others. He opened the book again and stared at the page until the words swam. Somewhere far below, machines were eating his home one bite at a time. He whispered to the empty room, “I don’t know what to do.” The room, of course, did not answer.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 4 executes its three-visitor structure with admirable economy and discipline. The plan called for three covert meetings—Consortium lawyer, Katya, Earth admiral—and the chapter delivers all three with distinct tonal registers and escalating stakes. The word count discipline is maintained, and the chapter advances the plot while deepening Arthur's isolation. The strongest element is Katya's visit. Her entrance—door opened without a chime, stolen uniform two sizes too big, bruise blooming, blood on knuckles—is rendered with precise, earned detail rather than cinematic flourish. The near-touch ("He reached out, stopped himself before touching the bruise") is the kind of restrained emotional beat that Asimov's best character work aspired to, and it lands cleanly. Her dialogue is consistent with her profile: direct, funny in a pained way, protective. "Don't say anything you'll regret. Just don't sign" is exactly right for her character. Park's scene is competent but thinner. "Face like a worried weasel" is a functional simile, not a forced one, though it's the kind of thing that could appear in any thriller. The dialogue here is serviceable rather than distinctive—Park functions primarily as an information delivery mechanism, and his characterization doesn't rise above "nervous fixer." The chapter plan described him as a "Consortium lawyer," but he arrives as a maintenance tech, which is a reasonable adaptation that adds texture. Admiral Rao is the most interesting of the three visitors structurally—his offer is the most morally complex (kill two hundred to save thousands, or centuries)—but his characterization is the flattest. "The expression of a man who had stopped sleeping years ago" is a cliché. His dialogue is functional but doesn't crackle. The black cube is an effective prop, though its significance remains deliberately opaque, which is appropriate for this stage of the story. The chapter's ending is its weakest section. "He lied to himself three different ways" followed by the three enumerated lies is a tell-don't-show moment—the author summarizes Arthur's internal conflict rather than dramatizing it. The final lines ("Somewhere far below, machines were eating his home one bite at a time. He whispered to the empty room, 'I don't know what to do.' The room, of course, did not answer.") are the chapter's most amateurish passage. "The room, of course, did not answer" is the kind of ironic aside that feels like a writer winking at the reader, and it undercuts the genuine pathos of Arthur's admission. It's the sort of line that tries to be dryly Asimovian but instead reads as self-conscious. The chapter plan specified that Arthur "lies to all three" and "doesn't know which lie is the worst." The chapter delivers this, but the lying is somewhat passive—he tells Park he'll think about it, tells Katya nothing (which is its own lie), and tells Rao to get out. The plan's promise of active deception is only partially fulfilled; Arthur is more paralyzed than strategically deceptive here, which is actually more interesting psychologically but slightly misaligns with the plan's framing. The Asimov retrofuturistic vibe is maintained through restraint rather than decoration—no purple dust storms, no heroic posturing. The chapter's pacing is tight. The emotional register is appropriately dry and occasionally cruel. The darkness is earned through accumulation rather than melodrama. The metaphor "machines were eating his home one bite at a time" is functional but not particularly fresh. "Kill-teams chewing through his overburden like rats through insulation" from earlier in the chapter is better—more specific, more Arthur's voice. Overall, this is a solid chapter that executes its plan competently, with one genuinely strong scene (Katya), one adequate scene (Park), one underdeveloped scene (Rao), and a weak ending that reaches for Asimovian irony and misses. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 7 Tell-Don't-Show: 8 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Chapter 5 – “The Footnote That Should Have Stayed Buried” Arthur stopped sleeping. He floated through the stacks at odd hours, following whispers of cross-references like a man chasing ghosts. The librarians ignored him now; he had become furniture. He found Singh’s full dissertation buried in a mis-catalogued folder labelled “Historical Curiosities—Pre-Combine.” It was 312 pages of dense equations and one photograph: a younger R. Singh standing beside a core sample, smiling like someone who still believed warnings mattered. Arthur read it twice. Then he read it in the shower, water running cold because he forgot to adjust the temperature. Then he read it while eating something that was supposed to be eggs. The conclusion was worse in full. The cascade wasn’t about heat. It was about entropy propagation through the planet’s iron-nickel lattice. Azra didn’t just speed decay; it synchronised it. Once the vein reached critical mass and purity, every radioactive isotope in the crust would begin ticking at the same accelerated rate. Quietly. Politely. Over decades. Eighty to a hundred and twenty years until the background radiation made surface habitation impossible. Another fifty until the magnetosphere collapsed from induced currents. Mars would die of old age while people were still living on it. The Combine had known since 2289. They had classified it, bought up every adjacent claim, and waited for the one idiot who would drill deep enough to expose the motherlode. Arthur was the idiot. He sat in the dark of his suite with Singh’s photograph pulled up on the tablet and stared at the dead man’s smile until it felt like an accusation. On the seventh night he asked the terminal for whiskey again. It delivered a whole bottle. He drank half and vomited most of it back up, then drank more because the taste in his mouth was worse than the whiskey. At 04:12 he opened a new file and started writing the thing he had been avoiding. A letter. Not to Cho, not to Katya, not even to the miners holding his claim with stolen lasers. To his mother. He wrote about the slow leak in sector four and how he had finally fixed it last year. About the way the dust looked at sunrise when Phobos was just a bright star. About how he had never told anyone he loved them because the words felt like spending air you might need later. He wrote that he was sorry he hadn’t been smarter. That he had found something beautiful and deadly and now everyone was going to pay for it. He did not write that he was scared. He was, but it felt too small a word. When the bottle was empty he fell asleep on the floor with his cheek against the cool decking and dreamed of his mother’s hand on his forehead checking for fever that wasn’t there. He woke to find Cho sitting in the single chair, watching him with the mild curiosity of a man observing an insect that had learned a new trick. “You’ve been busy,” Cho said. He held a printout—Arthur’s letter, somehow. “Touching. Really. Your mother would be proud.” Arthur pushed himself up. His mouth tasted like battery acid. “It’s true, isn’t it?” His voice came out ragged. “The cascade. You need my cooperation because if you just take it the feedback loop starts early. Months instead of centuries.” Cho folded the letter neatly and tucked it into his pocket. “Very good. Yes. Forced extraction collapses the wave function, as it were. We need the claim transferred willingly, peacefully, with full legal cover. Then we control the rate. We decide who gets how much, how fast. We decide when Mars dies, and we make sure it dies profitably.” Arthur laughed. It hurt his throat. “You knew the whole time.” “Of course.” Cho stood. “History is a machine, Mr Neegan. Machines require lubrication. Mars is the oil.” Arthur looked at the empty bottle, then at Cho’s perfect hands. “I want to see Katya.” “She’s dead,” Cho said gently. “The miners’ resistance collapsed twelve hours ago. Your foreman made a brave stand at shaft seven. She took four of my men with her.” The room went very quiet. Arthur felt something inside him break with a sound like ice cracking on a habitat wall. “You’re lying,” he whispered. “I’m really not.” Cho walked to the door. “Tomorrow we will try again. You’ll sign. Grief clarifies the mind wonderfully.” The door closed. Arthur sat on the floor until the lights dimmed for night cycle. Then he picked up the tablet and opened Singh’s paper one more time. He read the conclusion out loud, slowly, tasting every word. When he reached the end he closed the file and stared at the blank screen for a long time. Then he started a new one. This time he didn’t write a letter. He wrote a confession.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 5 is a strong piece of writing that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me examine it carefully across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter successfully executes its central revelation — the entropy propagation discovery — with admirable restraint. The science is presented through Arthur's comprehension rather than exposition dumps, which keeps it grounded in character. The detail that Azra "synchronised" decay rather than merely accelerating it is a genuinely interesting scientific wrinkle that feels earned rather than arbitrary. The pacing is well-managed. The chapter moves from intellectual discovery to emotional devastation (Katya's reported death) to quiet resolve, and each transition feels motivated. The letter to his dead mother is a particularly effective choice — it reveals character without being maudlin, and the detail about "spending air you might need later" as a metaphor for saying "I love you" is one of the chapter's best lines, entirely consistent with Arthur's established voice. Cho's appearance is well-handled. His line "History is a machine, Mr Neegan. Machines require lubrication. Mars is the oil" is exactly the kind of cold, elegant villainy the character profile demands — it's quotable without being purple. His casual cruelty in delivering Katya's death and then framing grief as a negotiating tool ("Grief clarifies the mind wonderfully") is chilling and consistent with his profile. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: Arthur finds the 2289 paper, understands the cascade, realizes the Combine has known for decades, and ends beginning his confession. The plan is executed with discipline. WEAKNESSES: The chapter occasionally tells rather than shows emotional states. "Something inside him break with a sound like ice cracking on a habitat wall" is a forced simile — ice cracking on a habitat wall is not a particularly evocative sound, and the construction "felt like" followed by a physical metaphor is doing work the prose should accomplish through behavior instead. Similarly, "tasting every word" when reading aloud is a cliché. The whiskey-and-vomiting sequence is slightly rushed. The emotional weight of discovering you've doomed a planet deserves more than a paragraph of drinking and vomiting. The chapter plan called for Arthur spending time in the stacks, and while the opening delivers this, the emotional processing feels compressed. The Cho scene, while effective, has one weak moment: "Very good. Yes." reads as slightly theatrical villain-speak. Cho as written in the profile is more subtle than this — he would likely not confirm so directly and immediately. The line "He read the conclusion out loud, slowly, tasting every word" before the final pivot to writing a confession is a bit of a writerly gesture that doesn't quite land — it's meant to feel ritualistic but reads as slightly precious. The chapter's darkness is well-earned overall. Katya's reported death lands hard precisely because it's delivered so casually by Cho, and the reader (having seen the chapter plan) suspects it may be a lie, which adds a layer of dread. The ending — beginning the confession — is appropriately quiet and purposeful. The Asimov retrofuturistic voice is maintained reasonably well, though Asimov himself would likely have more dialogue and less interior monologue. The chapter leans slightly more literary than classic Asimov, but this is a stylistic choice that mostly works. Character consistency is strong. Arthur's stubbornness, his emotional constipation (the letter to his dead mother rather than to living people), his intellectual persistence — all consistent. Cho's cold paternalism is well-rendered. Overall this is a competent-to-good chapter with some genuine moments of excellence and a few minor craft failures. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Chapter 6 – “The Rescue That Wasn’t” They came for him at 02:17 ship time, three figures in stolen charcoal uniforms, faces masked with breathers pulled high. Arthur was sitting on the floor, back against the bedframe, the tablet balanced on his knees. He had been typing for nineteen straight hours. The confession was at 4,312 words and growing. The lead figure raised a hand: wait. Then pulled the mask down. Katya. Her face was thinner, the scar on her cheek livid under the emergency lighting. She had a pulse rifle slung across her chest and blood on her sleeve that wasn’t hers. Arthur stood up so fast his vision blacked out at the edges. “You’re dead,” he said stupidly. “Not yet.” She crossed the room in four strides and grabbed his arm hard enough to bruise. “Move. Shuttle’s hot, window closes in eleven minutes.” Behind her the other two miners were already at the door, covering the corridor. Arthur let her pull him. His legs worked without permission. They ran. Down service corridors that stank of coolant and fear, past maintenance hatches Katya knew from some previous life she had never mentioned. One of the miners took a stunner hit in the thigh and went down cursing in Russian. Katya shot the guard twice in the chest without breaking stride. Arthur’s lungs burned. He hadn’t run since he was twenty-five. They reached the docking spur. A battered ore shuttle waited, ramp down, engines whining. Someone had painted FREE MARS in red letters across the hull, still wet. Katya shoved him up the ramp. “Strap in.” The shuttle lurched free before the ramp was fully closed. Alarms howled. Through the viewport Arthur saw the habitat ring recede, lights blinking out one by one as lockdown procedures kicked in. Katya dropped into the copilot seat, hands flying over the controls. The pilot was a kid, maybe nineteen, face pale and set. “We did it,” the kid said, voice cracking. “Holy shit, we actually did it.” Katya didn’t answer. She was staring at the rear sensors. Arthur unstrapped and moved forward. “Katya—” She turned then. Her eyes were wrong. Too bright. “Arthur,” she said softly, “you should have signed.” The shuttle shuddered as docking clamps engaged from outside. “No,” Arthur whispered. The ramp opened again. Charcoal uniforms poured in, weapons raised. The young pilot reached for his sidearm and took three rounds in the face. Katya didn’t move. She just looked at Arthur with something that might have been pity. The last miner tried to fight. He lasted four seconds. Then it was just Arthur and Katya and the smell of burnt meat. A Combine officer stepped aboard, helmet under his arm. “Foreman Volkov. Report.” Katya stood. “Package secured. Minimal resistance.” Arthur stared at her. The words weren’t making sense. “You’ve been very brave, Mr Neegan,” the officer said. “The foreman here volunteered to sell you hope at a premium. Hope is a powerful sedative.” Katya wouldn’t meet his eyes. Arthur found his voice. “Katya.” She flinched. “How much?” he asked. She laughed once, a sound like breaking glass. “Enough to buy out every indenture still breathing. Enough to get my sister off that rock before it kills her too.” Her voice cracked on the last word. The officer gestured. Two guards grabbed Arthur’s arms. Katya finally looked at him. “I’m sorry,” she said. And then, quieter: “You should have signed, you stupid bastard.” They shot her in the stomach with her own rifle. She folded slowly, hands pressed to the wound, blood already pooling black under the emergency lights. Arthur screamed. It came out raw and animal and nothing like his voice at all. They dragged him past her. He managed to get one hand free and reached for her fingers. She squeezed once, hard, then let go. The last thing he saw before they shoved him into the cruiser’s brig was Katya’s blood spreading across the deck in the perfect shape of Mars. They kept him sedated for the trip back. When he woke the newsfeeds were screaming his name. ARTHUR NEEGAN MASSACRE—MINERS BETRAYED—WANTED FOR MURDER His face everywhere, frozen in a frame grabbed from the shuttle’s security cam, eyes wide, mouth open in that scream. He stared at the screen until the words stopped meaning anything. Home was gone. Katya was gone. Everything was gone. He curled into the corner of the cell and waited for them to come finish the job. They never did. Instead they left him alone with the dark and the sound of his own breathing and the knowledge that he had eleven hours until they put him back in front of Cho. Eleven hours to decide what one honest man could do when the entire system wanted him broken. He laughed then. It sounded like Katya’s laugh, only worse. He stopped laughing and started thinking.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 6 executes the plan with considerable skill, though not without some stumbles. Let me work through this carefully. The chapter follows the plan closely: Katya leads the rescue, it turns out to be a Combine operation, Katya is shot, and Arthur is framed for the massacre. The plan called for Katya to say "You should have signed, you stupid bastard" as her last words, and this is delivered. The chapter hits its marks. Voice and character: Arthur's characterization is largely consistent with the profile. His stunned, animal reaction ("You're dead," he said stupidly) rings true to the socially awkward, emotionally constipated man described. The detail of his legs working "without permission" is good. Katya's betrayal is handled with genuine complexity—she sold him out to buy freedom for indentured workers including her sister, which is consistent with her profile's fierce protectiveness and her allegiance to the indentured. This moral ambiguity is the chapter's strongest element. The dialogue is mostly functional and occasionally sharp. "Hope is a powerful sedative" from the officer is a good line. Katya's explanation of her motives ("Enough to buy out every indenture still breathing") is economical and effective. The repeated "You should have signed, you stupid bastard" lands because it's been set up in the plan and carries the weight of her character. Where the chapter struggles: The action sequence is competent but somewhat generic—the corridor run, the stunner hit, the shuttle escape. It reads like standard thriller mechanics rather than Asimov-inflected science fiction. The retrofuturistic, intellectual quality of the prompt is largely absent during the action. "Katya's blood spreading across the deck in the perfect shape of Mars" is the chapter's most egregious moment. This is exactly the kind of forced, overwrought metaphor the writing plan explicitly warned against—"No cinematic sweeps, no heroic silhouettes." It's a purple flourish that doesn't earn itself and feels imported from a different, more melodramatic story. The plan's stated intention was to avoid precisely this kind of thing. The ending pivot—Arthur going from grief to laughter to thinking—is handled adequately but feels slightly rushed. The plan called for him to be "returned to custody" with the newsfeeds announcing his guilt, and this is delivered, but the emotional processing feels compressed in a way that slightly undermines the darkness. "It sounded like Katya's laugh, only worse" is a decent line that earns its sentiment without overreaching. The young pilot taking "three rounds in the face" is appropriately brutal and unsentimental, consistent with the tone targets. The chapter is well-paced for its length and doesn't overstay its welcome. The betrayal structure is genuinely affecting because Katya's motivation is understandable—she's not a villain, she's a person who made an impossible choice. This is the chapter's real achievement. The "FREE MARS in red letters across the hull, still wet" detail is good—specific, visual, and quietly ironic given what follows. Overall: a strong chapter with one significant tonal misstep (the Mars-shaped blood) and some generic action-sequence writing, but the emotional core is solid and the character work is mostly excellent. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 8 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 14

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Chapter 7 – “The Only Thing Left to Sell” The cell was two metres by three, polished steel, no sharp edges. They had taken everything except the jumpsuit and the tablet they’d forgotten to confiscate when they dragged him off the fake rescue shuttle. Arthur sat cross-legged on the floor, tablet balanced on his knees, fingers moving over the screen like a man playing a piano only he could hear. He had eleven hours. He started with the confession. All of it. Singh’s paper, the cascade equations, the 80–120 year clock ticking under every dome on Mars. The Combine’s need for his voluntary signature. Katya’s blood on the deck shaped like a planet dying. He recorded it straight to camera, no edits. His voice cracked on her name. He let it. Then he attached every scrap he had stolen from the stacks: the footnote, the censored dissertation, the assay maps, the refinement protocol he had pieced together from fragments like a child with a broken toy. At the eight-hour mark he added a cover note. This is what they wouldn’t let you know. Do whatever you want with it. Just leave me out of it. Arthur Neegan He set the file to broadcast on every open frequency, every newsfeed, every stock exchange, every naval command channel. No encryption. No delay. Then he hesitated. One more thing. He opened the camera again and looked straight into it. His eyes were red, his face grey with stubble and exhaustion. He looked like a man who had already died and forgotten to lie down. “I’m sorry,” he said to whoever would be watching. “I didn’t know how to save anyone. This is all I’ve got left.” He hit send. For three full minutes nothing happened. The tablet showed the little spinning wheel that meant uploading. Arthur waited for the guards to burst in. For Cho to appear with that gentle, terrible smile. For the ship to vent his cell into space. Instead the lights flickered. The tablet chimed: transmission complete. Arthur laughed once, a sound like gravel in a blender, and turned the screen off. The door opened ten minutes later. Four guards, rifles raised. They looked almost scared. He stood up slowly, hands visible. They didn’t speak. They just cuffed him and marched him to the observation lounge. Cho was waiting. The walls were transparent. Outside, the stars looked different—sharper, colder. The lounge smelled of oranges again. Cho stood with his back to the view, hands clasped behind him. “You are a difficult man, Mr Neegan.” Arthur’s mouth was dry. “You saw it.” “Everyone saw it.” Cho’s voice was soft, almost wondering. “The markets opened twenty minutes ago on Luna. Trading in Azra futures has been suspended indefinitely. Three governments have issued statements. The miners’ council on Mars has declared a general strike. Someone leaked your confession to the indentured comms net. They are reading it aloud in every dome right now.” Arthur felt his knees buckle. The guards held him up. Cho turned then, and for the first time the smile was gone. “You have broken the equation,” he said quietly. “Three hundred years of calculation. Gone.” Arthur found his voice. “Good.” Cho studied him for a long moment. “You understand what happens now.” “Mars dies anyway,” Arthur said. “But at least it dies free.” Cho nodded once, almost approving. “Yes. An untidy freedom. But freedom.” He gestured to the guards. “Take him back to his cell. No restraints.” Arthur stopped in the doorway. “Cho.” The minister raised an eyebrow. “Was any of it real?
